Traditional safety treats the robot like a knife stored in a locked drawer (caged, isolated, only the expert opens it). Collaborative robot (cobot) safety is the **modern safety razor** — engineered with built-in guards, sensors, and force limiters so even a beginner can use it without injury. The robot *itself* is made safe, not just *the environment* around it.

**Why this matters:** When a robotic arm moves at 2 m/s with a 10 kg payload, the kinetic energy alone can be lethal. Hence, safety is not an "add-on" but a **foundational design constraint** that dictates the mechanical, electronic, and software architecture of every robotic cell.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical & Regulatory Constants to Memorize:**
> - **Maximum safe collaborative robot speed:** $\mathbf{250 \text{ mm/s}}$ (ISO/TS 15066 quasi-static contact threshold)
> - **Maximum allowable transient contact force (neck region):** $\mathbf{140 \text{ N}}$
> - **Maximum stop time (Category 1 stop):** $\mathbf{\leq 1 \text{ second}}$ (per IEC 60204-1)
> - **Safety Integrity Level (SIL) for robotic E-Stop:** **SIL 2 or SIL 3** (per IEC 61508)
> - **Performance Level (PL) for safety functions:** **PL d minimum** (per ISO 13849-1)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hierarchy of Safety Controls (Defense-in-Depth)

KTU examiners frequently test whether students know the **order of priority** when applying safety measures. The hierarchy, mandated by **ISO 12100**, is:

1. **Inherent Safe Design** — Remove the hazard at the design stage (e.g., reduce robot reach, lower max payload, round sharp edges).
2. **Safeguarding / Engineering Controls** — Physical barriers, light curtains, safety mats, interlocks.
3. **Complementary Protective Measures** — Emergency stops, two-hand controls, awareness signals.
4. **Information for Use** — Training, signage, manuals (administrative control — *weakest* layer).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Rule:** Always apply controls from **top to bottom** of this hierarchy. A student who suggests "give the operator a manual" first loses marks; the correct first answer is always *design out the hazard*.

## 2.2 Categories of Robot Hazards

| Hazard Category | Example | Typical Safeguard |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical** | Crushing, shearing, entanglement | Cages, interlocks, force limiters |
| **Electrical** | Shock from capacitors/conductors | Lockout/Tagout, insulation |
| **Pneumatic/Hydraulic** | Sudden release of stored energy | Dump valves, bleeder valves |
| **Thermal** | Hot end-effector, welding sparks | Heat-resistant guarding, PPE |
| **Radiation** | Laser, arc-welding UV | Enclosures, UV-absorbing screens |
| **Ergonomic** | Repetitive loading during teach-pendant use | Reduced payload pendants, posture training |
| **Psychological** | Stress, complacency in cobot cells | Shift rotation, training |

## 2.3 Collaborative Robot Safety Modes (ISO/TS 15066)

This is a **high-yield KTU topic**. The standard defines **four collaborative operation modes**, and the question *"List and explain the four modes of collaborative operation"* appears almost every academic year.

1. **Safety-Rated Monitored Stop (SRMS):**
   The robot stops when a human enters the collaborative workspace. It cannot auto-resume until the human leaves. *No contact is allowed.*
2. **Hand-Guiding:**
   The operator physically grasps a handle on the end-effector; the robot moves only as directed. Force sensors detect intent.
3. **Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM):**
   The robot's speed is dynamically reduced as the human's separation distance decreases. The **protective separation distance** is given by:

$$
S_p(t_0) = v_{robot} \cdot T_r + v_{human} \cdot T_s + S_{int} + Z_b + Z_r
$$

   Where:
   - $S_p$ = Protective separation distance (m)
   - $v_{robot}$ = Robot approach speed (m/s)
   - $v_{human}$ = Human approach speed (assumed **1.6 m/s** per ISO/TS 15066)
   - $T_r$ = Robot reaction time (s)
   - $T_s$ = System stopping time (s)
   - $S_{int}$ = Intrusion distance (m)
   - $Z_b$ = Human position uncertainty (m)
   - $Z_r$ = Robot position uncertainty (m)

4. **Power and Force Limiting (PFL):**
   The robot's mechanical design and control loops ensure that any contact event produces forces below biomechanical thresholds. This is the only mode where **physical contact is permitted by design**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Karnopp Model — Energy-Based Contact Force Estimation:**
> For transient contact between robot and human tissue, the peak contact force is modeled as:
> $$F_{peak} = \sqrt{k \cdot m_{eff} \cdot v^2 + F_0^2}$$
> Where $k$ is the contact stiffness, $m_{eff}$ is the effective mass of the robot link, and $v$ is the relative velocity. The force must stay below the **140 N neck threshold** to be considered safe.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Standard | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Protective Separation Distance | $S_p = v_r T_r + v_h T_s + S_{int} + Z_b + Z_r$ | meters | SSM mode; ISO/TS 15066 |
| Peak Contact Force (Karnopp) | $F_{peak} = \sqrt{k m v^2 + F_0^2}$ | Newtons (N) | PFL mode |
| Stopping Distance | $d = v_0 t_{react} + \dfrac{v_0^2}{2 a_{decel}}$ | meters | $a_{decel}$ = deceleration |
| Kinetic Energy of Robot | $KE = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Joules (J) | Energy = fundamental hazard |
| Mechanical Advantage Risk | $F_{out} = F_{in} \times \dfrac{d_{in}}{d_{out}}$ | Newtons | Pinch points |
| Safety Reliability (PFH) | $PFH_d \leq 10^{-6}$ to $10^{-8}$ | 1/hr | PL d / SIL 2 |
| Mean Time To Dangerous Failure | $MTTF_d$ | years | Must be ≥ 30 yrs (PL d) |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These formulas are not academic exercises. Every collaborative robot cell in a Toyota, BMW, or Bosch factory is laid out using the **protective separation distance** equation. A miscalculation by even **100 mm** can result in a serious injury, which is why ISO/TS 15066 is enforced under CE marking and OSHA regulations globally.

## 2.5 Safety Sensor Technologies (PCRUT205 Cross-Reference)

Because this course is *Sensor Technology for Robotics*, the examiner expects you to **link safety measures to specific sensor modalities**:

| Safety Sensor | Operating Principle | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Light Curtain (AOPD)** | Array of infrared emitter-receiver beams | Perimeter guarding |
| **Safety Mat (Pressure-Sensitive)** | Short-circuit when stepped on | Floor-level guarding |
| **Safety Laser Scanner (LIDAR)** | Time-of-Flight laser | Mobile robot collision avoidance |
| **Tactile Skin Sensor** | Capacitive / piezoresistive | Cobot force limiting |
| **Vision-Based Safety Camera** | 3D depth / stereo vision | Dynamic SSM |
| **Capacitive Proximity Sensor** | Detects body capacitance | Hand-guiding mode |
| **Current/Torque Sensor** | Measures joint current deviation | Collision detection |
| **E-Stop Pushbutton (Mushroom)** | Mechanical normally-closed contact | Category 0 / 1 stop |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-Module Note:** This topic is heavily integrated with Module 2 (*Sensors in Robotics*) and Module 4 (*Robot Vision*). Many KTU questions combine them — e.g., *"Explain how a laser scanner implements speed-and-separation monitoring."*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Protective Separation Distance (SSM)

The **protective separation distance** $S_p$ is derived from the principle that, at any moment, the minimum permissible distance between the human and the moving robot must be sufficient for the robot to come to a **complete, safe stop** before contact occurs. We derive it step-by-step.

**Step 1 — Define the reference instant.**
Let $t_0$ be the moment when the safety system *detects* the human at the boundary of the collaborative workspace.

**Step 2 — Compute the distance the robot travels while reacting.**
During the **robot reaction time** $T_r$, the robot is unaware and continues at its current speed $v_{robot}$. The distance covered is:

$$
d_{robot, react} = v_{robot} \cdot T_r
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the distance the robot travels while stopping.**
The safety system issues a stop command at $t_0 + T_r$. The robot then decelerates from $v_{robot}$ to $0$ with constant deceleration $a$. The stopping distance is:

$$
d_{robot, stop} = \frac{v_{robot}^2}{2 a}
$$

**Step 4 — Account for human intrusion during the same window.**
During the system stopping time $T_s$ (the time for the safety output to physically halt motion), the human continues to advance at $v_{human} = 1.6 \text{ m/s}$:

$$
d_{human, advance} = v_{human} \cdot T_s
$$

**Step 5 — Add positional uncertainties and intrusion distance.**
To guarantee safety, we add the robot position uncertainty $Z_r$, human position uncertainty $Z_b$, and the intrusion distance $S_{int}$ (the distance the human's hand can travel through a sensing field before being reliably detected).

**Step 6 — Sum all contributions.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_p &= d_{robot, react} + d_{robot, stop} + d_{human, advance} + S_{int} + Z_b + Z_r \\
S_p &= v_{robot} \cdot T_r + \frac{v_{robot}^2}{2 a} + v_{human} \cdot T_s + S_{int} + Z_b + Z_r
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Practical simplification (ISO/TS 15066 form).**
The standard combines $T_r$ and $T_s$ into a single "total system stopping time" and absorbs the deceleration term implicitly, yielding the familiar engineering form used in cobot cell design:

$$
S_p(t_0) = v_{robot} \cdot T_{react, total} + v_{human} \cdot T_s + S_{int} + Z_b + Z_r
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Peak Contact Force (Power and Force Limiting)

**Step 1 — System model.**
Consider a robot link of effective mass $m_{eff}$ moving at velocity $v$ that strikes a stationary human body region (e.g., forearm) modeled as a spring of stiffness $k$ with an initial contact preload $F_0$.

**Step 2 — Energy balance.**
The kinetic energy of the moving link is $KE = \frac{1}{2} m_{eff} v^2$. On contact, this energy is partly stored in the spring and partly dissipated. At maximum compression, the spring force $F_{peak}$ satisfies:

$$
\frac{1}{2} m_{eff} v^2 + \frac{F_0^2}{2k} = \frac{F_{peak}^2}{2k}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $F_{peak}$.**
Multiplying both sides by $2k$:

$$
m_{eff} v^2 \cdot k + F_0^2 = F_{peak}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Final expression.**

$$
\boxed{F_{peak} = \sqrt{k \cdot m_{eff} \cdot v^2 + F_0^2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example (typical KTU problem).**
A cobot link of $m_{eff} = 5 \text{ kg}$ moving at $v = 0.25 \text{ m/s}$ contacts a human forearm with $k = 75{,}000 \text{ N/m}$ and $F_0 = 5 \text{ N}$. Compute $F_{peak}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{peak} &= \sqrt{75{,}000 \cdot 5 \cdot (0.25)^2 + 5^2} \\
&= \sqrt{75{,}000 \cdot 5 \cdot 0.0625 + 25} \\
&= \sqrt{23{,}437.5 + 25} \\
&= \sqrt{23{,}462.5} \\
&\approx 153.18 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **This value (153 N) exceeds the ISO/TS 15066 forearm threshold of 140 N for quasi-static contact.** Therefore, the speed must be reduced. By trial, $v \leq 0.235 \text{ m/s}$ is required for safe operation.

---

## 3.3 Stopping Distance of a Robot Arm

**Step 1 — Kinematic setup.**
Initial velocity $v_0$, reaction time $t_{react}$ (robot still moving), deceleration $a$ (constant).

**Step 2 — Reaction phase.**

$$
d_1 = v_0 \cdot t_{react}
$$

**Step 3 — Braking phase (using $v^2 = v_0^2 + 2 a s$ with final $v = 0$):**

$$
d_2 = \frac{v_0^2}{2 a}
$$

**Step 4 — Total stopping distance.**

$$
\boxed{d_{total} = v_0 \cdot t_{react} + \frac{v_0^2}{2 a}}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example.**
A robot arm moves at $v_0 = 1.5 \text{ m/s}$, $t_{react} = 0.05 \text{ s}$, $a = 6 \text{ m/s}^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{total} &= 1.5 \times 0.05 + \frac{(1.5)^2}{2 \times 6} \\
&= 0.075 + \frac{2.25}{12} \\
&= 0.075 + 0.1875 \\
&= 0.2625 \text{ m} \approx 26.25 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineer's Note:** A robot moving at only 1.5 m/s stops in **over 26 cm**! This is why safety sensors must be positioned *upstream* of the hazard zone — a 26 cm overrun easily crushes a human hand.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Safety Architectures

| Parameter | Industrial Caged Robot | Collaborative Robot (Cobot) |
|---|---|---|
| Workspace | **Isolated**, fenced | **Shared** with human |
| Primary Safety | Physical barrier + interlocks | Force/torque limiting + sensors |
| Speed | Up to 6 m/s | Typically ≤ 1.5 m/s; SSM-mandated reduction |
| Stop Type | Category 0 / 1 (hard stop) | Category 2 (safe stop, then monitor) |
| Standards | ISO 10218-1/-2 | ISO 10218 + ISO/TS 15066 |
| Contact Permitted? | **No** | Yes, if force-limited |
| Risk Level after mitigation | Very Low | Low (only if PFL/SSM correctly applied) |
| Typical Sensor Stack | Light curtains, safety mats, E-stop | Tactile skin, joint torque, vision, LIDAR |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Robot Safety Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H[Hazard Source: Robot Arm Motion] --> R[Risk Assessment per ISO 12100]
    R --> D1[Inherent Safe Design]
    R --> D2[Engineering Safeguards]
    R --> D3[Complementary Protective Devices]
    R --> D4[Administrative Information]

    D1 --> M1[Reduced reach and payload]
    D1 --> M2[Rounded end-effector geometry]
    D1 --> M3[Force-limited joint actuators]

    D2 --> S1[Physical fencing and interlocked gates]
    D2 --> S2[Safety-rated light curtains]
    D2 --> S3[Pressure-sensitive safety mats]
    D2 --> S4[Safety laser scanner LIDAR]

    D3 --> P1[Emergency stop pushbutton]
    D3 --> P2[Two-hand control station]
    D3 --> P3[Audible and visual warning signals]
    D3 --> P4[Enable switch on teach pendant]

    D4 --> A1[Operator training modules]
    D4 --> A2[Signage and lockout tagout]

    M3 --> CC[Collaborative Mode Decision]
    S4 --> CC
    P1 --> CC

    CC --> SRMS[Safety Rated Monitored Stop]
    CC --> HG[Hand Guiding Mode]
    CC --> SSM[Speed and Separation Monitoring]
    CC --> PFL[Power and Force Limiting]

    SRMS --> SA[Safe State: Robot Halts]
    HG --> SA
    SSM --> SA
    PFL --> SA
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block: Speed and Separation Monitoring State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NormalOperation
    NormalOperation --> HumanDetected : Laser scanner detects intrusion
    HumanDetected --> SpeedReduced : SSM algorithm reduces v_robot
    SpeedReduced --> MonitoredStop : Distance below critical threshold
    MonitoredStop --> Resuming : Human exits workspace
    MonitoredStop --> SafeShutdown : E-stop triggered
    Resuming --> NormalOperation
    SafeShutdown --> [*]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block: Risk Assessment Workflow (ISO 12100)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Identify Hazards] --> B[Estimate Risk for Each Hazard]
    B --> C{Risk Acceptable?}
    C -- No --> D[Apply Risk Reduction Measures]
    D --> E[Re-estimate Residual Risk]
    E --> C
    C -- Yes --> F[Document and Operate]
    F --> G[Periodic Review]
    G --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block above is an *examinable concept*, not a literal drawing. In your KTU answer sheet, you may reproduce a simplified version of these flowcharts using rectangular boxes and arrows to earn full marks on "explain with block diagram" questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "collaborative robot" and list the four modes of collaborative operation as per ISO/TS 15066.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **collaborative robot (cobot)** is a robot designed for direct operation within a defined workspace in which a human and the robot can perform tasks concurrently, in accordance with ISO/TS 15066.
The **four collaborative modes** are:
1. **Safety-Rated Monitored Stop (SRMS)**
2. **Hand-Guiding**
3. **Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM)**
4. **Power and Force Limiting (PFL)**

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing modes = 3 marks)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**What is a Category 0 stop as per IEC 60204-1? How does it differ from a Category 1 stop?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A **Category 0 stop** is an *uncontrolled* stop where the power supply to the robot actuators is **immediately removed**, causing an uncontrolled halt due to friction and inertia.
- A **Category 1 stop** is a *controlled* stop where power is maintained until the robot has come to a complete stop, after which power is removed.
- Key difference: Category 0 is **immediate and uncontrolled**; Category 1 is **coordinated and controlled** before power cutoff.

*(1 mark Category 0 + 1 mark Category 1 + 1 mark difference = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the **four modes of collaborative robot operation** as per ISO/TS 15066, with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The four modes defined by ISO/TS 15066 are:

**1. Safety-Rated Monitored Stop (SRMS)** — When a human enters the collaborative workspace, the robot executes a safety-rated stop and remains halted. Auto-resume occurs only after the human exits the workspace and a deliberate restart signal is given. *Example:* an assembly cobot that pauses when a worker reaches into the cell.

**2. Hand-Guiding** — The operator manually moves the robot end-effector via a hand-held handle. Force/torque sensors detect the operator's intent and replicate motion. *Example:* teaching a welding path on a heavy steel component where the operator physically guides the torch.

**3. Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM)** — The robot dynamically adjusts its speed based on the operator's separation distance, computed using the protective separation distance equation. *Example:* a cobot on a conveyor where workers pass nearby; the cobot slows down as they approach.

**4. Power and Force Limiting (PFL)** — Through inherent mechanical design and control, the robot's contact forces during a collision remain below biomechanical injury thresholds. *Example:* lightweight cobots like UR5, KUKA LBR iiwa performing polishing tasks alongside humans.

*(1.5 marks per mode × 4 = 6 marks + 1 mark for example = 7 marks)*

---

**Q. (b)** A collaborative robot link of effective mass **5 kg** moves at **250 mm/s** and contacts a human forearm modeled as a spring with stiffness **75,000 N/m** and an initial preload of **5 N**. Using the Karnopp model, determine the **peak contact force** and state whether the operation is **safe** as per ISO/TS 15066 forearm threshold. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Identify given data.**
- $m_{eff} = 5 \text{ kg}$
- $v = 250 \text{ mm/s} = 0.25 \text{ m/s}$
- $k = 75{,}000 \text{ N/m}$
- $F_0 = 5 \text{ N}$
- ISO/TS 15066 forearm quasi-static threshold = **140 N**

**Step 2 — Apply Karnopp formula.**

$$
F_{peak} = \sqrt{k \cdot m_{eff} \cdot v^2 + F_0^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{peak} &= \sqrt{75{,}000 \times 5 \times (0.25)^2 + (5)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{75{,}000 \times 5 \times 0.0625 + 25} \\
&= \sqrt{23{,}437.5 + 25} \\
&= \sqrt{23{,}462.5} \\
&\approx 153.18 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with threshold.**
$153.18 \text{ N} > 140 \text{ N} \Rightarrow$ **NOT SAFE**.

**Step 5 — Recommend corrective action.**
Reduce speed. Solving $F_{peak} = 140$ N for $v$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
140 &= \sqrt{75{,}000 \times 5 \times v^2 + 25} \\
19{,}600 &= 375{,}000 \, v^2 + 25 \\
v^2 &= \frac{19{,}575}{375{,}000} = 0.0522 \\
v &\approx 0.228 \text{ m/s} \approx 228 \text{ mm/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus, the operating speed must be reduced to **228 mm/s or lower** for safe operation.

*(2 marks formula + 2 marks calculation + 1 mark threshold comparison + 2 marks recommendation = 7 marks)*

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the **hierarchy of risk reduction** as per **ISO 12100** with a suitable example from a robotic welding cell. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The ISO 12100 hierarchy (top-down priority) is:

**1. Inherent Safe Design (highest priority):**
- Eliminate the hazard or reduce risk by changing the robot itself.
- *Welding cell example:* Use a low-spatter welding process (e.g., MIG instead of stick) to reduce fume and UV hazards; round the torch tip to prevent puncture injuries.

**2. Safeguarding / Engineering Controls:**
- Add guards or protective devices.
- *Example:* Install **light curtains** at the perimeter of the welding cell; install **interlocked gates** that prevent robot motion when opened.

**3. Complementary Protective Measures:**
- Add devices that supplement safeguards.
- *Example:* Mount **emergency stop pushbuttons** (Category 0) at all operator stations; install **audible sirens** that sound before welding arc ignition.

**4. Information for Use (lowest priority):**
- Train operators, post warnings.
- *Example:* Mandatory PPE (auto-darkening welding helmets, flame-resistant gloves) and signage indicating "ARC RAY HAZARD".

**Conclusion:** Per ISO 12100, you must attempt *each level* in order, only falling back to lower-priority measures when higher ones are not feasible. *(1.75 marks per level × 4 = 7 marks)*

---

**Q. (b)** Describe the **working principle of a safety laser scanner** and explain how it is used to implement **Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM)** in a cobot cell. Derive the protective separation distance for a robot moving at **1.0 m/s**, with $T_r = 0.1 \text{ s}$, $T_s = 0.2 \text{ s}$, $S_{int} = 0.1 \text{ m}$, $Z_b = 0.05 \text{ m}$, $Z_r = 0.05 \text{ m}$, and human approach speed = 1.6 m/s. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Safety laser scanner working principle.**
A safety laser scanner (e.g., SICK microScan3) emits a rotating infrared laser beam and measures the **time-of-flight** of reflections from surrounding objects. It computes a 2D map of distances in a horizontal plane (typically 5–9 m radius, 275° field of view). It defines multiple **protective fields** (warning field, protective field) using configurable safety zones.

**Step 2 — Application to SSM.**
- The scanner detects the operator's position and feeds it to the cobot's safety controller in real time.
- The controller continuously computes $S_p$ and adjusts the robot's speed.
- If the operator crosses the warning field → speed reduction; if protective field is breached → safety-rated stop.

**Step 3 — Apply protective separation distance formula.**

$$
S_p = v_{robot} \cdot T_r + v_{human} \cdot T_s + S_{int} + Z_b + Z_r
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_p &= 1.0 \times 0.1 + 1.6 \times 0.2 + 0.1 + 0.05 + 0.05 \\
&= 0.1 + 0.32 + 0.1 + 0.05 + 0.05 \\
&= 0.62 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation.**
The operator must not approach closer than **62 cm** from the robot's nearest moving part when the robot is moving at 1.0 m/s. If the operator does, the safety controller reduces speed or triggers a safe stop.

*(2 marks working principle + 2 marks SSM link + 2 marks calculation + 1 mark interpretation = 7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (How Students Fail This Topic):**
> 1. **Confusing ISO 10218 with ISO/TS 15066.** ISO 10218 is for *all* industrial robots (caged or not); ISO/TS 15066 is *only* for collaborative operation. Writing them interchangeably = -2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the units.** Writing $v = 250$ instead of $v = 0.25$ m/s in the Karnopp calculation yields an answer that is **1000× too large** and the safety conclusion is wrong. Examiner deducts the entire 7 marks.
> 3. **Omitting the deceleration term.** In SSM, some students forget the $v^2/2a$ term, or substitute the human approach speed as 1.0 m/s instead of the standardized 1.6 m/s. Memorize the constants.
> 4. **Listing safety devices without classifying them.** Saying "use a light curtain" without mentioning it's an **Active Opto-electronic Protective Device (AOPD)** Type 4 per IEC 61496 loses the technical depth marks.
> 5. **Confusing PL (Performance Level) and SIL (Safety Integrity Level).** PL is from ISO 13849-1; SIL is from IEC 61508. Mixing them up = -1 mark.
> 6. **Skipping the "residual risk" step.** ISO 12100 explicitly requires *re-evaluation* after applying each safeguard. Students who stop after one measure lose 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Safety Measures in Robotics**

- **Standards to memorize (verbatim):** ISO 10218-1, ISO 10218-2, ISO/TS 15066, ISO 12100, ISO 13849-1, IEC 60204-1, IEC 61508, IEC 61496.
- **Four collaborative modes:** SRMS, Hand-Guiding, SSM, PFL — remember acronym *"S-S-S-P"* (Stop, Steer, Slow, Soft).
- **ISO/TS 15066 constants:** human approach speed = **1.6 m/s**; forearm quasi-static force limit = **140 N**; max cobot speed in PFL = **250 mm/s**.
- **Hierarchy of risk reduction:** Inherent design → Safeguarding → Complementary measures → Information. (Top-down priority.)
- **Stop categories:** Cat 0 (immediate, uncontrolled) vs Cat 1 (controlled, then power off) vs Cat 2 (controlled, power retained).
- **Safety sensor families:** Light curtain, safety mat, laser scanner, tactile skin, joint torque, E-stop.
- **Key formulas (3 must-know):**
  - $S_p = v_r T_r + v_h T_s + S_{int} + Z_b + Z_r$
  - $F_{peak} = \sqrt{k m v^2 + F_0^2}$
  - $d_{total} = v_0 t_{react} + v_0^2 / (2a)$
- **Risk assessment is iterative:** Identify → Estimate → Reduce → Re-estimate. Never a one-shot process.
- **Defense-in-depth principle:** A single safeguard is *never* enough; always stack multiple layers.
- **Safety Integrity:** Robotic E-Stops typically require **SIL 2 / PL d** minimum, often **SIL 3 / PL e** for high-risk applications.
- **Cross-link to Module 2:** Every safety sensor mentioned (LIDAR, force sensor, tactile skin) is covered in detail in the sensors module — expect combined questions.
- **Always justify with a standard:** A complete answer names the relevant ISO/IEC clause alongside the engineering solution.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Robots: Manipulator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT205 - SENSOR TECHNOLOGY FOR ROBOTICS (ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Robots: Manipulator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> A **Robot Manipulator** is a mechanically constructed, multi-link, multi-jointed kinematic chain (typically resembling a human arm) whose primary function is to grasp, move, or manipulate objects in space with programmable precision, repeatability, and autonomy. It is composed of a sequence of rigid bodies (**links**) interconnected by **joints** (revolute or prismatic), terminating in an **end-effector** that performs the required task.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, a manipulator is the **mechanical arm subsystem** of an industrial robot — the part that physically moves and interacts with the environment. It does **not** include the controller, sensors, or power supply on its own; rather, it is the **actuated mechanical structure** under their command.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Human Arm"

Think of a robot manipulator as a **mechanical twin of a human arm**:

| Human Arm Component | Robot Manipulator Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Shoulder | Base joint (provides large angular/linear motion) |
| Upper arm | First link |
| Elbow | Middle joint |
| Forearm | Second link |
| Wrist | Wrist joints (orientation control) |
| Palm / Fingers | End-effector (gripper, tool, welding torch) |

Just as your arm is built from segments connected at joints, a robot manipulator is a **serial chain** of rigid links connected at motorized joints. The brain (controller) commands the muscles (motors) to move the skeleton (links) so the hand (end-effector) reaches the target.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology for KTU Board Exams:**
> - **Link** — A rigid body between two joints.
> - **Joint** — The relative motion provider between two links. Two fundamental types exist: **Revolute (R)** and **Prismatic (P)**.
> - **Degree of Freedom (DOF)** — The number of independent coordinates required to fully specify the configuration of the manipulator.
> - **End-Effector** — The tool mounted at the distal (free) end of the manipulator (gripper, weld gun, spray nozzle, etc.).
> - **Workspace (or Work Envelope)** — The 3D region of space that the end-effector (or a reference point on it) can reach.
> - **Base** — The fixed link of the manipulator, anchored to the floor or work cell.
> - **Payload** — The maximum mass the manipulator can carry while maintaining rated performance.

### 1.3 The Three Foundational Properties

Every KTU board question on manipulators implicitly tests these three:

1. **Configuration (Geometry)** — The arrangement of R and P joints defines the **type** of manipulator.
2. **Degrees of Freedom** — The number of independent motions, typically **3 to 6** for industrial arms.
3. **Workspace Shape** — Determined jointly by link lengths and joint limits.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification Framework

Manipulators are classified along **three orthogonal axes** as per the KTU 2024 syllabus:

#### A. By Kinematic Configuration (Geometry of Joint Arrangement)
This is the **most important classification** for board questions and dictates the manipulator's name and use case.

1. **Cartesian (Gantry) Manipulator** — Joint sequence **P-P-P** (all three prismatic)
2. **Cylindrical Manipulator** — Joint sequence **R-P-P** (revolute at base, then two prismatic)
3. **Spherical (Polar) Manipulator** — Joint sequence **R-R-P** (two revolute, one prismatic)
4. **Articulated (Revolute / Anthropomorphic) Manipulator** — Joint sequence **R-R-R** (all revolute — the "human-arm" geometry)
5. **SCARA (Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm)** — Joint sequence **R-R-P** with **vertical prismatic axis** (compliant in X-Y, stiff in Z)
6. **Parallel Manipulator (e.g., Delta)** — Multiple kinematic chains connecting the base to a common end-effector platform

#### B. By Method of Control
- **Servo-Controlled Manipulators** — Closed-loop position/velocity control using feedback sensors (encoders, resolvers).
- **Non-Servo (Open-Loop / "Bang-Bang") Manipulators** — Pre-set stops, no feedback; cheap pick-and-place units.

#### C. By Drive / Power Source
- **Electric** (DC servo, stepper, brushless) — Clean, precise, dominant in modern industry.
- **Hydraulic** — High payload, used in heavy machinery, mining.
- **Pneumatic** — Light duty, fast, simple pick-and-place.

### 2.2 The Logic of R and P Joints — The "Why" Behind Each Type

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the joint arrangement defines the entire robot class:**
> A manipulator's dexterity, reach, payload, and workspace are all *consequences* of whether its joints are **rotary (R)** or **linear (P)**. The convention used in KTU textbooks lists joints from **base → end-effector**.

- **Three P joints (Cartesian):** Every motion is along a straight axis → produces a **rectangular prismatic workspace**. Easy to visualize, simple kinematics, low dexterity.
- **One R + two P (Cylindrical):** The base rotates (R), then radial extension (P) and vertical lift (P) → produces a **hollow cylindrical workspace**.
- **Two R + one P (Spherical):** Two rotations followed by linear extension → produces a **partial spherical shell workspace**.
- **Three R (Articulated):** All rotations, like a shoulder-elbow-wrist → produces a **roughly spherical (donut-like) workspace** with high dexterity and the ability to "reach around" obstacles.
- **Two R + one P (SCARA):** Two parallel-axis rotations in the horizontal plane + a vertical prismatic → **cylindrical workspace**, ideal for vertical assembly.
- **Parallel (Delta):** Three (or more) R-R chains in parallel → **dome-shaped workspace**, extreme speed.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **must-memorize cheat sheet** for any KTU ESE question on manipulators. Note the use of `\vert` instead of `\|` to preserve table syntax.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Degrees of Freedom (Planar / Spatial) | $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | Grübler's equation; $n$ = links, $j_1$ = 1-DOF lower pairs, $j_2$ = 2-DOF higher pairs |
| 2 | Cartesian Workspace Volume | $V_C = 8 \cdot d_x \cdot d_y \cdot d_z$ | A rectangular box; $d_x, d_y, d_z$ = full stroke in each axis (m³) |
| 3 | Cylindrical Workspace Volume | $V_{Cy} = \pi \cdot h \cdot (r_{max}^2 - r_{min}^2)$ | Hollow cylinder; $h$ = vertical stroke, $r$ = radial reach (m³) |
| 4 | Spherical Workspace Volume | $V_S = \frac{4}{3} \pi (R_{max}^3 - R_{min}^3)$ | Spherical shell; $R$ = radial reach (m³) |
| 5 | Articulated Workspace (Approx.) | $V_A \approx \frac{4}{3} \pi (L_1 + L_2)^3$ | Reachable sphere minus unreachable interior sphere; $L_1, L_2$ = link lengths (m³) |
| 6 | Resolution (Joint $i$) | $R_i = \frac{\text{Encoder counts}}{\text{Per radian (or meter)}}$ | Smallest increment the controller can command (rad or m) |
| 7 | Repeatability | $\sigma_r \approx \pm 3 \sigma_{pos}$ | Statistical measure; about **±0.025 mm** for precision arms |
| 8 | Payload-to-Weight Ratio | $P/W = \frac{m_{payload}}{m_{manipulator}}$ | Indicates mechanical efficiency (dimensionless) |
| 9 | Homogeneous Transform (Joint) | $T = \begin{bmatrix} R & p \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $4 \times 4$ matrix; $R$ = rotation, $p$ = position (m) |
| 10 | Joint Variable Vector | $q = [q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n]^T$ | Vector of all joint variables (rad or m) |
| 11 | Forward Kinematics | $x = f(q)$ | Joint → Cartesian mapping (m) |
| 12 | Inverse Kinematics | $q = f^{-1}(x)$ | Cartesian → Joint mapping; often multi-valued |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Cartesian** → CNC gantries, 3D printers, PCB assembly.
- **Cylindrical** → Older generation machine tending; palletizing.
- **Spherical** → Early welding, forging, die casting.
- **Articulated** → **Universal** — automotive welding, painting, material handling, palletizing.
- **SCARA** → High-speed **vertical assembly** (electronics, small-parts pick-and-place).
- **Parallel/Delta** → Ultra-fast pick-and-place (food packaging, pharmaceutical sorting).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Grübler's Equation for Degrees of Freedom

We will derive the **Grübler's formula** for a planar mechanism, which is the foundation for DOF counting in manipulators.

**Step 1 — Set Up the Counting Problem**

A manipulator in 3D space, with $n$ rigid links (including ground), each free body has **6 degrees of freedom** in space (3 translations + 3 rotations). So the unrestricted freedom is:

$$
F_{free} = 6(n - 1)
$$

We subtract 1 because the ground is fixed.

**Step 2 — Account for Joint Constraints**

Each **1-DOF joint (lower pair)** removes **5 degrees of freedom** (it allows 1 motion and constrains the other 5). For example, a revolute joint allows only rotation about one axis; it prevents 2 translations + 2 rotations + 1 translation along the axis = 5.

Each joint of type $j_1$ contributes $-5$ constraints, and each joint of type $j_2$ (2-DOF) contributes $-4$ constraints. In general, a joint with $f_i$ DOF removes $6 - f_i$ constraints.

The general Grübler equation for **spatial mechanisms** is:

$$
M = 6(n-1) - \sum_{i=1}^{j} (6 - f_i)
$$

where $f_i$ is the DOF of the $i$-th joint.

**Step 3 — Specialize to a 1-DOF and 2-DOF Joint Mix**

If all joints are 1-DOF ($f_i = 1$) or 2-DOF ($f_i = 2$):

$$
M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2
$$

Expanding:

$$
M = 6n - 6 - 5j_1 - 4j_2
$$

This is the **generalized Kühlke / Grübler spatial equation** used in the KTU board exams.

**Step 4 — Specialize to a Planar Manipulator**

For **planar mechanisms** (motion confined to a plane), each link has only **3 DOF** (2 translations + 1 rotation). The same logic yields:

$$
M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2
$$

This is the **planar Grübler equation** appearing in most KTU numerical problems.

**Step 5 — Verify with a Simple 2-Link Planar Arm**

A 2-link planar RR arm: $n = 3$ (two links + ground), $j_1 = 2$ (two revolute joints), $j_2 = 0$.

$$
M = 3(3 - 1) - 2(2) - 0
$$

$$
M = 3 \cdot 2 - 4 = 6 - 4 = 2
$$

> **Interpretation:** The 2-link planar arm has **2 DOF**, matching our intuition — two joint angles fully define the end-effector position in the plane.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Workspace of a 2-Link Articulated Arm (Reach Envelope)

**Step 1 — Set Up the Geometry**

Consider a 2-link articulated arm with link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$ in the plane. The end-effector position $(x, y)$ is:

$$
x = L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

$$
y = L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Distance from Origin (Radius)**

$$
r^2 = x^2 + y^2
$$

Substituting and using $\cos A \cos B + \sin A \sin B = \cos(A-B)$:

$$
r^2 = L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_2
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the Maximum and Minimum Reach**

- **Maximum reach** occurs when $\cos\theta_2 = +1$ (arm fully extended, $\theta_2 = 0$):

$$
r_{max} = L_1 + L_2
$$

- **Minimum reach** occurs when $\cos\theta_2 = -1$ (arm folded back on itself, $\theta_2 = \pi$):

$$
r_{min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert
$$

**Step 4 — The Workspace Annulus**

The reachable region is the **annulus** (ring) between two concentric circles of radii $r_{min}$ and $r_{max}$ — provided $\theta_1$ can sweep a full $2\pi$. The area of this workspace is:

$$
A = \pi \left( r_{max}^2 - r_{min}^2 \right) = \pi \left[ (L_1+L_2)^2 - (L_1 - L_2)^2 \right]
$$

Expanding:

$$
(L_1+L_2)^2 - (L_1-L_2)^2 = 4 L_1 L_2
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{A = 4 \pi L_1 L_2}
$$

**Step 5 — Real-World Engineering Insight**

The workspace area depends on the **product** $L_1 L_2$, not the sum. This means a balanced design (e.g., $L_1 = L_2$) gives a *larger* area than an unbalanced one of equal total length — a key design trade-off taught in KTU robotics modules.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Manipulator Types

The following is the **most exam-critical table** in this topic. KTU questions often ask for a 7-mark comparison.

| Property | Cartesian (PPP) | Cylindrical (RPP) | Spherical (RRP) | Articulated (RRR) | SCARA (RRP-vertical) | Parallel (Delta) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Joint Sequence** | P-P-P | R-P-P | R-R-P | R-R-R | R-R-P (vertical) | Multiple R-R chains |
| **DOF (typical)** | 3 | 3 | 3 | 3 to 6 | 3 to 4 | 3 to 6 |
| **Workspace Shape** | Rectangular box | Hollow cylinder | Partial sphere | Spherical shell (donut) | Flat cylinder | Dome |
| **Reach Equation** | $V = 8 d_x d_y d_z$ | $V = \pi h (r_{max}^2 - r_{min}^2)$ | $V = \frac{4}{3} \pi (R_{max}^3 - R_{min}^3)$ | $V \approx \frac{4}{3} \pi (L_1+L_2)^3$ | $V = \pi R^2 h$ | $V \approx \frac{2}{3} \pi R^3$ |
| **Dexterity** | Low | Low-Medium | Medium | **Very High** | Medium (X-Y only) | High (in dome) |
| **Mechanical Rigidity** | **Highest** | High | Medium | Medium | Medium (compliant X-Y) | **Highest** (parallel structure) |
| **Accuracy** | High (linear axes) | High | Medium | Medium-High | **Very High** (X-Y) | **Very High** |
| **Speed** | Low-Medium | Medium | Medium | Medium | High | **Very High** |
| **Payload Capacity** | High | High | Very High | Medium-High | Low-Medium | Low-Medium |
| **Inverse Kinematics** | Trivial | Trivial | Simple | **Complex (multi-solution)** | Simple | Complex (polynomial) |
| **Typical Application** | CNC, 3D printing | Machine tending | Die casting, welding | Welding, painting, palletizing | PCB assembly, pick-and-place | Food/pharma packaging |
| **Drawback** | Large floor space, no dexterity | Limited reach overhead | Complex linkages | Singularities possible | Poor Z-stiffness, low payload | Small workspace, complex design |

### 3.4 Step-by-Step: Identifying a Manipulator Type from a Diagram

This is the **most common 3-mark question** pattern in KTU.

**Step 1** — Count the joints from base to end.
**Step 2** — Classify each joint as R or P.
**Step 3** — Write the joint sequence.
**Step 4** — Match to the standard type:
- **PPP** → Cartesian
- **RPP** → Cylindrical
- **RRP** → Could be Spherical *or* SCARA — check the **axis orientation** of the P joint (horizontal = Spherical, vertical = SCARA).
- **RRR** → Articulated.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Manipulator as a Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how the mechanical manipulator integrates with the broader robotic system, mapping the information and energy flow between subsystems. This is a high-yield diagram for KTU board questions that ask for "block diagram of a robot system."

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    J1[Base Pillar Anchor] --> J2[Revolute Joint Shoulder Axis]
    J2 --> J3[Upper Arm Link Segment 1]
    J3 --> J4[Revolute Joint Elbow Axis]
    J4 --> J5[Forearm Link Segment 2]
    J5 --> J6[Revolute Joint Wrist Axis]
    J6 --> J7[End Effector Mounting Flange]
    J7 --> J8[Tool Gripper or Welding Torch]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each rounded box represents a physical mechanical element; the arrows represent the rigid mechanical coupling between successive elements. The block diagram is read **left-to-right**, mirroring the **base-to-tool** joint sequence used in KTU notation.

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Tree for Manipulator Classification

The following decision tree is an excellent **revision aid**. Use it during your last-minute KTU exam review to identify a manipulator type from a joint sequence.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Joint Sequence] --> B[All Three Joints Linear]
    B -- YES --> C[Cartesian Manipulator]
    B -- NO --> D[First Joint Rotary]
    D --> E[Number of Rotary Joints]
    E -- One R then Two P --> F[Second and Third Joints Linear]
    F -- YES --> G[Cylindrical Manipulator]
    E -- Two R then One P --> H[Orientation of Prismatic Joint]
    H -- Prismatic is Horizontal --> I[Spherical Manipulator]
    H -- Prismatic is Vertical --> J[SCARA Manipulator]
    E -- Three Rotary Joints --> K[Articulated Manipulator]
    E -- Multiple Parallel Chains --> L[Parallel Delta Manipulator]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Hierarchical Classification of Manipulators

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ROOT[Robot Manipulator] --> CAT1[By Kinematic Configuration]
    ROOT --> CAT2[By Control Method]
    ROOT --> CAT3[By Power Source]
    CAT1 --> T1[Cartesian P P P]
    CAT1 --> T2[Cylindrical R P P]
    CAT1 --> T3[Spherical R R P]
    CAT1 --> T4[Articulated R R R]
    CAT1 --> T5[SCARA R R P vertical]
    CAT1 --> T6[Parallel Delta]
    CAT2 --> C1[Servo Controlled Closed Loop]
    CAT2 --> C2[Non Servo Open Loop]
    CAT3 --> P1[Electric Drives]
    CAT3 --> P2[Hydraulic Drives]
    CAT3 --> P3[Pneumatic Drives]
```

### 4.4 ASCII Schematic — 2-Link Articulated Arm Geometry

```
                   End-effector (x, y)
                         *
                        /|
                       / |
                  L2  /  |
                     /   |
                    / θ2 |
                   /     |
                  /      |
                 *-------*
            (Elbow)     ← L1 →  ← L2 →
                 θ1 (shoulder)
                 |
                 |
        =========|=========  (Base / Ground)
```

**Reading the schematic:**
- The **base** is the ground symbol at the bottom.
- $\theta_1$ is the **shoulder** angle measured from the horizontal X-axis.
- $\theta_2$ is the **elbow** angle measured from the upper arm to the forearm.
- $L_1$ and $L_2$ are the rigid link lengths.
- The end-effector position $(x, y)$ is determined by the formulas derived in Section 3.2.

> [!NOTE]
> In your KTU answer sheet, you may reproduce a simplified version of the above ASCII art. Mermaid diagrams cannot be drawn on paper, but the examiner will accept a hand-drawn kinematic diagram with joints labeled R or P and link lengths $L_i$ marked.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Differentiate between a robot manipulator and a mobile robot. List any four common configurations of a robot manipulator.**

**Model Answer:**

A **robot manipulator** is a stationary, arm-like mechanism anchored to a fixed base, designed to perform tasks by moving its end-effector within a defined workspace. In contrast, a **mobile robot** is mounted on a locomotion platform (wheels, legs, or tracks) and can travel through its environment; it is not constrained to a fixed base.

| Feature | Robot Manipulator | Mobile Robot |
|---|---|---|
| Base | Fixed | Mobile |
| Primary DOF | Arm joints (3 to 6) | Wheel/leg DOF (2 to 4) + arm |
| Workspace | Confined to work envelope | Entire floor / terrain |
| Sensors | Joint encoders, force sensors | LIDAR, GPS, cameras |

The **four common configurations** of a robot manipulator are:
1. **Cartesian (PPP)** — three linear joints.
2. **Cylindrical (RPP)** — one rotary + two linear joints.
3. **Spherical / Polar (RRP)** — two rotary + one linear joint.
4. **Articulated / Revolute (RRR)** — three rotary joints.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse SCARA with Spherical. Always state the **axis orientation** of the prismatic joint. SCARA's P joint is **vertical**; Spherical's P joint is **horizontal**. Marks are lost when this is omitted.

---

#### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]
**What is meant by "Degrees of Freedom" (DOF) of a robot manipulator? Why is a 6-DOF manipulator considered universally capable in 3D space?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** of a robot manipulator is the number of independent parameters (joint variables) required to completely specify the position and orientation of its end-effector with respect to the base.

**Why 6 DOF = Universal Capability in 3D:**

A rigid body in 3D space has **6 DOF**:
- **3 positional DOF:** $x$, $y$, $z$ — translation along the three Cartesian axes.
- **3 orientational DOF:** Roll, Pitch, Yaw — rotation about the three axes.

To fully position **and orient** the end-effector at an arbitrary target in 3D space, the manipulator must be able to independently command all 6 of these variables. Hence, a **6-DOF manipulator** (typically 3 arm joints + 3 wrist joints) is considered **universally capable** — it can reach any pose in its workspace.

A 3-DOF arm can position the end-effector anywhere in its workspace but cannot arbitrarily orient it. Adding 3 wrist DOF (roll, pitch, yaw) brings the total to 6 and provides full pose control.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do **not** state that 6 DOF gives "infinite dexterity." It gives **full pose control**, not infinite. The workspace is still bounded by link lengths and joint limits.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

#### Question A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]
**(a)** With the help of neat diagrams, explain the **Cartesian**, **Cylindrical**, and **Spherical** configurations of robot manipulators. For each, state the joint sequence, workspace shape, and two typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An articulated 2-link planar arm has link lengths $L_1 = 0.5$ m and $L_2 = 0.4$ m. Compute (i) the maximum reach, (ii) the minimum reach, and (iii) the area of its planar workspace. Also draw the workspace shape. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution to (a):

**1. Cartesian (Gantry) Manipulator**
- **Joint sequence:** P-P-P (three prismatic joints along mutually perpendicular X, Y, Z axes).
- **Diagram:** Three orthogonal linear slides stacked — base moves along X, arm moves along Y, tool moves along Z.
- **Workspace:** Rectangular box.
- **Applications:** (i) CNC machining centers, (ii) 3D printers, (iii) PCB assembly, (iv) Pick-and-place of large flat panels.

**2. Cylindrical Manipulator**
- **Joint sequence:** R-P-P (revolute joint at the base for rotation about a vertical axis, prismatic for radial extension, prismatic for vertical lift).
- **Diagram:** Rotating column on the base, telescoping arm extending outward, vertical slider for height.
- **Workspace:** Hollow cylinder (annular cylinder).
- **Applications:** (i) Machine tending, (ii) Palletizing, (iii) Coating operations, (iv) Assembly of round components.

**3. Spherical (Polar) Manipulator**
- **Joint sequence:** R-R-P (base rotation, shoulder pitch, linear extension).
- **Diagram:** Like a tank turret — base rotates, arm tilts up/down, telescopes in/out.
- **Workspace:** Partial spherical shell.
- **Applications:** (i) Die casting, (ii) Forging, (iii) Gas/arc welding (older generation), (iv) Handling of heavy parts.

##### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:** $L_1 = 0.5$ m, $L_2 = 0.4$ m

**(i) Maximum reach** — arm fully extended ($\theta_2 = 0$):

$$
r_{max} = L_1 + L_2 = 0.5 + 0.4 = 0.9 \text{ m}
$$

**(ii) Minimum reach** — arm fully folded ($\theta_2 = \pi$):

$$
r_{min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert = \vert 0.5 - 0.4 \vert = 0.1 \text{ m}
$$

**(iii) Workspace area** (annulus between two circles):

$$
A = \pi (r_{max}^2 - r_{min}^2)
$$

$$
A = \pi (0.9^2 - 0.1^2) = \pi (0.81 - 0.01) = 0.80 \pi \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A \approx 2.513 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Workspace shape:** A flat annulus (ring) of inner radius **0.1 m** and outer radius **0.9 m**, provided $\theta_1$ sweeps a full $2\pi$ radians. The end-effector can reach every point inside this ring but not the central disk.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Question A(b):** A common error is writing the workspace area as $A = \pi r_{max}^2$ (the full disk) instead of the **annulus** $A = \pi(r_{max}^2 - r_{min}^2)$. This mistake alone costs **3 out of 7 marks**. Always check whether the arm is a **single link** (disk) or a **multi-link** (annulus).

---

#### Question B: [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified for 2024 Scheme] — Internal Choice Alternative
**(a)** Compare **Articulated (RRR)** and **SCARA** manipulators under the following heads: joint sequence, workspace shape, DOF, mechanical stiffness, accuracy, typical application, and one limitation each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using Grübler's equation, verify the degrees of freedom of a **3-link planar articulated arm** with three revolute joints. Also compute the degrees of freedom of a **Cartesian manipulator** with 3 prismatic joints. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution to (a):

| Comparison Head | Articulated (RRR) | SCARA |
|---|---|---|
| **Joint sequence** | R-R-R (all revolute, axes parallel in pairs) | R-R-P (two horizontal-axis revolute + one vertical prismatic) |
| **Workspace shape** | Spherical shell (donut) | Flat cylindrical volume |
| **DOF** | 3 to 6 (3 arm + up to 3 wrist) | 3 to 4 |
| **Mechanical stiffness** | Moderate; weaker at full reach | Stiff in Z (vertical), compliant in X-Y plane |
| **Accuracy** | Medium-High (0.1 mm typical) | Very High in X-Y (0.01 mm) |
| **Typical application** | Automotive welding, painting, palletizing | PCB assembly, vertical insertion of components |
| **Limitation** | Complex inverse kinematics; singularities | Low payload; poor stiffness against horizontal forces |

**Key distinguishing feature:** SCARA is **selectively compliant** — it is **stiff in the vertical (Z) direction** (ideal for insertion tasks) and **compliant in the horizontal X-Y plane** (allowing small lateral flexing to absorb assembly tolerances).

##### Model Solution to (b):

**Case 1 — 3-link planar articulated arm (RRR)**

- Number of links: $n = 4$ (3 arm links + 1 ground/base link)
- Number of 1-DOF joints: $j_1 = 3$ (all revolute, each with 1 DOF)
- Number of 2-DOF joints: $j_2 = 0$

Applying the **planar Grübler equation**:

$$
M = 3(n - 1) - 2 j_1 - j_2
$$

$$
M = 3(4 - 1) - 2(3) - 0
$$

$$
M = 3(3) - 6 = 9 - 6 = 3
$$

> **Verification:** The 3-link planar RRR arm has **3 DOF**. This matches intuition — the three joint angles $\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3$ are the three independent coordinates needed to fully define the planar end-effector pose (x, y, $\phi$).

**Case 2 — Cartesian manipulator (PPP) with 3 prismatic joints**

- Number of links: $n = 4$ (3 sliding links + 1 base)
- Number of 1-DOF joints: $j_1 = 3$ (all prismatic, each with 1 DOF)
- Number of 2-DOF joints: $j_2 = 0$

Applying the **planar Grübler equation**:

$$
M = 3(4 - 1) - 2(3) - 0 = 9 - 6 = 3
$$

> **Verification:** The 3-joint planar Cartesian arm has **3 DOF** — the three slider positions $d_1, d_2, d_3$ uniquely determine the planar end-effector coordinates.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Question B(b):** A common mistake is to use the **spatial** Grübler equation ($M = 6(n-1) - 5 j_1 - 4 j_2$) for a *planar* problem, or vice versa. The choice of equation depends on whether the motion is confined to a plane (**3 DOF per link**) or in 3D space (**6 DOF per link**). Misapplying the equation costs the full 7 marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- A **robot manipulator** is a multi-link, multi-jointed mechanical arm with a fixed base, designed for programmed material handling or tool use.
- The **two fundamental joint types** are **Revolute (R)** — rotation, and **Prismatic (P)** — linear sliding.
- The **joint sequence** (written base-to-end) defines the manipulator **class**:
  - **PPP** → Cartesian
  - **RPP** → Cylindrical
  - **RRP** (horizontal P) → Spherical
  - **RRP** (vertical P) → **SCARA**
  - **RRR** → Articulated
  - **Multiple parallel R-R chains** → Parallel (Delta)
- **Cartesian** manipulators have a **rectangular workspace**; **Cylindrical** have a **hollow cylindrical workspace**; **Spherical** have a **partial spherical shell**; **Articulated** have a **spherical shell / donut**; **SCARA** has a **flat cylindrical**; **Parallel/Delta** has a **dome**.
- The **DOF of a manipulator in 3D** must be at least **6** for full position-and-orientation control of the end-effector (3 translation + 3 rotation).
- **Grübler's Equation (planar):** $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$
- **Grübler's Equation (spatial):** $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2$
- **Articulated arms** have the **highest dexterity** and the **most complex inverse kinematics** — multi-solution and prone to **singularities**.
- **SCARA** is **selectively compliant** — stiff in Z, compliant in X-Y — making it ideal for **vertical assembly** of small parts.
- **Parallel manipulators (Delta)** offer the **highest speed and rigidity** but have a **small workspace**.
- **Cartesian arms** offer the **simplest kinematics and highest accuracy** in linear motions but suffer from **large floor space** and **low dexterity**.
- The reachable area of a **2-link planar arm** is an **annulus** with area $A = 4\pi L_1 L_2$.
- Always state the **axis orientation** of any prismatic joint when classifying an RRP manipulator (distinguishes Spherical from SCARA).
- For KTU answer sheets, always **draw a labeled kinematic diagram** with joint axes and link lengths; this typically carries **2 to 3 marks** by itself in long-answer questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Legged Robot
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT205 - SENSOR TECHNOLOGY FOR ROBOTICS (ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Legged Robot

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Legged Robots — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1. Formal Academic Definition

A **Legged Robot** (also termed a *perceptively actuated legged machine* or *multi-legged autonomous system*) is a class of mobile robot that uses a discrete number of articulated, articulated rigid limbs — each terminated by a *foot* or *contact point* — to achieve locomotion over uneven, discontinuous, or unknown terrains. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PCRUT205, Module 1), legged robots are formally classified under *Mobile Robots* based on the **locomotion mechanism** and contrasted with *wheeled* and *tracked* systems.

Mathematically, a legged robot with $n$ legs, each having $k$ actuated degrees of freedom (DOF), constitutes a holonomic-constrained kinematic chain whose generalized coordinates $\mathbf{q} \in \mathbb{R}^{nk+6}$ are governed by the dynamics:

$$\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau} + \mathbf{J}^{T}\mathbf{F}_{c}$$

where $\mathbf{M}$ is the inertia matrix, $\mathbf{C}$ the Coriolis matrix, $\mathbf{G}$ the gravity vector, $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ the actuator torques, and $\mathbf{F}_{c}$ the ground-contact reaction forces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Legged robots are a *locomotion topology* — not a sensor. The sensors onboard (force/torque, IMU, encoders) are the focus of PCRUT205. However, *understanding the gait and stability of the leg is essential* to choose and place the right sensor.

## 2. Intuition — Plain English Analogy

Think of a legged robot as a **walking table on stilts that thinks**:
- A *wheeled robot* is like a shopping trolley — efficient on flat tiles, but the moment a stair appears, it is helpless.
- A *legged robot* is like a *mountain goat* — it lifts one leg at a time, finds footholds, and steps over cracks, rocks, and rubble.

The fundamental design philosophy: *replace a continuous contact patch (the wheel) with a sequence of discrete contact points (the feet)*. The robot is statically or dynamically balanced by the **support polygon** formed by the feet currently on the ground.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world example:** Boston Dynamics' *Spot* (quadruped) and *Atlas* (humanoid biped) are the canonical KTU-referenced commercial legged robots. Honda's *ASIMO* and Agility Robotics' *Digit* are standard textbook examples.

## 3. Classification of Legged Robots (by Number of Legs)

| Number of Legs | Common Name | Static Stability | Example |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| **1** | Monopod / Hopper | Unstable without dynamics | Raibert's hopper |
| **2** | Biped / Humanoid | Marginally stable (ZMP needed) | ASIMO, Atlas |
| **3** | Tripod (rare) | Marginally stable statically | Stanley (Stanford) |
| **4** | Quadruped | Statically stable (3 legs on ground) | Spot, ANYmal |
| **6** | Hexapod | Highly statically stable (5 legs on ground) | Hexapod insects |
| **8+** | Octopod / Multi-leg | Maximum redundancy | Climbing rescue robots |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "more legs = more stable":** With more legs, the robot can always keep its **Center of Gravity (CoG)** projection inside a *support polygon* formed by feet — this is called **static stability**.

## 4. Standard Reference Metrics (KTU Frequently Asked)

- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** of a leg: typically **2 or 3** (hip abduction/adduction + hip flexion/extension + knee).
- **Stride Length (S):** linear distance traversed per gait cycle, measured in **meters (m)**.
- **Step Frequency (f):** gait cycles per second, measured in **Hertz (Hz)**.
- **Average Walking Speed (v):** $v = S \cdot f$, measured in **m/s**.
- **Static Stability Margin (SSM):** minimum distance from CoG projection to support polygon edge, measured in **meters (m)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 1. Walking Gait — Theoretical Foundation

A *gait* is the **time-ordered sequence of leg-lift and leg-place events** that collectively propel the robot. Two principal categories exist:

### A. Static Walking Gait

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A gait is *static* if and only if the projection of the robot's Center of Gravity (CoG) onto the ground plane lies **strictly inside** the convex hull of the support legs at **every instant** — including during *leg transfer*.

Conditions:
- At least **3 non-collinear legs** must touch the ground at all times.
- Acceleration forces are negligible compared to gravity (low speed).
- CoG must be **low** and **central** to maximize margin.

**Examples:** Hexapod *alternating tripod gait* (legs 1,3,5 then 2,4,6), quadruped *crawl gait*.

### B. Dynamic Walking Gait

The robot is **never statically stable** — it *falls forward continuously* and catches itself on the next foot placement. This is how humans walk!

Conditions:
- CoG projection may *leave* the support polygon transiently.
- Stability is achieved through **angular momentum control** and **Zero Moment Point (ZMP)** regulation.
- Faster and more energy-efficient than static gait.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Exam Gold:** The relationship is — *static walking is a special case of dynamic walking where inertial terms $\to 0$*.

## 2. KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Name / Description | Units | Valid For |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | $v = S \cdot f$ | Average forward velocity | m/s | All legged systems |
| 2 | $\text{SSM} = \min_{i} d(\text{CoG}_{proj}, \text{edge}_{i})$ | Static Stability Margin (shortest distance from CoG projection to any edge of support polygon) | m | Static gait |
| 3 | $\text{SSM} = \min_{i} \frac{(p_{i+1,x} - p_{i,x})\cdot p_{G,y} - (p_{i+1,y} - p_{i,y})\cdot p_{G,x} + p_{i+1,y}p_{i,x} - p_{i+1,x}p_{i,y}}{\sqrt{(p_{i+1,x}-p_{i,x})^{2}+(p_{i+1,y}-p_{i,y})^{2}}}$ | Analytical SSM for polygon vertices $p_i$ | m | Static gait |
| 4 | $\mathbf{p}_{ZMP} = \frac{\sum_{i} p_{i}\cdot F_{z,i}}{\sum_{i} F_{z,i}}$ | Zero Moment Point — horizontal coordinates (vertical moment = 0) | m | Dynamic gait |
| 5 | $p_{ZMP,x} = x_{CoG} - \frac{(I_{CoG} + m\cdot \mathbf{r}^{2})\ddot{\theta} + mg\cdot \mathbf{r}}{F_{z}}$ | ZMP under inverted pendulum model | m | Biped dynamic walk |
| 6 | $n_{\text{stable}} = n_{\text{legs}} - 2$ | Minimum statically stable legs during lift | count | Static gait |
| 7 | $P_{\text{mech}} = mg \cdot v \cdot \tan(\alpha)$ | Mechanical power for incline $\alpha$ at speed $v$ | W | Locomotion analysis |
| 8 | $\text{Froude No.} = \frac{v^{2}}{g \cdot L}$ | Dimensionless gait similarity number | unitless | Bio-locomotion scaling |
| 9 | $T_{\text{stride}} = \frac{1}{f}$ | Stride period | s | Gait timing |
| 10 | $\eta = \frac{P_{\text{useful}}}{P_{\text{total}}} = \frac{mgv}{P_{\text{electric}}}$ | Locomotion efficiency | % | Energy analysis |

## 3. Why These Formulas Matter — Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SSM (Eq. 2 & 3):** Used by quadruped control loops (e.g., MIT Mini-Cheetah) to plan foot placement that guarantees the CoG stays over the support triangle.
- **ZMP (Eq. 4 & 5):** The cornerstone of **bipedal humanoid control** — ASIMO, Atlas, HRP-2, NAO all use ZMP-based preview control for stable walking over flat ground.
- **Froude Number (Eq. 8):** Used by biomechanicists to compare robot walking to animals (humans walk at $Fr \approx 0.2$, run at $Fr \approx 1$).
- **Power formula (Eq. 7):** Critical for designing battery capacity in field-deployed search-and-rescue legged robots.

## 4. Operational Logic — Walking Cycle Decomposition

Every legged gait cycle consists of four phases (Kumar & Waldron nomenclature):

1. **Lift Phase** — actuator raises the swing leg against gravity.
2. **Transfer Phase** — swing leg moves forward (foot clears ground).
3. **Place Phase** — swing leg touches down, contact force ramps up.
4. **Support Phase** — leg is in stance, bears body weight.

The *duty factor* $\beta$ (fraction of cycle a leg is in support) determines gait type:
- $\beta = 0.5$ → **walking** (some legs always lifted)
- $\beta > 0.5$ → **slow walk / crawl**
- $\beta = 1$ → all legs always in stance (impossible for forward motion)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Static Stability Margin (SSM) for a Quadruped Tripod Support

### Setup
Consider a quadruped standing on legs 1, 2, 3 (leg 4 is in swing). The foot contact points in the body frame (sagittal $x$–lateral $y$ plane) are:

$$\mathbf{p}_{1} = (a, b), \quad \mathbf{p}_{2} = (-a, b), \quad \mathbf{p}_{3} = (-a, -b)$$

The Center of Gravity (CoG) projects at $\mathbf{p}_{G} = (x_G, y_G)$ in the body frame.

### Step 1 — Identify the support polygon
The three foot points form a triangle. The edges are $E_{12}$ (between $\mathbf{p}_{1}$ and $\mathbf{p}_{2}$), $E_{23}$ (between $\mathbf{p}_{2}$ and $\mathbf{p}_{3}$), and $E_{31}$ (between $\mathbf{p}_{3}$ and $\mathbf{p}_{1}$).

### Step 2 — Signed distance from a point to a line
For a line segment from $\mathbf{p}_{i} = (x_i, y_i)$ to $\mathbf{p}_{j} = (x_j, y_j)$, the perpendicular distance from $\mathbf{p}_{G} = (x_G, y_G)$ to the infinite line is given by the standard 2D point-to-line formula. **Because the pipe character `|` would break markdown tables, we use $\vert$ for the absolute value operator.**

$$d_{ij} = \frac{\vert (y_{j} - y_{i})x_G - (x_{j} - x_{i})y_G + x_{j}y_{i} - y_{j}x_{i} \vert}{\sqrt{(x_{j}-x_{i})^{2} + (y_{j}-y_{i})^{2}}}$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate each edge

**Edge 12:** from $\mathbf{p}_{1} = (a, b)$ to $\mathbf{p}_{2} = (-a, b)$:

$$d_{12} = \frac{\vert (b - b)x_G - (-a - a)y_G + (-a)(b) - (b)(a) \vert}{\sqrt{(-a-a)^{2} + (b-b)^{2}}}$$

$$d_{12} = \frac{\vert 0 \cdot x_G - (-2a)y_G - ab - ab \vert}{\sqrt{4a^{2} + 0}}$$

$$d_{12} = \frac{\vert 2a \cdot y_G - 2ab \vert}{2a} = \frac{2a \cdot \vert y_G - b \vert}{2a} = \vert y_G - b \vert$$

**Edge 23:** from $\mathbf{p}_{2} = (-a, b)$ to $\mathbf{p}_{3} = (-a, -b)$:

$$d_{23} = \frac{\vert (-b - b)x_G - (-a - (-a))y_G + (-a)(b) - (-b)(-a) \vert}{\sqrt{(-a - (-a))^{2} + (-b - b)^{2}}}$$

$$d_{23} = \frac{\vert -2b \cdot x_G - 0 \cdot y_G - ab - ab \vert}{\sqrt{0 + 4b^{2}}}$$

$$d_{23} = \frac{\vert -2b \cdot x_G - 2ab \vert}{2b} = \vert x_G + a \vert$$

**Edge 31:** from $\mathbf{p}_{3} = (-a, -b)$ to $\mathbf{p}_{1} = (a, b)$:

$$d_{31} = \frac{\vert (b - (-b))x_G - (a - (-a))y_G + (a)(-b) - (b)(-a) \vert}{\sqrt{(a-(-a))^{2} + (b-(-b))^{2}}}$$

$$d_{31} = \frac{\vert 2b \cdot x_G - 2a \cdot y_G - ab + ab \vert}{\sqrt{4a^{2} + 4b^{2}}}$$

$$d_{31} = \frac{2 \vert b \cdot x_G - a \cdot y_G \vert}{\sqrt{4(a^{2}+b^{2})}} = \frac{\vert b \cdot x_G - a \cdot y_G \vert}{\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}}}$$

### Step 4 — Take the minimum
By definition, the **Static Stability Margin** is the minimum of all edge distances:

$$\boxed{\text{SSM} = \min\bigl\{\vert y_G - b \vert, \;\; \vert x_G + a \vert, \;\; \tfrac{\vert b \cdot x_G - a \cdot y_G \vert}{\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}}}\bigr\}}$$

### Step 5 — Physical interpretation
- The first term $\vert y_G - b \vert$ measures lateral margin.
- The second term $\vert x_G + a \vert$ measures longitudinal margin.
- The third term measures the diagonal (hypotenuse) margin.
- For **stability**: $\text{SSM} > 0$ must hold, AND the CoG projection must be on the *interior* side of every edge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion logic note:** The reason we use *signed distance* before taking the absolute value is to also test the *interior/exterior* side. A positive SSM with correct sign convention means the CoG is *inside* the support polygon.

---

## Derivation 2: Zero Moment Point (ZMP) for a Biped — Inverted Pendulum Model

### Setup
A bipedal robot's body is modeled as a **point mass $m$ at the CoG** supported by a massless leg of length $L$ contacting the ground at foot point $F = (x_F, 0)$. The body is subject to gravity $g$ downward and ground reaction $\mathbf{F}_{gr}$.

### Step 1 — Equations of motion
For the point-mass body, Newton's second law in the horizontal direction gives:

$$m \ddot{x}_{CoG} = F_{gr,x}$$

In the vertical direction (body height is approximately constant at $z_{CoG}$):

$$F_{gr,z} - mg = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_{gr,z} = mg$$

### Step 2 — Moment balance about the foot
Taking moments about the foot contact point $F$ (where $y$-axis is vertical), and equating to the angular acceleration of the body mass about $F$:

$$m \ddot{x}_{CoG} \cdot z_{CoG} + m g (x_{CoG} - x_{F}) = I_{F} \ddot{\theta}$$

But for a point mass, $I_{F} = m L^{2}$ and $\ddot{\theta} = \ddot{x}_{CoG} / z_{CoG}$ (small-angle). Substituting:

$$m \ddot{x}_{CoG} z_{CoG} + mg(x_{CoG} - x_{F}) = m z_{CoG}^{2} \cdot \frac{\ddot{x}_{CoG}}{z_{CoG}}$$

$$m \ddot{x}_{CoG} z_{CoG} + mg(x_{CoG} - x_{F}) = m z_{CoG} \ddot{x}_{CoG}$$

The $m\ddot{x}_{CoG}z_{CoG}$ terms **cancel exactly**, yielding:

$$mg(x_{CoG} - x_{F}) = 0$$

This implies $x_{F} = x_{CoG}$ — the foot must be directly under the CoG for static balance.

### Step 3 — Generalization to ZMP
When the robot is **accelerating**, the foot placement is governed by the **ZMP**, defined as the point on the ground where the **net moment of the ground reaction force is zero**. Setting $M_{y}^{ZMP} = 0$ about a general ground point $(p_{ZMP,x}, 0)$:

$$-mg \cdot (x_{CoG} - p_{ZMP,x}) + m\ddot{x}_{CoG} \cdot z_{CoG} = 0$$

Solving for the ZMP:

$$\boxed{p_{ZMP,x} = x_{CoG} - \frac{z_{CoG}}{g}\ddot{x}_{CoG}}$$

By symmetry in the lateral direction:

$$p_{ZMP,y} = y_{CoG} - \frac{z_{CoG}}{g}\ddot{y}_{CoG}$$

### Step 4 — Stability criterion
The robot is **dynamically stable** if and only if the ZMP lies **inside the support polygon** (the convex hull of all ground-contact points). This is the famous **ZMP stability criterion** used in every humanoid walking controller.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** The ZMP is *not* a physical point on the robot — it is a *virtual point* computed from forces and accelerations. The controller then chooses footstep positions such that the predicted ZMP is inside the foot's footprint (typically a $25 \times 15$ cm rectangle for human-sized humanoids).

---

## Analytical Comparison Matrix — Gait Types

| Property | Static Gait (Crawl) | Dynamic Gait (Trot) | Walking vs Running |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Speed | Low | Medium–High | Crossover at $Fr \approx 1$ |
| Energy efficiency | Low | High | Walk is efficient at low speed |
| Control complexity | Simple | Complex (ZMP) | Requires flight phase in run |
| Stability margin | Large (3+ legs) | Zero (only 2 legs) | Zero during flight |
| Best robot type | Hexapod | Quadruped | Quadruped (e.g., Cheetah) |
| Sensors required | Joint encoders, contact | + IMU, force/torque | + all of the above |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1 — Legged Robot Classification & Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Mobile Robot] --> B[Wheeled]
    A --> C[Tracked]
    A --> D[Legged]
    D --> D1[1 Leg - Hopping]
    D --> D2[2 Legs - Biped]
    D --> D3[3 Legs - Tripod]
    D --> D4[4 Legs - Quadruped]
    D --> D5[6 Legs - Hexapod]
    D --> D6[8+ Legs - Multi-leg]
    D2 --> D2A[Static Walk - ZMP]
    D2 --> D2B[Dynamic Walk - Inverted Pendulum]
    D4 --> D4A[Crawl Gait]
    D4 --> D4B[Trot Gait]
    D4 --> D4C[Bound Gait]
    D4 --> D4D[Pace Gait]
    D5 --> D5A[Alternating Tripod]
    D5 --> D5B[Wave Gait]
    D5 --> D5C[Ripple Gait]
    D1 --> D1A[Raibert Hopper Model]
    D3 --> D3A[Stanley Stanford Tripod]
```

## Diagram 2 — Functional Block Architecture of a Legged Robot Control System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Terrain Perception] -->|Point Cloud| B[Sensor Fusion Unit]
    B --> C[State Estimator]
    C --> D[Gait Planner]
    D --> E[Footstep Planner]
    E --> F[Inverse Dynamics Solver]
    F --> G[Inverse Kinematics Module]
    G --> H[Joint Actuator Commands]
    H --> I[Legged Robot Body]
    I -->|IMU Data| C
    I -->|Joint Encoders| C
    I -->|Force Torque at Foot| B
    I -->|Ground Contact| B
    J[Operator Command] --> D
    style A fill:#FFE4B5
    style B fill:#87CEEB
    style C fill:#90EE90
    style D fill:#FFB6C1
    style E fill:#DDA0DD
    style F fill:#F0E68C
    style G fill:#FFA07A
    style H fill:#B0E0E6
    style I fill:#FF6347
    style J fill:#D3D3D3
```

## Diagram 3 — Gait Cycle State Machine (Single Leg)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Stance
    Stance --> LiftUp: Triggered
    LiftUp --> Swing: Foot clears ground
    Swing --> TouchDown: Foot contacts ground
    TouchDown --> Stance: Weight transfer complete
    Stance --> [*]
    note right of Stance: Supports body weight
    note right of Swing: Foot in air, moves forward
    note right of LiftUp: Actuator raises leg
    note right of TouchDown: Impact, force ramps up
```

## Diagram 4 — ZMP Concept Visualization (Biped)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[CoG - Center of Gravity] -->|mg - gravity| B[Ground Reaction Force]
    B --> C[ZMP - Zero Moment Point]
    C --> D{Inside Support Polygon?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Stable Walk]
    D -->|No| F[Robot Falls]
    G[Body Acceleration] -->|Inertial term| C
    H[Foot Contact Area] --> D
    style A fill:#FFD700
    style C fill:#32CD32
    style E fill:#90EE90
    style F fill:#FF6347
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a legged robot. How is it classified based on the number of legs? Give two advantages over wheeled robots.**

**Model Answer:**

A legged robot is an autonomous machine that uses a finite number of articulated limbs, each terminated with a foot or contact point, to achieve locomotion by alternately lifting and placing the legs. It is classified as:
- **Monopod** (1 leg)
- **Biped** (2 legs)
- **Triped** (3 legs)
- **Quadruped** (4 legs)
- **Hexapod** (6 legs)
- **Multi-leg** (8+ legs)

**Two advantages over wheeled robots:**
1. **Terrain adaptability:** Legged robots can traverse uneven, rocky, stepped, or rubble-filled terrain that would immobilize a wheeled robot.
2. **Discrete foot placement:** Allows active selection of stable contact points, useful in disaster zones, staircases, and unstructured environments (e.g., search-and-rescue, planetary exploration).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between static and dynamic walking gaits. Give one example of a robot for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Static Walking Gait | Dynamic Walking Gait |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Definition** | CoG projection always inside support polygon | CoG projection may leave support polygon |
| **Speed** | Slow | Fast |
| **Number of legs needed** | ≥ 3 (in contact) | Even 2 (alternating) |
| **Control method** | Pre-computed foot placement, simple | ZMP / momentum-based feedback |
| **Energy** | Less efficient (always at least 3 legs lifting) | More efficient (leverages gravity) |
| **Example robot** | Hexapod (e.g., RHex-style, alternating tripod) | Humanoid (e.g., ASIMO, Atlas) |
| **Stability metric** | Static Stability Margin (SSM) | Zero Moment Point (ZMP) |

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model Paper]`

**(a)** A quadruped robot stands on a tripod support formed by feet at body-frame coordinates:
$$\mathbf{p}_{1} = (0.3, 0.2), \quad \mathbf{p}_{2} = (-0.3, 0.2), \quad \mathbf{p}_{3} = (-0.3, -0.2)$$
The CoG projects at $\mathbf{p}_{G} = (0.05, 0.05)$ m. **Compute the Static Stability Margin (SSM).** Is the configuration stable? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Zero Moment Point (ZMP)** concept for a biped robot. Derive the ZMP equation for a simple inverted-pendulum biped model. Why is the ZMP *not* the same as the CoG projection? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a) — SSM Computation

**Given:** $a = 0.3$ m, $b = 0.2$ m, $(x_G, y_G) = (0.05, 0.05)$ m.

Apply the formula derived in Section 3:

**Edge 12 (between $p_1$ and $p_2$):**
$$d_{12} = \vert y_G - b \vert = \vert 0.05 - 0.2 \vert = 0.15 \text{ m}$$

**Edge 23 (between $p_2$ and $p_3$):**
$$d_{23} = \vert x_G + a \vert = \vert 0.05 + 0.3 \vert = 0.35 \text{ m}$$

**Edge 31 (between $p_3$ and $p_1$):**
$$d_{31} = \frac{\vert b \cdot x_G - a \cdot y_G \vert}{\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}}} = \frac{\vert 0.2 \cdot 0.05 - 0.3 \cdot 0.05 \vert}{\sqrt{0.09 + 0.04}}$$

$$d_{31} = \frac{\vert 0.01 - 0.015 \vert}{\sqrt{0.13}} = \frac{0.005}{0.3606} = 0.01386 \text{ m}$$

**SSM** = minimum of the three:
$$\boxed{\text{SSM} = \min\{0.15, \; 0.35, \; 0.01386\} = 0.01386 \text{ m} \approx 13.86 \text{ mm}}$$

**Stability verdict:** Since SSM > 0, the CoG projection lies inside the support triangle. **The configuration IS statically stable**, but the margin is small (≈14 mm), which is risky — any disturbance or dynamic effect could destabilize the robot. Recommendation: shift CoG projection toward the centroid of the triangle to increase the margin.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b) — ZMP Concept and Derivation

**Concept:** The **Zero Moment Point (ZMP)** is the point on the ground (or support surface) where the *net moment* of the ground reaction force (active + inertial) is **zero**. If the ZMP lies inside the support polygon (convex hull of ground-contact feet), the robot will not tip over. It is a *virtual* point — it need not coincide with the CoG projection.

**Assumptions of the inverted-pendulum model:**
- The robot's body is a **point mass $m$ at the CoG**.
- The massless leg connects the CoG to a foot contact point.
- Body height $z_{CoG}$ is approximately constant.

**Derivation (key steps):**

Apply Newton's law in horizontal:
$$m \ddot{x}_{CoG} = F_{gr,x}$$

Vertical equilibrium:
$$F_{gr,z} = mg$$

Moment balance about the ZMP point $(p_{ZMP,x}, 0)$, setting $\sum M_{y} = 0$:
$$-mg \cdot (x_{CoG} - p_{ZMP,x}) + m\ddot{x}_{CoG} \cdot z_{CoG} = 0$$

Solving for $p_{ZMP,x}$:
$$\boxed{p_{ZMP,x} = x_{CoG} - \frac{z_{CoG}}{g}\ddot{x}_{CoG}}$$

**Why ZMP ≠ CoG projection:**
- The **CoG projection** is a *kinematic* quantity (where the body's mass is "above").
- The **ZMP** is a *dynamic* quantity — it includes the inertial effect $m\ddot{x}_{CoG}$.
- Under **static** conditions ($\ddot{x}_{CoG} = 0$), ZMP = CoG projection.
- Under **acceleration** (e.g., start of step, deceleration), ZMP **shifts backward** (or forward) from the CoG projection, and may even exit the support polygon — causing a fall.

**Stability criterion:** $\text{ZMP} \in \text{Support Polygon} \Leftrightarrow \text{Robot is dynamically stable}$.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Supplementary]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **functional architecture** of a quadruped robot's control system. Identify the role of each major sensor used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hexapod robot uses an **alternating tripod gait** with a stride length of $S = 0.2$ m and a step frequency of $f = 1.5$ Hz. Calculate the **average forward velocity** and the **duty factor** if each leg spends 0.4 s in swing per cycle. Comment on the gait's stability. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a) — Quadruped Control Architecture

The control hierarchy of a quadruped robot (such as Boston Dynamics' Spot) consists of the following layers (refer to Diagram 2 in Section 4 for visual block diagram):

| Layer | Function | Key Sensors Used |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **1. Perception** | Builds a local terrain map and detects obstacles | **LiDAR**, **Depth cameras** (stereo / RGB-D) |
