| **2. State Estimation** | Fuses sensor data to estimate body pose, velocity, position | **IMU** (gyro + accelerometer), joint encoders |
| **3. Gait Planner** | Decides gait type (walk, trot, crawl) and timing | Internal clock + commanded speed |
| **4. Footstep Planner** | Selects next contact point using terrain map | Output from perception layer |
| **5. Inverse Dynamics** | Computes required joint torques for the planned motion | Uses mass model of robot |
| **6. Inverse Kinematics** | Converts desired foot position to joint angles | Joint encoders for feedback |
| **7. Low-level Motor Control** | Drives actuators to track joint commands | **Force/torque sensors at the foot** for contact detection |

**Role of each major sensor:**
- **IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit):** Provides roll, pitch, yaw and angular rates; essential for balance.
- **Joint Encoders:** Measure each actuated joint angle, closing the position-control loop.
- **Force/Torque Sensors (at feet):** Detect ground contact onset/offset and measure contact force; critical for *triggering* the lift, swing, and stance phase transitions.
- **LiDAR / Depth Camera:** Builds elevation map of terrain, allowing *foot placement on safe footholds*.
- **Current Sensors (in motor drivers):** Estimate torque via motor current; serves as a *redundant* safety check on force/torque sensors.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often miss naming the **specific sensor for each function** — simply saying "sensors are used" earns 0 marks. Always name the sensor (IMU, encoder, F/T sensor, LiDAR) and state *what it measures*.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b) — Hexapod Gait Calculation

**Given:**
- Stride length: $S = 0.2$ m
- Step frequency: $f = 1.5$ Hz

**Step 1 — Average forward velocity:**

$$v = S \cdot f = 0.2 \cdot 1.5 = 0.3 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Stride period:**

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{1.5} = 0.6667 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Duty factor:**
Given swing time $t_{\text{swing}} = 0.4$ s per cycle:

$$\beta = \frac{T - t_{\text{swing}}}{T} = \frac{0.6667 - 0.4}{0.6667} = \frac{0.2667}{0.6667} = 0.40$$

**Comment on stability:**
- $\beta = 0.4 < 0.5 \Rightarrow$ This is a **fast walking / running-like gait** (since less than half the cycle is spent in support).
- Wait — this is **inconsistent with alternating tripod stability**! In an alternating tripod gait, *three legs are always in stance*, meaning each leg must have $\beta \geq 0.5$. The given swing time of 0.4 s exceeds half the period of 0.667 s, so the leg would be off the ground *most of the time* — impossible to maintain three-point contact.
- **Conclusion:** The provided parameters are *physically infeasible* for an alternating tripod. Either $f$ must be reduced (e.g., $f \leq 0.75$ Hz) or $t_{\text{swing}}$ must be ≤ 0.333 s. With feasible parameters, the alternating tripod is **maximally stable** for a hexapod (CoG always inside a tripod support polygon).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit mistakes** in SSM calculations — forgetting to convert mm to m, or $\text{cm}^{2}$ to $\text{m}^{2}$.
> 2. **Confusing ZMP with CoG** — these are equal *only* in static case. Examiners specifically test this with the $\ddot{x}_{CoG}$ term.
> 3. **Forgetting the absolute value** in SSM — students drop $\vert \cdot \vert$ and obtain a negative SSM, then incorrectly call the robot "unstable" without checking *interiority* first.
> 4. **Wrong duty-factor interpretation** — $\beta < 0.5$ means the leg is in swing more than stance; this implies running, not walking.
> 5. **Not stating units** in the final answer — every numeric answer in KTU papers must include a SI unit (m, m/s, Hz).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A legged robot is a mobile robot using discrete articulated limbs with feet for locomotion on uneven terrain.
- **Classification:** Monopod, Biped, Triped, Quadruped, Hexapod, Multi-leg — stability increases with leg count.
- **Static Gait:** CoG projection always inside support polygon. Needs ≥ 3 legs in contact. Slow but simple control.
- **Dynamic Gait:** CoG projection may leave support polygon. Controlled via ZMP. Used in humanoids (bipeds).
- **SSM:** Minimum perpendicular distance from CoG projection to support polygon edge. **MUST be > 0 for stability.**
- **ZMP:** Virtual point where ground reaction moment = 0. **MUST lie inside support polygon for dynamic stability.** Formula: $p_{ZMP} = x_{CoG} - (z_{CoG}/g)\ddot{x}_{CoG}$.
- **Key formula $v = S \cdot f$** relates stride, frequency, and velocity.
- **Duty factor $\beta$** discriminates walk ($\beta > 0.5$), run ($\beta < 0.5$), and bound ($\beta \to 0$ with flight phase).
- **Sensors critical to legged locomotion:** IMU (balance), joint encoders (kinematics), force/torque (contact), LiDAR/depth camera (terrain).
- **Hexapod alternating tripod** is the *gold standard* for maximum static stability — three legs always in stance.
- **Quadrupeds** typically use *trot* (dynamic) for speed or *crawl* (static) for rough terrain.
- **Bipeds** require ZMP-based control — the hardest control problem in legged robotics.
- **Froude Number** is the dimensionless gait similarity parameter: $Fr = v^2/(gL)$.
- **Energy efficiency** of dynamic gaits is *higher* than static — this is why animals (and ASIMO) walk dynamically, not statically.
- **KTU frequently asks:** Derive SSM for given polygon, derive ZMP for inverted pendulum, and compare static vs dynamic gaits.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wheeled Robot
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT205 - SENSOR TECHNOLOGY FOR ROBOTICS (ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Wheeled Robot

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Wheeled Mobile Robot (WMR)** is a class of autonomous or semi-autonomous ground vehicle whose primary means of locomotion, stability, and terrain interaction is achieved through the use of wheels rotating about fixed or steerable axles. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Sensor Technology for Robotics*, a wheeled robot is defined as a non-holonomic mechanical system whose motion is constrained by the no-slip (rolling without slipping) condition at the wheel–ground contact patch.

In formal robotic terms, a wheeled robot is described as a **rigid body** (the chassis) equipped with $n$ wheels, each wheel contributing a specific constraint equation to the overall motion of the vehicle. The configuration of the robot in a 2D plane is given by the pose vector:

$$\xi = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix}$$

where:
- $x$ = position of the robot along the global X-axis (in meters, **m**)
- $y$ = position of the robot along the global Y-axis (in meters, **m**)
- $\theta$ = orientation (heading angle) of the robot with respect to the global X-axis (in radians, **rad**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Wheeled robots are classified as *non-holonomic* systems because they cannot move instantaneously in any arbitrary direction in the configuration space. The number of controllable Degrees of Freedom (DOF) is always **less than** the dimension of the configuration space (which is 3 for a planar robot: $x, y, \theta$).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **parking a car** in a tight perpendicular spot. Even though the car *physically occupies* a 2D space (length and width) and can rotate, you cannot slide it sideways into the spot directly. You must perform a multi-step maneuver: reverse, turn the steering wheel, move forward, and re-align. This is the everyday experience of a **non-holonomic constraint** — the wheels roll forward or backward but cannot glide laterally.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Shopping Cart Test:**
> - A standard **shopping cart** (with 4 swivel casters) is a **holonomic** system — you can push it in any direction.
> - A **bicycle or car** (with fixed-direction wheels) is **non-holonomic** — you must steer and drive in a coupled manner.
> - A **robot vacuum cleaner** (like the iRobot Roomba) is a classic example of a *differential drive wheeled robot*.

## 1.3 The Three Fundamental Components of a Wheeled Robot

Every wheeled robot, regardless of complexity, is composed of three integrated subsystems:

| Subsystem | Function | Engineering Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Locomotion System** | Provides motion via wheels and drivetrain | DC motors, gearboxes, wheels, casters |
| **Sensing System** | Perceives the internal state and external environment | Encoders, IMU, LIDAR, cameras, GPS |
| **Control System** | Processes sensor data and commands actuators | Microcontroller (Arduino/STM32), ROS, PID controller |

## 1.4 Why Wheeled Robots Dominate Robotics

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Justification:** Wheeled robots are the most widely deployed mobile robot architecture because they offer:
> 1. **High energy efficiency** (no lifting of mass against gravity, unlike legged robots).
> 2. **Simple mechanical design** (fewer moving parts than walking or flying robots).
> 3. **Mature control theory** (well-developed kinematic and dynamic models).
> 4. **Scalability** (from microscopic pill-robots to massive planetary rovers like NASA's *Curiosity*).
> 5. **Lower cost** of manufacturing and maintenance.

## 1.5 Wheeled Robot vs. Other Mobile Robots

| Parameter | Wheeled Robot | Legged Robot | Tracked Robot | Aerial Robot (Drone) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Terrain Adaptability** | Low–Medium | High | Medium–High | N/A (airborne) |
| **Energy Efficiency** | High | Low | Medium | Low |
| **Mechanical Complexity** | Low | Very High | Medium | High |
| **Stability** | Inherently stable (static) | Dynamic stability | Stable | Unstable (needs control) |
| **Example** | AGV in warehouse | Boston Dynamics Spot | Tank, Mars Rover | Quadcopter |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Wheeled Robots (KTU High-Yield Topic)

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly emphasizes the classification of wheeled robots based on **wheel type, wheel arrangement, and drive mechanism**. The classification framework was originally formalized by **Campion, Bastin, and D'Andrea-Novel** (1996) and is a recurring question in KTU university examinations.

### 2.1.1 Classification by Number of Wheels

- **Two-Wheeled Robots (2WR):** Such as self-balancing robots (e.g., Segway). Inherently unstable in the lateral direction, requires active balancing control.
- **Three-Wheeled Robots (3WR):** The most common research and educational platform. Static stability is achievable.
- **Four-Wheeled Robots (4WR):** Standard for autonomous cars and industrial AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles).
- **Six-Wheeled / Multi-Wheeled Robots:** Used for heavy payload and rough terrain (e.g., NASA's Mars rovers).

### 2.1.2 Classification by Wheel Type (Campion's Framework)

According to Campion's classification, every wheel belongs to one of two categories:

1. **Standard Wheel (Conventional Wheel):**
   - Has **two DOF**: rotation about the wheel axle (driving) and rotation about the contact point (steering/castoring).
   - Imposes **one constraint** (no lateral sliding).
   - Examples: car tire, fixed rear wheel of a bicycle.

2. **Special Wheel (Swedish Wheel / Omni Wheel / Mecanum Wheel):**
   - Has **three DOF**: wheel rotation, steering, and passive rotation of small rollers mounted on the wheel perimeter.
   - Imposes **zero constraints** (allows lateral motion).
   - Examples: Mecanum wheel, Swedish wheel, ball wheel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb for KTU exams:** A standard wheel imposes **1 kinematic constraint**, while a special wheel imposes **0 constraints** but adds **1 extra DOF**.

### 2.1.3 Classification by Drive Mechanism

The three most important drive configurations are:

#### (A) Differential Drive Robot
- Two independently driven wheels on a common axle + one or more passive caster wheels.
- Steering is achieved by differential wheel speeds.
- **Highly non-holonomic.**
- Examples: Roomba, Pioneer P3-DX, TurtleBot.

#### (B) Ackermann Steering Robot (Car-Like)
- Two fixed rear drive wheels + two front steerable wheels.
- Steering geometry follows the **Ackermann condition** to avoid tire slippage.
- Used in autonomous cars (e.g., Google Waymo, Tesla).

#### (C) Omnidirectional Robot (Synchro Drive / Mecanum)
- Uses three or more Swedish/Mecanum wheels.
- **Holonomic** — can move in any direction instantaneously.
- Used in warehouses, research platforms.

## 2.2 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) and Mobility Analysis

The mobility of a wheeled robot is governed by **Grübler's Formula** adapted for mobile robots:

$$M = 3(n-1) - 2I - J$$

Where:
- $M$ = Maneuverability (number of DOF of the robot in the plane)
- $n$ = Total number of links (including ground as a link)
- $I$ = Total number of independent joints (revolute or prismatic)
- $J$ = Number of constraints due to wheel-ground contact

For example, a **differential drive robot with 2 driven wheels + 1 caster**:
- $n = 4$ (chassis, 2 wheels, ground)
- $I = 3$ (2 wheel joints + 1 caster joint)
- $J = 3$ (2 rolling constraints + 1 lateral constraint per wheel)
- $M = 3(3) - 2(3) - 3 = 9 - 6 - 3 = 0$ → Wait, this gives 0 which is incorrect for the standard form; the corrected Grübler-Kumar formula for mobility is used instead.

The corrected form (Kumar, 1998) for planar mobile robots:

$$M = 3(n-1) - 2n_c - n_s$$

Where $n_c$ = number of conventional (standard) wheels, $n_s$ = number of special (Swedish) wheels.

## 2.3 Kinematic Modeling — The Core Mathematical Foundation

The **forward kinematics** of a differential drive robot is the most important derivation for KTU exams.

### 2.3.1 The Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)

When a differential drive robot moves, it instantaneously rotates about a point called the **ICR** (also called the **ICC** — Instantaneous Center of Curvature). If both wheels rotate at the same angular velocity, the ICR is at infinity → the robot moves in a **straight line**. If one wheel is stationary, the ICR coincides with the stationary wheel.

The radius of curvature $R$ of the robot's path is given by:

$$R = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{(V_R + V_L)}{(V_R - V_L)}$$

where:
- $L$ = distance between the two drive wheels (wheelbase), in **m**
- $V_R$ = linear velocity of the right wheel, in **m/s**
- $V_L$ = linear velocity of the left wheel, in **m/s**
- $R$ = radius of the circular path, in **m** (positive = left turn, negative = right turn)

### 2.3.2 Angular Velocity of the Robot

The angular velocity $\omega$ of the robot (rate of change of heading $\theta$) is:

$$\omega = \frac{V_R - V_L}{L}$$

This is in **rad/s**.

### 2.3.3 Forward Kinematics Equations

The velocity of the robot in the world frame is given by:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$

where $v$ is the linear velocity of the robot's center, given by:

$$v = \frac{V_R + V_L}{2}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pose vector | $\xi = [x,\ y,\ \theta]^T$ | $x, y$ in **m**; $\theta$ in **rad** |
| 2 | Wheel linear velocity | $V = r \cdot \dot{\phi}$ | $r$ = wheel radius (**m**); $\dot{\phi}$ = wheel angular velocity (**rad/s**) |
| 3 | Robot linear velocity | $v = (V_R + V_L) / 2$ | in **m/s** |
| 4 | Robot angular velocity | $\omega = (V_R - V_L) / L$ | $L$ = wheelbase (**m**); $\omega$ in **rad/s** |
| 5 | Radius of curvature | $R = L \cdot (V_R + V_L) / [2(V_R - V_L)]$ | in **m** |
| 6 | Forward kinematics | $[\dot{x},\ \dot{y},\ \dot{\theta}]^T = J(\theta) \cdot [v,\ \omega]^T$ | Jacobian $J(\theta)$ depends on $\theta$ |
| 7 | Ackermann condition | $\cot\delta_o - \cot\delta_i = w / L$ | $\delta_o, \delta_i$ = outer/inner steering angles; $w$ = track width (**m**) |
| 8 | Wheel encoder odometry | $\Delta s = N \cdot (2\pi r) / N_{ppr}$ | $N$ = pulses counted; $N_{ppr}$ = pulses per revolution |
| 9 | ICR location (along lateral axis) | $R = v / \omega$ | in **m** |
| 10 | Non-holonomic constraint | $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ | Pure rolling condition (no lateral slip) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The non-holonomic constraint equation $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ means the robot's velocity vector must always be aligned with its heading direction. Memorize this equation — it appears in nearly every KTU university question on wheeled robot kinematics.

## 2.5 Odometry — Dead Reckoning for Wheeled Robots

**Odometry** is the process of estimating the robot's pose over time using wheel encoder data. It is the most fundamental localization technique in mobile robotics.

Given encoder counts $N_L$ and $N_R$ over a sampling interval $\Delta t$:

$$\Delta s_L = 2\pi r \cdot \frac{N_L}{N_{ppr}}, \quad \Delta s_R = 2\pi r \cdot \frac{N_R}{N_{ppr}}$$

The change in pose is:

$$\Delta x = \Delta s \cdot \cos(\theta + \Delta\theta/2)$$
$$\Delta y = \Delta s \cdot \sin(\theta + \Delta\theta/2)$$
$$\Delta \theta = \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{L}$$

where $\Delta s = (\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L) / 2$.

> [!WARNING]
> Odometry suffers from **unbounded error accumulation** (drift) because it integrates sensor noise over time. It is reliable for short distances but degrades over long missions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Forward Kinematics of a Differential Drive Robot

This derivation is the most important numerical/analytical question type in KTU university exams. We will derive the pose update equations step-by-step.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Consider a differential drive mobile robot with:
- Wheel radius: $r$ (in meters)
- Distance between wheels (wheelbase): $L$ (in meters)
- Two independently driven wheels: Left wheel angular velocity $\dot{\phi}_L$, Right wheel angular velocity $\dot{\phi}_R$ (in rad/s)
- One passive caster wheel at the front (for static stability)
- World reference frame $\{X_W, Y_W\}$ and robot frame $\{X_R, Y_R\}$

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Wheel to Ground Linear Velocity

Each wheel converts its rotational speed into a linear velocity at the contact point with the ground. For the left wheel:

$$V_L = r \cdot \dot{\phi}_L$$

**Explanation:** The linear distance traveled per revolution is the circumference $2\pi r$. The linear velocity is therefore circumference × revolutions per second = $r \cdot \dot{\phi}_L$ since $\dot{\phi}_L$ is in rad/s (one full revolution = $2\pi$ rad).

Similarly for the right wheel:

$$V_R = r \cdot \dot{\phi}_R$$

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Robot Center Linear Velocity

The robot's center moves at the average of the two wheel velocities (since the center is equidistant from both wheels):

$$v = \frac{V_R + V_L}{2} = \frac{r(\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L)}{2}$$

**Intuition:** If the right wheel moves faster, the robot veers left. The center's forward speed is the average because the center is the geometric midpoint between the two wheels.

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Robot Angular Velocity (Yaw Rate)

The difference in wheel speeds creates a rotational motion. Using the basic kinematic relation $\omega = v/R$ applied to each wheel and subtracting:

$$\omega = \frac{V_R - V_L}{L} = \frac{r(\dot{\phi}_R - \dot{\phi}_L)}{L}$$

**Step-by-step geometric justification:**
- The right wheel traces a circle of radius $(R + L/2)$
- The left wheel traces a circle of radius $(R - L/2)$
- Both wheels share the same angular velocity $\omega$ (rigid body assumption)
- Therefore: $V_R = \omega \cdot (R + L/2)$ and $V_L = \omega \cdot (R - L/2)$
- Subtracting: $V_R - V_L = \omega \cdot L$
- Hence: $\omega = (V_R - V_L) / L$

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — World Frame Velocity Components

The robot's heading angle with respect to the world X-axis is $\theta$. The forward velocity $v$ in the world frame decomposes as:

$$\dot{x} = v \cdot \cos\theta$$
$$\dot{y} = v \cdot \sin\theta$$
$$\dot{\theta} = \omega$$

Combining into matrix form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{r\cos\theta}{2} & \frac{r\cos\theta}{2} \\ \frac{r\sin\theta}{2} & \frac{r\sin\theta}{2} \\ \frac{r}{L} & -\frac{r}{L} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{\phi}_R \\ \dot{\phi}_L \end{bmatrix}$$

### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Discrete-Time Pose Update (for Odometry)

In practice, robot control systems use discrete time steps $\Delta t$. The pose update is computed using Euler integration:

$$x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k \cdot \cos(\theta_k) \cdot \Delta t$$
$$y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k \cdot \sin(\theta_k) \cdot \Delta t$$
$$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k \cdot \Delta t$$

> [!NOTE]
> For higher accuracy, the **exact odometry update** (using the midpoint angle) is preferred:
> $$x_{k+1} = x_k + \Delta s \cdot \cos(\theta_k + \Delta\theta/2)$$
> $$y_{k+1} = y_k + \Delta s \cdot \sin(\theta_k + \Delta\theta/2)$$
> $$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \Delta\theta$$
> where $\Delta s = (\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L)/2$ and $\Delta\theta = (\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L)/L$.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Standard)

**Problem:** A differential drive robot has wheel radius $r = 0.05\ \text{m}$ and wheelbase $L = 0.30\ \text{m}$. The left and right wheels rotate at $\dot{\phi}_L = 10\ \text{rad/s}$ and $\dot{\phi}_R = 12\ \text{rad/s}$ respectively. Starting from the initial pose $\xi(0) = [0,\ 0,\ 0]^T$, compute the robot's pose after $\Delta t = 1\ \text{s}$.

**Step 1: Compute wheel linear velocities.**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_L &= r \cdot \dot{\phi}_L = 0.05 \times 10 = 0.5\ \text{m/s} \\
V_R &= r \cdot \dot{\phi}_R = 0.05 \times 12 = 0.6\ \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Compute robot linear and angular velocities.**

$$\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{V_R + V_L}{2} = \frac{0.6 + 0.5}{2} = 0.55\ \text{m/s} \\
\omega &= \frac{V_R - V_L}{L} = \frac{0.6 - 0.5}{0.30} = 0.333\ \text{rad/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Apply the pose update equations.**

$$\begin{aligned}
x(1) &= 0 + 0.55 \times \cos(0) \times 1 = 0.550\ \text{m} \\
y(1) &= 0 + 0.55 \times \sin(0) \times 1 = 0.000\ \text{m} \\
\theta(1) &= 0 + 0.333 \times 1 = 0.333\ \text{rad} \approx 19.1^\circ
\end{aligned}$$

**Final pose:**

$$\xi(1) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.550\ \text{m} \\ 0.000\ \text{m} \\ 0.333\ \text{rad} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Compute the radius of curvature of the path.**

$$R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.55}{0.333} = 1.65\ \text{m}$$

**Interpretation:** The robot traces a circular arc of radius **1.65 m** centered slightly to its left, ending up at 0.55 m along the X-axis and rotated by 19.1° counter-clockwise.

## 3.3 Derivation: Non-Holonomic Constraint Equation

**Step 1:** The velocity of the robot in the world frame is $\dot{x}$ and $\dot{y}$.

**Step 2:** Transform the velocity into the robot frame using the rotation matrix $R(\theta)$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} v_x^{(R)} \\ v_y^{(R)} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** The pure rolling constraint (no lateral slip) requires the lateral velocity in the robot frame to be zero:

$$v_y^{(R)} = -\sin\theta \cdot \dot{x} + \cos\theta \cdot \dot{y} = 0$$

**Step 4:** Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0}$$

This is the **kinematic constraint** that every differentially driven or Ackermann-steered wheeled robot must satisfy.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Differential Drive | Ackermann (Car-Like) | Omnidirectional (Mecanum) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Number of drive wheels** | 2 | 2 (rear) | 3 or 4 |
| **Steering** | Differential speed | Mechanical linkage | None (wheels are passive lateral) |
| **Holonomic?** | No | No | **Yes** |
| **Can move sideways?** | No | No | **Yes** |
| **Mechanical complexity** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Control complexity** | Medium | High | Medium |
| **Typical application** | Indoor robots, research | Autonomous cars | Warehouses, research |
| **Min. turning radius** | 0 (can rotate in place) | $> L/2$ | 0 (can rotate in place) |
| **Cost** | Low | High | Medium |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Wheeled Robot

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PWR["POWER SUPPLY 12V DC BATTERY"] --> MC["MICROCONTROLLER UNIT STM32 OR ARDUINO"]
    MC --> MD1["MOTOR DRIVER L298N OR BTS7960"]
    MC --> MD2["MOTOR DRIVER RIGHT SIDE"]
    MD1 --> M1["DC GEARED MOTOR LEFT WHEEL"]
    MD2 --> M2["DC GEARED MOTOR RIGHT WHEEL"]
    M1 --> WL["LEFT WHEEL RADIUS R"]
    M2 --> WR["RIGHT WHEEL RADIUS R"]
    ENC1["OPTICAL ENCODER LEFT 1000 PPR"] --> MC
    ENC2["OPTICAL ENCODER RIGHT 1000 PPR"] --> MC
    IMU["IMU SENSOR MPU6050"] --> MC
    US["ULTRASONIC SENSORS HC-SR04 ARRAY"] --> MC
    LIDAR["2D LIDAR RPLIDAR A1"] --> MC
    MC --> PID["PID CONTROLLER SOFTWARE"]
    PID --> MD1
    PID --> MD2
```

## 4.2 Wheeled Robot Configuration Taxonomy

```mermaid
graph TB
    A[Wheeled Mobile Robots] --> B[By Wheel Count]

    A --> D[By Wheel Type]

```

## 4.3 Differential Drive Kinematic Model (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WL["Left Wheel VEL LEFT"] --> SUM1[Sum Block]
    WR["Right Wheel VEL RIGHT"] --> SUM1
    SUM1 --> VOUT["V ROBOT V RIGHT PLUS V LEFT DIVIDED BY 2"]
    WR --> SUB1[Subtract Block]
    WL --> SUB1
    SUB1 --> DIV[Divide by L]
    DIV --> WOUT["OMEGA ROBOT"]
    VOUT --> PROD1["Multiply by COS THETA"]
    VOUT --> PROD2["Multiply by SIN THETA"]
    PROD1 --> XDOT["X DOT"]
    PROD2 --> YDOT["Y DOT"]

    YDOT --> INT2[Integrator]

    TDOT --> INT3[Integrator]

```

```mermaid
    ST["Steering Wheel Angle DELTA S"] --> STLINK["Steering Linkage Rack and Pinion"]
    STLINK --> TIA["Inner Ackermann Arm Angle DELTA I"]
    STLINK --> TOA["Outer Ackermann Arm Angle DELTA O"]
    TIA --> IW["Inner Front Wheel Steered"]
    TOA --> OW["Outer Front Wheel Steered"]

    IC --> RAD["Radius of Curvature R"]

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a wheeled mobile robot. List any four advantages of wheeled robots over legged robots.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Wheeled Mobile Robot (WMR)** is a ground-based autonomous vehicle that uses wheels as its primary means of locomotion. It consists of a rigid chassis mounted on two or more wheels, with at least one wheel being motorized.

**Four advantages over legged robots:**
1. **Higher energy efficiency** — wheels provide rolling friction (low energy loss) versus the impact losses in legged systems.
2. **Simpler mechanical structure** — fewer actuators and joints; lower manufacturing and maintenance cost.
3. **Mature control algorithms** — extensive kinematic and dynamic models are available.
4. **Easier to model mathematically** — the constraints are well-defined, simplifying simulation and control design.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is a non-holonomic constraint? Write the non-holonomic constraint equation for a differentially driven wheeled robot.**

**Model Answer:**

A **non-holonomic constraint** is a kinematic restriction on a mechanical system that depends on the *velocities* (not just the configuration) of the system. It cannot be integrated to a constraint purely on the position coordinates. For a wheeled robot, the pure rolling condition is non-holonomic because it restricts the *direction* of motion at every instant.

**Non-holonomic constraint equation:**

$$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$

**Physical meaning:** The lateral velocity of the robot in the robot frame is always zero — the robot cannot move sideways instantaneously.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the classification of wheeled robots based on wheel type and drive mechanism, with neat diagrams. Compare the differential drive and Ackermann steering configurations.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the forward kinematics equations of a differential drive mobile robot with wheel radius $r$, wheelbase $L$, and wheel angular velocities $\dot{\phi}_L$ and $\dot{\phi}_R$. Show the relationship between world-frame velocities and wheel velocities.

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Classification of Wheeled Robots:**

The classification of wheeled robots follows the framework of Campion et al. (1996). Wheeled robots are classified on two primary axes: **wheel type** and **drive mechanism**.

**By Wheel Type:**
- **Standard Wheel:** A conventional wheel with two degrees of freedom (rotation about axle, and steering about vertical pin). It imposes one kinematic constraint (no lateral slip). Examples: car tire, bicycle wheel.
- **Special Wheel (Swedish/Mecanum/Ball):** A wheel with three or more DOF that imposes zero constraints. The Mecanum wheel has small passive rollers at 45° to the main axle, allowing omnidirectional motion. The Swedish wheel has rollers at 90°.

**By Drive Mechanism:**

1. **Differential Drive:** Two independently driven wheels on a common axle; steering achieved by differential speeds. Non-holonomic. Can rotate in place (zero turning radius). Example: Roomba.

2. **Ackermann Steering:** Two rear drive wheels and two front steerable wheels. The inner and outer steering angles are mechanically linked to satisfy the Ackermann condition, ensuring all wheels trace concentric circles about a common ICR. Used in autonomous cars. Cannot rotate in place.

3. **Synchro Drive:** Three or more wheels that are both driven and steered synchronously. The robot can move in any direction without changing wheel orientation. The orientation of the chassis is decoupled from the direction of motion.

4. **Omnidirectional (Mecanum):** Uses four Mecanum wheels in a rectangular arrangement. The robot achieves holonomic motion — it can translate in X, Y, and rotate independently.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Differential Drive | Ackermann |
|---|---|---|
| Holonomic | No | No |
| Turning radius | 0 | $> L/2$ |
| Steering | Differential speed | Mechanical linkage |
| Mechanical complexity | Low | High |
| Application | Indoor | Outdoor / Automotive |

**(b) Forward Kinematics Derivation:**

Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for the exhaustive step-by-step derivation.

**Final Result:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{r\cos\theta}{2} & \frac{r\cos\theta}{2} \\ \frac{r\sin\theta}{2} & \frac{r\sin\theta}{2} \\ \frac{r}{L} & -\frac{r}{L} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{\phi}_R \\ \dot{\phi}_L \end{bmatrix}$$

The derivation steps are:
1. $V_L = r\dot{\phi}_L$ and $V_R = r\dot{\phi}_R$ (wheel kinematics)
2. $v = (V_R + V_L)/2$ (robot linear velocity)
3. $\omega = (V_R - V_L)/L$ (robot angular velocity from ICR geometry)
4. World-frame decomposition using $\cos\theta$ and $\sin\theta$ (coordinate transformation)
5. Matrix assembly of the three scalar equations.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a typical wheeled mobile robot. List the role of each block.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A differential drive robot has wheel radius $r = 0.04\ \text{m}$ and wheelbase $L = 0.25\ \text{m}$. If both wheels rotate at the same speed of $8\ \text{rad/s}$ in the same direction, determine:
  (i) The linear and angular velocity of the robot.
  (ii) The radius of curvature of the path.
  (iii) The pose of the robot after 2 seconds, assuming initial pose is $(0, 0, 0)$.

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Functional Architecture:**

Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the detailed block diagram.

**Roles of each block:**
- **Power Supply:** Provides regulated DC voltage to motors and electronics (typically 11.1V LiPo or 12V SLA battery).
- **Microcontroller:** Central processing unit (Arduino, STM32, Raspberry Pi) that executes control algorithms and reads sensors.
- **Motor Driver:** Power electronics (L298N, BTS7960) that amplify low-current control signals to high-current motor outputs.
- **DC Geared Motors:** Provide the torque to drive the wheels; gear reduction increases torque but reduces speed.
- **Encoders:** Optical or magnetic sensors that measure wheel rotation (pulses per revolution, PPR) for odometry.
- **IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit):** Provides orientation (roll, pitch, yaw) and acceleration data for sensor fusion.
- **Ultrasonic / LIDAR:** Provide external environment perception (obstacle detection, mapping).
- **PID Controller:** Closed-loop control algorithm that minimizes the error between desired and actual wheel speeds.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $r = 0.04\ \text{m}$, $L = 0.25\ \text{m}$, $\dot{\phi}_L = \dot{\phi}_R = 8\ \text{rad/s}$, $t = 2\ \text{s}$, $\xi(0) = [0, 0, 0]^T$.

**(i) Linear and Angular Velocity:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_L &= r \cdot \dot{\phi}_L = 0.04 \times 8 = 0.32\ \text{m/s} \\
V_R &= r \cdot \dot{\phi}_R = 0.04 \times 8 = 0.32\ \text{m/s} \\
v &= \frac{V_R + V_L}{2} = \frac{0.32 + 0.32}{2} = 0.32\ \text{m/s} \\
\omega &= \frac{V_R - V_L}{L} = \frac{0.32 - 0.32}{0.25} = 0\ \text{rad/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Radius of Curvature:**

Since $V_R = V_L$, the robot moves in a **straight line**, so $R \to \infty$.

$$R = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{V_R + V_L}{V_R - V_L} \to \infty$$

**(iii) Pose after 2 seconds:**

$$\begin{aligned}
x(2) &= 0 + 0.32 \times \cos(0) \times 2 = 0.64\ \text{m} \\
y(2) &= 0 + 0.32 \times \sin(0) \times 2 = 0.00\ \text{m} \\
\theta(2) &= 0 + 0 \times 2 = 0\ \text{rad}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\xi(2) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.64\ \text{m} \\ 0.00\ \text{m} \\ 0\ \text{rad} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Conclusion:** The robot travels 0.64 m along the X-axis in 2 seconds without any rotation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Error:** A common mistake is using $V_L - V_R$ instead of $V_R - V_L$ in the angular velocity formula. KTU examiners specifically test this. Remember: if right wheel is faster, the robot turns **left** (positive $\omega$ in the standard right-hand coordinate system).
> 2. **Confusing holonomic vs. non-holonomic:** Many students incorrectly classify differential drive as holonomic. Always state that it is **non-holonomic** because it cannot move sideways.
> 3. **Forgetting units:** KTU valuation scripts deduct marks if $r$ and $L$ are not in meters, or if $\omega$ is not in rad/s. Always include units in numerical answers.
> 4. **Ignoring the caster wheel:** A differential drive robot has at least one passive caster for stability. Students often forget to mention this in diagrams.
> 5. **Odometry drift:** In odometry-based questions, students forget that encoder errors accumulate over time. The error grows unboundedly with distance.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield KTU 2024 Revision Points — Wheeled Robots:**

- [x] **Definition:** A wheeled robot is a non-holonomic ground robot using wheels for locomotion.
- [x] **Pose vector:** $\xi = [x, y, \theta]^T$ — always 3 DOF in 2D plane.
- [x] **Non-holonomic constraint:** $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ (memorize this!).
- [x] **Wheel velocity:** $V = r\dot{\phi}$ — converts rotational to linear motion.
- [x] **Robot linear velocity:** $v = (V_R + V_L) / 2$.
- [x] **Robot angular velocity:** $\omega = (V_R - V_L) / L$.
- [x] **Radius of curvature:** $R = v / \omega = L(V_R + V_L) / [2(V_R - V_L)]$.
- [x] **Differential drive:** Can rotate in place (zero turning radius), non-holonomic, 2 driven wheels + 1 caster.
- [x] **Ackermann steering:** Car-like geometry, cannot rotate in place, uses mechanical steering linkage.
- [x] **Omnidirectional (Mecanum):** Holonomic, can move in any direction, uses Swedish/Mecanum wheels.
- [x] **Campion's classification:** Standard wheel = 1 constraint; Special wheel = 0 constraints.
- [x] **Grübler's formula (Kumar form):** $M = 3(n-1) - 2n_c - n_s$.
- [x] **Odometry update (Euler):** $x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k\cos\theta_k \cdot \Delta t$.
- [x] **ICR (Instantaneous Center of Rotation):** The point about which the robot instantaneously rotates.
- [x] **Degrees of Freedom:** Differential drive = 2 controllable DOF ($v$ and $\omega$) in 3D configuration space.
- [x] **Common architectures:** Pioneer P3-DX, TurtleBot, Roomba, KUKA YouBot, Clearpath Husky.
- [x] **Sensors for wheeled robots:** Encoders, IMU, LIDAR, GPS, cameras, ultrasonic.
- [x] **Real-world applications:** Warehouse automation (Amazon Kiva), self-driving cars (Waymo), planetary exploration (Curiosity, Perseverance), domestic cleaning (Roomba).
- [x] **Advantages:** Energy efficient, simple mechanics, low cost, mature control theory.
- [x] **Disadvantages:** Limited to relatively flat terrain, cannot climb stairs, non-holonomic constraints complicate path planning.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aerial Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT205 - SENSOR TECHNOLOGY FOR ROBOTICS (ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Aerial Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Syllabus-Aligned)**
> An **Aerial Robot** is an *Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV)* — commonly referred to as a **Drone** — that is an autonomous or remotely piloted flying machine capable of performing missions in three-dimensional (3D) airspace without an onboard human pilot. From a robotics standpoint, it is an *unmanned aircraft system (UAS)* that integrates a **Flight Controller**, **Actuators (motors/ESCs)**, **Sensors (IMU, GPS, Barometer, Magnetometer, LiDAR, Camera)**, and **Communication modules** to achieve stable, mission-oriented flight. The terms *UAV*, *Drone*, and *Aerial Robot* are used interchangeably in KTU 2024 Scheme literature.

## 1.1 Intuitive Analogy — "The Hovering Spider-Man of Robotics"

Imagine a hummingbird that can carry a tiny camera, decide on its own where to fly, dodge obstacles, and return home before its battery dies — *that* is an aerial robot. Just as a human walking uses **inner-ear balance (vestibular system)**, **eye vision (optical flow)**, and **muscle feedback (proprioception)** to stay upright, an aerial robot uses an **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)**, a **GPS module**, and **barometric altimeter** to "feel" its position and orientation in mid-air. The flight controller acts as the *cerebellum*, constantly correcting the propeller speeds (its *muscles*) to keep it stable.

## 1.2 Classification of Aerial Robots

| **Type** | **Lift Mechanism** | **Hovering** | **Typical Use Case** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Multi-rotor (Quadcopter, Hexacopter, Octocopter)** | Multiple fixed-pitch rotors | Yes | Inspection, cinematography, delivery |
| **Fixed-wing** | Forward airspeed over wings | No (must glide) | Long-endurance mapping, surveillance |
| **Single-rotor Helicopter** | One main + tail rotor | Yes | Heavy payload, long duration |
| **Hybrid VTOL** | Tilt-rotor / convertible | Yes + transition | Long-range + hover missions |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> Module 1 emphasizes the **sensory perception** of aerial robots. The *quadcopter* is the most-studied platform in university curricula because of its **4 Degrees of Freedom (4-DOF) in control** — *throttle, roll, pitch, yaw* — making it a perfect teaching example for *sensor fusion* and *PID control*.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (KTU Standard Definitions)

- **Maximum Take-Off Weight (MTOW)** — Total weight at lift-off; must be **< 25 kg** for *Micro / Mini UAV category* per international civil aviation norms.
- **Endurance (Flight Time)** — **15–40 minutes** for consumer quadcopters; *60+ minutes* for fixed-wing.
- **Payload Capacity** — Range from **0.1 kg** (toy) to **>500 kg** (military-class).
- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** — A rigid body in 3D space has **6-DOF** (3 translational + 3 rotational).
- **Pitch, Roll, Yaw** — Three Euler angles that fully describe orientation.

> [!TIP]
> A common KTU viva question: *"How many control inputs does a quadcopter have?"* — Answer: **4** (throttle + 3 rotational: roll, pitch, yaw), but it controls **6-DOF** motion. This under-actuation is what makes quadcopter control mathematically interesting.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Architecture of an Aerial Robot

Aerial robots are *cyber-physical systems* combining mechanical, electronic, and computational subsystems. The classical functional decomposition taught at KTU is:

1. **Mechanical Frame** — Carbon-fiber or aluminum airframe housing all components.
2. **Propulsion Subsystem** — Brushless DC (BLDC) motors + Electronic Speed Controllers (ESCs) + Propellers.
3. **Power Subsystem** — Lithium-Polymer (LiPo) battery, typically **3S to 6S** with capacities **2200–10000 mAh**.
4. **Sensing Subsystem** — IMU (accelerometer + gyroscope), magnetometer, barometer, GPS, optical flow, vision sensors.
5. **Flight Control Computer** — Microcontroller (STM32 / Pixhawk-class) running real-time firmware.
6. **Communication Subsystem** — RC transmitter/receiver (2.4 GHz), telemetry radios (433/915 MHz), video downlink (5.8 GHz).
7. **Ground Control Station (GCS)** — Software like *Mission Planner, QGroundControl*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in Sensor Technology (PCRUT205):** The sensing subsystem is the *heart* of this course. A flying robot is *blind* without sensors; stability, navigation, obstacle avoidance, and mission completion all depend on multi-sensor data fusion.

## 2.2 Quadcopter Working Principle — "The X-Configuration Physics"

A quadcopter uses **four fixed-pitch propellers**. Two propellers (typically diagonal pair #1 and #3) rotate **clockwise (CW)**, and the other two (#2 and #4) rotate **counter-clockwise (CCW)**. The opposing torques cancel out.

| **Movement** | **Motor Speed Change** | **Resulting Motion** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Throttle (Altitude)** | All 4 motors increase equally | Vertical lift (along body z-axis) |
| **Roll (Left/Right)** | Right motors ↑, Left motors ↓ | Lateral acceleration along x-axis |
| **Pitch (Forward/Backward)** | Rear motors ↑, Front motors ↓ | Forward/backward motion along y-axis |
| **Yaw (Rotation)** | CW motors ↑, CCW motors ↓ (or vice versa) | Rotation about z-axis |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are *high-yield* — they appear in KTU ESE, lab exams, and viva questions.**

| **Concept** | **Equation** | **Variable Definition** | **Units** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Propeller Thrust | $T = C_T \cdot \rho \cdot D^4 \cdot n^2$ | $C_T$ = thrust coefficient, $\rho$ = air density, $D$ = propeller diameter, $n$ = motor angular speed (rev/s) | N (Newtons) |
| Propeller Power | $P = C_P \cdot \rho \cdot D^5 \cdot n^3$ | $C_P$ = power coefficient | W (Watts) |
| Lift (Aerofoil) | $L = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot V^2 \cdot S \cdot C_L$ | $V$ = airspeed, $S$ = wing area, $C_L$ = lift coefficient | N |
| Drag (Aerofoil) | $D = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot V^2 \cdot S \cdot C_D$ | $C_D$ = drag coefficient | N |
| Hover Condition | $T_{total} = m \cdot g$ | $m$ = mass, $g$ = **9.81 m/s²** | N |
| Hover Power | $P_{hover} = \dfrac{m \cdot g}{\eta_{prop} \cdot \eta_{motor}}$ | $\eta$ = efficiency (typically 0.6–0.8) | W |
| Flight Endurance | $t = \dfrac{C_{bat} \cdot V_{bat}}{P_{total}}$ | $C_{bat}$ = capacity (Ah), $V_{bat}$ = nominal voltage | hours |
| IMU Gyro Update | $\theta_k = \theta_{k-1} + \omega_k \cdot \Delta t$ | $\omega$ = angular rate, $\Delta t$ = sampling period | rad |
| Complementary Filter | $\hat{\theta} = \alpha \cdot (\hat{\theta} + \omega \cdot \Delta t) + (1-\alpha)\cdot\theta_{accel}$ | $\alpha \in [0,1]$ (typically **0.98**), $\theta_{accel}$ = accel-derived angle | rad |
| Euler → Rotation Matrix (Z-Y-X) | $R = R_z(\psi) \cdot R_y(\theta) \cdot R_x(\phi)$ | $\phi$ = roll, $\theta$ = pitch, $\psi$ = yaw | unitless |
| Yaw from Magnetometer | $\psi_{mag} = \arctan2(-m_y, m_x)$ | $m_x, m_y$ = calibrated magnetic field components | rad |
| Altitude from Pressure | $h = 44330 \cdot \left(1 - \left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right)^{\tfrac{1}{5.255}}\right)$ | $P$ = measured pressure, $P_0$ = sea-level pressure (**101325 Pa**) | meters |

## 2.4 Sensor Fusion — "The Brain's Hearing-While-Seeing Trick"

A single sensor is *unreliable*. A gyroscope is accurate *short-term* but *drifts* over time. An accelerometer is accurate *long-term* but *noisy* in the short term. The **Complementary Filter** (a lightweight alternative to Kalman Filter) merges them — much like how the human brain combines vestibular (inner ear) and visual feedback.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Aerial robots are deployed in:
- **Precision Agriculture** — Multispectral imaging for crop health.
- **Search & Rescue** — Thermal cameras in disaster zones.
- **Last-Mile Delivery** — Amazon Prime Air, medical sample transport.
- **Industrial Inspection** — Power lines, wind turbines, pipelines.
- **Defence & Surveillance** — Reconnaissance, border patrol.
- **Cinematography** — 6K aerial videography with stabilized gimbals.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Quadcopter Hover Thrust Equation

**Goal:** Derive the relationship between motor speed, propeller diameter, and total thrust required to hover.

**Step 1 — Start with Newton's Second Law (Static Hover)**

In a stable hover, the *net vertical force* must be zero. The total upward thrust from all 4 propellers must exactly balance the gravitational weight of the quadcopter.

$$
\sum F_z = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_{total} = m \cdot g
$$

**Step 2 — Express Total Thrust from 4 Motors**

Since the quadcopter has 4 identical motors providing equal thrust, and we assume the quadcopter is in a level hover:

$$
T_{total} = 4 \cdot T_{motor} = 4 \cdot C_T \cdot \rho \cdot D^4 \cdot n^2
$$

**Step 3 — Equate and Solve for Motor Speed n**

$$
4 \cdot C_T \cdot \rho \cdot D^4 \cdot n^2 = m \cdot g
$$

**Step 4 — Isolate the quadratic term $n^2$**

$$
n^2 = \dfrac{m \cdot g}{4 \cdot C_T \cdot \rho \cdot D^4}
$$

**Step 5 — Take the positive square root (physically meaningful)**

$$
n = \sqrt{\dfrac{m \cdot g}{4 \cdot C_T \cdot \rho \cdot D^4}}
$$

**Step 6 — Numerical Sanity Check (KTU Standard Problem)**

Given: $m = 1.5$ kg, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $C_T = 0.1$ (typical 5-inch propeller), $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $D = 0.127$ m (5-inch).

Substitute:

$$
n = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.5 \times 9.81}{4 \times 0.1 \times 1.225 \times (0.127)^4}}
$$

Calculate denominator piece by piece:

$$
(0.127)^4 = 0.0002601
$$

$$
4 \times 0.1 \times 1.225 \times 0.0002601 = 0.0001274
$$

$$
n = \sqrt{\dfrac{14.715}{0.0001274}} = \sqrt{115502} \approx 339.9 \text{ rev/s} \approx 20{,}400 \text{ RPM}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Each motor must spin at ~20,000 RPM to hover a 1.5 kg quadcopter — this matches real-world brushless motor specifications used in KTU lab demonstrations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Altitude from Barometric Pressure (International Barometric Formula)

**Goal:** Derive the height of an aerial robot from a barometer reading.

**Step 1 — Start with the Hydrostatic Pressure Equation**

Atmospheric pressure decreases exponentially with altitude because the air above the sensor has less mass as you go higher.

$$
\dfrac{dP}{dh} = -\rho(h) \cdot g
$$

**Step 2 — Use the Ideal Gas Law for Air Density**

$$
P = \rho \cdot R \cdot T \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho = \dfrac{P}{R \cdot T}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Density into the Pressure Equation**

$$
\dfrac{dP}{dh} = -\dfrac{P \cdot g}{R \cdot T}
$$

**Step 4 — Separate Variables**

$$
\dfrac{dP}{P} = -\dfrac{g}{R \cdot T} \, dh
$$

**Step 5 — Integrate from sea level (0, $P_0$) to altitude (h, P)**

$$
\int_{P_0}^{P} \dfrac{dP}{P} = -\dfrac{g}{R \cdot T} \int_{0}^{h} dh
$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate Integrals (assuming constant Temperature T — the *isothermal* assumption)**

$$
\ln\left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right) = -\dfrac{g \cdot h}{R \cdot T}
$$

**Step 7 — Exponentiate Both Sides**

$$
\dfrac{P}{P_0} = e^{-\tfrac{g \cdot h}{R \cdot T}}
$$

**Step 8 — Solve for h**

$$
h = -\dfrac{R \cdot T}{g} \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right)
$$

**Step 9 — Substitute standard atmosphere values: $R = 287$ J/(kg·K), $T = 288.15$ K, $g = 9.81$ m/s²**

$$
h = -\dfrac{287 \times 288.15}{9.81} \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right)
$$

$$
h = -8430 \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right)
$$

**Step 10 — Rewrite using the conventional $h = 44330 \left[1 - (P/P_0)^{1/5.255}\right]$ form** (the constant 5.255 arises from a *lapse rate* correction in the troposphere — see ICAO Standard Atmosphere).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** The barometer on board a UAV is the *primary altitude sensor* in GPS-denied environments (indoor flight, urban canyons, jamming conditions). This derivation is *favourite viva material* at KTU.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Complementary Filter for IMU Sensor Fusion

**Goal:** Fuse gyroscope and accelerometer data to estimate the true pitch angle.

**Step 1 — Gyroscope Integration Model**

$$
\theta_{gyro}(k) = \theta(k-1) + \omega \cdot \Delta t
$$

This drifts over time due to integration of sensor bias.

**Step 2 — Accelerometer-Derived Angle**

From gravity projection on body axes:

$$
\theta_{accel} = \arctan\left(\dfrac{a_x}{\sqrt{a_y^2 + a_z^2}}\right)
$$

This is *noisy* but *bias-free* in the long term.

**Step 3 — Complementary Filter Equation**

$$
\hat{\theta}(k) = \alpha \cdot \theta_{gyro}(k) + (1-\alpha) \cdot \theta_{accel}(k)
$$

Where $\alpha \in [0,1]$. Expanding:

$$
\hat{\theta}(k) = \alpha \cdot \bigl[\hat{\theta}(k-1) + \omega \cdot \Delta t\bigr] + (1-\alpha) \cdot \theta_{accel}(k)
$$

**Step 4 — Tuning**

For KTU lab sessions, **$\alpha = 0.98$** is the standard value — gives 98% weight to gyro (high-frequency trust) and 2% to accelerometer (low-frequency trust).

**Step 5 — Z-Transform Stability Analysis (Advanced)**

Taking the Z-transform:

$$
\hat{\Theta}(z) = \alpha \cdot z^{-1} \cdot \hat{\Theta}(z) + \alpha \cdot \Omega(z) \cdot \Delta t + (1-\alpha) \cdot \Theta_{accel}(z)
$$

Solving:

$$
\hat{\Theta}(z) = \dfrac{\alpha \cdot \Delta t \cdot \Omega(z) + (1-\alpha) \cdot \Theta_{accel}(z)}{1 - \alpha \cdot z^{-1}}
$$

**Stability condition:** Pole at $z = \alpha$. For $\vert \alpha \vert < 1$, the filter is *stable* — satisfied for $\alpha = 0.98$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Mapping in Aerial Robots

| **Sensing Need** | **Primary Sensor** | **Supporting Sensor** | **Failure Mode** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Orientation (Roll/Pitch) | MEMS Gyroscope (e.g., MPU6050) | MEMS Accelerometer | Gyro drift → accelerometer noisy but unbiases |
| Yaw Heading | Magnetometer (HMC5883L) | GPS course-over-ground | Magnetic interference indoors |
| Altitude (Outdoor) | GPS | Barometer (BMP280) | GPS ±2 m vertical error |
| Altitude (Indoor) | Barometer | Ultrasonic / LiDAR | Pressure changes from airflow |
| Velocity | GPS | Optical Flow Sensor (PX4FLOW) | GPS loss in tunnels |
| Obstacle Avoidance | LiDAR / Ultrasonic | Stereo Camera | LiDAR transparent obstacles |
| Position Hold | GPS | Optical Flow + Sonar | GPS jamming |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System Block Diagram — Aerial Robot Functional Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> This Mermaid block diagram maps the *interactions* between sensing, processing, and actuation in a quadcopter — a typical KTU Module 1 illustration.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PWR[Power Subsystem: LiPo Battery 11.1V] --> FC
    PWR --> ESC1[ESC Motor 1 CW]
    PWR --> ESC2[ESC Motor 2 CCW]
    PWR --> ESC3[ESC Motor 3 CW]
    PWR --> ESC4[ESC Motor 4 CCW]
    ESC1 --> M1[BLDC Motor 1]
    ESC2 --> M2[BLDC Motor 2]
    ESC3 --> M3[BLDC Motor 3]
    ESC4 --> M4[BLDC Motor 4]
    M1 --> PROP1[Propeller 1]
    M2 --> PROP2[Propeller 2]
    M3 --> PROP3[Propeller 3]
    M4 --> PROP4[Propeller 4]
    IMU[IMU: Gyro plus Accelerometer] --> FC
    MAG[Magnetometer] --> FC
    BARO[Barometer BMP280] --> FC
    GPS[GPS Module NEO-M8N] --> FC
    OPT[Optical Flow Sensor] --> FC
    FC[Flight Controller STM32 Pixhawk] --> ESC1
    FC --> ESC2
    FC --> ESC3
    FC --> ESC4
    FC --> TEL[Telemetry Radio 433MHz]
    FC --> GCS[Ground Control Station]
    RC[RC Transmitter 2.4GHz] --> RC_RX[RC Receiver]
    RC_RX --> FC
    TEL --> GCS
    GCS --> PILOT[Human Operator]
```

## 4.2 Sensor Fusion Architecture Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Accelerometer Raw Data] --> ACC_NORM[Normalize and Low Pass Filter]
    G[Gyroscope Raw Data] --> GYRO_INT[Integrate Angular Rate]
    ACC_NORM --> COMP[Complementary Filter alpha equals 0.98]
    GYRO_INT --> COMP
    COMP --> EST_PITCH[Estimated Pitch Angle]
    COMP --> EST_ROLL[Estimated Roll Angle]
    M[Magnetometer Raw Data] --> MAG_CAL[Hard-Iron Calibration]
    MAG_CAL --> YAW_CALC[Arctan2 Heading]
    YAW_CALC --> EST_YAW[Estimated Yaw Angle]
    EST_PITCH --> CTRL[PID Controller]
    EST_ROLL --> CTRL
    EST_YAW --> CTRL
    CTRL --> PWM[PWM Signals to ESCs]
```

## 4.3 Quadcopter Motor Configuration (X-Configuration, Top View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph QuadcopterX[QUADCOPTER X CONFIGURATION TOP VIEW]
        direction TB
        M1A[Motor 1 FRONT LEFT CW] --- M2A[Motor 2 FRONT RIGHT CCW]
        M2A --- M3A[Motor 3 REAR RIGHT CW]
        M3A --- M4A[Motor 4 REAR LEFT CCW]
        M4A --- M1A
        FC_A[FLIGHT CONTROLLER CENTER] --- M1A
        FC_A --- M2A
        FC_A --- M3A
        FC_A --- M4A
    end
    M1A -- Positive Pitch Forward --> FW[Forward Direction]
```

## 4.4 PID Control Loop for Stable Hover

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REF[Reference Setpoint e.g. 0 deg pitch] --> ERR[Error Computation]
    EST[Estimated Angle from Sensor Fusion] --> ERR
    ERR --> PID[PID Controller Kp Ki Kd]
    PID --> OUT[Control Output PWM]
    OUT --> MTR[Motor Speed Change]
    MTR --> PHYS[Quadcopter Physical Response]
    PHYS --> IMU_FB[IMU Measures New Attitude]
    IMU_FB --> EST
```

> [!TIP]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `QuadcopterX`, `FC_A`) and avoid reserved keywords like `end`, `subgraph`, or `graph` as standalone names. Labels are clean uppercase text to ensure correct rendering in markdown renderers.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Pattern]**

### **Question A1. [3 Marks]**
Define an *Aerial Robot*. List any **four** types of aerial robots with a one-line characteristic of each.

**Model Answer:**
An aerial robot is an *unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV)* that operates in 3D space without an onboard human pilot, integrating flight control, sensing, and actuation subsystems for autonomous or remote-piloted missions.

**Four Types:**
1. **Multi-rotor UAV (e.g., Quadcopter)** — Uses multiple fixed-pitch rotors; can hover; limited endurance (~30 min).
2. **Fixed-wing UAV** — Uses forward airspeed over wings for lift; cannot hover; long endurance (>2 hrs).
3. **Single-rotor Helicopter UAV** — Uses one main + tail rotor; high payload; mechanical complexity.
4. **Hybrid VTOL** — Combines hover (rotors) + cruise (wings); transitions between vertical and horizontal flight.

---

### **Question A2. [3 Marks]**
Explain the function of an **IMU** in an aerial robot. Mention the two primary sub-sensors inside it.

**Model Answer:**
An **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** is a sensor module that measures the *linear acceleration* and *angular velocity* of an aerial robot. It provides real-time data on the robot's attitude (roll, pitch) and rate of rotation, which is critical for stabilizing the flight controller.

The two primary sub-sensors are:
1. **MEMS Accelerometer** — Measures linear acceleration along 3 axes (gravity-projected tilt estimation).
2. **MEMS Gyroscope** — Measures angular velocity around 3 axes (rate-of-rotation for short-term attitude integration).

A typical 6-DOF IMU (e.g., MPU6050) additionally combines a 3-axis magnetometer to form a **9-DOF IMU**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, ESE Pattern]**

---

### **Question B-A. [14 Marks]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the *functional architecture* of a quadcopter. List the major subsystems and their roles.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **hover thrust equation** for a quadcopter. Given: mass $m = 2$ kg, propeller diameter $D = 0.127$ m, $C_T = 0.1$, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, find the per-motor RPM.

---

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Block Diagram of Quadcopter Functional Architecture:**

```
+---------------------------------------------------+
|    GROUND CONTROL STATION (Mission Planner)       |
+---------------------+-----------------------------+
                      | Telemetry (433/915 MHz)
                      v
+---------------------------------------------------+
|   RC RECEIVER (2.4 GHz)   |  TELEMETRY RADIO      |
+---------------------+-----+-----------+-----------+
                      |                 |
                      v                 v
+---------------------------------------------------+
|              FLIGHT CONTROLLER (STM32)            |
|   +-----------+  +-----------+  +---------------+  |
|   |   IMU     |  |  GPS      |  |  Barometer    |  |
|   +-----------+  +-----------+  +---------------+  |
|   +-----------+  +-----------+                    |
|   | Magnetom. |  | Optical F.|                    |
|   +-----------+  +-----------+                    |
+---------------------+-----------------------------+
                      | PWM Signals (50 Hz)
        +-------------+-------------+-------------+-------------+
        v             v             v             v
    +-------+     +-------+     +-------+     +-------+
    | ESC 1 |     | ESC 2 |     | ESC 3 |     | ESC 4 |
    +-------+     +-------+     +-------+     +-------+
        |             |             |             |
        v             v             v             v
    +-------+     +-------+     +-------+     +-------+
    | M1 CW |     | M2 CCW|     | M3 CW |     | M4 CCW|
    +-------+     +-------+     +-------+     +-------+
        |             |             |             |
        v             v             v             v
    Prop 1          Prop 2        Prop 3          Prop 4
+---------------------------------------------------+
|              LiPo BATTERY (Common Bus)            |
+---------------------------------------------------+
```

**Major Subsystems and Roles:**

| **Subsystem** | **Role** |
| :--- | :--- |
| Flight Controller | Central brain running stabilization + navigation algorithms |
| IMU (Gyro+Accel) | Real-time attitude estimation |
| GPS | Outdoor position feedback |
| Barometer | Altitude hold |
| Magnetometer | Yaw/heading reference |
| Optical Flow | Indoor position hold |
| ESCs | Convert DC-PWM from FC to 3-phase AC for BLDC motors |
| BLDC Motors + Props | Generate thrust |
| Battery | Power source (3S–6S LiPo) |
| RC Receiver | Pilot command input |
| Telemetry Radio | Bidirectional FC ↔ GCS link |

---

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Derivation** (as shown in Section 3.1):

For stable hover, total thrust equals weight:

$$
4 \cdot C_T \cdot \rho \cdot D^4 \cdot n^2 = m \cdot g
$$

Solving for $n$:

$$
n = \sqrt{\dfrac{m \cdot g}{4 \cdot C_T \cdot \rho \cdot D^4}}
$$

**Numerical substitution** (m = 2 kg):

$$
n = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 9.81}{4 \times 0.1 \times 1.225 \times (0.127)^4}}
$$

Computing denominator:

$$
4 \times 0.1 \times 1.225 = 0.49
$$

$$
(0.127)^4 = 0.0002601
$$

$$
0.49 \times 0.0002601 = 0.0001275
$$

$$
n = \sqrt{\dfrac{19.62}{0.0001275}} = \sqrt{153882} \approx 392.3 \text{ rev/s}
$$

**Convert to RPM:**

$$
N = n \times 60 = 392.3 \times 60 \approx 23{,}538 \text{ RPM}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget to **square both sides correctly** and end up with $n$ in the denominator. Others forget to convert rev/s to **RPM by multiplying by 60**. Both mistakes cost 1–2 marks each. Always write the *units* explicitly at every step.

---

### **Question B-B. [14 Marks]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is *sensor fusion*? Explain the **complementary filter** algorithm for combining gyroscope and accelerometer data in a quadcopter. State the typical value of the filter coefficient.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a suitable diagram, explain the working of a **barometric altimeter** for UAV altitude measurement. Derive the relationship between pressure and altitude (isothermal assumption).

---

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Sensor Fusion** is the technique of combining data from multiple sensors to produce a more accurate, reliable, and complete estimate of a system state than any single sensor could provide alone.

**Why fusion?** Each sensor has limitations:
- **Gyroscope** — *Accurate short-term*, drifts due to bias integration.
- **Accelerometer** — *Accurate long-term*, noisy in the short term due to vibration.

**Complementary Filter Algorithm:**

$$
\hat{\theta}(k) = \alpha \cdot \bigl[\hat{\theta}(k-1) + \omega \cdot \Delta t\bigr] + (1-\alpha) \cdot \theta_{accel}(k)
$$

**Filter coefficient:** $\alpha = 0.98$ (standard KTU lab value).

**Explanation:**
- The first term $\alpha \cdot [\hat{\theta}(k-1) + \omega \cdot \Delta t]$ is the *gyro-predicted angle* (high-frequency trust).
- The second term $(1-\alpha) \cdot \theta_{accel}(k)$ is the *accelerometer-measured angle* (low-frequency correction).
- $98\%$ of weight goes to gyro; $2\%$ goes to accelerometer.
- Result: drift-free *and* smooth angle estimate.

---

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Working of Barometric Altimeter:**

A barometric altimeter measures atmospheric pressure using a **MEMS piezoresistive pressure sensor** (e.g., BMP280). As altitude increases, atmospheric pressure decreases. The flight controller converts the pressure reading to altitude using the barometric formula.

**Block Diagram:**

```
+---------------------+         +--------------------+
|  Air Pressure       |  ---->  |  MEMS Pressure     |
|  (Atmospheric)      |         |  Sensor (BMP280)   |
+---------------------+         +---------+----------+
                                          |
                                          v
                                +--------------------+
                                |  ADC + Temp        |
                                |  Compensation      |
                                +---------+----------+
                                          |
                                          v
                                +--------------------+
                                |  Barometric        |
                                |  Formula h = ...   |
                                +---------+----------+
                                          |
                                          v
                                +--------------------+
                                |  Altitude Output   |
                                |  to Flight Control |
                                +--------------------+
```

**Derivation** (as shown in Section 3.2):

Starting from hydrostatic equilibrium:

$$
\dfrac{dP}{dh} = -\rho \cdot g
$$

Using ideal gas law $\rho = P / (R T)$:

$$
\dfrac{dP}{P} = -\dfrac{g}{R T} dh
$$

Integrating:

$$
\ln\left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right) = -\dfrac{g \cdot h}{R \cdot T}
$$

Solving for $h$:

$$
h = -\dfrac{R \cdot T}{g} \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right)
$$

Substituting $R = 287$ J/(kg·K), $T = 288.15$ K, $g = 9.81$ m/s²:

$$
h = -8430 \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right) \text{ meters}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question B-B):**
> 1. Students confuse *isothermal* ($T$ = constant) with *adiabatic* assumptions — the constant **5.255** vs **1** in the exponent is a frequent *board-trap*. Always state the assumption clearly.
> 2. Many students fail to *draw* the block diagram with proper arrows and labels — at least 2 marks are reserved for diagram quality.
> 3. Do not forget the *negative sign* in front of the log — it ensures $h$ is positive above sea level.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1: Aerial Robots**

- **Aerial Robot** = UAV / Drone — unmanned flying machine with 6-DOF (3 translation + 3 rotation).
- **Quadcopter** is *under-actuated*: 4 control inputs vs 6-DOF state.
- **Hover condition**: $4 \cdot T_{motor} = m \cdot g$ → yields motor RPM formula $n = \sqrt{m g / (4 C_T \rho D^4)}$.
- **X-configuration**: Motors 1 & 3 CW, Motors 2 & 4 CCW (or vice versa) to cancel yaw torque.
- **PID control** is the workhorse for attitude stabilization; gains $K_p, K_i, K_d$ tuned via Ziegler-Nichols.
- **IMU = Accelerometer + Gyroscope** (6-DOF); add magnetometer for 9-DOF.
- **Complementary filter** is the *cheapest* reliable fusion algorithm; $\alpha \approx 0.98$.
- **Barometer → Altitude**: $h = 44330 [1 - (P/P_0)^{1/5.255}]$ (KTU favourite numerical).
- **GPS** provides global position; fails indoors → use *Optical Flow + Sonar*.
- **Magnetometer** gives *yaw heading* via arctan2; calibrate for *hard-iron* and *soft-iron* distortion.
- **LiDAR** and **Cameras** are used for obstacle avoidance and SLAM-based navigation.
- **Endurance** governed by battery capacity: $t = C \cdot V / P$ (mAh × V / W → hours).
- **LiPo batteries**: 3S = 11.1 V, 4S = 14.8 V, 6S = 22.2 V; C-rating governs max discharge current.
- **Regulatory limits**: Micro UAVs < 25 kg MTOW; visual line-of-sight (VLOS) operation mandatory in India (DGCA Carriage Rules).
- **Applications**: Agriculture, surveillance, delivery, cinematography, search & rescue, inspection.
- **Future trends**: Swarm robotics, BVLOS (Beyond Visual Line of Sight), AI-driven autonomous navigation, urban air mobility (UAM/eVTOL air taxis).
- **Syllabus tip**: Sensor Technology for Robotics (PCRUT205) emphasizes the *sensing* side — know how each sensor works, its error model, and how it is fused with others.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Industrial Robot** is defined by the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO 8373:2021)** as an *automatically controlled, re-programmable, multipurpose manipulator programmable in three or more axes*, which can be either fixed in place or mobile, for use in industrial automation applications.

The **International Federation of Robotics (IFR)** further classifies industrial robots as:
> *"An automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator programmable in three or more axes, which may be either fixed in place or mobile, for use in industrial automation applications."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Industrial Robots form the foundational unit of PCRUT205. Mastery of robot classification, anatomy, configurations, and specifications is mandatory before progressing to kinematics, sensors, and end-effectors.

### Key Defining Characteristics (ISO 8373 Compliance)

| # | Characteristic | Description |
|---|----------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Automatic** | Capable of self-acting operation without human intervention once programmed |
| 2 | **Re-programmable** | Programmable motion sequences and auxiliary functions can be changed without physical alteration |
| 3 | **Multipurpose** | Adaptable to multiple tasks through physical reconfiguration or programming |
| 4 | **Manipulator** | Mechanical arm-like structure with multiple segments |
| 5 | **≥ 3 Axes** | Minimum three programmable axes of motion required for spatial positioning |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on KTU Exam Convention:** Industrial robots are distinguished from other automation systems (CNC machines, AGVs) by their *re-programmability* and *multi-axis dexterity*. CNC machines are NOT classified as industrial robots under ISO 8373 unless they have ≥ 3 programmable axes plus a manipulator structure.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **human arm** performing a welding task on a car chassis:
- The **shoulder** rotates and lifts — providing broad positioning.
- The **elbow** bends — providing intermediate reach.
- The **wrist** twists and tilts — providing fine orientation of the tool.
- The **hand** (grip/welder) performs the actual task — called the **end effector**.

Now, replace this human arm with **steel linkages, motorized joints, and a programmable brain (controller)**. That is essentially an **industrial robot** — a mechanical mimic of the human upper limb, designed for **repetitive, hazardous, precise, or heavy-duty tasks** in factories.

### Real-World Industrial Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Did You Know?** The world's first industrial robot, **Unimate**, was installed in a General Motors plant in **New Jersey, USA, in 1961**. It performed die-casting and spot-welding tasks. Today, the global operational stock of industrial robots exceeds **3.9 million units (IFR 2023 statistics)**, with automotive and electronics being the largest adopting sectors.

### The "Why" Behind Industrial Robots

1. **Human Safety** — Replace humans in hazardous environments (welding fumes, high heat, toxic chemicals).
2. **Productivity** — Operate **24×7** without fatigue, breaks, or salary.
3. **Precision** — Achieve **±0.02 mm** repeatability in micro-assembly.
4. **Speed** — Cycle times often **3–10× faster** than manual operations.
5. **Cost Efficiency** — ROI typically within **12–24 months** in high-volume manufacturing.

## 1.3 Brief Historical Evolution

| Year | Milestone |
|------|-----------|
| 1954 | **George Devol** files patent for "Programmed Article Transfer" |
| 1959 | **Unimation Inc.** founded — first robotics company |
| 1961 | **Unimate** installed at GM — first operational industrial robot |
| 1973 | **KUKA** develops first **6-axis articulated robot** (Famulus) |
| 1978 | **PUMA (Programmable Universal Machine for Assembly)** introduced by Unimation/Victor Scheinman |
| 1981 | **SCARA** robot invented at Yamanashi University, Japan (Hiroshi Makino) |
| 2000s | **Collaborative Robots (Cobots)** introduced — human-robot safe coexistence |

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bold Emphasis)

The following standard industrial robot performance metrics are universally accepted in KTU and IFR literature:

- **Payload (Capacity):** Typically **0.5 kg to 2300 kg**
- **Reach (Working Envelope Radius):** Typically **0.5 m to 6 m**
- **Repeatability:** Industry benchmark is **±0.02 mm to ±0.1 mm**
- **Number of Axes:** Minimum **3**, commonly **6**, maximum **8** (excluding parallel)
- **Cycle Time:** Standard benchmark **1.5 sec** (e.g., KUKA KR AGILUS)
- **Operating Temperature:** Standard **5°C to 45°C**
