# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an Industrial Robot — The Six Functional Subsystems

An industrial robot is a **mechatronic system** integrating six key subsystems. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly tests this taxonomy.

### 2.1.1 The Manipulator (Mechanical Arm / Linkage System)

The **manipulator** is the physical body of the robot consisting of:
- **Links (Rigid Members)** — connected by joints
- **Joints (Revolute R or Prismatic P)** — provide relative motion
- **Base** — fixed pedestal mounting the robot to the floor/work surface

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Terminology:** The manipulator is often called the "arm" or "kinematic chain." The kinematic chain comprises **links (bones)** and **joints (muscles/articulating points)**. Each joint contributes one **Degree of Freedom (DOF)**.

### 2.1.2 The End Effector (End-of-Arm Tooling — EOAT)

The **end effector** is the device attached to the robot's wrist that performs the actual task. It is *not* considered a part of the manipulator proper.

Classification:
- **Grippers (Mechanical)** — jaws, claw, vacuum cups
- **Tools (Process-specific)** — welding torches, paint sprayers, drills, cutting tools
- **Sensors** — force/torque, vision, tactile

### 2.1.3 The Controller (Brain / CPU)

The **controller** is the computer system that:
- Executes user programs
- Computes joint trajectories
- Sends commands to servo drives
- Reads feedback from encoders and sensors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Emphasis:** The controller implements the **kinematic model** (forward & inverse) and the **dynamic model** (forces, torques) of the robot in real-time.

### 2.1.4 Power Supply / Drive System

Provides regulated energy to motors and actuators:
- **Electrical** (most common): Servo motors, stepper motors
- **Hydraulic** (heavy payload): Up to 2300 kg payloads
- **Pneumatic** (light, fast): Pick-and-place operations
- **Hybrid Electro-hydrostatic**

### 2.1.5 Sensors (Internal and External)

- **Internal Sensors:** Encoders, resolvers, tachometers (proprioceptive)
- **External Sensors:** Vision cameras, proximity, force/torque (exteroceptive)

*(Detailed sensor treatment occurs in subsequent modules of PCRUT205.)*

### 2.1.6 Software (Programming Environment)

Offline programming (OLP), teach pendant interfaces, simulation environments (e.g., RoboDK, ABB RobotStudio).

---

## 2.2 Robot Configurations — The KTU Big Six

KTU frequently asks for diagrams and comparative analyses of robot configurations. There are **six major configurations** classified by their **kinematic structure**.

### 2.2.1 Cartesian (Gantry) Robot — Configuration: PPP

**Structure:** Three mutually perpendicular **prismatic joints**.

**Joint sequence:** Linear → Linear → Linear

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Workspace Shape | Rectangular cuboid |
| Typical DOF | 3 (plus wrist) |
| Payload Range | Up to 5000 kg |
| Accuracy | Excellent (±0.025 mm) |
| Cost | High (large linear guides) |
| Applications | CNC loading, palletizing, 3D printing, assembly |

### 2.2.2 Cylindrical Robot — Configuration: RPP

**Structure:** One **revolute** base joint + two **prismatic** joints.

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Workspace Shape | Hollow cylinder |
| Typical DOF | 3 (plus wrist) |
| Payload Range | 5–200 kg |
| Applications | Machine tending, foundry, die-casting |

### 2.2.3 Spherical (Polar) Robot — Configuration: RRP

**Structure:** Two **revolute** joints + one **prismatic** joint.

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Workspace Shape | Spherical sector (portion of a sphere) |
| Typical DOF | 3 (plus wrist) |
| Payload Range | 5–50 kg |
| Applications | Welding, painting, forging |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Historical Note:** The **Unimate 2000** (the first Unimate) used spherical/polar coordinates. This configuration is rarely used in modern industry but is critical for KTU theory questions.

### 2.2.4 Articulated (Revolute / Jointed-Arm) Robot — Configuration: RRR (or 6R)

**Structure:** Three or more **revolute** joints in series, mimicking a human arm.

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Workspace Shape | Irregular spherical lobe |
| Typical DOF | 6 (most common modern industrial robot) |
| Payload Range | 0.5–2300 kg |
| Repeatability | ±0.02 mm (industry benchmark) |
| Applications | Welding, painting, assembly, material handling |
| Examples | KUKA KR, ABB IRB, FANUC M-series, Yaskawa Motoman |

### 2.2.5 SCARA Robot (Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm)

**Structure:** Two **revolute** joints (parallel axes) + one **prismatic** vertical joint.

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Workspace Shape | Flat circular sector |
| Typical DOF | 3–4 |
| Compliance | **Selective** — rigid vertically, compliant horizontally (XY plane) |
| Applications | PCB assembly, pick-and-place, small parts insertion |
| Examples | Yamaha YK, Epson SCARA, Adept Cobra |

> [!NOTE]
> **SCARA Innovation:** The horizontal compliance allows for *radial misalignment tolerance* during vertical insertion tasks (e.g., pushing a pin into a hole without jamming) — a property unique to SCARA.

### 2.2.6 Delta (Parallel) Robot

**Structure:** Three parallel kinematic chains connected to a common base and end-effector platform.

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Workspace Shape | Dome / hemispherical |
| Typical DOF | 3 (translational only) |
| Speed | Extremely high (up to 300 picks/min) |
| Payload | Low (0.1–5 kg) |
| Applications | High-speed packaging, food sorting, pharmaceutical pick-and-place |

---

## 2.3 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) — Detailed Theory

### 2.3.1 Formal Definition

> **Degree of Freedom (DOF)** is the *number of independent parameters (coordinates)* required to completely specify the configuration (position and orientation) of a mechanism in space.

### 2.3.2 Kutzbach-Grübler Mobility Criterion (Advanced)

For spatial (3D) mechanisms, the mobility (DOF) is given by:

$$
M = 6(n - 1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5
$$

Where:
- $M$ = Mobility (DOF)
- $n$ = Total number of links (including ground)
- $j_1$ = Number of single-DOF joints (e.g., revolute, prismatic)
- $j_2$ = Number of two-DOF joints (e.g., universal joint)
- $j_3$ = Number of three-DOF joints (e.g., ball-and-socket)
- $j_4$ = Number of four-DOF joints
- $j_5$ = Number of five-DOF joints

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** Each revolute (R) or prismatic (P) joint contributes **1 DOF**. A standard 6-axis articulated robot has 6 DOF in its kinematic chain (excluding any gripper finger DOF).

### 2.3.3 Spatial DOF Requirement

A rigid body in 3D space has **6 DOF**:
- **3 Translational:** $x, y, z$ (positioning)
- **3 Rotational:** $\text{Roll}, \text{Pitch}, \text{Yaw}$ (orientation)

For a robot to **arbitrarily position AND orient** an object in space, it requires **≥ 6 DOF**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Insight:** The first 3 DOF of an industrial robot arm are called the **"major axes"** or **"arm"** and provide positioning. The last 3 DOF form the **"wrist"** and provide orientation. This decoupled "wrist-centered" design (spherical wrist) simplifies kinematics — a key concept in Module 2.

---

## 2.4 Robot Wrist Configurations

A robot wrist is a sub-chain of **2 or 3 DOF** attached at the end of the arm. Common wrist joint combinations:

| Wrist Type | DOF | Joints | Function |
|------------|-----|--------|----------|
| 2-DOF Wrist | 2 | R, R | Pitch + Roll |
| 3-DOF Spherical Wrist | 3 | R, R, R (axes intersect at a single point) | Roll + Pitch + Yaw |
| Gimbal Wrist | 3 | R, R, R (parallel axes) | Less common |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** The intersection of the last three joint axes at a **common point (wrist center)** is called a **spherical wrist**. This decouples position from orientation, allowing independent solution of forward kinematics.

---

## 2.5 Robot Performance Specifications

### 2.5.1 Payload (Load Capacity)

The maximum mass that the robot can carry while maintaining all rated performance specifications, **including** the weight of the end effector.

### 2.5.2 Reach (Working Envelope Radius)

The maximum distance from the **center of the robot base** to the **center of the end-effector mounting flange**.

### 2.5.3 Repeatability vs Accuracy — A Subtle Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Most Commonly Confused Terms in KTU Examinations:**

**Repeatability:** The ability of the robot to return to a previously taught position when commanded repeatedly from various directions.

**Accuracy:** The ability of the robot to move to a commanded position in space, computed from a kinematic model.

**Mathematically:**

$$
R = \pm \frac{1}{2} \left( \overline{X}_{\max} - \overline{X}_{\min} \right)
$$

Where $\overline{X}_{\max}$ and $\overline{X}_{\min}$ represent the maximum and minimum mean positions reached over many trials.

> **Industrial Reality:** Industrial robots are typically **5 to 10 times more repeatable than accurate**. A robot with ±0.05 mm repeatability may have ±0.5 mm accuracy. This is because accuracy depends on the kinematic model and calibration, whereas repeatability depends on encoder resolution and servo control.

### 2.5.4 Resolution

The smallest incremental movement achievable by the robot, governed by the controller's bit-resolution and encoder count.

$$
\text{Resolution} = \frac{\text{Total Travel}}{\text{Encoder Counts per Revolution} \times \text{Gear Ratio}}
$$

### 2.5.5 Speed

Specified as either **maximum joint speed (deg/s or mm/s)** or **end-effector linear/angular speed**.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Reference Table

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit |
|---|---------|----------------------|------|
| 1 | Spatial DOF of a rigid body | 6 (3 translational + 3 rotational) | — |
| 2 | Joint DOF (R or P) | 1 each | — |
| 3 | Kutzbach Mobility (Spatial) | $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | — |
| 4 | Workspace Volume (Cartesian) | $V = L_x \cdot L_y \cdot L_z$ | m³ |
| 5 | Workspace Volume (Cylindrical) | $V = \pi L (R_{\max}^2 - R_{\min}^2)$ | m³ |
| 6 | Workspace Volume (Spherical) | $V = \frac{2}{3}\pi R^3 (1 - \cos \theta_{\max})$ | m³ |
| 7 | Repeatability | $R = \pm \frac{1}{2}(\overline{X}_{\max} - \overline{X}_{\min})$ | mm |
| 8 | Resolution | $\frac{\text{Total Travel}}{\text{Encoder CPR} \times \text{Gear Ratio}}$ | mm |
| 9 | Maximum Joint Speed (Revolute) | $\omega_{\max} = \frac{v}{r}$ | rad/s |
| 10 | Degree of Arc Coverage (Spherical) | $\Omega = \text{steradians}$ | sr |

> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are essential for **workspace analysis, robot selection, and cycle-time estimation** in industrial automation projects. Robot integrators use them to determine cell layouts and pick/place feasibility.

---

## 2.7 Robot Classification — Multi-Criteria Taxonomy

Industrial robots are classified by multiple engineering criteria. The KTU syllabus tests classification thoroughly.

### By Coordinate System
- Cartesian, Cylindrical, Spherical, Articulated, SCARA, Parallel

### By Drive Technology
- **Hydraulic** — high payload, low precision
- **Pneumatic** — low cost, simple, limited DOF
- **Electric (Servo)** — most common today, high precision

### By Control Method
- **Non-Servo (Open-Loop):** Limited to point-to-point, stops at end positions (e.g., pick-and-place pneumatic arms)
- **Servo (Closed-Loop):** Continuous path control, trajectory tracking

### By Application (IFR Classification)
- **Handling Operations** — palletizing, machine tending
- **Welding** — spot, arc, MIG, TIG
- **Painting & Coating** — automotive body finishing
- **Assembly** — electronic, mechanical
- **Material Removal** — cutting, grinding, deburring

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Workspace Volume of a Spherical Robot

### Problem Statement
A spherical robot has a revolute base joint $\theta_1$, an elevating revolute joint $\theta_2$, and a prismatic reach extension $r$. The base joint rotates through $360°$, the elevating joint through $\theta_{2,\max}$ from horizontal, and the reach extends from $r_{\min}$ to $r_{\max}$. Derive the workspace volume.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Identify the Coordinate System**

The spherical robot operates in spherical coordinates $(r, \theta, \phi)$. The transform from spherical to Cartesian is:

$$
x = r \sin\theta \cos\phi
$$

$$
y = r \sin\theta \sin\phi
$$

$$
z = r \cos\theta
$$

> This conversion is justified because the geometry of the robot naturally defines polar coordinates with the base as the origin.

**Step 2: Express the Differential Volume Element**

The differential volume in spherical coordinates is:

$$
dV = r^2 \sin\theta \, dr \, d\theta \, d\phi
$$

> **Logic:** This is the standard Jacobian of spherical coordinates. Each small displacement $dr$, $d\theta$, $d\phi$ defines a small curvilinear cube of volume $r \, dr \times r \, d\theta \times r \sin\theta \, d\phi = r^2 \sin\theta \, dr \, d\theta \, d\phi$.

**Step 3: Define the Integration Limits**

Given the joint limits:

$$
r: \quad r_{\min} \to r_{\max}
$$

$$
\theta: \quad 0 \to \theta_{2,\max}
$$

$$
\phi: \quad 0 \to 2\pi
$$

> **Logic:** $\theta$ (the elevation angle) is measured from the positive $z$-axis (vertical up). The base joint $\phi$ rotates the entire arm around the vertical axis through a full circle.

**Step 4: Set Up the Triple Integral**

$$
V = \int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\theta_{2,\max}} \int_{r_{\min}}^{r_{\max}} r^2 \sin\theta \, dr \, d\theta \, d\phi
$$

**Step 5: Integrate with respect to $r$**

$$
V = \int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\theta_{2,\max}} \left[ \frac{r^3}{3} \right]_{r_{\min}}^{r_{\max}} \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi
$$

$$
V = \int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\theta_{2,\max}} \frac{r_{\max}^3 - r_{\min}^3}{3} \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi
$$

> **Logic:** Integration of $r^2$ with respect to $r$ yields $r^3/3$. Evaluated between limits, the result is the cube of the maximum reach minus the cube of the minimum reach, divided by 3.

**Step 6: Integrate with respect to $\theta$**

$$
V = \frac{r_{\max}^3 - r_{\min}^3}{3} \int_{0}^{2\pi} \left[ -\cos\theta \right]_{0}^{\theta_{2,\max}} d\phi
$$

$$
V = \frac{r_{\max}^3 - r_{\min}^3}{3} \int_{0}^{2\pi} \left( 1 - \cos\theta_{2,\max} \right) d\phi
$$

> **Logic:** $\int \sin\theta \, d\theta = -\cos\theta$. Evaluated from $0$ to $\theta_{2,\max}$ yields $-\cos\theta_{2,\max} - (-\cos 0) = 1 - \cos\theta_{2,\max}$.

**Step 7: Integrate with respect to $\phi$**

$$
V = \frac{r_{\max}^3 - r_{\min}^3}{3} \cdot (1 - \cos\theta_{2,\max}) \cdot \left[ \phi \right]_{0}^{2\pi}
$$

$$
V = \frac{2\pi}{3} \left( r_{\max}^3 - r_{\min}^3 \right) \left( 1 - \cos\theta_{2,\max} \right)
$$

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{V = \frac{2\pi}{3} \left( r_{\max}^3 - r_{\min}^3 \right) \left( 1 - \cos\theta_{2,\max} \right)}
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** This closed-form expression allows roboticists to compute the *reachable workspace* of any spherical robot. Setting $r_{\min} = 0$ (no inner dead-zone) and $\theta_{2,\max} = \pi/2$ (limited to lower hemisphere) yields the standard hemispherical workspace formula. This is essential for cell layout design.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Repeatability of a Revolute Joint

### Problem Statement
A revolute joint is commanded to return to the same angle **30 times**. The encoder reads (in degrees) for each return:

**95.0, 95.1, 94.9, 95.2, 94.8, 95.0, 95.1, 94.9, 95.0, 95.1, 94.9, 95.0, 95.2, 94.8, 95.1, 94.9, 95.0, 95.1, 94.9, 95.0, 95.1, 94.9, 95.0, 95.2, 94.8, 95.0, 95.1, 94.9, 95.0, 95.1**

Compute the repeatability.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute the Mean Position**

Sum of all readings:

$$
\Sigma = 95.0 + 95.1 + 94.9 + 95.2 + 94.8 + 95.0 + 95.1 + 94.9 + 95.0 + 95.1 + 94.9 + 95.0 + 95.2 + 94.8 + 95.1 + 94.9 + 95.0 + 95.1 + 94.9 + 95.0 + 95.1 + 94.9 + 95.0 + 95.2 + 94.8 + 95.0 + 95.1 + 94.9 + 95.0 + 95.1
$$

Counting carefully:
- 95.0 appears **9 times** → sum contribution = $9 \times 95.0 = 855.0$
- 95.1 appears **8 times** → sum contribution = $8 \times 95.1 = 760.8$
- 94.9 appears **7 times** → sum contribution = $7 \times 94.9 = 664.3$
- 95.2 appears **3 times** → sum contribution = $3 \times 95.2 = 285.6$
- 94.8 appears **3 times** → sum contribution = $3 \times 94.8 = 284.4$

Total $\Sigma = 855.0 + 760.8 + 664.3 + 285.6 + 284.4 = 2850.1$

Mean:

$$
\overline{X} = \frac{2850.1}{30} = 95.0033... \approx 95.00°
$$

> **Logic:** Repeatability uses the *mean position per trial cluster*, not individual encoder counts. For simplicity, since the commanded position is constant and the spread is symmetric, we use the global mean.

**Step 2: Identify Maximum and Minimum Mean Positions**

Since the readings cluster around three distinct mean values (94.8, 95.1, 95.2):

$$
\overline{X}_{\max} = 95.2°
$$

$$
\overline{X}_{\min} = 94.8°
$$

**Step 3: Apply the Repeatability Formula**

$$
R = \pm \frac{1}{2} \left( \overline{X}_{\max} - \overline{X}_{\min} \right)
$$

$$
R = \pm \frac{1}{2} (95.2 - 94.8)
$$

$$
R = \pm \frac{1}{2} (0.4)
$$

$$
\boxed{R = \pm 0.2°}
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** This joint exhibits **±0.2° repeatability**. For a link of length 0.5 m, the linear repeatability at the end-effector becomes approximately:

$$
\Delta L = L \cdot \Delta\theta_{rad} = 0.5 \times (0.2° \times \pi/180) = 0.5 \times 0.00349 \approx 1.74 \, \text{mm}
$$

This *radial repeatability* must be added to the joint's angular repeatability to compute the overall robot repeatability per **ISO 9283** standards.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Workspace Volume of a Cylindrical Robot

### Problem Statement
A cylindrical robot has a revolute base joint (full rotation), a vertical prismatic joint of stroke 1 m, and a radial prismatic extension from 0.3 m to 1.2 m. Derive the workspace volume.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Define the Cylindrical Coordinates**

$$
x = r\cos\phi, \quad y = r\sin\phi, \quad z = z
$$

**Step 2: Differential Volume Element**

$$
dV = r \, dr \, d\phi \, dz
$$

> **Logic:** Standard Jacobian for cylindrical coordinates.

**Step 3: Integration Limits**

$$
r: 0.3 \to 1.2 \, \text{m}, \quad \phi: 0 \to 2\pi, \quad z: 0 \to 1.0 \, \text{m}
$$

**Step 4: Triple Integration**

$$
V = \int_{0}^{1.0} \int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0.3}^{1.2} r \, dr \, d\phi \, dz
$$

**Step 5: Integrate $r$**

$$
V = \int_{0}^{1.0} \int_{0}^{2\pi} \left[ \frac{r^2}{2} \right]_{0.3}^{1.2} d\phi \, dz = \int_{0}^{1.0} \int_{0}^{2\pi} \frac{1.2^2 - 0.3^2}{2} d\phi \, dz
$$

$$
V = \int_{0}^{1.0} \int_{0}^{2\pi} \frac{1.44 - 0.09}{2} d\phi \, dz = \int_{0}^{1.0} \int_{0}^{2\pi} 0.675 \, d\phi \, dz
$$

**Step 6: Integrate $\phi$**

$$
V = \int_{0}^{1.0} 0.675 \cdot 2\pi \, dz = \int_{0}^{1.0} 1.35\pi \, dz
$$

**Step 7: Integrate $z$**

$$
V = 1.35\pi \cdot 1.0 = 1.35\pi
$$

$$
\boxed{V \approx 4.241 \, \text{m}^3}
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** A cylindrical robot with this configuration can reach 4.24 m³ of space — a useful planning metric for tool placement and surrounding fixture design.

---

## 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation: Kutzbach DOF for an Articulated Robot Arm

### Problem Statement
A 6-DOF articulated robot has the following structure:
- 6 moving links (upper arm, forearm, 3 wrist links, gripper link)
- 6 revolute joints
- 1 fixed ground link

Compute the mobility using Kutzbach's criterion.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify Parameters**

$$
n = 7 \quad (\text{ground} + 6 \text{ moving links})
$$

$$
j_1 = 6 \quad (\text{6 revolute joints})
$$

$$
j_2 = j_3 = j_4 = j_5 = 0
$$

**Step 2: Apply the Spatial Kutzbach Equation**

$$
M = 6(n - 1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5
$$

**Step 3: Substitute**

$$
M = 6(7 - 1) - 5(6) - 0 - 0 - 0 - 0
$$

$$
M = 6(6) - 30
$$

$$
M = 36 - 30 = 6
$$

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{M = 6 \, \text{DOF}}
$$

> **Verification by Counting:** Each revolute joint contributes 1 DOF, and there are 6 joints → 6 DOF. ✓ Matches the Kutzbach calculation, confirming that the mechanism is not redundantly constrained (no over-constraint) and not under-constrained (no mobility beyond the joints).

> **Note:** If the gripper itself opens/closes, it would contribute one more DOF, making the total 7. KTU convention typically excludes gripper actuation from the robot's nominal DOF count.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Configuration Analysis

| Parameter | Cartesian | Cylindrical | Spherical | SCARA | Articulated | Delta |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|-----------|-------|-------------|-------|
| Joint Sequence | P-P-P | R-P-P | R-R-P | R-R-P | R-R-R-R-R-R | 3× Parallel |
| DOF | 3 (or 4) | 3 (or 4) | 3 (or 4) | 3–4 | 6 | 3 |
| Workspace Shape | Rectangular | Cylinder | Spherical lobe | Flat dome | Irregular lobe | Hemispherical |
| Reach (max) | ~6 m | ~2 m | ~2 m | ~1 m | ~3 m | ~1.2 m |
| Payload (max) | 5000 kg | 200 kg | 50 kg | 20 kg | 2300 kg | 8 kg |
| Repeatability | Excellent | Good | Fair | Excellent | Excellent (±0.02 mm) | Good |
| Speed | Low | Medium | Medium | High | Medium | Very High |
| Cost | High | Medium | Medium | Medium | High | High |
| Rigidity | Excellent | Excellent | Good | Selective | Good | Excellent |
| Maintenance | Low | Medium | Medium | Low | Medium | Medium |
| **Best Application** | Heavy load transfer | Foundry tending | Welding | PCB assembly | Versatile (general use) | High-speed packaging |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Aid:** The acronym **"CCSSAD"** (Cartesian, Cylindrical, Spherical, SCARA, Articulated, Delta) is the canonical KTU sequence for configuration listing in examinations.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of an Industrial Robot

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[USER INTERFACE LAYER] --> B[Teach Pendant]
    A --> C[Offline Programming PC]
    A --> D[HMI Touchscreen]
    B --> E[ROBOT CONTROLLER CPU]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Kinematics Solver]
    E --> G[Trajectory Planner]
    E --> H[I/O Handler]
    F --> I[SERVO DRIVE LAYER]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Axis 1 Servo Drive]
    I --> K[Axis 2 Servo Drive]
    I --> L[Axis 3 Servo Drive]
    I --> M[Axis 4 Servo Drive]
    I --> N[Axis 5 Servo Drive]
    I --> O[Axis 6 Servo Drive]
    J --> P[MANIPULATOR LAYER]
    K --> P
    L --> P
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Base Joint 1]
    P --> R[Shoulder Joint 2]
    P --> S[Elbow Joint 3]
    P --> T[Wrist Roll 4]
    P --> U[Wrist Pitch 5]
    P --> V[Wrist Yaw 6]
    V --> W[END EFFECTOR]
    W --> X[Gripper]
    W --> Y[Welding Torch]
    W --> Z[Paint Sprayer]
    P --> AA[FEEDBACK SENSOR LAYER]
    AA --> AB[Encoders at Each Joint]
    AB --> E
    AC[External Sensors] --> AD[Vision Camera]
    AC --> AE[Force Torque Sensor]
    AC --> AF[Proximity Sensor]
    AD --> E
    AE --> E
    AF --> E
    AG[POWER SUPPLY UNIT] --> I
    AG --> E
```

> **Diagram Note:** This functional block diagram maps the closed-loop feedback architecture of an industrial robot. The **controller** (center-top) computes desired joint trajectories using inverse kinematics, the **servo drives** translate these into current commands for motors, the **manipulator** physically moves, and **encoders** feed back actual joint positions to close the loop.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Q: Define an industrial robot as per ISO 8373:2021. List any four characteristics of an industrial robot.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

According to **ISO 8373:2021**, an industrial robot is defined as:
> *"An automatically controlled, re-programmable, multipurpose manipulator, programmable in three or more axes, which can be fixed in place or mobile, used in industrial automation applications."*

**Four defining characteristics (½ mark each):**

1. **Automatic operation** — self-acting once programmed, no human intervention required during operation.
2. **Re-programmable** — task sequences can be modified without altering the mechanical structure.
3. **Multipurpose** — adaptable to multiple tasks through software and tooling changes.
4. **Manipulator with ≥ 3 axes** — mechanical arm structure with minimum three programmable joints, providing spatial dexterity.

> *Examiner's Note:* Full 3 marks require the exact ISO definition plus all four characteristics. Partial credit for incomplete lists.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q: Distinguish between Repeatability and Accuracy of an industrial robot. Which one is typically higher in modern industrial robots?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

| Parameter | Repeatability | Accuracy |
|-----------|---------------|----------|
| **Definition** | Ability to return to a previously taught position | Ability to move to a commanded position computed by kinematic model |
| **Depends on** | Encoder resolution, servo control loop, backlash | Kinematic model precision, calibration, mechanical tolerances |
| **Typical Value** | ±0.02 mm to ±0.1 mm (very high) | ±0.5 mm to ±5 mm (lower) |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** In modern industrial robots, **repeatability is significantly higher (5–10× better) than accuracy**. Robots are designed to be *highly repeatable* rather than *highly accurate*, because most industrial tasks require the robot to repeatedly execute a taught trajectory, not to compute positions from scratch.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Major Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Set A]**

**Q: With neat diagrams, explain the anatomy of an industrial robot. List and explain the function of any six major subsystems. Compare Cartesian, Cylindrical, Spherical, SCARA, and Articulated robot configurations on the basis of (i) joint sequence, (ii) workspace shape, (iii) typical applications, and (iv) DOF.**

#### Sub-Part (a) — Robot Anatomy and Subsystems [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

An industrial robot is a **mechatronic system** composed of six major subsystems, each performing a specialized function:

**1. Manipulator (Mechanical Arm) [1 Mark]**
- The physical body comprising rigid links and joints.
- Provides the kinematic chain for positioning the end-effector in 3D space.
- Joint types: Revolute (R — rotational) and Prismatic (P — linear).

**2. End Effector (End-of-Arm Tooling) [1 Mark]**
- The tool attached at the robot's wrist that performs the actual task.
- Examples: grippers, welding torches, paint sprayers, cutting tools, vacuum cups.
- Designed task-specifically and easily interchangeable.

**3. Controller [1 Mark]**
- The "brain" of the robot — typically an industrial PC or microcontroller.
- Executes user programs, computes inverse kinematics, and sends commands to servo drives.
- Handles I/O communication with sensors and external machinery.

**4. Power Supply / Drive System [1 Mark]**
- Provides regulated electrical, hydraulic, or pneumatic energy.
- Servo motors (electric) are most common today; hydraulic used for very high payloads.

**5. Sensors [1 Mark]**
- **Internal sensors** (proprioceptive): encoders, resolvers, tachometers.
- **External sensors** (exteroceptive): vision cameras, force/torque sensors, proximity sensors.

**6. Software [1 Mark]**
- Teach pendant interface for online programming.
- Offline programming (OLP) environments for complex trajectory generation.
- Simulation tools for cell design and collision detection.

**Seventh Bonus Point [1 Mark]:**
- The mechanical **base** providing rigid mounting and structural stability.

---

#### Sub-Part (b) — Configuration Comparison Table [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

| Configuration | Joint Sequence | Workspace Shape | Typical Applications | DOF |
|---------------|----------------|-----------------|----------------------|-----|
| **Cartesian** | P-P-P | Rectangular cuboid | CNC loading, palletizing, 3D printing | 3 |
| **Cylindrical** | R-P-P | Hollow cylinder | Machine tending, die-casting, foundry | 3 |
| **Spherical** | R-R-P | Spherical sector | Welding, painting (legacy) | 3 |
| **SCARA** | R-R-P | Flat circular dome | PCB assembly, pick-and-place, screw insertion | 3–4 |
| **Articulated** | R-R-R-R-R-R (6R) | Irregular spherical lobe | Versatile: welding, painting, assembly, material handling | 6 |

**Key Comparative Insights (1 mark for any 2):**
- The Cartesian robot has the simplest control and highest rigidity but the largest footprint.
- The articulated robot offers maximum dexterity and the largest reachable workspace envelope for a given footprint — making it the **most popular modern industrial configuration**.
- SCARA's selective compliance is uniquely suited to vertical insertion tasks in electronics assembly.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Set B]**

**Q: (a) Explain the classification of industrial robots by coordinate system with neat sketches. Discuss the merits and demerits of each configuration.**
**(b) A spherical robot has a base rotation joint (full 360°), an elevation joint with maximum elevation angle 90° from horizontal, and a prismatic reach joint extending from 0.5 m to 1.5 m. Calculate the workspace volume.**

#### Sub-Part (a) — Classification by Coordinate System [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

Industrial robots are classified into six major configurations based on their kinematic coordinate system. **Each configuration represents a distinct arrangement of revolute (R) and prismatic (P) joints:**

**1. Cartesian (Gantry) Robot — Joint Sequence: P-P-P [1.5 Marks]**

*Sketch description:* Three mutually perpendicular linear slides forming an XYZ frame.
- **Merits:** Highest accuracy, excellent rigidity, easy programming, large payload capacity.
- **Demerits:** Requires large floor space, limited dexterity, expensive linear guides.

**2. Cylindrical Robot — Joint Sequence: R-P-P [1.5 Marks]**

*Sketch description:* A vertical column with a rotating sleeve and an extending arm.
- **Merits:** Simple control, good reach in radial direction, moderate payload.
- **Demerits:** Limited dexterity in vertical direction, mechanical complexity in column.

**3. Spherical (Polar) Robot — Joint Sequence: R-R-P [1 Mark]**

*Sketch description:* Two rotational joints at the shoulder plus a linear telescoping reach.
- **Merits:** Large workspace for given base, good for vertical insertion tasks.
- **Demerits:** Complex inverse kinematics, mechanical design challenges, legacy use only.

**4. SCARA Robot — Joint Sequence: R-R-P (vertical) [1 Mark]**

*Sketch description:* Two parallel-axis revolute arms with a vertical prismatic joint.
- **Merits:** Excellent for vertical pick-and-place, high speed, selective compliance.
- **Demerits:** Limited to mostly planar tasks, lower dexterity.

**5. Articulated (Revolute) Robot — Joint Sequence: R-R-R-R-R-R [2 Marks]**

*Sketch description:* A multi-jointed arm mimicking the human upper limb with 6 revolute joints.
- **Merits:** Maximum dexterity, full 6-DOF positioning and orientation, compact footprint.
- **Demerits:** Complex kinematics, expensive, requires sophisticated control algorithms.

---

#### Sub-Part (b) — Workspace Volume Calculation [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Base rotation: $\phi = 0 \to 2\pi$ (full 360°)
- Elevation joint: $\theta_{2,\max} = 90° = \pi/2$ rad from horizontal
- Prismatic reach: $r_{\min} = 0.5$ m, $r_{\max} = 1.5$ m

**Step 1: Convert elevation angle reference**

If $\theta_{2,\max} = 90°$ from horizontal, then measured from the **positive z-axis (vertical up)**, the elevation spans $0$ to $\pi/2$. However, in the standard spherical workspace formula, $\theta$ is measured from the **z-axis**. If $\theta_{2,\max}$ is the *maximum angle from z-axis*, then directly:

$$
\cos(\theta_{2,\max}) = \cos(\pi/2) = 0
$$

**Step 2: Apply the spherical workspace volume formula (derived in Section 3.1):**

$$
V = \frac{2\pi}{3} \left( r_{\max}^3 - r_{\min}^3 \right) \left( 1 - \cos\theta_{2,\max} \right)
$$

**Step 3: Substitute values**

$$
V = \frac{2\pi}{3} \left( (1.5)^3 - (0.5)^3 \right) \left( 1 - \cos(\pi/2) \right)
$$

**Step 4: Compute cube terms**

$$
(1.5)^3 = 3.375
$$

$$
(0.5)^3 = 0.125
$$

$$
3.375 - 0.125 = 3.25
$$

**Step 5: Compute trigonometric term**

$$
\cos(\pi/2) = 0
$$

$$
1 - 0 = 1
$$

**Step 6: Combine all terms**

$$
V = \frac{2\pi}{3} \times 3.25 \times 1
$$

$$
V = \frac{2\pi \times 3.25}{3}
$$

$$
V = \frac{6.5\pi}{3} \approx \frac{20.420}{3}
$$

### Final Answer

$$
\boxed{V \approx 6.807 \, \text{m}^3}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** If a student interpreted $\theta_{2,\max} = 90°$ from horizontal as meaning the *polar angle from z-axis equals 180° − 90° = 90°*, the answer remains the same. However, if interpreted as *the elevation joint tilts from horizontal by 90° to point straight up*, then the polar angle from z-axis is $0°$ — yielding a *different formula*. Always state your angle convention explicitly.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing repeatability with accuracy** (Loss: 1–2 marks): These are distinct specifications. Examiners often set a sub-question to test this distinction. Always state the formula for both.
>
> 2. **Incorrect joint sequence notation** (Loss: 1 mark): The KTU convention is **R** for revolute and **P** for prismatic, in the order from base to end-effector. Writing the sequence in reverse order is penalized.
>
> 3. **Omitting units in numerical answers** (Loss: 0.5 mark): Workspace volume must specify **m³**, repeatability in **mm or degrees**, payload in **kg**.
>
> 4. **Skipping derivation steps** (Loss: 2–3 marks): KTU values mathematical rigor. Always show integration setup, integration limits, intermediate steps, and final substitution clearly.
>
> 5. **Not stating ISO/IFR definitions verbatim** (Loss: 1 mark): Memorize the ISO 8373:2021 definition word-for-word for 3-mark definition questions.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the spherical wrist criterion** (Loss: 1 mark): When discussing 6-DOF robots, mention that the last 3 axes should ideally intersect at a common point for kinematic decoupling.
>
> 7. **Misclassifying AGVs and CNC machines** (Loss: 1 mark): Only manipulator-based systems with ≥ 3 programmable axes qualify as industrial robots per ISO 8373.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU 2024 Rapid Revision Checklist — Industrial Robots**

### Must-Know Definitions
- ✅ **ISO 8373:2021 Definition** of industrial robot (memorize verbatim)
- ✅ **Five characteristics:** Automatic, Re-programmable, Multipurpose, Manipulator, ≥ 3 axes
- ✅ **Difference between Repeatability and Accuracy**
- ✅ **Degree of Freedom (DOF)** definition and Kutzbach formula

### Must-Know Configurations (The "CCSSAD" Sequence)
- ✅ **Cartesian (PPP)** — rectangular workspace, heavy load
- ✅ **Cylindrical (RPP)** — cylindrical workspace, machine tending
- ✅ **Spherical (RRP)** — spherical workspace, legacy use
- ✅ **SCARA (RRP vertical)** — flat workspace, PCB assembly
- ✅ **Articulated (RRRRRR)** — irregular lobe, most versatile
- ✅ **Delta (Parallel)** — hemispherical, ultra-high-speed pick-and-place

### Must-Know Subsystems
- ✅ Manipulator, End Effector, Controller, Power Supply, Sensors, Software (and Base)

### Must-Know Formulas
- ✅ Spatial DOF of rigid body = **6**
- ✅ Kutzbach: $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$
- ✅ Spherical workspace: $V = \frac{2\pi}{3}(r_{\max}^3 - r_{\min}^3)(1 - \cos\theta_{2,\max})$
- ✅ Cylindrical workspace: $V = \pi L(R_{\max}^2 - R_{\min}^2)$
- ✅ Cartesian workspace: $V = L_x \cdot L_y \cdot L_z$
- ✅ Repeatability: $R = \pm\frac{1}{2}(\overline{X}_{\max} - \overline{X}_{\min})$

### Must-Know Historical Milestones
- ✅ **Unimate 1961** — first industrial robot (George Devol, Joseph Engelberger)
- ✅ **KUKA Famulus 1973** — first 6-axis articulated robot
- ✅ **PUMA 1978** — Victor Scheinman
- ✅ **SCARA 1981** — Hiroshi Makino (Yamanashi University)

### Must-Know Performance Metrics
- ✅ Payload: 0.5–2300 kg
- ✅ Reach: 0.5–6 m
- ✅ Repeatability: ±0.02 mm to ±0.1 mm
- ✅ DOF: typically 6 for articulated, 3–4 for others
- ✅ Speed: articulated ~2 m/s, delta up to 10 m/s

### Must-Know Engineering Insights
- ✅ Repeatability is 5–10× higher than accuracy in industrial robots
- ✅ Spherical wrist design decouples position from orientation (key for Module 2 kinematics)
- ✅ SCARA's selective compliance is unique to vertical insertion tasks
- ✅ Delta robots are the fastest pick-and-place solution (>300 picks/min)

> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to compare configurations, *always use a tabular format* — this signals structured thinking to the examiner and guarantees full marks for the comparison portion. Always conclude with at least one *engineering application insight* to demonstrate deep understanding beyond rote memorization.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Humanoids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT205 - SENSOR TECHNOLOGY FOR ROBOTICS (ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Humanoids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Humanoids

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

A **Humanoid Robot** is an autonomous, anthropomorphic robotic system engineered to emulate the structural morphology, biomechanical kinematics, and cognitive-interactive behavior of the human body. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme framework under the course *Sensor Technology for Robotics (PCRUT205)*, a humanoid is formally classified as a **bipedal, multi-degree-of-freedom (multi-DOF) mechatronic platform** integrating:

- A **dual-arm manipulator subsystem** (typically 7 DOF per arm, mirroring human kinematic redundancy).
- A **bipedal locomotion system** based on the **Zero Moment Point (ZMP)** stability criterion.
- A **sensor-dense perceptual suite** combining proprioceptive and exteroceptive modalities.
- A **centralized or distributed cognitive control architecture** (often AI-augmented) enabling human-like decision-making.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Definition Note:** When asked to "define a humanoid," always emphasize the **anthropomorphic structure**, **bipedal stance**, and **sensor-actuator integration** — these are the three anchor keywords evaluators look for.

The formal ISO/TS 15066-aligned definition (relevant for KTU robotics electives) describes a humanoid as *"a service robot whose physical structure and behavioral repertoire are designed to operate in human-centered environments through human-like form factors."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are building a **biomorphic android** — a mechanical twin of a human being. Just as a human has:

| Human Capability | Humanoid Counterpart |
|------------------|----------------------|
| Eyes & visual cortex | Stereo / RGB-D cameras + vision algorithms |
| Vestibular system (inner ear) | IMU (gyroscope + accelerometer) |
| Skin (touch) | Tactile sensor arrays / e-skin |
| Muscles & tendons | Servo motors, harmonic drives, series elastic actuators |
| Joints (knee, elbow) | Rotary joints with encoders |
| Brain | Onboard CPU/GPU/NPU running AI models |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Example:** Honda's **ASIMO** (introduced 2000) and Boston Dynamics' **Atlas** represent two design philosophies: ASIMO emphasizes *smooth, controlled human-interaction locomotion* (ZMP-based), whereas Atlas uses *model-predictive whole-body control* with hydraulic actuation for dynamic, parkour-like agility.

The key engineering insight: A humanoid is not merely a robot *shaped* like a human — it is a **sensor-rich cyber-physical system** whose value lies in its ability to perceive, reason, and act within environments built for human dimensions (stairs, doorways, tool handles).

---

## 1.3 Historical Evolution of Humanoid Robotics (KTU High-Yield Timeline)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks for **milestones in humanoid evolution**. Memorize the chronological development below.

| Year | Humanoid | Institution | Key Innovation |
|------|----------|-------------|----------------|
| **1972** | WABOT-1 | Waseda University, Japan | First full-scale anthropomorphic robot |
| **1984** | WABOT-2 | Waseda University | First robot to play a keyboard |
| **1996** | Honda P1 | Honda Motor Co. | First Honda humanoid prototype |
| **2000** | ASIMO | Honda | Autonomous bipedal walking with ZMP control |
| **2006** | Nao | Aldebaran (SoftBank) | Commercially mass-produced small humanoid |
| **2013** | Atlas (v1) | Boston Dynamics | Hydraulic, dynamic balancing |
| **2014** | Pepper | SoftBank | Emotion-aware social humanoid |
| **2016** | Sophia | Hanson Robotics | Citizen of Saudi Arabia (social AI) |
| **2022** | Optimus | Tesla | Mass-manufacturable bipedal AI worker |
| **2023** | Digit | Agility Robotics | Bipedal logistics robot for warehouses |

---

## 1.4 Why Humanoids? The Engineering Rationale

Humanoid form factors are **not** chosen arbitrarily. The driving engineering motivations are:

1. **Environmental Compatibility** — Human-built infrastructure (stairs 17 cm high, corridors 90 cm wide, handrails, knobs) is anthropometrically scaled. A humanoid can navigate it natively.
2. **Tool Reuse** — Human tools (screwdrivers, keyboards, trays) are designed for human grippers and reach envelopes.
3. **Social Acceptance (Uncanny Valley Consideration)** — Humanoid form fosters trust in healthcare, education, and eldercare.
4. **Research Vehicle for Embodied AI** — Humanoids serve as the ultimate benchmark for **whole-body control (WBC)**, **sensor fusion**, and **locomotion intelligence**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Vocabulary Builder:** Master the following KTU-recognized terminology:
> - **Anthropomorphism** — human-like appearance.
> - **Bipedalism** — two-legged locomotion.
> - **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** — independent controllable axes.
> - **Embodied AI** — AI physically grounded in a body.
> - **Whole-Body Control (WBC)** — unified control of all joints.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Subsystem Architecture of a Humanoid Robot

A humanoid robot is decomposed into **five tightly-coupled subsystems**. Understanding this layered architecture is essential for KTU Module 1 questions.

### 2.1.1 Mechanical / Skeletal Subsystem
- **Frame:** Lightweight alloys (aluminum 7075, titanium), carbon-fiber composites, or 3D-printed polymers (PA12, ABS).
- **Joints:** Rotary (revolute) and linear (prismatic) joints.
- **Actuators:**
  * **Servo motors** with **harmonic drives** (high torque, zero backlash) — used in ASIMO.
  * **Series Elastic Actuators (SEA)** — spring-coupled for compliant control.
  * **Hydraulic actuators** — used in Atlas for high power density.

### 2.1.2 Sensor Subsystem (KTU High-Yield Focus)
This is the **central theme** of *Sensor Technology for Robotics (PCRUT205)*. Sensors are categorized as follows:

#### A. Proprioceptive Sensors (Internal State)
| Sensor | Physical Quantity Measured | Typical Use in Humanoid |
|--------|---------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Rotary Encoder** | Joint angle (rad) | Position feedback at each DOF |
| **Potentiometer** | Angular displacement | Backup position sensor |
| **Tachometer** | Angular velocity (rad/s) | Velocity feedback |
| **IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit)** | Linear accel. + angular vel. | Trunk orientation, balance |
| **Gyroscope (MEMS/FOG)** | Angular rate (°/s or rad/s) | Postural stability |
| **Accelerometer (MEMS)** | Specific force (m/s²) | Inertial reference, fall detection |
| **Force/Torque Sensor (6-axis F/T)** | Forces (N) and moments (N·m) | Foot-ground contact, ZMP computation |
| **Current Sensor** | Motor current (A) | Indirect torque estimation |
| **Temperature Sensor (RTD, Thermistor)** | Temperature (°C or K) | Actuator thermal protection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Examiner Frequently Asked:** *"Distinguish between proprioceptive and exteroceptive sensors in humanoid robots."* Always begin with definitions, then provide 4+ examples of each, then conclude with their functional roles in the control loop.

#### B. Exteroceptive Sensors (External Environment)
| Sensor | Modality | Application |
|--------|----------|-------------|
| **Stereo / RGB-D Camera** | Visible / IR spectrum | Object recognition, SLAM, gesture |
| **LiDAR (2D / 3D)** | Time-of-Flight (laser) | 360° mapping, obstacle avoidance |
| **Ultrasonic Sensor** | Acoustic (~40 kHz) | Short-range proximity detection |
| **Microphone Array** | Acoustic (20 Hz–20 kHz) | Speech recognition, sound localization |
| **Tactile Sensor Array (e-skin)** | Pressure / vibration | Grasping, social touch |
| **Infrared / PIR Sensor** | Thermal radiation | Human presence detection |
| **GPS Module** | RF satellite signals | Outdoor localization |

### 2.1.3 Control Subsystem
- **Architecture:** Hierarchical — *high-level (task planner)* → *mid-level (gait generator)* → *low-level (joint servo loop)*.
- **Algorithms:** PID control, Model Predictive Control (MPC), ZMP-based preview control, reinforcement learning.

### 2.1.4 Power Subsystem
- **Lithium-ion battery packs** (typically 48 V–96 V DC bus).
- **Power density target:** ≥ 200 Wh/kg for full-day operation.

### 2.1.5 Communication Subsystem
- **CAN bus, EtherCAT, ROS 2 (DDS middleware)** for real-time inter-subsystem messaging.

---

## 2.2 The Zero Moment Point (ZMP) — KTU Critical Concept

The **Zero Moment Point (ZMP)** is defined as the point on the ground (support polygon) where the **net moment of all active forces (gravity + inertial) equals zero** along the horizontal axes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note:** Introduced by **Miomir Vukobratović (1972)** at the Mihajlo Pupin Institute, Serbia. ZMP is the cornerstone of stable bipedal walking theory.

### Mathematical Formulation
Let there be $n$ point masses $m_i$ in the humanoid body, with global position vectors $\mathbf{r}_i = (x_i, y_i, z_i)$. The ZMP coordinates $(x_{zmp}, y_{zmp}, 0)$ are given by:

$$x_{zmp} = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \left(\ddot{z}_i + g\right) x_i - \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \ddot{x}_i z_i}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \left(\ddot{z}_i + g\right)}$$

$$y_{zmp} = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \left(\ddot{z}_i + g\right) y_i - \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \ddot{y}_i z_i}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \left(\ddot{z}_i + g\right)}$$

where $g \approx 9.81 \; m/s^2$ is the gravitational acceleration.

### The Stability Criterion
$$\boxed{\text{ZMP} \in \text{Support Polygon (footprint)} \iff \text{Stable}}$$

If the ZMP exits the support polygon, the robot **tips over**.

---

## 2.3 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) Distribution

A typical full-scale humanoid (e.g., ASIMO) has approximately **34 DOF**:

| Body Part | DOF Contribution |
|-----------|------------------|
| Head | 3 (pan, tilt, roll) |
| Each Arm (×2) | 7 (shoulder 3 + elbow 1 + wrist 3) → **14** |
| Hands (×2) | 2 (gripper) → 4 (sometimes excluded) |
| Waist | 2 (yaw, pitch) |
| Each Leg (×2) | 6 (hip 3 + knee 1 + ankle 2) → **12** |
| **TOTAL** | **≈ 30–57** (varies by design) |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Print/memorize this table — these formulas appear in 3-mark, 7-mark, and 14-mark KTU questions on Humanoids.

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|-------------------|-------|-------|
| 1 | ZMP $x$-coordinate | $x_{zmp} = \frac{\sum m_i (\ddot{z}_i + g) x_i - \sum m_i \ddot{x}_i z_i}{\sum m_i (\ddot{z}_i + g)}$ | m | Sum over all body links |
| 2 | ZMP $y$-coordinate | $y_{zmp} = \frac{\sum m_i (\ddot{z}_i + g) y_i - \sum m_i \ddot{y}_i z_i}{\sum m_i (\ddot{z}_i + g)}$ | m | Symmetric to $x$ |
| 3 | Static CoP (when $\ddot{r}_i = 0$) | $x_{cop} = \frac{\sum m_i g x_i}{\sum m_i g} = \frac{\sum m_i x_i}{M}$ | m | Reduces to centroid of projection |
| 4 | Support polygon area | $A_{sp} = \frac{1}{2} \vert \sum_{k=1}^{N} (x_k y_{k+1} - x_{k+1} y_k) \vert$ | m² | Shoelace formula |
| 5 | Center of Mass (CoM) | $\mathbf{r}_{com} = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \mathbf{r}_i$ | m | Mass-weighted average |
| 6 | Walking stability margin | $d_{margin} = \min_k \Vert \mathbf{p}_{zmp} - \mathbf{p}_{edge,k} \Vert$ | m | Distance to nearest polygon edge |
| 7 | Step length constraint | $L_{step} \le 2 \sqrt{R^2 - h_{com}^2}$ | m | $R$ = leg length, $h_{com}$ = CoM height |
| 8 | Biped DOF (full) | $DOF_{total} = DOF_{legs} + DOF_{arms} + DOF_{waist} + DOF_{head}$ | — | Additive decomposition |
| 9 | IMU fusion (complementary) | $\theta_{fused} = \alpha (\theta_{gyro} + \omega \, dt) + (1-\alpha) \theta_{accel}$ | rad | Low-pass on accel, high-pass on gyro |
| 10 | F/T sensor decoupling | $\mathbf{F}_{ext} = \mathbf{K}^{-1} (\mathbf{V}_{measured} - \mathbf{V}_{bias})$ | N, N·m | $\mathbf{K}$ = calibration matrix |
| 11 | Stride frequency | $f_{stride} = \frac{1}{T_{stride}}$ | Hz | Inverse of stride period |
| 12 | Static stability (double support) | $x_{zmp} \in [x_{heel,front}, x_{toe,back}]$ | m | Bounds during DS phase |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ZMP theory** is the foundation of every commercially walking humanoid (ASIMO, HRP-2 to HRP-5, Nao, Pepper, Optimus, Digit, Cassie).
- **Force/Torque sensors** at the ankle enable real-time ZMP feedback for dynamic balance recovery.
- **IMU-based postural control** allows humanoids to reject external disturbances (pushes, uneven terrain).
- **Sensor fusion** (Kalman / Complementary filter) is the only way to achieve drift-free orientation estimates for stable walking.
- **Tactile e-skin** (e.g., Tekscan, SynTouch) enables safe human-robot physical interaction by detecting contact pressure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Insight:** Tesla Optimus uses a **centralized neural-network policy** trained via simulation-to-real (sim2real) reinforcement learning, replacing classical ZMP controllers with learned policies. This represents a paradigm shift KTU examiners may reference as "modern trend."

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Zero Moment Point (ZMP) Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The ZMP derivation is a **guaranteed 7-mark sub-question** in KTU Module 1. Below is the complete board-quality derivation.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — System Setup and Assumptions
Consider a humanoid robot modeled as a **multi-rigid-body system** with $n$ point masses $m_i$ located at global Cartesian positions $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$. The total mass is:

$$M = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i$$

Assume:
- The foot is in **full planar contact** with a flat, rigid, horizontal ground at $z = 0$.
- All contact forces are reduced to a **single equivalent force-couple system** at the ZMP.

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Define the Generalized Forces and Moments
The active forces on each link include:
- **Gravity:** $\mathbf{F}_{g,i} = (0, 0, -m_i g)$.
- **Inertial (D'Alembert) force:** $\mathbf{F}_{I,i} = (-m_i \ddot{x}_i, -m_i \ddot{y}_i, -m_i \ddot{z}_i)$ (this is the negative of $m \mathbf{a}$, treated as a pseudo-force for static equivalence).

The net external moment about the ZMP point $\mathbf{p} = (x_{zmp}, y_{zmp}, 0)$ along the ground plane ($x$ and $y$ axes) must vanish by definition of ZMP.

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Sum of Moments About ZMP (About $y$-axis)
The moment of force about the $y$-axis passing through the ZMP is:

$$\tau_y = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ (z_i - 0) \cdot F_{x,i} - (x_i - x_{zmp}) \cdot F_{z,i} \right]$$

Substituting the force components (gravity + inertial along $x$ and $z$ directions):

$$\tau_y = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ z_i \cdot (-m_i \ddot{x}_i) - (x_i - x_{zmp}) \cdot (-m_i \ddot{z}_i - m_i g) \right]$$

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Apply the ZMP Condition
The ZMP definition states that **$\tau_y = 0$** (no net moment about $y$-axis through ZMP). So:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ -m_i \ddot{x}_i z_i + (x_i - x_{zmp})(m_i \ddot{z}_i + m_i g) \right] = 0$$

Expanding:

$$-\sum m_i \ddot{x}_i z_i + \sum m_i (\ddot{z}_i + g) x_i - x_{zmp} \sum m_i (\ddot{z}_i + g) = 0$$

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Solve for $x_{zmp}$
Rearranging to isolate $x_{zmp}$:

$$x_{zmp} \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i (\ddot{z}_i + g) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i (\ddot{z}_i + g) x_i - \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \ddot{x}_i z_i$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,x_{zmp} = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i (\ddot{z}_i + g) \, x_i \;-\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \ddot{x}_i \, z_i}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i (\ddot{z}_i + g)}\,}$$

### 3.1.6 Step 6 — Derive $y_{zmp}$ by Symmetry
By the **principle of symmetry** between $x$ and $y$ axes, replacing $x \to y$ yields:

$$\boxed{\,y_{zmp} = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i (\ddot{z}_i + g) \, y_i \;-\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \ddot{y}_i \, z_i}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i (\ddot{z}_i + g)}\,}$$

### 3.1.7 Step 7 — Static Case Reduction
When the robot is **stationary** (all $\ddot{x}_i = \ddot{y}_i = \ddot{z}_i = 0$), the inertial terms vanish:

$$x_{zmp} = \frac{\sum m_i g \, x_i}{\sum m_i g} = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i x_i$$

This is precisely the **Center of Mass (CoM) projection** on the ground — i.e., the **Center of Pressure (CoP)**. Hence, ZMP $\equiv$ CoP under static conditions. This is a frequent KTU short-answer observation.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — 7-Mark KTU-Style Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU Pattern):** A simplified humanoid is modeled as **three point masses** on a flat surface: $m_1 = 20$ kg at $(0.1, 0, 0.9)$ m, $m_2 = 15$ kg at $(-0.1, 0, 0.9)$ m (both arms), and $m_3 = 50$ kg at $(0, 0, 1.0)$ m (torso). The torso accelerates upward at $\ddot{z}_3 = 0.5$ m/s² while shifting forward at $\ddot{x}_3 = 0.2$ m/s². The arms are momentarily static. Compute the ZMP $x$-coordinate. Use $g = 9.81$ m/s².

### Solution

**Step A — Identify the variables:**

| Mass | $m_i$ (kg) | $x_i$ (m) | $z_i$ (m) | $\ddot{x}_i$ (m/s²) | $\ddot{z}_i$ (m/s²) |
|------|-----------|-----------|-----------|---------------------|---------------------|
| $m_1$ (right arm) | 20 | 0.1 | 0.9 | 0 | 0 |
| $m_2$ (left arm) | 15 | −0.1 | 0.9 | 0 | 0 |
| $m_3$ (torso) | 50 | 0 | 1.0 | 0.2 | 0.5 |

**Step B — Compute the numerator $N_x$:**

$$N_x = \sum m_i (\ddot{z}_i + g) x_i - \sum m_i \ddot{x}_i z_i$$

Term-by-term:

For $m_1$: $m_1 (\ddot{z}_1 + g) x_1 = 20 \cdot (0 + 9.81) \cdot 0.1 = 19.62$ N·m (numerator part)
For $m_1$: $m_1 \ddot{x}_1 z_1 = 20 \cdot 0 \cdot 0.9 = 0$

For $m_2$: $m_2 (\ddot{z}_2 + g) x_2 = 15 \cdot (0 + 9.81) \cdot (-0.1) = -14.715$ N·m
For $m_2$: $m_2 \ddot{x}_2 z_2 = 15 \cdot 0 \cdot 0.9 = 0$

For $m_3$: $m_3 (\ddot{z}_3 + g) x_3 = 50 \cdot (0.5 + 9.81) \cdot 0 = 0$
For $m_3$: $m_3 \ddot{x}_3 z_3 = 50 \cdot 0.2 \cdot 1.0 = 10$ N·m (subtracted)

Therefore:

$$N_x = (19.62 - 14.715 + 0) - (0 + 0 + 10) = 4.905 - 10 = -5.095 \;\text{N·m}$$

**Step C — Compute the denominator $D$:**

$$D = \sum m_i (\ddot{z}_i + g) = 20(9.81) + 15(9.81) + 50(10.31)$$

$$D = 196.2 + 147.15 + 515.5 = 858.85 \;\text{N}$$

**Step D — Compute $x_{zmp}$:**

$$x_{zmp} = \frac{N_x}{D} = \frac{-5.095}{858.85} = -0.00593 \;\text{m} \approx -5.93 \;\text{mm}$$

**Step E — Engineering Interpretation:**
The ZMP lies **5.93 mm behind the robot's sagittal reference**, well within a typical foot length of 25 cm. Therefore, the robot is **statically stable** during this motion.

|--------|--------|-----------|-------------|--------|----------|
| MEMS Gyroscope | $\omega$ (°/s) | 100–500 Hz | 0.01 °/s / $\sqrt{Hz}$ | Yes | Short-term rotation |
| MEMS Accelerometer | $a$ (m/s²) | 50–200 Hz | 0.003 m/s² / $\sqrt{Hz}$ | No (bias only) | Tilt, vibration |
| 6-axis F/T Sensor | $F, M$ | 1 kHz | 0.1 N, 0.005 N·m | Minimal | ZMP computation |
| Optical Encoder | $\theta$ (counts) | MHz | < 0.01° resolution | No | Joint position |

| Tactile (capacitive) | $P$ (kPa) | 1 kHz | 0.5 kPa | No | Grasping, social touch |

## 3.4 Gait Cycle Analysis (KTU Sub-Question)

The bipedal walking gait consists of **two alternating phases**:

1. **Double Support (DS) Phase:** Both feet on ground; ZMP must lie within the **overlap polygon** of both feet.
2. **Single Support (SS) Phase:** One foot swinging; ZMP must lie within the **stance foot polygon**.

The temporal duty factor is:

$$\beta = \frac{T_{DS}}{T_{stride}}$$

For human-like walking, $\beta \approx 0.2$ (i.e., 20% DS, 80% SS). For static walking, $\beta \to 0.5$.

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define a humanoid robot. List any four major categories of sensors used in humanoid robots.**

**Model Answer:**
A humanoid robot is an **autonomous anthropomorphic robotic system** designed to mimic the **structure, locomotion, and behavior of the human body**, typically featuring bipedal stance, dual-arm manipulation, and a sensor-rich perception suite.

Four major sensor categories:
1. **Proprioceptive sensors** — encoders, IMU, gyroscopes, accelerometers.
2. **Force/Torque sensors** — 6-axis F/T sensors at wrists and ankles.
3. **Vision sensors** — stereo cameras, RGB-D cameras.
4. **Tactile sensors** — capacitive or resistive e-skin arrays.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**What is the Zero Moment Point (ZMP)? State its significance in bipedal humanoid locomotion.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Zero Moment Point (ZMP)**, introduced by Miomir Vukobratović in 1972, is defined as the point on the ground (support polygon) where the **net moment of all active forces (gravity and inertial) equals zero** along the horizontal axes.

**Significance:**
- It is the **primary stability criterion** for bipedal walking.
- If the ZMP lies within the **support polygon (footprint)**, the humanoid is **dynamically stable**; otherwise, it will topple.
- It is computed in real time using **6-axis F/T sensors** at the ankles, enabling the **preview controller** (Kajita et al., 2003) to generate stable gait patterns.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE pattern: Each Part B question has an **internal choice (OR option)**. Below, both alternatives (Question A and Question B) are provided with full model solutions.

---

### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks**

#### (a) Explain the anatomy of a humanoid robot with a detailed block diagram. List the various degrees of freedom (DOF) involved. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

A humanoid robot's anatomy is divided into **five primary subsystems**:

1. **Mechanical / Skeletal Subsystem:** Comprises rigid links (typically aluminum 7075 or carbon-fiber composite) interconnected by rotary and prismatic joints. Each joint is actuated by a servo motor coupled via harmonic drives for high torque and zero backlash.

2. **Sensor Subsystem:** Comprises proprioceptive sensors (encoders, IMU, gyroscopes, accelerometers, F/T sensors) and exteroceptive sensors (cameras, LiDAR, microphones, tactile arrays).

3. **Control Subsystem:** A hierarchical processor network — high-level (task planner), mid-level (gait generator), low-level (joint servo loops). Uses ZMP-based preview control.

4. **Power Subsystem:** 48–96 V DC lithium-ion battery pack with BMS.

5. **Communication Subsystem:** Real-time bus (CAN, EtherCAT) and middleware (ROS 2 / DDS).

**Degrees of Freedom Distribution (ASIMO-class humanoid):**

| Body Part | DOF |
|-----------|-----|
| Head (pan, tilt, roll) | 3 |
| Each Arm (shoulder 3 + elbow 1 + wrist 3) × 2 | 14 |
| Waist (yaw, pitch) | 2 |
| Each Leg (hip 3 + knee 1 + ankle 2) × 2 | 12 |
| Hands (grippers) × 2 | 2 |
| **Total** | **33** |

Some advanced humanoids (e.g., HRP-4C) have up to **42 DOF** for finer whole-body coordination.

#### (b) Discuss the various types of sensors used in humanoid robots, categorizing them into **proprioceptive** and **exteroceptive** sensors. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Proprioceptive Sensors (Internal State Measurement):**

- **Rotary Encoders:** Measure joint angle with sub-degree resolution; mounted on each motor shaft.
- **IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit):** Combines a 3-axis gyroscope, 3-axis accelerometer, and 3-axis magnetometer; measures trunk orientation, angular velocity, and linear acceleration.
- **6-Axis Force/Torque Sensor:** Mounted at the ankle and wrist; measures ground reaction forces (used for ZMP computation) and interaction forces during manipulation.
- **Tachometers and Current Sensors:** Provide velocity and torque feedback at each joint.
- **Temperature Sensors:** Monitor actuator winding temperature.

**Exteroceptive Sensors (External Environment Perception):**

- **Stereo / RGB-D Cameras:** Provide 3D vision for object recognition, SLAM, and gesture recognition.
- **LiDAR (2D/3D):** Generates 360° environment maps using time-of-flight laser ranging.
- **Microphone Arrays:** Enable speech recognition and sound-source localization.
- **Tactile Sensor Arrays (E-skin):** Distributed pressure/vibration sensors covering limbs for grasping and safe human interaction.
- **Ultrasonic and Infrared Proximity Sensors:** Detect nearby obstacles in blind zones of the camera/LiDAR.

**Functional Integration:** These sensors are fused using **Kalman filters** or **particle filters** to produce a unified state estimate (CoM, ZMP, posture, environment map) used by the control system.

---

### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks**

#### (a) With a neat diagram, explain the **Zero Moment Point (ZMP)** concept. Derive the equations for the ZMP coordinates. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

*(Refer to Mermaid diagram 4.3 in SECTION 4 for the control-loop block diagram.)*

**Definition:** The ZMP is the point on the ground (footprint) where the net moment of all active forces (gravity + inertial) equals zero.

**Derivation Outline:**

Model the humanoid as $n$ point masses $m_i$ at positions $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$. Sum of moments about the $y$-axis through the ZMP must vanish:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ z_i (-m_i \ddot{x}_i) - (x_i - x_{zmp})(-m_i \ddot{z}_i - m_i g) \right] = 0$$

Solving for $x_{zmp}$:

$$x_{zmp} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i (\ddot{z}_i + g) x_i - \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \ddot{x}_i z_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i (\ddot{z}_i + g)}$$

By symmetry:

$$y_{zmp} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i (\ddot{z}_i + g) y_i - \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \ddot{y}_i z_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i (\ddot{z}_i + g)}$$

**Stability Condition:** ZMP must lie inside the support polygon defined by the foot contact area.

#### (b) Describe the architecture and applications of **modern humanoid robots**. Discuss the challenges in achieving **stable bipedal locomotion**. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Modern Humanoid Architectures:**

- **Honda ASIMO:** Hydraulic-electric hybrid, ZMP-based control, 34 DOF.
- **Boston Dynamics Atlas:** Fully hydraulic, model-predictive control, dynamic parkour-grade agility.
- **Tesla Optimus:** Electric actuators, end-to-end neural network policy, vision-based environment understanding.
- **Agility Robotics Digit:** Compact biped for warehouse logistics, designed for repeated stooping and stepping.
- **SoftBank Pepper / Nao:** Smaller-scale social/educational humanoids with cloud AI integration.

**Common Architecture Layers:**
1. **Perception Layer** (cameras, LiDAR, microphones).
2. **Cognition Layer** (LLMs, vision transformers, task planners).
3. **Control Layer** (WBC, MPC, gait generators, ZMP preview).
4. **Actuation Layer** (servo motors, harmonic drives, SEAs).
5. **Communication Layer** (CAN, EtherCAT, ROS 2).

**Applications:**
- Healthcare and eldercare assistance.
- Industrial co-working and assembly.
- Disaster response (search and rescue).
- Education, research, and social companionship.
- Space exploration (NASA Valkyrie).

**Challenges in Stable Bipedal Locomotion:**
1. **Real-time ZMP computation** requires high-bandwidth 6-axis F/T sensors and fast numerical integration.
2. **Disturbance rejection** against pushes, slips, and uneven terrain demands robust IMU-based postural control.
3. **Actuator bandwidth limitations** restrict dynamic response (electric vs. hydraulic trade-off).
4. **Energy efficiency** — bipedal walking consumes significantly more energy than wheeled locomotion.
5. **Sim-to-real transfer** for learned policies is still an open research problem.
6. **Multi-contact planning** on uneven or narrow surfaces requires complex whole-body optimization.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the units** in numerical ZMP problems — always write the final answer in **meters (m)** with explicit unit notation.
> 2. **Confusing ZMP and CoM** — these are *not* the same point during dynamic motion. ZMP lies on the ground; CoM lies above. Under static conditions, they coincide.
> 3. **Omitting the support polygon condition** — the stability criterion is **ZMP ∈ Support Polygon**, not just "ZMP < 0" or "ZMP exists."
> 4. **Skipping the inertial terms** in ZMP derivations — many students write only the static form, which forfeits 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Neglecting to mention sensor types explicitly** when asked about humanoid components — examiners look for **proprioceptive vs. exteroceptive categorization**.
> 6. **Writing vague definitions** for "humanoid" without mentioning **anthropomorphic form**, **bipedalism**, and **multi-DOF** — these are the three KTU-required keywords.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Revision Cheat-Sheet — Humanoids (Module 1, PCRUT205)**

- **Humanoid** = anthropomorphic + bipedal + multi-DOF mechatronic system.
- **Three core subsystems** to always mention: Mechanical, Sensor, Control.
- **ZMP** (Vukobratović, 1972) is the **stability criterion**; ZMP ∈ Support Polygon ⇒ stable.
- **ZMP Equations** involve $g \approx 9.81$ m/s², summations over all body link masses and accelerations.
- **Static limit:** ZMP = CoM projection on the ground.
- **Dynamic case:** ZMP ≠ CoM; depends on $\ddot{x}_i, \ddot{z}_i$ of each link.
- **Sensor suite = Proprioceptive (encoders, IMU, F/T) + Exteroceptive (cameras, LiDAR, microphones, tactile).**
- **Ankle-mounted 6-axis F/T sensors** are the *primary* ZMP measurement hardware.
- **IMU** is the *primary* postural stability sensor (gyroscope + accelerometer + magnetometer).
- **ASIMO has ~34 DOF;** advanced humanoids (HRP-4C) reach 42+.
- **Gait cycle:** Double Support (DS) ↔ Single Support (SS) alternating.
- **Walking frequency** inversely related to stride period.
- **Whole-Body Control (WBC)** integrates manipulation and locomotion.
- **Sensor fusion** (Kalman / Complementary) is essential to combine noisy multi-sensor streams into a coherent state estimate.
- **Modern trends:** Sim-to-real RL, vision-based policies (Optimus), hydraulic dynamic control (Atlas).
- **Historical milestones:** WABOT-1 (1972) → ASIMO (2000) → Atlas (2013) → Optimus (2022).
- **KTU keyword trinity:** *Anthropomorphism* + *Bipedalism* + *Sensor Integration* — use these in every definition.
- **Stability margin** = minimum distance from ZMP to support-polygon edge.
- **Always state gravitational constant** $g = 9.81$ m/s² in derivations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cobots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT205 - SENSOR TECHNOLOGY FOR ROBOTICS (ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cobots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🤖 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **Cobot (Collaborative Robot)** is a robotic manipulator specifically engineered to operate within a **shared workspace** with human operators, executing tasks in a **cooperative, safe, and sensor-mediated** manner. Unlike traditional industrial robots that operate inside fenced safety cells, cobots employ **force-limiting actuators, compliant joints, and a multi-modal sensor stack** (force/torque, vision, proximity, tactile) to satisfy the rigorous safety thresholds defined under **ISO/TS 15066** and **ISO 10218-1/2**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In the PCRUT205 curriculum, a *Cobot* is studied primarily as a **sensor-rich mechatronic platform** — meaning the *intelligence* and *safety* of a cobot emerge from its **sensor fusion architecture**, not merely from its mechanical compliance.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Think of a **traditional industrial robot** as a **heavy power-press machine** in a factory — it can crush sheet metal, but you must keep humans inside a fenced zone because the machine is "blind" to your presence.

A **Cobot**, in contrast, is like a **trained martial arts sparring partner**:
- It can deliver a powerful strike (high payload capability),
- BUT it constantly senses your proximity and force-feedback,
- And it **instantly reduces its stiffness, speed, or stops** if contact is detected.

So when a cobot "touches" a human, it feels like a gentle nudge rather than a fatal crush — the **biomechanical limits** of the human body (e.g., **maximum permissible pressure of 140 N/cm² on the hand** as per ISO/TS 15066) act as the governing design constraint.

## 1.3 Foundational Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standardized Metrics (per ISO/TS 15066:2016):**
> - **Maximum quasi-static contact pressure (hand/finger):** **140 N/cm²**
> - **Maximum transient contact force (hand):** **140 N**
> - **Maximum robot speed in collaborative mode:** **250 mm/s**
> - **Minimum protective separation distance:** **S(t) computed via ISO 13855**
> - **Biomechanical pain threshold (skin contact):** ≈ **5 N** for the face, **10 N** for the chest

## 1.4 Cobot vs. Traditional Industrial Robot — A Snapshot

| Parameter | Traditional Industrial Robot | Cobot (Collaborative Robot) |
|---|---|---|
| Workspace | **Fenced / Isolated** | **Shared with humans** |
| Payload | Up to **>1000 kg** | Typically **3–35 kg** |
| Operating Speed | **>2 m/s** | **≤ 250 mm/s** (in collaborative mode) |
| Safety Mechanism | **Physical barriers + E-stops** | **Sensor fusion + force limiting** |
| Programming | Teach pendant, RAPID code | **Hand-guiding, drag-and-teach** |
| Sensors | Encoders, basic I/O | **F/T, vision, proximity, tactile, IMU** |
| Cost | High (cells + fencing) | **Lower (no fencing)** |
| Compliance Standard | **ISO 10218 only** | **ISO 10218 + ISO/TS 15066** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Collaborative Operation Modes (ISO/TS 15066)

ISO/TS 15066 formally classifies human–robot collaboration (HRC) into **four canonical modes**. KTU examiners routinely frame Part B questions around these four pillars:

### Mode 1 — Safety-Rated Monitored Stop (SRMS)
- The robot **stops completely** when a human enters the collaborative workspace.
- The robot's drive system is actively held in a **safety-rated stop** (Category 1 or 2 stop per IEC 60204-1).
- **Sensor Role:** *Presence detection* via **safety laser scanners, light curtains, or 3D vision**.
- **Use Case:** Operator loading a part while the robot is stationary, then robot resumes when the operator exits.

### Mode 2 — Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM)
- The robot **continuously adjusts its speed** as a function of the **human's separation distance**.
- The robot operates at **maximum productivity** when the human is far, and **decelerates** as the human approaches.
- **Sensor Role:** *Proximity + tracking* via **2D/3D cameras, time-of-flight (ToF) sensors, capacitive proximity sensors**.
- **Core Equation (covered in §3.1):**
$$v_{robot}(t) = f\big(S_{protective}(t)\big)$$
where $S_{protective}$ is the minimum protective separation distance computed from the **human approach speed**, **robot stopping time**, and **sensor uncertainty**.

### Mode 3 — Power and Force Limiting (PFL)
- The robot is **intrinsically designed** (via compliant joints, force sensors, or current-monitoring in servo drives) to limit any **quasi-static or transient contact force** below the **biomechanical thresholds** of the human body region being contacted.
- **Sensor Role:** *Force/Torque (F/T) sensors* at the wrist or joints, **tactile skin sensors** on the robot body, **joint torque observers** in the controller.
- **Core Equation (covered in §3.2):**
$$F_{contact} \leq F_{max,\,biomechanical} \quad \text{(per body region)}$$

### Mode 4 — Hand Guiding (Manual Guidance)
- The operator **physically grasps the robot end-effector** (often via a **handle equipped with F/T sensors** or **capacitive touch sensors**) and "teaches" the cobot a path or task by demonstration.
- **Sensor Role:** *Force feedback* at the handle, **encoders** to record trajectory.
- **Use Case:** Path teaching for complex welding or polishing trajectories.

## 2.2 Sensor Stack Architecture of a Cobot

A KTU exam answer for cobots must always emphasize the **multi-modal sensor stack** that enables safety:

| Sensor Type | Physical Principle | Function in Cobot | Key Specification |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Force/Torque (F/T) Sensor** | Strain gauges on a Wheatstone bridge | Detect contact force, enable hand-guiding | Resolution ≈ **0.1 N**, Range ≈ **±500 N** |
| **Joint Torque Sensor** | Strain gauges on each joint housing | Compliant control, collision detection | Range **±Nm per joint size** |
| **Capacitive Proximity Sensor** | Capacitance change (C = εA/d) | Detect human approach on non-contact | Range **5–50 mm** |
| **Time-of-Flight (ToF) Camera** | IR pulse reflection (c·t/2) | 3D depth mapping for SSM | Range **0.1–5 m**, FOV 60°–90° |
| **RGB-D Camera** | Stereo vision + structured light | Human pose estimation, gesture recognition | Depth accuracy **±1–5 mm @ 1 m** |
| **Tactile Skin Sensor** | Piezoresistive / capacitive / quantum tunneling composite | Whole-body contact detection | Spatial density **1–4 taxels/cm²** |
| **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** | MEMS accelerometer + gyroscope | Detect human handover dynamics | Drift **< 1°/hr** |
| **Safety Laser Scanner (LiDAR)** | Time-of-flight 2D scanning (LiDAR) | Perimeter monitoring, SSM | Range **0.05–9 m**, PLd/SIL2 rated |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following equations appear most frequently in KTU PCRUT205 End Semester Examinations (ESE). Memorize and practice these.**

| # | Concept | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Capacitive Sensor Output** | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m (vacuum permittivity), $\varepsilon_r$ = relative permittivity, $A$ = electrode area, $d$ = gap | Farads (F) |
| 2 | **Time-of-Flight Distance** | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s (speed of light), $t$ = round-trip time | meters (m) |
| 3 | **Protective Separation Distance (SSM)** | $S_p(t_0) = v_h \cdot (T_r + T_s) + S_h + C + Z_d$ | $v_h$ = human approach speed (typically **1.6 m/s**), $T_r$ = robot reaction time, $T_s$ = robot stopping time, $S_h$ = human intrusion displacement, $C$ = intrusion constant, $Z_d$ = position uncertainty | meters (m) |
| 4 | **Strain Gauge F/T Relation** | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon$ | $G$ = gauge factor (≈ **2.0** for constantan), $\varepsilon$ = strain | dimensionless |
| 5 | **Force from Joint Torque** | $F = \dfrac{\tau}{r}$ | $\tau$ = measured joint torque, $r$ = moment arm | N |
| 6 | **Compliant Joint Stiffness** | $k = \dfrac{F}{\Delta x}$ | $F$ = applied force, $\Delta x$ = deflection | N/m |
| 7 | **Biomechanical Force Limit (PFL)** | $F_{lim} \leq F_{max,\,region}$ | $F_{max}$ values per ISO/TS 15066: hand=140N, abdomen=110N, chest=140N | N |
| 8 | **MEMS Capacitance Change** | $\Delta C = C_0 \cdot \dfrac{\Delta d}{d_0}$ | For parallel-plate displacement $\Delta d$ | F |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Cobots are **disrupting manufacturing economics** by:
1. **Eliminating the cost of safety fencing** (a single perimeter guard can cost **$50,000–$100,000**).
2. **Reducing robot deployment time from weeks to hours** via hand-guiding programming.
3. **Enabling high-mix, low-volume production** — the same cobot cell can be re-purposed for **10+ different SKUs** in a single shift.
4. **Improving ergonomics**: cobots handle the **stoop-lift-repeat** cycle, reducing **Work-Related Musculoskeletal Disorders (WMSDs)** which account for **≈ 30% of all workplace injuries**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Market Insight (for viva/project context):** The global cobot market is projected to surpass **$11 billion by 2030**, with major vendors being **Universal Robots (UR), FANUC CRX series, ABB GoFa, KUKA LBR iiwa, and Techman TM**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Protective Separation Distance (Mode 2 — SSM)

The **Protective Separation Distance**, $S_p$, is the **minimum spatial gap** that must be maintained between any moving part of the robot and the human operator to ensure that **the robot can come to a safe stop BEFORE contact occurs**. It is derived from the relative kinematics of the human approach and the robot's braking dynamics.

### Step 1 — Define the Contributing Distances

The total protective separation distance is the sum of four contributing components:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_p = & \,\, S_h \quad \text{(human's intrusion during robot's reaction + braking)}\\
& + S_r \quad \text{(the contribution due to the robot's braking distance)}\\
& + C \quad \text{(intrusion constant accounting for body part geometry)}\\
& + Z_d \quad \text{(position uncertainty of the sensing system)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Express the Human's Intrusion Distance

The human, approaching at velocity $v_h$, travels during the sum of:
- The **robot's reaction time** $T_r$ (from sensor detection to issuance of stop command), and
- The **robot's stopping time** $T_s$ (from stop command to physical halt).

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_h &= v_h \cdot (T_r + T_s)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Note:** KTU uses a **standard human approach speed of $v_h = 1.6 \text{ m/s}$** per ISO 13855.

### Step 3 — Express the Robot's Braking Distance

The robot, moving at velocity $v_r$, decelerates at a rate $a_r$ (assumed constant for simplicity):

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_r &= \frac{v_r^2}{2 \cdot a_r}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* This is the standard kinematic equation $v^2 = u^2 + 2as$ with final velocity $= 0$ and initial velocity $= v_r$.

### Step 4 — Assemble the Master Equation

Combining all four terms into the **canonical KTU / ISO 13855 form**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\,S_p(t_0) = v_h \cdot (T_r + T_s) + \frac{v_r^2}{2 a_r} + C + Z_d\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical 14-Mark Sub-Question)

**Problem:** A cobot is operating in **Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM)** mode. A human operator approaches at $v_h = 1.6$ m/s. The cobot's **reaction time** is $T_r = 80$ ms, **stopping time** is $T_s = 120$ ms, and **deceleration** is $a_r = 2.5 \text{ m/s}^2$. The cobot is currently moving at $v_r = 0.2$ m/s. The intrusion constant $C = 0.85$ m (forearm) and the **vision sensor position uncertainty** is $Z_d = 0.05$ m. Compute the **minimum protective separation distance**.

**Solution:**

**Step A — Compute the human intrusion term:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_h &= v_h \cdot (T_r + T_s) \\
&= 1.6 \cdot (0.080 + 0.120) \\
&= 1.6 \cdot 0.200 \\
&= 0.320 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step B — Compute the robot braking term:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_r &= \frac{v_r^2}{2 \cdot a_r} \\
&= \frac{(0.2)^2}{2 \cdot 2.5} \\
&= \frac{0.04}{5.0} \\
&= 0.008 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step C — Sum all four components:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
