S_p &= 0.320 + 0.008 + 0.85 + 0.05 \\
S_p &= 1.228 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The human must remain **at least 1.228 m away** from any moving part of the cobot at all times. If the sensor detects the human at a distance less than this value, the cobot must initiate an **immediate safety-rated stop**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Force Calculation in a Joint-Torque Sensor (PFL Mode)

In **Power and Force Limiting (PFL)** mode, the cobot detects contact by monitoring the **torque deviation** at each joint. The relationship is derived from the principle of **static equilibrium**.

### Step 1 — Free Body Diagram of a Cobot Link

A single cobot link of length $L$ experiences:
- A **payload force** $F_p$ at the end-effector,
- **Gravitational force** $mg$ at the center of mass,
- A **joint reaction torque** $\tau_j$ at the proximal joint,
- An **external contact force** $F_c$ at distance $r_c$ along the link.

### Step 2 — Sum of Torques about the Joint

Taking moments about the joint axis (static equilibrium, $\sum \tau = 0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_j &= (F_p + mg) \cdot L \cdot \cos(\theta) + F_c \cdot r_c \cdot \cos(\phi)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\theta$ is the link angle w.r.t. horizontal, and $\phi$ is the contact angle.

### Step 3 — Isolate the Contact Force

In practice, the **expected torque** $\tau_{expected}$ (computed from the known payload and gravity model) is **subtracted from the measured torque** $\tau_{measured}$ to obtain the contact-induced deviation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta \tau &= \tau_{measured} - \tau_{expected} \\
\Delta \tau &= F_c \cdot r_c \cdot \cos(\phi)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for the Contact Force

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\,F_c = \frac{\Delta \tau}{r_c \cdot \cos(\phi)}\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** If $F_c$ exceeds the **biomechanical limit** (e.g., **140 N for the hand** per ISO/TS 15066), the cobot controller **triggers an immediate protective stop** and reduces the motor current to release the contact.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Capacitive Proximity Sensor for Human Detection

A capacitive sensor on the cobot's outer skin detects human approach via the change in capacitance caused by the human body (a conductor with relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r \approx 50$ for muscle tissue, ≈ 1000 for sweat-laden skin).

### Step 1 — Parallel-Plate Capacitor Equation

For a sensor with electrode area $A$ and initial gap $d_0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_0 &= \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Capacitance Change Due to Approach

When a human (treated as a grounded conductor) approaches at distance $d < d_0$, the effective gap reduces, increasing capacitance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta C &= C_{new} - C_0 = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \left(\frac{1}{d} - \frac{1}{d_0}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Normalized Form (Useful for KTU Derivations)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\,\frac{\Delta C}{C_0} = \frac{d_0}{d} - 1 = \frac{d_0 - d}{d}\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** This shows that the **fractional change in capacitance** is **inversely proportional to the gap distance** — meaning the sensor exhibits **high sensitivity at close range** and **diminishing sensitivity at long range** (a key design tradeoff).

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numeric Check:** If $d_0 = 10$ mm and the human is at $d = 2$ mm, then $\Delta C / C_0 = (10/2) - 1 = 4$, i.e., a **400% increase** in capacitance — easily detectable by a standard capacitance-to-digital converter (CDC).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Sensor Trade-offs in Cobot Design

| Design Goal | Preferred Sensor | Trade-off | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Detect contact < 50 ms** | Joint torque sensor | Mechanical compliance reduces rigidity | Series Elastic Actuator (SEA) design |
| **Long-range human tracking (>2 m)** | 2D/3D vision | Latency ≈ 30–100 ms; sensitive to lighting | **Sensor fusion** with LiDAR |
| **Tactile whole-body coverage** | Piezoresistive skin | Low spatial resolution, fragile | Modular replaceable taxel arrays |
| **Compliance with ISO 13849 PLd** | Safety laser scanner | 2D plane only | **Vertical stacking of scanners** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Cobot Sensor Fusion Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[External Environment] --> B[Force Torque Sensor at Wrist]
    A --> C[Time of Flight Camera]
    A --> D[Capacitive Proximity Skin]
    A --> E[Safety Laser Scanner LiDAR]
    A --> F[RGB-D Vision for Pose Estimation]

    B --> G[Sensor Fusion Processor FPGA based]
    C --> G
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G

    G --> H{Decision Engine}
    H --> I[Mode 1 SRMS Stop Command]
    H --> J[Mode 2 SSM Speed Adjustment]
    H --> K[Mode 3 PFL Compliance Control]
    H --> L[Mode 4 Hand Guiding]

    I --> M[Cobot Drive Motors]
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M

    M --> N[Physical Robot Motion Output]
    G --> O[Operator HMI and Status Display]
    G --> P[Safety PLC for Hardwired Cutoff]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: The Four Collaborative Modes — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Cobot System Startup] --> B{Human Detected in Workspace?}
    B -->|No| C[Operate at Full Productivity Mode]
    B -->|Yes| D{Which Mode is Configured?}

    D -->|SRMS| E[Issue Safety Rated Stop]
    D -->|SSM| F[Compute Separation Distance Sp]
    F --> G{Sp above Threshold?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Operate at Reduced Speed]
    G -->|No| E

    D -->|PFL| I[Monitor Joint Torques Continuously]
    I --> J{Contact Force above Limit?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Reduce Motor Current within 50 ms]
    J -->|No| L[Continue Task at Limited Power]

    D -->|Hand Guide| M[Activate Force Handle Sensors]
    M --> N[Record Trajectory at Operator Demonstrated Path]
    N --> O[Playback at Configured Velocity]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Cobot Sensor Stack — Layered Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Layer 1 Physical Sensors] --> L2[Layer 2 Signal Conditioning]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3 Analog to Digital Conversion]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4 Digital Filtering and Calibration]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5 Sensor Fusion Kalman Filter]
    L5 --> L6[Layer 6 Safety Logic ISO 10218 and ISO TS 15066]
    L6 --> L7[Layer 7 Motion Controller Servo Drives]
    L7 --> L8[Layer 8 Mechanical Joints with Compliance]

    L1a[Force Torque] --> L1
    L1b[Vision 2D and 3D] --> L1
    L1c[Proximity Capacitive and Inductive] --> L1
    L1d[Tactile Skin] --> L1
    L1e[IMU and Encoders] --> L1
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: F/T Sensor Signal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Mechanical Strain on Cobot Wrist] --> B[Wheatstone Bridge of Strain Gauges]
    B --> C[Differential Amplifier INA128]
    C --> D[Low Pass Filter fc equal to 1 kHz]
    D --> E[24 bit ADC ADS1256]
    E --> F[Microcontroller DSP TMS320F28379D]
    F --> G[Cross Talk Compensation Matrix]
    G --> H[Ethernet CAT to Cobot Controller]
    H --> I[Robot Motion Controller]
    I --> J{Force Exceeds 140 N Limit?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Emergency Stop Trigger]
    J -->|No| L[Continue Normal Operation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide for Mermaid:** Each box represents a **physical or logical block**, and arrows represent **signal flow or data flow direction**. Mermaid renders these in a top-to-bottom (TB) or left-to-right (LR) directed graph. Copy any block into the [Mermaid Live Editor](https://mermaid.live/) for interactive visualization.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 🎯 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

### **Q1. Define a Cobot and list any four distinguishing features from a traditional industrial robot.** `[3 marks]`

**Model Answer:**
A **Cobot (Collaborative Robot)** is a robotic manipulator designed to **physically interact and share a workspace** with a human operator in a **safe, sensor-mediated** manner, in compliance with **ISO 10218** and **ISO/TS 15066**.

**Four distinguishing features:**

1. **Shared workspace** — operates alongside humans without safety fencing.
2. **Sensor-rich safety architecture** — employs force/torque, vision, and proximity sensors for real-time human detection.
3. **Power and force limiting (PFL)** — mechanically and electronically limits contact forces to **biomechanically safe levels** (e.g., ≤ 140 N on the hand).
4. **Intuitive programming** — supports hand-guiding and teach-by-demonstration rather than complex offline programming.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's tip:** A common mistake is to confuse "cobot" with "service robot." A **cobot** is *industrial* in origin (factory floor), while a *service robot* performs non-industrial tasks (cleaning, healthcare). Keep this distinction crisp in your answer.

---

### **Q2. What is the role of a Force/Torque (F/T) sensor in Power and Force Limiting (PFL) mode?** `[3 marks]`

**Model Answer:**
In **Power and Force Limiting (PFL) mode**, the F/T sensor acts as the **primary contact-detection transducer** for the cobot:

1. **Contact Detection:** It measures **deviations in the joint torque** ($\Delta\tau$) from the expected torque computed by the dynamic model. Any deviation signals **unintended contact** with a human or obstacle.

2. **Force Quantification:** The contact force is computed as $F_c = \dfrac{\Delta\tau}{r_c \cdot \cos(\phi)}$, where $r_c$ is the distance from the joint to the contact point.

3. **Safety Action:** If $F_c$ exceeds the **biomechanical limit** of the contacted body region (e.g., **140 N for the hand** per ISO/TS 15066), the controller **triggers an immediate protective stop** by reducing motor current, thereby releasing the contact pressure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Viva tip:** A common follow-up is *"Why is the F/T sensor placed at the wrist and not at the base?"* — Answer: A wrist-mounted F/T sensor is **closer to the contact point**, providing higher signal-to-noise ratio and **faster response** since the mechanical filtering effect of the link is minimized.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module Internal Choice Pattern]**

### **QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**

**Q.A(a)** Explain the **four collaborative operation modes** defined in **ISO/TS 15066**, with a clear diagram of the **sensor stack** used in each mode. **7 marks**

**Q.A(b)** A cobot is in **Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM)** mode. The human operator approaches at $v_h = 1.6$ m/s. The cobot's reaction time $T_r = 100$ ms, stopping time $T_s = 150$ ms, deceleration $a_r = 2.0$ m/s², current speed $v_r = 0.25$ m/s, intrusion constant $C = 0.85$ m, and sensor uncertainty $Z_d = 0.05$ m. Compute the **minimum protective separation distance** and interpret the result. **7 marks**

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Q.A(a)

The four collaborative operation modes defined in **ISO/TS 15066:2016** are:

**1. Safety-Rated Monitored Stop (SRMS):**
The robot operates at full speed when no human is detected. Upon human entry, a **safety-rated stop** is issued (per IEC 60204-1, Stop Category 0 or 1). The robot remains halted until the human exits the workspace.
- *Sensors used:* Safety laser scanner, light curtain, 3D vision system.

**2. Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM):**
The robot **continuously modulates its speed** as a function of the human's separation distance. The closer the human, the slower the robot — following the protective separation distance formula derived in §3.1.
- *Sensors used:* 2D/3D ToF camera, vision-based skeletal tracking, LiDAR.

**3. Power and Force Limiting (PFL):**
The robot is **mechanically and electronically constrained** to ensure that any inadvertent contact remains **below the biomechanical limits** of the human body region. This is achieved via:
- **Compliant joints** (Series Elastic Actuators),
- **Force/Torque sensors** at the wrist and joints,
- **Joint torque observers** in the controller.
- *Sensors used:* F/T sensor, tactile skin, joint torque sensors.

**4. Hand Guiding (Manual Guidance):**
The operator **physically grasps a force-sensing handle** on the end-effector and demonstrates a trajectory, which the cobot records and replays. The cobot's servo system maintains a **virtual zero-impedance** at the handle.
- *Sensors used:* F/T sensor on the handle, encoders, optional 6-DoF IMU on the handle.

**Sensor Stack Diagram (in text form):**

| Layer | SRMS | SSM | PFL | Hand Guiding |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Proximity** | Laser scanner | ToF camera | Tactile skin | — |
| **Force/Torque** | — | — | Wrist F/T + joint torque | Handle F/T |
| **Vision** | 2D safety camera | RGB-D pose tracker | — | (Optional) |
| **Position** | Safety encoder | Vision-based 3D | Encoder | Encoder |

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Q.A(b)

**Given Data:**
- $v_h = 1.6$ m/s (human approach speed)
- $T_r = 100$ ms $= 0.100$ s (robot reaction time)
- $T_s = 150$ ms $= 0.150$ s (robot stopping time)
- $a_r = 2.0$ m/s² (robot deceleration)
- $v_r = 0.25$ m/s (current cobot speed)
- $C = 0.85$ m (intrusion constant for forearm)
- $Z_d = 0.05$ m (sensor position uncertainty)

**Formula (per ISO 13855 / §3.1):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_p &= v_h \cdot (T_r + T_s) + \frac{v_r^2}{2 a_r} + C + Z_d
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Human Intrusion Term:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_h &= v_h \cdot (T_r + T_s) \\
&= 1.6 \cdot (0.100 + 0.150) \\
&= 1.6 \cdot 0.250 \\
&= 0.400 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Robot Braking Term:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_r &= \frac{v_r^2}{2 \cdot a_r} \\
&= \frac{(0.25)^2}{2 \cdot 2.0} \\
&= \frac{0.0625}{4.0} \\
&= 0.015625 \text{ m} \approx 0.016 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum All Components:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_p &= 0.400 + 0.016 + 0.85 + 0.05 \\
&= 1.316 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:**
The human operator must maintain a **minimum distance of 1.316 m** from any moving part of the cobot. If the **2D/3D vision sensor** detects a human at a separation less than this value, the cobot controller must **immediately issue a stop command** to avoid contact. This distance acts as a **dynamic safety bubble** that **shrinks when the cobot moves slowly** and **grows when the cobot moves fast or has a long stopping time** — a key design principle of **SSM mode**.

---

### **QUESTION B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks**

**Q.B(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **complete sensor signal chain** of a **Force/Torque sensor** used in a cobot wrist, from the mechanical strain input to the controller-level force readout. **7 marks**

**Q.B(b)** Discuss the **four primary sensors** (force/torque, vision, capacitive proximity, safety laser scanner) used in cobots. For each, state the **physical principle**, **one key specification**, and **one limitation**. **7 marks**

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Q.B(a)

A **Force/Torque (F/T) sensor** in a cobot wrist converts mechanical strain into a calibrated 6-DoF force/torque reading. The complete signal chain is:

**Stage 1 — Mechanical Strain Generation:**
When a force or torque is applied to the end-effector, the **deformation element** (a precisely machined metal flexure with machined cutouts) deforms elastically. This strain is on the order of **microstrains** ($\mu\varepsilon$).

**Stage 2 — Strain Gauge Transduction:**
**Semiconductor or foil strain gauges** are bonded to the flexure in a **Wheatstone bridge** configuration. The fractional change in resistance follows:
$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon$$
where $G \approx 2.0$ for metallic gauges and $G \approx 100$ for semiconductor gauges.

**Stage 3 — Signal Conditioning:**
The differential millivolt signal from the bridge is amplified by an **instrumentation amplifier** (e.g., INA128, gain ≈ 100–500). A **low-pass filter** ($f_c \approx 1$ kHz) removes high-frequency noise.

**Stage 4 — Analog-to-Digital Conversion:**
A **24-bit delta-sigma ADC** (e.g., ADS1256) digitizes the signal at sampling rates up to **30 kSPS**, achieving force resolutions of **≈ 0.05 N**.

**Stage 5 — Decoupling Matrix:**
The cross-coupling between the 6 axes (Fx, Fy, Fz, Tx, Ty, Tz) is removed by multiplying the raw ADC readings with a **factory-calibrated 6×6 decoupling matrix** $D$:
$$\begin{bmatrix} F_x \\ F_y \\ F_z \\ T_x \\ T_y \\ T_z \end{bmatrix} = D \cdot \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \\ V_3 \\ V_4 \\ V_5 \\ V_6 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Stage 6 — Digital Communication:**
The decoded 6-DoF force/torque vector is transmitted to the cobot controller via **EtherCAT, CAN, or RS-485** at update rates of **1 kHz or higher**.

**Stage 7 — Safety Logic:**
The controller compares the measured force with the **biomechanical limit** (e.g., 140 N for the hand). Exceeding this limit triggers a **safety-rated stop** within **< 50 ms**.

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Q.B(b)

| # | Sensor | Physical Principle | Key Specification | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Force/Torque Sensor** | Piezoresistive strain gauges on a flexure | Resolution ≈ **0.1 N**, Range ≈ **±500 N** | Sensitive to **thermal drift**; requires periodic recalibration |
| 2 | **Vision (RGB-D) Camera** | Stereo triangulation + IR structured light | Depth accuracy **±2 mm @ 1 m** | **Sensitive to ambient lighting**; occlusions; privacy concerns |
| 3 | **Capacitive Proximity Sensor** | Change in capacitance ($C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$) | Sensing range **5–50 mm** | **Short range**; affected by humidity; non-metallic objects have lower $\varepsilon_r$ |
| 4 | **Safety Laser Scanner (LiDAR)** | Time-of-flight of laser pulse ($d = c t / 2$) | Range up to **9 m**, PLd / SIL2 certified | **2D scan plane only**; cannot detect objects above/below the plane |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing SRMS with Emergency Stop (E-Stop):** SRMS is a *collaborative* stop triggered by sensor input; an E-Stop is a *hardware-button-driven* hard cutoff. KTU answers often erroneously equate the two — **avoid this**.
> 2. **Forgetting the constant $C$:** In the SSM distance formula, students frequently omit the **intrusion constant $C$** (the body part's geometric reach) and the **sensor uncertainty $Z_d$**, leading to a non-compliant answer.
> 3. **Using the wrong human approach speed:** Always use $v_h = 1.6$ m/s per ISO 13855 unless the problem explicitly states otherwise.
> 4. **Skipping the unit conversions:** $T_r$ and $T_s$ are usually given in **milliseconds (ms)** in KTU problems — **always convert to seconds** before substituting.
> 5. **Confusing *cobot* with *AGV* (Automated Guided Vehicle):** A cobot has **arms and manipulators**; an AGV has **wheels and moves payloads**. They are entirely different robotic classes.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Cobot = Collaborative Robot** = safe human–robot coexistence, ISO/TS 15066.
- [x] **Four Modes:** SRMS, SSM, PFL, Hand Guiding — *memorize the difference*.
- [x] **SRMS** uses *presence detection*; **SSM** uses *distance-based speed control*; **PFL** uses *force/torque limiting*; **Hand Guiding** uses *force handle trajectory recording*.
- [x] **Maximum hand contact force limit = 140 N** (ISO/TS 15066).
- [x] **Maximum cobot speed in collaborative mode = 250 mm/s**.
- [x] **Master Formula:** $S_p = v_h (T_r + T_s) + \dfrac{v_r^2}{2 a_r} + C + Z_d$
- [x] **Standard human approach speed = 1.6 m/s**.
- [x] **F/T sensor uses Wheatstone bridge of strain gauges** with $G \approx 2$ (foil) or $G \approx 100$ (semiconductor).
- [x] **Capacitive sensor:** $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ — sensitivity $\propto 1/d$.
- [x] **ToF distance:** $d = c t / 2$ with $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.
- [x] **Sensor fusion** is what makes a cobot *intelligent and safe* — no single sensor is sufficient.
- [x] **Standards to memorize:** ISO 10218-1/-2, ISO/TS 15066, ISO 13855, ISO 13849-1 (PLd), IEC 60204-1.
- [x] **Pioneering institutions:** Northwestern University (J. Edward Colgate & Michael Peshkin, 1996 — *first cobot patent*).
- [x] **Major commercial cobots:** Universal Robots (UR3/UR5/UR10/UR16e), FANUC CRX, ABB GoFa, KUKA LBR iiwa, Techman TM.
- [x] **Key vendors of F/T sensors:** ATI Industrial Automation (Nano17, Gamma), OnRobot, Robotiq.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Autonomous Robots and Swarm Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT205 - SENSOR TECHNOLOGY FOR ROBOTICS (ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Autonomous Robots and Swarm Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Autonomous Robots — Formal Definition

An **Autonomous Robot** is a mechatronic or robotic system that is capable of performing desired tasks in unstructured, unpredictable environments **without continuous human intervention or explicit guidance**, by relying on its own perception, cognition, decision-making, and actuation subsystems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Spirit):**
> An autonomous robot senses its environment, interprets the sensed data using onboard intelligence, makes decisions, and executes actions **independently** to accomplish a goal — all in real time and without external control.

---

### 1.2 Levels of Robot Autonomy (KTU High-Yield Classification)

| Level | Autonomy Class | Human Role | Example System |
|:-----:|:--------------|:-----------|:---------------|
| 0 | Manual / Teleoperated | Full control | Bomb disposal joystick |
| 1 | Assisted | Human operates, robot assists | Power steering in cars |
| 2 | Partial Automation | Conditional human override | Adaptive cruise control |
| 3 | Conditional Automation | Robot decides, human fallback | Highway autopilot |
| 4 | High Automation | Robot decides within defined scope | Self-driving taxis (geofenced) |
| 5 | Full Autonomy | None | ASIMO, Mars rovers, Drones |

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 Scheme, when examiners ask "Define an autonomous robot," they expect you to **explicitly mention**: *sensing → perception → planning → action* cycle (Sense-Plan-Act loop), and the **absence of human-in-the-loop** during operation.

---

### 1.3 Swarm Robots — Formal Definition

A **Swarm Robotic System** is a multi-robot system composed of a large number of **simple, homogeneous (or heterogeneous), physically embodied robots** that coordinate their behaviour **decentralizedly** using **local sensing and communication**, to achieve a **collective goal** that no individual robot could achieve alone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Doctrine (Inspired by Natural Swarms):** No central controller. No global knowledge. Each robot is "dumb" but the swarm is "intelligent" — this is **emergent behaviour**.

---

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🧠 Real-World Analogy — Autonomous Robot:**
> Think of a **self-driving car** (e.g., a Waymo taxi). It has a *body* (chassis), *eyes* (LiDAR, cameras, RADAR), a *brain* (GPU + AI chip running perception algorithms), and *muscles* (steering, throttle, brake actuators). It does not need a human to drive it. It sees the road, decides when to brake, turns the wheel, and reaches the destination **entirely on its own** — that is autonomy.

> [!NOTE]
> **🐜 Real-World Analogy — Swarm Robot:**
> Imagine a colony of **10,000 ants carrying a leaf back to the nest.** No single ant knows the full route. Each ant follows 3 simple rules: (1) follow the pheromone trail, (2) lay a pheromone while carrying, (3) avoid obstacles. Yet collectively, they form a perfectly efficient transport line. **Swarm robotics mimics this**: hundreds of tiny robots, each running 3–4 simple rules, cooperating to perform search-and-rescue, terrain mapping, or military reconnaissance.

---

### 1.5 Physical & Engineering Constants (Memorize for KTU)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Use |
|:-------|:------:|:-------------:|:----|
| Reynolds Number (micro-swarm regime) | $Re$ | $< 2000$ | Determines flow regime in micro-swarm fluids |
| Communication Range (typical swarm) | $R_c$ | **1–10 m** | Robot-to-robot radio/IR range |
| Mean Inter-Robot Distance | $d$ | **0.5 m – 2 m** | Determines swarm density |
| Decision Cycle Time | $T_c$ | **10–100 ms** | Onboard CPU loop time |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architecture of an Autonomous Robot

The canonical architecture of an autonomous robot is built on the **Sense → Plan → Act (SPA) paradigm**, often extended to a closed loop with feedback.

#### 2.1.1 Functional Sub-Systems

- **Perception Layer (Sensors):** LiDAR, IMU, GPS, encoders, cameras, force/torque sensors. Converts physical phenomena into digital signals.
- **Cognition Layer (Controller / AI):** Microcontroller, embedded SBC (e.g., Raspberry Pi, Jetson Nano), running SLAM, path planning, behaviour trees, or neural networks.
- **Actuation Layer (Drivers & Motors):** DC motors, servo motors, BLDC motors, pneumatic/hydraulic cylinders; controlled via PWM, H-bridges, motor drivers (e.g., L298N).
- **Power Layer:** Li-Po, Li-ion, or NiMH battery packs with BMS (Battery Management System).
- **Communication Layer:** Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, Zigbee, CAN bus, ROS topics.

#### 2.1.2 The Three-Layer Control Architecture (KTU Favourite Diagram)

Many KTU questions expect this **3-layer decomposition**:

| Layer | Function | Time Scale | Example |
|:------|:---------|:-----------|:--------|
| **Reactive (Lowest)** | Direct sensor-to-actuator mapping; obstacle avoidance | **< 10 ms** | Subsumption Architecture (Brooks) |
| **Executive (Middle)** | Task sequencing, finite state machines | **100 ms – 1 s** | FSM / Behaviour Trees |
| **Deliberative (Top)** | Path planning, world modelling, reasoning | **> 1 s** | A*, RRT, Dijkstra |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three layers?**
> Because **deliberative planning** is too slow for fast obstacle avoidance, and a purely reactive robot cannot plan a long route. The middle layer *glues* them by choosing which behaviour is active at any instant.

---

### 2.2 Mathematical Model of an Autonomous Mobile Robot

The **kinematic model** of a differential-drive autonomous robot (the most common KTU 2024 exam setup) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} &= v \cos(\theta) \\
\dot{y} &= v \sin(\theta) \\
\dot{\theta} &= \omega
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $x, y$ = position of the robot in the world frame (in metres)
- $\theta$ = heading angle (in radians)
- $v$ = linear velocity (m/s)
- $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s)

For a **differential-drive chassis** with wheel radius $r$ and wheelbase $L$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{r}{2} (\omega_R + \omega_L) \\
\omega &= \frac{r}{L} (\omega_R - \omega_L)
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $\omega_R$ = angular velocity of right wheel (rad/s)
- $\omega_L$ = angular velocity of left wheel (rad/s)
- $r$ = wheel radius (m)
- $L$ = distance between wheel contact points (m)

---

### 2.3 Swarm Robotics — Mathematical Foundations

#### 2.3.1 Reynolds' Boids Rules (1986) — The Foundation of Swarm Behaviour

Craig Reynolds proposed three rules that every "boid" (bird-oid) follows. **Every KTU examiner loves this.**

| Rule | Name | Mathematical Form | Meaning |
|:----:|:-----|:-----------------|:--------|
| 1 | **Separation** | $\vec{F}_s = \sum_{j \neq i} \frac{\vec{p}_i - \vec{p}_j}{\vert \vec{p}_i - \vec{p}_j \vert^2}$ within radius $r_s$ | Avoid crowding neighbours |
| 2 | **Alignment** | $\vec{F}_a = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \vec{v}_j$ within radius $r_a$ | Steer towards average heading |
| 3 | **Cohesion** | $\vec{F}_c = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \vec{p}_j - \vec{p}_i$ within radius $r_c$ | Move towards the centre of mass of neighbours |

The resultant velocity update of robot $i$ is:

$$
\vec{v}_i(t+\Delta t) = \vec{v}_i(t) + w_s \vec{F}_s + w_a \vec{F}_a + w_c \vec{F}_c
$$

Where $w_s, w_a, w_c$ are tunable weight coefficients, and $\Delta t$ is the control loop timestep.

#### 2.3.2 Swarm Density Metric

$$
\rho_s = \frac{N}{\pi R_c^2}
$$

Where:
- $N$ = number of robots in the swarm
- $R_c$ = communication/sensing range (m)
- $\rho_s$ = swarm density (robots per m²)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A swarm is considered **operationally stable** when $0.1 \leq \rho_s \leq 2.0$ robots/m². Below 0.1 = disconnected; above 2.0 = collision-prone.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Variables | Units | KTU Frequency |
|:-:|:------------------|:----------|:-----:|:-------------:|
| 1 | $\dot{x} = v \cos\theta$ | Forward kinematics | m/s, m, rad | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | $\dot{y} = v \sin\theta$ | Forward kinematics | m/s, m, rad | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | $\dot{\theta} = \omega$ | Yaw rate | rad/s | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | $v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$ | Diff-drive linear vel | m/s | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | $\omega = \frac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$ | Diff-drive angular vel | rad/s | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 6 | Reynolds Separation force $\vec{F}_s$ | Neighbour repulsion | N (normalized) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 7 | Reynolds Alignment force $\vec{F}_a$ | Neighbour velocity avg | m/s | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 8 | Reynolds Cohesion force $\vec{F}_c$ | Centroid attraction | m | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 9 | $\rho_s = N / (\pi R_c^2)$ | Swarm density | robots/m² | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 10 | $T_c \leq 100\,\text{ms}$ | Real-time loop bound | ms | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 11 | Reynolds Number $Re = \rho v L / \mu$ | Micro-swarm fluid flow | dimensionless | ⭐⭐ |
| 12 | Subsumption priority arbitration | Brooks' layers | — | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where this matters in industry:**
> - **Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs)** in Amazon warehouses (Kiva/Proteus) use SPA architecture for shelf transport.
> - **Mars Rovers** (Perseverance, Curiosity) are Level-5 autonomous — NASA uses SLAM + hazard detection onboard.
> - **Swarm drones** (e.g., DARPA OFFSET program) use Reynolds' Boids + task allocation for military ISR (Intelligence, Surveillance, Reconnaissance).
> - **Agricultural swarms** (e.g., XAG drones) use cohesion + alignment rules for synchronized spraying.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Differential-Drive Kinematic Model

**Problem Setup:** A two-wheeled differential-drive autonomous robot has wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m and wheel separation $L = 0.30$ m. Both wheels are rolling without slipping. Derive the linear and angular velocity expressions.

#### Step 1 — Define Contact Point Velocities

Each wheel touches the ground and rolls. By the no-slip condition, the linear velocity of each wheel's contact point with the ground equals the wheel's angular velocity times its radius.

For the **right wheel**:

$$
v_R = r \cdot \omega_R
$$

For the **left wheel**:

$$
v_L = r \cdot \omega_L
$$

These velocities are tangential to the wheel at the contact point, directed along the robot's local $x$-axis.

#### Step 2 — Define Robot Body Linear Velocity

In a differential-drive configuration, the **centre of the robot** (point C, midway between the wheels) has a linear velocity that is the **average** of the two wheel velocities (since the body is rigid and translates without rotation about its vertical axis from the wheels' contributions).

$$
v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2}
$$

Substituting the contact point velocities from Step 1:

$$
v = \frac{r \cdot \omega_R + r \cdot \omega_L}{2}
$$

Factor out $r$:

$$
v = \frac{r}{2} \left( \omega_R + \omega_L \right)
$$

This is the standard KTU formula for the linear velocity of a differential-drive robot.

#### Step 3 — Define Robot Body Angular Velocity

When the two wheels spin at different rates, the robot rotates about a point called the **Instantaneous Center of Curvature (ICC)**, located on the line joining the two wheels.

The distance from ICC to the right wheel is $R + L/2$, and to the left wheel is $R - L/2$, where $R$ is the radius of the circular path of the robot's centre.

Setting tangential velocities equal to $\omega \cdot \text{radius}$:

$$
r \cdot \omega_R = \omega \left( R + \frac{L}{2} \right)
$$

$$
r \cdot \omega_L = \omega \left( R - \frac{L}{2} \right)
$$

**Step 3a — Subtract the two equations** to eliminate $R$:

$$
r \cdot \omega_R - r \cdot \omega_L = \omega \left( R + \frac{L}{2} \right) - \omega \left( R - \frac{L}{2} \right)
$$

Simplify the right-hand side:

$$
r (\omega_R - \omega_L) = \omega \cdot L
$$

**Step 3b — Solve for $\omega$:**

$$
\omega = \frac{r}{L} (\omega_R - \omega_L)
$$

This is the KTU formula for angular velocity of a differential-drive robot.

#### Step 4 — Assemble the Full State-Space Model

Combining with the world-frame equations from §2.2, the complete forward kinematic model of a differential-drive autonomous robot is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} &= \frac{r}{2} (\omega_R + \omega_L) \cos(\theta) \\
\dot{y} &= \frac{r}{2} (\omega_R + \omega_L) \sin(\theta) \\
\dot{\theta} &= \frac{r}{L} (\omega_R - \omega_L)
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 5 — Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Typical Problem)

**Given:** $r = 0.05$ m, $L = 0.30$ m, $\omega_R = 10$ rad/s, $\omega_L = 8$ rad/s, $\theta = \pi/4$ rad.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{0.05}{2} (10 + 8) = 0.025 \times 18 = 0.45 \;\text{m/s} \\
\omega &= \frac{0.05}{0.30} (10 - 8) = 0.1667 \times 2 = 0.3333 \;\text{rad/s} \\
\dot{x} &= 0.45 \times \cos(45^\circ) = 0.45 \times 0.7071 = 0.3182 \;\text{m/s} \\
\dot{y} &= 0.45 \times \sin(45^\circ) = 0.45 \times 0.7071 = 0.3182 \;\text{m/s} \\
\dot{\theta} &= 0.3333 \;\text{rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** The robot moves forward at 0.45 m/s while turning left at 0.33 rad/s, advancing at equal $\dot{x}$ and $\dot{y}$ because of the 45° heading.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reynolds' Boids Velocity Update from First Principles

**Problem Setup:** Derive the velocity update equation for a swarm of $N$ boids in 2D, given neighbour positions $\vec{p}_j$ and velocities $\vec{v}_j$.

#### Step 1 — Define Neighbour Set

A robot $i$ considers robot $j$ as a **neighbour** if the Euclidean distance is less than the perception radius $R$:

$$
\mathcal{N}_i = \{ j \neq i \mid \Vert \vec{p}_i - \vec{p}_j \Vert < R \}
$$

#### Step 2 — Separation Force Derivation

To prevent collision, robot $i$ must move **away** from each neighbour. The direction of repulsion is the unit vector from neighbour $j$ to $i$:

$$
\hat{d}_{ij} = \frac{\vec{p}_i - \vec{p}_j}{\Vert \vec{p}_i - \vec{p}_j \Vert}
$$

The magnitude of the repulsive force should **increase as distance decreases** (inverse-square law, capped to avoid singularities):

$$
\vec{F}_s = \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} \frac{\vec{p}_i - \vec{p}_j}{\Vert \vec{p}_i - \vec{p}_j \Vert^2 + \epsilon}
$$

where $\epsilon$ is a small positive constant (e.g., $\epsilon = 0.01$) added to prevent division by zero when two robots coincide.

#### Step 3 — Alignment Force Derivation

To align with neighbours, robot $i$ averages the velocities of its neighbours and steers towards that average. In vector form:

$$
\vec{v}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{N}_i \vert} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} \vec{v}_j
$$

The alignment steering force is the difference between the average neighbour velocity and the robot's current velocity:

$$
\vec{F}_a = \vec{v}_{\text{avg}} - \vec{v}_i = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{N}_i \vert} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} \vec{v}_j - \vec{v}_i
$$

#### Step 4 — Cohesion Force Derivation

To move towards the centroid of neighbours, robot $i$ computes the average position of its neighbours:

$$
\vec{p}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{N}_i \vert} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} \vec{p}_j
$$

The cohesion steering force is the vector from $i$'s current position to the neighbour centroid:

$$
\vec{F}_c = \vec{p}_{\text{avg}} - \vec{p}_i = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{N}_i \vert} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} \vec{p}_j - \vec{p}_i
$$

#### Step 5 — Combined Velocity Update (Discrete-Time Euler Integration)

The total steering acceleration is the weighted sum:

$$
\vec{a}_i = w_s \vec{F}_s + w_a \vec{F}_a + w_c \vec{F}_c
$$

Integrating over timestep $\Delta t$ (Euler method):

$$
\vec{v}_i(t + \Delta t) = \vec{v}_i(t) + \vec{a}_i \cdot \Delta t
$$

Substituting:

$$
\vec{v}_i(t + \Delta t) = \vec{v}_i(t) + \Delta t \left( w_s \vec{F}_s + w_a \vec{F}_a + w_c \vec{F}_c \right)
$$

Position update (also Euler):

$$
\vec{p}_i(t + \Delta t) = \vec{p}_i(t) + \vec{v}_i(t + \Delta t) \cdot \Delta t
$$

#### Step 6 — Stability Bound on $\Delta t$

For numerical stability of the explicit Euler scheme, the timestep must satisfy the Courant–Friedrichs–Lewy (CFL) condition:

$$
\Delta t \leq \frac{\Delta x}{v_{\max}}
$$

Where $\Delta x$ is the minimum inter-robot spacing and $v_{\max}$ is the maximum allowed velocity. A typical KTU-acceptable value is $\Delta t = 0.05$ s for a swarm with $v_{\max} = 1$ m/s and $\Delta x = 0.05$ m.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Autonomous vs Swarm Robots

| Parameter | Autonomous Robot (Single) | Swarm Robot (Multi) |
|:----------|:--------------------------|:--------------------|
| **Number of agents** | 1 | **10 – 10,000** |
| **Central control** | Self-contained (centralized) | **None (decentralized)** |
| **Global knowledge** | Full (own map) | **None — only local sensing** |
| **Communication** | Usually none (or with operator) | **Local neighbour-only** |
| **Failure tolerance** | **Zero tolerance** (single point of failure) | **High (graceful degradation)** |
| **Scalability** | Limited by sensors/compute | **Highly scalable** |
| **Cost per unit** | High (₹₹₹) | Low (₹) |
| **Hardware complexity** | Complex (multiple sensors, GPU) | **Simple (1–2 sensors, MCU)** |
| **Decision-making** | Deliberative + Reactive | Reactive + Emergent |
| **Typical algorithm** | SLAM, A*, MPC | Reynolds' Boids, PSO, ACO |
| **Real-world example** | Mars Perseverance Rover | Kilobot swarm (Harvard) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Sense–Plan–Act (SPA) Loop of an Autonomous Robot

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Environment / World] -->|Stimuli| B[Sensor Array: LiDAR, IMU, Camera, GPS]
    B -->|Raw Data| C[Perception Module: SLAM, Feature Extraction]
    C -->|World Model| D[Planner: A*, RRT, Behaviour Tree]
    D -->|Motor Commands| E[Actuator Drivers: PWM, H-Bridge]
    E -->|Motion| F[Robot Body / Wheels / Manipulator]
    F -->|Action on World| A
    D -.Feedback.-> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The flow is a closed loop. The robot acts on the world, the world changes, sensors perceive the new state, the planner re-computes, and the cycle repeats at frequency $1/T_c$. For mobile robots, $T_c \in [10, 100]$ ms.

---

### 4.2 Three-Layer Architecture of an Autonomous Robot (Brooks' Subsumption Style)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Deliberative Layer: A*, Mission Planning, World Map]
    B[Executive Layer: FSM, Task Sequencing, Behaviour Selection]
    C[Reactive Layer: Obstacle Avoidance, Reflex Behaviours]
    S[Sensor Input Stream]
    M[Motor Output Stream]
    S --> C
    C --> M
    S --> B
    B --> M
    S --> A
    A --> B
    A -.Override.-> C
    B -.Inhibit.-> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Subsumption Architecture (Rodney Brooks, 1986):** Lower layers operate independently of higher layers. Higher layers can *inhibit* (override) lower layers when needed. This is what gives autonomous robots their **fast reactive reflexes + slow deliberative planning** hybrid behaviour.

---

### 4.3 Reynolds' Boids Interaction Model (Swarm)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Boid A] -->|perceives| P2[Boid B]
    P2 -->|perceives| P1
    P1 -->|perceives| P3[Boid C]
    P3 -->|perceives| P1
    P2 -->|perceives| P3
    P3 -->|perceives| P2
    P1 --> R1[Rule 1: Separation]
    P1 --> R2[Rule 2: Alignment]
    P1 --> R3[Rule 3: Cohesion]
    R1 --> V1[Velocity Update]
    R2 --> V1
    R3 --> V1
```

> [!NOTE]
> Each boid independently runs the three rules using only **locally perceived** information. No boid has a global map. Yet the **emergent** flocking, herding, and obstacle-avoidance behaviour arises naturally.

---

### 4.4 Functional Block Architecture — Autonomous vs Swarm Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AUTONOMOUS[Autonomous Robot - Single Agent]
        A1[High-End GPU / SBC] --> A2[Multi-Sensor Fusion: LiDAR+Camera+IMU]
        A2 --> A3[SLAM and Path Planning]
        A3 --> A4[Motion Controller]
        A4 --> A5[High-Torque Actuators]
    end

    subgraph SWARM[Swarm Robot - Multi Agent Collective]
        S1[Low-Cost MCU per robot] --> S2[Single Sensor: IR or Sonar]
        S2 --> S3[Local Neighbour Rules]
        S3 --> S4[Simple Wheel Drive]
        S4 -.Wireless Mesh.-> S1
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway from the block diagram:** The autonomous robot pushes complexity into the **central processor**, while the swarm pushes complexity into the **interaction rules** between many simple processors. Both reach intelligence, but via opposite engineering trade-offs.

---

### 4.5 Swarm Decision Cycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Initialize Position and Velocity] --> S1[Scan Neighbours within Rc]
    S1 --> S2[Compute Separation Force]
    S1 --> S3[Compute Alignment Force]
    S1 --> S4[Compute Cohesion Force]
    S2 --> S5[Weighted Sum: Steering Vector]
    S3 --> S5
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6[Update Velocity: vi new = vi + dt times a]
    S6 --> S7[Update Position: pi new = pi + vi new times dt]
    S7 --> Check{Goal Achieved?}
    Check -->|No| S1
    Check -->|Yes| Stop[Terminate]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Style]

> **Q: Define an autonomous robot. List any four sensors commonly used in autonomous mobile robots.**

**Model Answer:**

An **autonomous robot** is a robotic system capable of performing tasks in an unstructured environment **without human intervention**, by autonomously sensing, planning, and acting upon its surroundings through its onboard intelligence.

Four commonly used sensors in autonomous mobile robots are:

1. **LiDAR** — for 2D/3D mapping and obstacle detection using time-of-flight laser ranging.
2. **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** — for orientation, angular rate, and acceleration measurement (9-DOF: gyro + accelerometer + magnetometer).
3. **GPS (Global Positioning System)** — for absolute outdoor localization (accuracy ±2.5 m CEP).
4. **Wheel Encoders (Optical Encoders)** — for odometry-based dead-reckoning of position.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** KTU 2024 examiners award 1 mark for the *definition* and 0.5 marks × 4 = 2 marks for sensor enumeration with **one-line functional description** for each. Do not just list names — *describe* the role.

---

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model]

> **Q: What is a swarm robot? State any three characteristics that distinguish swarm robotics from traditional multi-robot systems.**

**Model Answer:**

A **swarm robotic system** is a collection of large numbers of **simple, homogeneous robots** that coordinate their actions through **local sensing, communication, and decentralized control** to achieve a **collective emergent behaviour**.

Three distinguishing characteristics are:

1. **Decentralized Control:** No central supervisor or leader. Each robot makes its own decisions based only on local information.
2. **Local Sensing and Communication:** Robots can only perceive and communicate with **immediate neighbours** (within range $R_c$), not the entire swarm.
3. **Emergent Collective Behaviour:** The global intelligent behaviour of the swarm (e.g., flocking, foraging) **emerges** from the local interactions of individuals, even though no individual robot possesses global knowledge.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always use the term **"emergent behaviour"** in swarm answers — KTU examiners specifically test this vocabulary. It is worth at least 1 mark.

---

### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style, Module 1]

> **Q (a) [7 Marks]:** With a neat block diagram, explain the **three-layer (deliberative–executive–reactive) architecture** of an autonomous robot. Compare the time scales of operation of each layer.

> **Q (b) [7 Marks]:** A differential-drive autonomous robot has wheel radius $r = 0.04$ m and wheelbase $L = 0.25$ m. If $\omega_R = 12$ rad/s and $\omega_L = 8$ rad/s, compute the linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$ of the robot. If the current heading is $\theta = 60°$, find $\dot{x}$ and $\dot{y}$.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Three-Layer Architecture:**

The three-layer architecture decomposes the autonomous robot's control into a hierarchy of increasing time-scale and decreasing immediacy.

**Layer 1 — Reactive Layer (lowest, fastest):**
- Time scale: **< 10 ms** (often 1–10 ms loop).
- Function: Direct sensor-to-actuator mapping. Handles **reflexive** behaviours such as emergency obstacle avoidance, collision reflexes, and force compliance.
- Example algorithm: Potential field method, Vector Field Histogram (VFH).
- Characteristic: **No memory, no planning** — purely instantaneous sensor reading → motor command.

**Layer 2 — Executive Layer (middle, intermediate):**
- Time scale: **100 ms – 1 s**.
- Function: Sequences tasks, manages sub-goals, decides which reactive behaviour to activate, and handles finite state machine (FSM) transitions.
- Example: Behaviour Trees, FSM, hierarchical task networks.
- Characteristic: Implements *deliberative decisions* by selecting the appropriate reactive rule for the current world state.

**Layer 3 — Deliberative Layer (highest, slowest):**
- Time scale: **> 1 s** (often seconds to minutes).
- Function: Performs global path planning, world modelling, mission-level reasoning, and goal setting.
- Example algorithms: A*, Dijkstra, RRT (Rapidly-exploring Random Tree), D* Lite.
- Characteristic: **Computationally expensive**, requires a maintained world model.

> **[Block diagram — see SECTION 4.2 above]**

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | Reactive | Executive | Deliberative |
|:---------|:---------|:----------|:-------------|
| Time scale | < 10 ms | 100 ms – 1 s | > 1 s |
| Awareness | Instantaneous | Local context | Global map |
| Memory | None | Short-term | Long-term |
| Failure impact | Catastrophic if wrong | Moderate | Tolerable |
| Example | Bumper-stop | "Reach room B" | "Plan path to B" |

**Marks Distribution (7):** 2 marks for diagram, 2 marks for layer description, 2 marks for time-scale table, 1 mark for example algorithms.

---

**Part (b) — Differential-Drive Calculation:**

**Given:** $r = 0.04$ m, $L = 0.25$ m, $\omega_R = 12$ rad/s, $\omega_L = 8$ rad/s, $\theta = 60°$.

**Step 1 — Linear velocity $v$:**

$$
v = \frac{r}{2} (\omega_R + \omega_L) = \frac{0.04}{2} (12 + 8) = 0.02 \times 20 = 0.4 \;\text{m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Angular velocity $\omega$:**

$$
\omega = \frac{r}{L} (\omega_R - \omega_L) = \frac{0.04}{0.25} (12 - 8) = 0.16 \times 4 = 0.64 \;\text{rad/s}
$$

**Step 3 — $\dot{x}$ at $\theta = 60°$:**

$$
\dot{x} = v \cos(\theta) = 0.4 \times \cos(60°) = 0.4 \times 0.5 = 0.20 \;\text{m/s}
$$

**Step 4 — $\dot{y}$ at $\theta = 60°$:**

$$
\dot{y} = v \sin(\theta) = 0.4 \times \sin(60°) = 0.4 \times 0.8660 = 0.3464 \;\text{m/s}
$$

**Final Answer:** $v = 0.4$ m/s, $\omega = 0.64$ rad/s, $\dot{x} = 0.20$ m/s, $\dot{y} = 0.3464$ m/s.

**Marks Distribution (7):** 2 marks for $v$, 2 marks for $\omega$, 1.5 marks for $\dot{x}$, 1.5 marks for $\dot{y}$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice, KTU 2024 Internal Choice Pattern]

> **Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the **Reynolds' Boids model** for swarm robotics. Derive the equations for the three rules (separation, alignment, cohesion) and write the final velocity update law.

> **Q (b) [7 Marks]:** With a neat block diagram, describe the **Sense–Plan–Act (SPA) architecture** of an autonomous robot. Mention the role of the **SLAM** algorithm in the perception module.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Reynolds' Boids Model:**

The Reynolds' Boids model (1986) is the foundational mathematical model of swarm behaviour. It was originally proposed to simulate the flocking of birds but is universally applied in swarm robotics.

**Assumption:** Every robot $i$ in the swarm has position $\vec{p}_i$, velocity $\vec{v}_i$, and a finite perception radius $R$ within which it can sense other robots.

**Rule 1 — Separation:** Robot $i$ steers to **avoid crowding** its neighbours. The force is:

$$
\vec{F}_s = \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} \frac{\vec{p}_i - \vec{p}_j}{\Vert \vec{p}_i - \vec{p}_j \Vert^2 + \epsilon}
$$

The denominator uses a small constant $\epsilon$ to avoid singularity at zero distance.

**Rule 2 — Alignment:** Robot $i$ steers towards the **average heading** of its neighbours:

$$
\vec{F}_a = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{N}_i \vert} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} \vec{v}_j - \vec{v}_i
$$

**Rule 3 — Cohesion:** Robot $i$ steers towards the **average position (centroid)** of its neighbours:

$$
\vec{F}_c = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{N}_i \vert} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} \vec{p}_j - \vec{p}_i
$$

**Final Velocity Update (Euler Integration):**

$$
\vec{v}_i(t + \Delta t) = \vec{v}_i(t) + \Delta t \left( w_s \vec{F}_s + w_a \vec{F}_a + w_c \vec{F}_c \right)
$$

$$
\vec{p}_i(t + \Delta t) = \vec{p}_i(t) + \vec{v}_i(t + \Delta t) \cdot \Delta t
$$

Where $w_s, w_a, w_c$ are tunable weights and $\Delta t$ is the loop time-step.

**Marks Distribution (7):** 2 marks for model assumption + neighbour definition, 1.5 marks each for the three rules, 1 mark for the final update law.

---

**Part (b) — Sense–Plan–Act (SPA) Architecture:**

> **[Block diagram — see SECTION 4.1 above]**

**Sense Stage:** Sensor array (LiDAR, IMU, cameras, encoders, GPS) captures physical phenomena and converts them into digital signals. The perception module runs **SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping)** to build a 2D/3D map of the environment while simultaneously estimating the robot's pose within that map.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Role of SLAM in SPA:**
> SLAM solves the "chicken-and-egg" problem — you need a map to know where you are, and you need to know where you are to build a map. Algorithms include **EKF-SLAM, FastSLAM, Graph-SLAM, ORB-SLAM3, and Cartographer**. Modern systems use LiDAR-Visual-Inertial fusion for robustness.

**Plan Stage:** The planner (using A*, RRT, or behaviour trees) uses the SLAM-built world model to compute a collision-free trajectory from the current pose to the goal pose.

**Act Stage:** The motion controller converts the planned trajectory into PWM signals that drive the BLDC / DC motors through H-bridge motor drivers.

**Marks Distribution (7):** 2 marks for block diagram, 1.5 marks for each of the three stages, 2 marks for SLAM explanation.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks on this topic (KTU 2024 Examiner Reports):**
> 1. **Confusing "autonomous" with "automated":** Automated = pre-programmed repetitive task (e.g., a factory arm). Autonomous = self-deciding in unknown environments. Examiners *will* deduct 1 mark if you interchange these.
> 2. **Writing "swarm robots use AI" without specifying *what* kind:** Swarm robots use **emergent collective intelligence from local rules**, NOT centralized deep learning. Writing "swarm uses neural networks" is a 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Forgetting units in numerical problems:** Always write m/s, rad/s, m, m². KTU deducts 0.5 marks per missing unit.
> 4. **In the differential-drive formula, using the wrong sign convention:** Some textbooks write $v = r(\omega_R + \omega_L)$ without the factor of $1/2$. KTU's official formula is $v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$. Mismatch = 0 mark.
> 5. **Drawing diagrams without arrows or labels:** A Sense–Plan–Act diagram without arrows showing the loop direction loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Mixing up cohesion and separation:** Cohesion is *attraction* to the centre of mass; separation is *repulsion* from neighbours. KTU examiners catch this.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **📌 Must-Memorize for KTU 2024 Exam:**
>
> - **Autonomous Robot** = Sense + Plan + Act loop, **no human-in-the-loop** during operation.
> - **Three-layer architecture** = Reactive (< 10 ms) / Executive (100 ms – 1 s) / Deliberative (> 1 s).
> - **Subsumption Architecture** (Brooks, 1986) — higher layers inhibit lower layers.
> - **SLAM** = Simultaneous Localization and Mapping, performed in the perception module.
> - **Differential-drive forward kinematics** — $v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$, $\omega = \frac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$.
> - **World-frame state equations** — $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$, $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$, $\dot{\theta} = \omega$.
> - **Swarm robot** = Large number (N ≥ 10) of simple, homogeneous, decentralized robots.
> - **Reynolds' Three Rules** = Separation (repulsion) + Alignment (velocity match) + Cohesion (centroid attraction).
> - **Emergent behaviour** = Global intelligence from local rules. **Always** use this term in swarm answers.
> - **Swarm density** $\rho_s = N / (\pi R_c^2)$, optimum range 0.1–2.0 robots/m².
> - **Communication range** $R_c$ = 1–10 m (typical), local only.
> - **Decision cycle time** $T_c$ = 10–100 ms for real-time performance.
> - **Real-world examples to quote:** Perseverance Rover (autonomous), Kilobot swarm (Harvard), Amazon Kiva (AMR), DARPA OFFSET (swarm drones).
> - **Key contrast:** Autonomous = centralized complexity in one robot; Swarm = distributed simplicity across many robots.
> - **Algorithm names to drop in answers:** SLAM, A*, RRT, Reynolds Boids, PSO (Particle Swarm Optimization), ACO (Ant Colony Optimization).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robotics Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT205 - SENSOR TECHNOLOGY FOR ROBOTICS (ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Robotics Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Robotics Applications** refer to the deployment of autonomous or semi-autonomous robotic systems integrated with multi-modal sensor suites (proprioceptive and exteroceptive) to execute tasks in structured, unstructured, hazardous, precision-critical, or human-assistive environments. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, these applications are evaluated through the lens of *Sensor Fusion*, *Human-Robot Interaction (HRI)*, and *Degrees of Freedom (DOF)* demanded by the operational workspace.

According to the **International Federation of Robotics (IFR)** and the **ISO 8373:2021** standard, robotics applications are classified into two macro-categories:
- **Industrial Robots** (used in automated manufacturing, ISO 10218 compliant).
- **Service Robots** (used for non-manufacturing humanitarian, domestic, or professional tasks, ISO 13482 compliant).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a robot like a smartphone with arms.** Just as your smartphone has a camera, GPS, accelerometer, gyroscope, and microphone (sensors) to understand the world and respond intelligently, a robot uses *cameras, LiDAR, force/torque sensors, encoders, and IMUs* to *see*, *feel*, and *navigate* its environment. The **application** is simply *what useful job* the smartphone-with-arms is hired to do — like welding a car frame, performing brain surgery, or vacuuming your living room.

The richness of a robotic application is **directly proportional** to the *quality, density, and fusion algorithm* of its sensor stack. A robot with only **1 sensor** is essentially blind; a robot with **fused multi-sensor arrays** perceives contextually.

### 1.3 Key Classification Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value / Range | Significance in KTU Context |
|---|---|---|
| **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** | **3 to 7+ DOF** | Defines task dexterity (e.g., 6-DOF for surgery) |
| **Payload Capacity** | **0.5 kg to 2000 kg** | Industrial vs. service classification |
| **Repeatability** | **±0.02 mm to ±0.1 mm** | Critical for assembly & micro-machining |
| **Operational Reach** | **0.3 m to 4 m** | Determines workspace envelope |
| **Sensor Latency** | **<10 ms** (real-time control) | Required for closed-loop feedback |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For Module 1, focus on **why a specific sensor configuration is chosen for a specific application**, not merely *what the robot does*. Examiners reward *sensor-to-task mapping logic*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Robotics Applications

Robotics applications can be decomposed along **four analytical axes**:

**Axis 1 — Operational Environment:**
- **Structured** (factory floors — known coordinates, fixed obstacles).
- **Semi-Structured** (warehouses — known layout, dynamic obstacles).
- **Unstructured** (search & rescue, space — unknown terrain).

**Axis 2 — Human Proximity (per ISO/TS 15066):**
- **Collaborative (Cobots)** — shared workspace with speed/force monitoring.
- **Non-Collaborative** — physically isolated behind safety cages.

**Axis 3 — Autonomy Level (per SAE J3016 adapted for robots):**
- **Level 0** — Fully teleoperated.
- **Level 1** — Assisted control.
- **Level 2** — Partial autonomy.
- **Level 3** — Conditional autonomy.
- **Level 4** — High autonomy.
- **Level 5** — Full autonomy (AGI-grade).

**Axis 4 — Sensor-Dominant Modality:**
- **Vision-dominated** (inspection, surgical).
- **Force-dominated** (polishing, deburring).
- **Proximity-dominated** (AGV navigation).
- **Multi-modal fusion** (autonomous vehicles).

### 2.2 Application-to-Sensor Mapping Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick Question:** *"Why is LiDAR preferred over cameras in warehouse AGVs?"* — Answer must reference **deterministic ranging**, **insensitivity to ambient light**, and **computational efficiency for SLAM** in structured environments.

| Application Domain | Primary Sensors | Secondary Sensors | Sensor Fusion Technique |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Industrial Welding** | Current/Voltage sensors, Arc sensors | Vision (seam tracking), Thermographic | Kalman Filter |
| **Surgical Robotics (Da Vinci)** | Force/Torque, Hyperspectral imaging | EMG, Tactile arrays | Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) |
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | LiDAR, Radar, Camera | Ultrasonic, IMU, GPS | Particle Filter + EKF |
| **Warehouse AGV/AMR** | 2D LiDAR, Wheel Encoders | RGB-D Camera, IMU | Grid-based SLAM |
| **Underwater ROV** | Sonar (forward-looking), Pressure | DVL (Doppler), Magnetometer | Complementary Filter |
| **Space Robotics (Mars Rover)** | Stereo Vision, LiDAR | Sun tracker, IMU, Tactile | Visual-Inertial Odometry (VIO) |
| **Agricultural Robots** | Hyperspectral, GPS/RTK | Soil moisture, Lidar | Bayesian Network |
| **Humanoid Robots** | IMU, Force/Torque in joints | Depth cameras, Microphones | Sensor Fusion with ZMP control |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below are the **mathematically critical equations** tied to robotics application performance. Master these for the 14-mark analytical questions.

| # | Formula | Description | Application Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_{\text{control}} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{sensor}}}$ | Sensor sampling frequency (Hz) | Real-time control loop design |
| 2 | $\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_{\text{signal}}}{V_{\text{noise}}}\right)$ dB | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | Sensor selection for noisy environments |
| 3 | $\sigma_r = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | Radar/LiDAR range resolution | Obstacle detection accuracy |
| 4 | $\theta_{\text{res}} = \frac{\lambda}{D}$ | Angular resolution (rad) | Imaging & LiDAR |
| 5 | $F_{\text{safety}} = m \cdot (a_{\text{max}} + g)$ | Safe stop force (cobot) | Collaborative robot safety (ISO/TS 15066) |
| 6 | $v_{\text{max}} = \sqrt{\frac{\mu \cdot g}{K}} \cdot \sqrt{r}$ | Maximum safe speed vs. curvature | Mobile robot dynamics |
| 7 | $\mathbf{x}_{k} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}_{k-1} + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}_{k} + \mathbf{w}_{k-1}$ | Kalman State Prediction | Sensor fusion |
| 8 | $\mathbf{P}_{k\mid k-1} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{P}_{k-1}\mathbf{A}^{\top} + \mathbf{Q}$ | Kalman Covariance Update | Uncertainty propagation |
| 9 | $D_{\text{resolution}} = \frac{c}{2B}$ | Ultrasonic depth resolution | Subsea mapping |
| 10 | $Z = m \cdot g + m \cdot \frac{v^{2}}{r}$ | Centripetal + gravity ZMP | Bipedal humanoid balance |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do **not** confuse **range resolution** ($\sigma_r$) with **range accuracy**. Resolution is the *minimum distinguishable distance between two objects*; accuracy is the *deviation from true range*.

### 2.4 Why Sensor Choice Dictates Application Viability

The application domain imposes a **hard constraint set** on the sensor stack:

- **High temperature / arc environments** (welding) → Optical sensors fail; **current/voltage transducers** dominate.
- **Sterile / magnetic-sensitive** (MRI surgery) → Piezoelectric motors, **fiber-optic sensors** (no electromagnetic interference).
- **Underwater light-starved** → Cameras fail; **sonar & DVL** become primary.
- **Dust/smoke-filled** (mining, fire rescue) → Vision degrades; **thermal imaging, radar, and gas sensors** lead.

> The **WHY** is what KTU examiners want — *not* just the *WHAT*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Derivations

### 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Sensor Sampling Rate for a Mobile Robot

**Problem Statement:** A warehouse AGV travels at maximum velocity $v_{\text{max}} = 2 \, \text{m/s}$. The obstacle detection sensor must detect an object of width $w = 0.1 \, \text{m}$ with a safety margin. Derive the minimum sensor sampling frequency $f_{\text{min}}$.

**Step 1 — Define the time window for detection.**
The robot must detect the obstacle while traversing a distance equal to the obstacle width $w$ at maximum velocity $v_{\text{max}}$.

$$
T_{\text{detect}} = \frac{w}{v_{\text{max}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
T_{\text{detect}} = \frac{0.1 \, \text{m}}{2 \, \text{m/s}} = 0.05 \, \text{s} = 50 \, \text{ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Nyquist-Shannon sampling criterion.**
For reliable detection, the sampling period $T_s$ must be **at most half** the detection time window.

$$
T_s \leq \frac{T_{\text{detect}}}{2}
$$

$$
T_s \leq \frac{0.05}{2} = 0.025 \, \text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to sampling frequency.**

$$
f_{\text{sample}} = \frac{1}{T_s} \geq \frac{1}{0.025} = 40 \, \text{Hz}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering safety factor.**
Apply a safety multiplier of **1.5×** for redundancy and sensor latency.

$$
f_{\text{min,design}} = 1.5 \times 40 = 60 \, \text{Hz}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** A 2D LiDAR with **≥ 60 Hz** scan rate is required. Most industrial LiDARs (e.g., SICK, Hokuyo) operate at **10–50 Hz**, justifying why AGV velocity is often limited to **1.5 m/s** in practice.

### 3.2 Derivation: Force-Limited Safe Speed for a Cobot

**Problem Statement:** Per **ISO/TS 15066**, the maximum permissible quasi-static contact force on a human body region (e.g., hand) is $F_{\text{limit}} = 140 \, \text{N}$. Derive the maximum safe joint velocity $v_{\text{joint}}$ for a cobot with effective mass $m_{\text{eff}} = 5 \, \text{kg}$ and contact stiffness $K = 10{,}000 \, \text{N/m}$.

**Step 1 — Apply Hooke's Law for the contact event.**

$$
F_{\text{contact}} = K \cdot \delta
$$

where $\delta$ is the indentation depth. Set $F_{\text{contact}} = F_{\text{limit}}$.

$$
\delta = \frac{F_{\text{limit}}}{K} = \frac{140}{10{,}000} = 0.014 \, \text{m} = 14 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Work-Energy Theorem.**
The kinetic energy of the moving cobot mass is converted into elastic potential energy at contact.

$$
\frac{1}{2} m_{\text{eff}} v_{\text{joint}}^{2} = \frac{1}{2} K \delta^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $v_{\text{joint}}$.**

$$
v_{\text{joint}}^{2} = \frac{K \delta^{2}}{m_{\text{eff}}}
$$

$$
v_{\text{joint}} = \delta \cdot \sqrt{\frac{K}{m_{\text{eff}}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the values.**

$$
v_{\text{joint}} = 0.014 \times \sqrt{\frac{10{,}000}{5}} = 0.014 \times \sqrt{2000}
$$

$$
v_{\text{joint}} = 0.014 \times 44.72 = 0.626 \, \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM) reduction.**
For collaborative operation, limit to **250 mm/s** (ISO/TS 15066 default).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** $v_{\text{joint, max}} \approx 0.626 \, \text{m/s}$, but **regulatory limit = 0.25 m/s** governs. This is why **cobot speed caps** exist — not just mechanical limits.

### 3.3 Sensor Fusion Worked Example: Kalman Filter State Estimation

**Problem:** A 1-D mobile robot uses wheel odometry (noisy) and a GPS sensor (noisy). Derive the fused position estimate $\hat{x}_k$.

**Step 1 — Define the linear system model.**

$$
\mathbf{x}_k = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}_{k-1} + \mathbf{B}u_k + w_{k-1}
$$

For 1-D motion with constant velocity $v$ over $\Delta t$:

$$
\mathbf{x}_k = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & \Delta t \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_{k-1} \\ v_{k-1} \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ a_k \Delta t \end{bmatrix} + w_{k-1}
$$

**Step 2 — Define observation model.**

$$
z_k = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{x}_k + v_k = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_k \\ v_k \end{bmatrix} + v_k
$$

**Step 3 — Prediction step (time update).**

$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k-1} = \mathbf{A}\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1\mid k-1} + \mathbf{B}u_k
$$

$$
\mathbf{P}_{k\mid k-1} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{P}_{k-1\mid k-1}\mathbf{A}^{\top} + \mathbf{Q}
$$

**Step 4 — Update step (measurement update).**

$$
\mathbf{K}_k = \mathbf{P}_{k\mid k-1}\mathbf{H}^{\top}\left(\mathbf{H}\mathbf{P}_{k\mid k-1}\mathbf{H}^{\top} + \mathbf{R}\right)^{-1}
$$

$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k-1} + \mathbf{K}_k\left(z_k - \mathbf{H}\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k-1}\right)
$$

$$
\mathbf{P}_{k\mid k} = \left(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_k\mathbf{H}\right)\mathbf{P}_{k\mid k-1}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Where:** $\mathbf{Q}$ is process noise covariance (odometry drift), $\mathbf{R}$ is measurement noise covariance (GPS noise ~ 2.5 m), and $\mathbf{K}_k$ is the **Kalman Gain** that optimally weights the two sources.

**Step 5 — Physical interpretation.**
- If $\mathbf{R}$ is large (GPS very noisy), $\mathbf{K}_k \to 0$ → trust odometry.
- If $\mathbf{Q}$ is large (model is uncertain), $\mathbf{K}_k \to 1$ → trust GPS.

This is the **mathematical core** of how autonomous vehicles fuse LiDAR + GPS + IMU in real time.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define the term "Collaborative Robot (Cobot)" and mention any two sensors critical for ensuring human safety during human-robot collaboration.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Collaborative Robot (Cobot)** is a robot designed for direct operation within a defined collaborative workspace where it shares the working area with humans, as defined by **ISO/TS 15066**. Unlike traditional industrial robots that require safety cages, cobots employ power and force limiting (PFL) and speed and separation monitoring (SSM).
*Two critical safety sensors:*
1. **Force/Torque sensor** at the end-effector or joints to detect collision forces.
2. **Proximity / capacitive skin sensors** to detect human approach before contact.

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for cobot concept + 1 mark for two sensors = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Expected]**
*List any three applications of robots in the medical field and identify the primary sensor used in each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| # | Application | Primary Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Minimally Invasive Surgery (Da Vinci) | **3D Vision + Force/Torque** |
| 2 | Rehabilitation Exoskeleton | **EMG + IMU** |
| 3 | Robotic Prosthetics | **Myoelectric (sEMG) sensors** |

*(1 mark per correct application-sensor pair = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an industrial arc-welding robot system. List the **five major sensor types** used and justify why each is necessary. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An autonomous mobile robot (AMR) in a warehouse travels at $v = 1.5 \, \text{m/s}$. The LiDAR sensor has a range resolution of $\sigma_r = 0.03 \, \text{m}$ and a scan rate of $25 \, \text{Hz}$. Calculate:
   (i) The minimum detectable obstacle width.
   (ii) The time between successive scans.
   (iii) The distance traveled between scans. Comment on whether the sensor is adequate. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**
```
Robot Controller → Servo Drive → Manipulator (6-DOF) ↔ Welding Torch
       ↓                              ↑
  Position Sensor (Encoder)      Arc Sensor (Voltage/Current)
       ↓                              ↑
  Path Planner                  Seam Tracking Vision
       ↓                              ↑
  Safety Interlock            Gas Flow & Wire Feed Sensors
```

**Five Major Sensor Types with Justification (4 marks):**
1. **Arc Sensor (Voltage/Current)** — Real-time monitoring of arc stability; detects weld pool fluctuations. (1 mark)
2. **Seam Tracking Vision Sensor** — Compensates for workpiece tolerances and thermal distortion. (1 mark)
3. **Torch Proximity Sensor (Capacitive/Inductive)** — Maintains constant tip-to-work distance (CTWD ~ 3 mm). (0.5 marks)
4. **Joint Position Encoders** — Closed-loop position control of the 6-DOF arm. (0.5 marks)
5. **Thermographic / Pyrometer Sensor** — Monitors interpass temperature to prevent defects. (1 mark)

---

**(b) [7 marks]**

**Given:** $v = 1.5 \, \text{m/s}$, $\sigma_r = 0.03 \, \text{m}$, $f = 25 \, \text{Hz}$.

**(i) Minimum detectable obstacle width** = range resolution
$$
w_{\min} = \sigma_r = 0.03 \, \text{m} = 30 \, \text{mm} \quad \text{(1 mark)}
$$

**(ii) Time between successive scans:**
$$
T_s = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{25} = 0.04 \, \text{s} = 40 \, \text{ms} \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

**(iii) Distance traveled between scans:**
$$
d = v \cdot T_s = 1.5 \times 0.04 = 0.06 \, \text{m} = 60 \, \text{mm} \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

**Comment (2 marks):** The distance traveled between scans (60 mm) is **twice** the minimum detectable width (30 mm). This means the robot could potentially traverse a **small obstacle** (e.g., a 40 mm bolt on the floor) between two consecutive scans **without detection**. Hence, the sensor is **marginally adequate** but a higher scan rate (≥ 50 Hz) is recommended for safety. This is precisely why **industrial AGVs use 40–75 Hz LiDARs** in practice.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Expected]**
**(a)** Explain the **four major classifications** of service robots with one real-world example for each. Discuss the role of **sensor fusion** in enabling service robots to operate in human environments. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A quadruped robot (e.g., Boston Dynamics Spot) uses an IMU and force sensors in its feet. The robot's body mass is $m = 32 \, \text{kg}$. During a dynamic gait transition, the IMU reports a vertical acceleration $a_z = 0.5g$ where $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$. The foot force sensor measures $F_{\text{foot}} = 200 \, \text{N}$.
   (i) Calculate the apparent weight sensed by the IMU.
   (ii) Determine whether the robot is in contact with the ground (use the static weight as reference).
   (iii) Justify why **fusing** IMU and foot force data is superior to using either alone. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]**

**Four classifications of service robots (4 marks):**
1. **Domestic/Household Robots** — e.g., Roomba vacuum cleaner (uses LiDAR + IR cliff sensors).
2. **Professional/Field Robots** — e.g., agricultural harvesting robot (uses hyperspectral imaging + GPS-RTK).
3. **Medical/Healthcare Robots** — e.g., Da Vinci surgical system (uses 3D HD vision + haptic force feedback).
4. **Logistics/AMRs** — e.g., Amazon Kiva robots (uses 2D LiDAR + wheel odometry + IMU).

**Role of sensor fusion (3 marks):**
- **Redundancy:** If a camera is blinded by glare, LiDAR provides geometric data.
- **Complementarity:** Cameras provide *semantic* understanding; LiDAR provides *metric* depth.
- **Robustness:** Multi-modal fusion (EKF/Particle Filter) handles sensor failures gracefully.
- **Reduced uncertainty:** Combined covariance $\mathbf{P}_{\text{fused}} < \min(\mathbf{P}_{\text{cam}}, \mathbf{P}_{\text{lidar}})$.

---

**(b) [7 marks]**

**Given:** $m = 32 \, \text{kg}$, $a_z = 0.5g = 4.905 \, \text{m/s}^2$, $F_{\text{foot}} = 200 \, \text{N}$.

**(i) Apparent weight sensed by IMU:**
$$
F_{\text{IMU}} = m(g + a_z) = 32 \times (9.81 + 4.905) = 32 \times 14.715
$$
$$
F_{\text{IMU}} = 470.88 \, \text{N} \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

**(ii) Comparison with static weight and ground contact determination:**
Static weight reference:
$$
F_{\text{static}} = m \cdot g = 32 \times 9.81 = 313.92 \, \text{N} \quad \text{(1 mark)}
$$

Since $F_{\text{foot}} = 200 \, \text{N} < F_{\text{static}} = 313.92 \, \text{N}$, the robot is in **partial unweighting** — likely mid-stride or transitioning gait. The robot **is in contact** but with reduced loading (force < weight → the leg is in a push-off or landing phase). (2 marks)

**(iii) Justification of sensor fusion (2 marks):**
- **IMU alone** is plagued by **drift** in long-term integration.
- **Force sensor alone** only measures *contact* events — cannot detect swing-phase orientation.
- **Fused**, they provide: IMU gives **orientation + angular velocity**; force sensor gives **ground truth for contact detection**, enabling **zero-moment-point (ZMP) feedback** for dynamic balance.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Deductions:**
> - **Lose 1 mark** for not specifying **sensor units** (e.g., N vs. kgf).
> - **Lose 1 mark** for confusing **range resolution** with **range accuracy**.
> - **Lose 2 marks** for omitting the **Nyquist criterion** in sampling rate derivations.
> - **Lose 1 mark** for failing to cite the **ISO standard** (ISO 10218, ISO/TS 15066, ISO 13482) in cobot/safety questions.
> - **Lose 1 mark** for not mentioning **drift compensation** when discussing IMU.
> - **Lose 1 mark** for treating "sensor fusion" and "sensor integration" as synonyms — they are **not** the same.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Read 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- ⭐ **Sensor choice = Application viability.** Always justify *why* a sensor is selected, not just *what* it does.
- ⭐ **Industrial robots** rely on **encoders + force/torque + vision** for closed-loop control.
- ⭐ **Medical robots** use **haptic feedback + 3D vision** for surgeon immersion (Da Vinci: 10 ms latency budget).
- ⭐ **AGVs/AMRs** use **2D LiDAR + IMU + wheel odometry** for SLAM-based navigation.
- ⭐ **Space robots** (Mars rovers) use **visual-inertial odometry (VIO)** because GPS is unavailable.
- ⭐ **Underwater ROVs** use **sonar + DVL + depth sensors** because light is absorbed within ~ 200 m.
- ⭐ **Cobots** are governed by **ISO/TS 15066**: max contact force = **140 N** (hand), max speed = **250 mm/s**.
- ⭐ **Kalman Filter** is the workhorse of sensor fusion; **EKF** for non-linear, **Particle Filter** for non-Gaussian.
- ⭐ **Nyquist criterion:** Sensor sampling rate must be **≥ 2×** the highest frequency component of interest.
- ⭐ **Range resolution** ≠ **Range accuracy**; resolution is the *minimum distinguishable distance*.
- ⭐ **ZMP (Zero Moment Point)** is the foundational balance criterion for humanoid robots.
- ⭐ **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** 6-DOF needed for arbitrary 3D positioning; 7-DOF adds redundancy for obstacle avoidance.
- ⭐ **AGV safety:** Scan rate ≥ 40 Hz for 2 m/s velocity with 100 mm obstacle detection.
- ⭐ **Drift** in IMU is corrected by fusing with absolute sensors (GPS, vision, LiDAR).
- ⭐ **Three pillars of service robot acceptance:** **Safety (ISO 13482)**, **Reliability**, **Social acceptability (HRI)**.
- ⭐ **Sensor modalities** for hazardous environments (fire, mining, EOD): **thermal imaging, radar, gas sensors, X-ray** — NOT standard vision.
- ⭐ **Master-slave teleoperation** uses **force reflection** to give the operator haptic awareness of remote contact forces.
- ⭐ **Agri-robots** use **hyperspectral imaging** to assess crop health at the **leaf-level reflectance** scale (NDVI).
- ⭐ **Drone swarms** rely on **UWB + IMU + barometer** for indoor GPS-denied coordination.

> **End of Topic Notes — Robotics Applications | Module 1 | PCRUT205**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
