# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between active and passive sensors. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Active Sensor | Passive Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| External power | Not required | Required |
| Output generation | Self-generated (EMF, charge) | Modulation of R/L/C |
| Working principle | Direct energy conversion | Parameter variation |
| Signal level | Usually strong | Weak, needs amplification |
| Examples | Thermocouple, Piezoelectric sensor, Photovoltaic cell, LVDT | Thermistor, LDR, Strain gauge, Potentiometer |

> [!NOTE]
> Active sensors are also called *self-generating* sensors. Passive sensors are called *externally powered* sensors.

**[3 Marks: Definition 1M, Comparison 1M, Examples 1M]**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is meant by a "smart sensor"? How does it differ from a conventional sensor?**

**Model Answer:**

A **smart sensor** is a sensor that integrates the sensing element, signal conditioning circuitry, an analog-to-digital converter (ADC), and a digital communication interface (I²C, SPI, UART) on a single chip or module.

| Feature | Conventional Sensor | Smart Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Analog (raw voltage) | Digital (calibrated, linearized) |
| Signal conditioning | External | On-chip |
| Calibration | Manual | Factory-calibrated |
| Communication | Direct wire | Digital protocol |
| Example | Bare thermistor | DHT22, BMP280, MPU6050 |

**[3 Marks: Definition 1.5M, Differences 1.5M]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students often confuse *active* with *analog*. These are **independent** classifications. A sensor can be active AND analog (thermocouple) or active AND digital (smart encoder).
> 2. When asked to "classify" a given sensor, students give only ONE category. KTU requires classification along **multiple dimensions** (active/passive, analog/digital, measurand, transduction, contact, application).
> 3. The derivation of the Wheatstone bridge sensitivity is frequently asked but commonly botched. Ensure you derive it from first principles — do not memorize the final formula.
> 4. Always mention **units** and **practical applications** in robotics to score full marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Explain in detail the classification of sensors based on (i) energy source, (ii) output signal, and (iii) measurand. Provide at least two examples for each category. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A platinum resistance thermometer (PRT) with nominal resistance $R_0 = 100 \, \Omega$ at $0^\circ \text{C}$ is connected in a Wheatstone bridge. The other three arms have fixed resistors of $100 \, \Omega$ each. The supply voltage is $V_s = 5 \, \text{V}$. The PT100 has a temperature coefficient of $\alpha = 0.00385 \, /^\circ \text{C}$. Find the bridge output voltage when the temperature is $T = 100^\circ \text{C}$. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Classification of Sensors — Detailed Explanation**

**(i) Classification by Energy Source:**

- **Active sensors** (self-generating): These sensors directly convert the measurand energy into electrical energy without requiring external power. They work on principles such as the Seebeck effect (thermocouple), piezoelectric effect (force/pressure sensors), and photovoltaic effect (solar cells). Examples: Type K thermocouple, piezoelectric accelerometer, photovoltaic cell, electromagnetic tachogenerator.

- **Passive sensors** (parameter-modulating): These sensors require an external excitation voltage or current. The measurand modifies an internal electrical parameter such as resistance, capacitance, or inductance. Examples: Thermistor, LDR, strain gauge, potentiometer, capacitive humidity sensor.

**(ii) Classification by Output Signal:**

- **Analog sensors** produce a continuous output voltage or current proportional to the measurand. They require an external ADC for digital processing. Examples: LM35 temperature sensor, analog Hall sensor, piezoelectric microphone.

- **Digital sensors** produce a discrete binary output, often using protocols like I²C, SPI, UART, or 1-Wire. Examples: DHT22 temperature/humidity sensor, MPU6050 IMU, HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor, incremental encoder.

**(iii) Classification by Measurand:**

- **Kinematic sensors:** Position (LVDT, encoder), velocity (tachometer), acceleration (accelerometer), angular rate (gyroscope).
- **Mechanical sensors:** Force (load cell), torque, pressure (piezoresistive), strain (strain gauge), touch (FSR).
- **Thermal sensors:** Temperature (thermocouple, RTD, thermistor), heat flux, thermal conductivity.
- **Electromagnetic sensors:** Light (LDR, photodiode), color (TCS3200), magnetic field (Hall, MR), IR (PIR).
- **Chemical sensors:** Gas (MQ series), pH, humidity (DHT22), VOC, oxygen.
- **Acoustic sensors:** Microphone, ultrasonic (HC-SR04), vibration sensor.
- **Biological sensors:** Glucose meter, pulse oximeter, ECG electrode.

**[7 Marks: Each category with 2 examples]**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $R_0 = 100 \, \Omega$ at $T_0 = 0^\circ \text{C}$
- Fixed arms: $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = 100 \, \Omega$
- $V_s = 5 \, \text{V}$
- $\alpha = 0.00385 \, /^\circ \text{C}$
- $T = 100^\circ \text{C}$

**Step 1 — Compute the resistance of the PT100 at 100 °C.**

The RTD resistance formula is:

$$R_T = R_0 \left[ 1 + \alpha (T - T_0) \right]$$

Substituting:

$$R_T = 100 \left[ 1 + 0.00385 \times (100 - 0) \right]$$

$$R_T = 100 \left[ 1 + 0.385 \right]$$

$$R_T = 100 \times 1.385 = 138.5 \, \Omega$$

**Step 2 — Identify the change in resistance.**

$$\Delta R = R_T - R_0 = 138.5 - 100 = 38.5 \, \Omega$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Wheatstone bridge output formula (from Section 3.2).**

For one active arm, the exact expression is:

$$V_o = V_s \left[ \dfrac{R_1 + \Delta R}{2R_1 + \Delta R} - \dfrac{1}{2} \right]$$

Substituting $R_1 = 100 \, \Omega$, $\Delta R = 38.5 \, \Omega$, $V_s = 5 \, \text{V}$:

$$V_o = 5 \left[ \dfrac{100 + 38.5}{2(100) + 38.5} - \dfrac{1}{2} \right]$$

$$V_o = 5 \left[ \dfrac{138.5}{238.5} - 0.5 \right]$$

$$V_o = 5 \left[ 0.5807 - 0.5 \right]$$

$$V_o = 5 \times 0.0807 = 0.4035 \, \text{V}$$

**Step 4 — Approximation check using the linearized formula.**

$$V_o \approx \dfrac{V_s}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = \dfrac{5}{4} \cdot \dfrac{38.5}{100} = 1.25 \times 0.385 = 0.481 \, \text{V}$$

The exact value (0.4035 V) and the approximation (0.481 V) differ by about 19%, showing that for large $\Delta R$ the approximation breaks down. In practice, a linearizing amplifier or software compensation is used.

**Final Answer:** $V_o = 0.4035 \, \text{V}$ (exact), or $V_o \approx 0.481 \, \text{V}$ (approximate).

**[7 Marks: Resistance calculation 2M, Bridge equation 2M, Substitution 2M, Final result 1M]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Classify sensors used in robotics based on the **transduction principle**. Explain with a neat diagram the working of a piezoelectric sensor and list three of its robotic applications. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A strain gauge with nominal resistance $R = 350 \, \Omega$ and gauge factor $G = 2.1$ is bonded to a steel cantilever beam. The beam experiences a strain of $\varepsilon = 500 \, \mu\varepsilon$ (microstrain). The gauge is connected in a quarter-Wheatstone bridge with the other three arms as fixed $350 \, \Omega$ resistors. The excitation voltage is $V_s = 10 \, \text{V}$. Determine: (i) the change in gauge resistance $\Delta R$, (ii) the bridge output voltage $V_o$. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Classification by Transduction Principle:**

| Principle | Physical Effect | Sensor Examples |
|---|---|---|
| Resistive | Resistance change | Thermistor, LDR, Strain gauge |
| Capacitive | Capacitance change | Proximity sensor, Humidity |
| Inductive | Inductance change | LVDT, Eddy current |
| Piezoelectric | Charge generation under stress | Force sensor, Accelerometer |
| Photoelectric | Photon → Electron | Photodiode, Solar cell |
| Thermoelectric | Seebeck EMF | Thermocouple |
| Magnetoresistive | Resistance change with B | Hall sensor |

**Working of a Piezoelectric Sensor (Block Diagram Description):**

A piezoelectric sensor contains a piezoelectric crystal (e.g., quartz, PZT — lead zirconate titanate). When mechanical force $F$ is applied along a specific axis, the crystal deforms. This deformation displaces the positive and negative charge centers within the crystal lattice, generating a net surface charge $Q$ on the electrodes.

The governing equation is:

$$Q = d \cdot F$$

where $d$ is the piezoelectric charge coefficient (Coulomb/Newton). The corresponding open-circuit voltage is:

$$V = \dfrac{Q}{C_p} = \dfrac{d \cdot F}{C_p}$$

where $C_p$ is the capacitance of the crystal.

A charge amplifier (op-amp based) is typically used to convert the high-impedance charge output into a low-impedance voltage output. A block diagram representation:

```
[Mechanical Force] → [Piezo Crystal] → [Charge Q] → [Charge Amplifier] → [Output Voltage] → [ADC] → [Microcontroller]
```

**Three Robotic Applications:**

1. **Vibration monitoring in robot joints** — detects bearing wear and imbalance in motors.
2. **Force/pressure feedback in grippers** — enables delicate object handling.
3. **Accelerometer for inertial navigation** — measures linear acceleration in IMUs for drones and mobile robots.
4. **Touch sensing on robot skin** — detects contact events for safe human-robot interaction.

**[7 Marks: Classification table 3M, Working 2M, Applications 2M]**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $R = 350 \, \Omega$
- $G = 2.1$
- $\varepsilon = 500 \, \mu\varepsilon = 500 \times 10^{-6} = 5 \times 10^{-4}$
- Quarter-bridge: three fixed arms $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = 350 \, \Omega$
- $V_s = 10 \, \text{V}$

**(i) Change in Gauge Resistance:**

The strain gauge equation is:

$$\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon$$

Therefore:

$$\Delta R = R \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon$$

$$\Delta R = 350 \times 2.1 \times 5 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\Delta R = 350 \times 1.05 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\Delta R = 0.3675 \, \Omega$$

**(ii) Bridge Output Voltage:**

Using the exact quarter-bridge formula:

$$V_o = V_s \left[ \dfrac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R} - \dfrac{1}{2} \right]$$

Substituting $R = 350 \, \Omega$, $\Delta R = 0.3675 \, \Omega$, $V_s = 10 \, \text{V}$:

$$V_o = 10 \left[ \dfrac{350.3675}{700.3675} - 0.5 \right]$$

$$V_o = 10 \left[ 0.500262 - 0.5 \right]$$

$$V_o = 10 \times 0.000262 = 2.62 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{V}$$

$$V_o = 2.62 \, \text{mV}$$

**Approximate check (valid for $\Delta R \ll R$):**

$$V_o \approx \dfrac{V_s}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = \dfrac{10}{4} \times \dfrac{0.3675}{350} = 2.5 \times 1.05 \times 10^{-3} = 2.625 \, \text{mV}$$

The approximation is excellent because $\Delta R \ll R$ (ratio ≈ 0.001).

**Final Answers:**
- $\Delta R = 0.3675 \, \Omega$
- $V_o = 2.62 \, \text{mV}$

**[7 Marks: Strain gauge equation 2M, ΔR calculation 2M, Bridge equation 1.5M, Final V_o 1.5M]**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Classification of Sensors**

### Core Concepts (MUST remember)
- A **sensor** is a device that converts a physical measurand into an electrical signal.
- A **transducer** is a broader term for any device that converts energy forms; sensors are a subset.
- **Active sensors** generate their own electrical output (no external power needed).
- **Passive sensors** modulate an electrical property and require external excitation.

### Six Classification Axes (MUST remember)
1. **By energy source:** Active vs. Passive.
2. **By output:** Analog vs. Digital.
3. **By measurand:** Kinematic, Mechanical, Thermal, Electromagnetic, Chemical, Acoustic, Biological.
4. **By transduction principle:** Resistive, Capacitive, Inductive, Piezoelectric, Optical, Thermoelectric, Magnetoresistive.
5. **By contact:** Contact vs. Non-contact.
6. **By application:** Industrial, Medical, Automotive, Robotics, Environmental.

### Critical Formulas (MUST memorize)
- **Sensitivity:** $S = \dfrac{\Delta Y}{\Delta X}$
- **Linearity error:** $e_L = \dfrac{Y_{actual} - Y_{ideal}}{Y_{FS}} \times 100\%$
- **Hysteresis error:** $e_H = \dfrac{Y_{up} - Y_{down}}{Y_{FS}} \times 100\%$
- **Wheatstone bridge (quarter, small-signal):** $V_o \approx \dfrac{V_s}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$
- **Wheatstone bridge (quarter, exact):** $V_o = V_s \left[ \dfrac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R} - \dfrac{1}{2} \right]$
- **Strain gauge:** $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon$
- **Piezoelectric charge:** $Q = d \cdot F$
- **RTD resistance:** $R_T = R_0 \left[ 1 + \alpha (T - T_0) \right]$

### Classic Active vs. Passive Examples (KTU favorite)
- **Active:** Thermocouple, Piezoelectric, Photovoltaic, LVDT, Tachogenerator.
- **Passive:** Thermistor, LDR, Strain gauge, Potentiometer, RTD.

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- ✗ Confusing *active* with *analog* — they are **orthogonal** classifications.
- ✗ Memorizing bridge output without deriving — KTU will mark derivations, not just answers.
- ✗ Using the small-signal approximation when $\Delta R$ is large (>5% of $R$) — use the exact formula.
- ✗ Forgetting units in the final answer.
- ✗ Not mentioning the measurand AND the transduction principle in classification answers.

### Robotic Application Mapping (Quick Recall)
| Task in Robot | Sensor Used | Classification Tag |
|---|---|---|
| Obstacle avoidance | Ultrasonic HC-SR04 | Passive, Digital, Acoustic |
| Line following | IR photodiode | Active, Analog, Optical |
| Motor speed | Optical encoder | Active, Digital, Optical |
| Gripper force | FSR / Strain gauge | Passive, Analog, Resistive |
| Balance / IMU | MPU6050 | Active, Digital, MEMS |
| Outdoor nav | GPS NEO-6M | Passive, Digital, RF |
| Battery monitoring | Thermistor NTC | Passive, Analog, Thermal |

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When a 14-mark question says *"classify the given sensor"*, list at least **5 classification tags** (active/passive, analog/digital, measurand, transduction, contact type) with one example each. Always close with a real-world robotic application. This structure is what examiners are trained to reward.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robotic Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT205 - SENSOR TECHNOLOGY FOR ROBOTICS (ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Robotic Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Robotic Sensor (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *robotic sensor* is an electronic, electromechanical, or electro-optical transducer device that converts a physical, chemical, or biological stimulus from the robot's internal state (proprioceptive) or external environment (exteroceptive) into a measurable, processable electrical signal (analog or digital) for the robot's perception, decision, and control subsystems.

In KTU terminology, sensors are the **perceptual organs** of a robot. Without sensors, a robot is essentially a "blind" mechanical structure performing only pre-programmed open-loop motion. Sensors close the loop between the **physical world** and the **controller**, enabling intelligent behaviour, autonomy, and adaptive control.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Robot as a Human Body

Imagine a human walking across a dark room:
- **Eyes** → cameras, LiDAR (vision sensors)
- **Skin** → tactile and force sensors
- **Ears** → microphones, ultrasonic sensors
- **Inner ear (vestibular system)** → IMU, gyroscope (balance/orientation)
- **Muscle spindles / joints** → encoders, resolvers (proprioception — knowing where your arm is)
- **Nose / tongue** → chemical / gas sensors

> [!NOTE]
> **Proprioceptive vs. Exteroceptive — A Critical Distinction:**
> - **Proprioceptive sensors** measure the robot's *internal* state — joint angles, motor speeds, wheel rotations, battery voltage, current, torque at joints. (Example: optical encoder on a servo motor).
> - **Exteroceptive sensors** measure the *external* environment — distance to obstacles, light intensity, temperature, object recognition, force from a grasped object. (Example: ultrasonic range finder).
> KTU board questions frequently test this classification — remember it as **"PROPRIO = inside, EXTERO = outside."**

### 1.3 Why Robotic Sensors Are Special

Unlike industrial sensors used in fixed installations, robotic sensors must satisfy demanding constraints:

| Constraint | Typical Value / Description |
|---|---|
| **Size & Weight** | Compact, lightweight (avoid affecting dynamics) |
| **Bandwidth** | High (robots move fast — typically **> 1 kHz** for joint control) |
| **Resolution** | Fine (sub-millimeter for precision tasks) |
| **Ruggedness** | Shock, vibration, EMI resistant |
| **Real-time output** | Low latency for closed-loop control |
| **Power consumption** | Low (especially for mobile robots) |

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** KTU Module 2 emphasizes the *classification*, *operating principles*, and *selection criteria* of sensors used in robotic arms, mobile robots, and humanoid platforms. Focus on **optical encoders, resolvers, LVDTs, ultrasonic, infrared, vision, force/torque, IMUs, and tactile sensors** — these appear repeatedly in past papers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Master Classification of Robotic Sensors

The KTU syllabus expects students to fluently classify sensors along multiple axes. Below is the structured hierarchy you must memorize.

**A) Based on Signal Output Type**
- **Analog sensors** → continuous voltage/current output (e.g., potentiometer, thermistor, LVDT)
- **Digital sensors** → discrete pulses/binary codes (e.g., optical encoder, Hall-effect switch)
- **Pseudo-digital / PWM sensors** → modulated digital (e.g., some IMU modules)

**B) Based on Energy / Power Requirement**
- **Active sensors** → need external excitation (e.g., strain gauge, LVDT, RTD)
- **Passive sensors** → self-generating (e.g., piezoelectric, thermocouple, solar cell)

**C) Based on Contact**
- **Contact sensors** → require physical touch (e.g., limit switch, tactile sensor)
- **Non-contact sensors** → sense without touching (e.g., IR, ultrasonic, capacitive proximity)

**D) Based on Measurement Direction (MOST IMPORTANT FOR ROBOTICS)**
- **Proprioceptive** — internal state
- **Exteroceptive** — external world

### 2.2 Operating Principles of Major Robotic Sensors

#### 2.2.1 Optical Encoders (The Workhorse of Joint Position Feedback)

- A **disk** with alternating transparent and opaque slots rotates between an **LED** and a **photodetector**.
- Rotation generates **N pulses per revolution** (N = number of slots/lines).
- **Incremental encoder** → outputs two square waves **A and B** in **quadrature** (90° out of phase). Direction is decoded from the leading channel.
- **Absolute encoder** → uses a unique Gray code pattern; every shaft position has a unique binary word — survives power loss.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quadrature Decoding Multiplies Resolution by 4.** If an encoder has N lines, using both edges of A and B gives **4N counts per revolution** — the controller's *effective* resolution. This is a *favourite* KTU short-answer question.

#### 2.2.2 Resolvers

- A **rotating transformer** with primary on rotor, two secondaries on stator (placed 90° apart).
- Output voltages are sinusoidally modulated: V₁ = V₀ sin(ωt) sin(θ), V₂ = V₀ sin(ωt) cos(θ).
- Demodulation gives **θ = arctan(V₁/V₂)**.
- Used in *harsh* environments (high temperature, vibration, EMI) — common in aerospace and military robotics.

#### 2.2.3 LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)

- Three-coil transformer: **primary P**, two secondaries **S1 and S2** connected in series opposition.
- A movable **ferromagnetic core** shifts the magnetic coupling.
- Output **V_out = V_S1 − V_S2** is linear with displacement over a specified range.
- Extremely robust, infinite resolution, frictionless — used for linear joint position in hydraulic robots.

#### 2.2.4 Ultrasonic Range Sensor

- Emits a **~40 kHz** burst from a piezoelectric transducer.
- Measures time-of-flight **t** of the echo.
- Distance: **d = (v × t) / 2** (factor of 2 — sound travels to object and back).
- Speed of sound in air at 20°C: **v ≈ 343 m/s**.
- Affected by temperature: v(T) = 331.4 + 0.6 × T (m/s).

#### 2.2.5 Infrared (IR) Proximity Sensor

- Emits IR light; reflected light intensity is measured by a photodiode.
- Uses **triangulation** in sharp sensors: angle of reflected ray → distance.
- Short range (cm to few m), fast, cheap — ideal for obstacle avoidance in mobile robots.

#### 2.2.6 Force / Torque Sensor (Strain-Gauge Based)

- A machined **elastomer body** with strategically placed **strain gauges** (Wheatstone bridge configuration).
- Strain **ε = ΔL/L**; resistance change **ΔR/R = G_f × ε**, where **G_f = gauge factor** (typically ~2 for metal foils).
- Six-axis force/torque sensors measure **F_x, F_y, F_z, M_x, M_y, M_z** simultaneously.
- Critical for **compliant control, assembly, and grasping**.

#### 2.2.7 Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)

- Combines **3-axis accelerometer** (measures linear acceleration 'a' including gravity) and **3-axis gyroscope** (measures angular velocity 'ω').
- Often includes a **3-axis magnetometer** (compass heading).
- Used in drones, humanoid balance, SLAM.

#### 2.2.8 Tactile / Skin Sensors

- Arrays of pressure-sensitive elements (capacitive, piezoresistive, optical, MEMS barometers).
- Provide **contact localization, pressure distribution, slip detection**.
- Mimic human skin — used in prosthetics and dexterous robot hands.

#### 2.2.9 Vision Sensors (Cameras)

- **Monocular / Stereo / RGB-D** cameras.
- Stereo vision: depth = (f × B) / d, where **f = focal length, B = baseline, d = disparity**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Sensor / Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Encoder angular resolution | θ_res = 360° / N | N = number of lines per revolution |
| 2 | Quadrature-decoded resolution | θ_res_q = 360° / (4N) | 4 edges per line (A↑, A↓, B↑, B↓) |
| 3 | Encoder measured position | θ = (N_count / N_total) × 360° | N_count = pulses counted |
| 4 | Encoder angular velocity | ω = Δθ / Δt | rad/s or deg/s |
| 5 | Resolver demodulated angle | θ = arctan(V_1 / V_2) | V_1, V_2 = secondary voltages |
| 6 | LVDT displacement | x = k × V_out | k = calibration constant (m/V) |
| 7 | Ultrasonic distance | d = (v × t) / 2 | v = speed of sound (m/s), t = echo time (s) |
| 8 | Speed of sound in air | v = 331.4 + 0.6 T | T in °C, v in m/s |
| 9 | Strain gauge relation | ΔR / R = G_f × ε | G_f = gauge factor, ε = strain |
| 10 | Bridge output voltage | V_out = V_ex × (ΔR / R) / 4 | For full-bridge, all gauges active |
| 11 | Accelerometer output | V_out = V_0 + S × a | S = sensitivity (V/g), a in g or m/s² |
| 12 | Gyroscope output rate | ω = V_out / S | S = sensitivity (V/(°/s) or V/(rad/s)) |
| 13 | Force from F/T sensor | F = (V_out − V_0) / S_F | S_F = force sensitivity (V/N) |
| 14 | Stereo depth (vision) | Z = (f × B) / d | f = focal length, B = baseline, d = disparity |
| 15 | Camera field of view | FOV = 2 arctan(d_sensor / (2 f)) | d_sensor = sensor dimension |

> [!NOTE]
> **Unit conversion constant (always memorize):** 1 g = 9.81 m/s². Accelerometer datasheets quote sensitivity in V/g, V/(m/s²), or mV/g — **convert before plugging into formulas** in KTU numericals.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial robotic arms** (e.g., KUKA, FANUC, ABB) use **absolute optical encoders + resolvers** for precise joint control.
- **Mobile robots (AGVs, AMRs)** use **LiDAR + wheel encoders + IMU** for SLAM and navigation.
- **Surgical robots (Da Vinci)** use **high-resolution optical encoders + force/torque sensors** for haptic feedback.
- **Humanoid robots (ASIMO, Atlas, Optimus)** rely on **IMUs + force sensors in feet + joint encoders** for dynamic balancing.
- **Drones** use **IMU + barometer + GPS + optical flow** for stable flight.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below are *full*, step-by-step derivations of every formula in the cheat sheet. KTU exam valuation rewards showing every algebraic step — you get *method marks* even if the final number is wrong.

### 3.1 Derivation: Optical Encoder Resolution and Quadrature Decoding

**Given:** An incremental optical encoder disk has **N = 500** transparent slots (lines) per revolution. Determine (i) the basic angular resolution, (ii) the quadrature-decoded resolution, (iii) the minimum angular velocity detectable if the controller can sample at 1 kHz.

**Step 1 — Basic resolution (one count per slot):**

$$
\theta_{\text{res}} \;=\; \frac{360^{\circ}}{N}
$$

$$
\theta_{\text{res}} \;=\; \frac{360^{\circ}}{500} \;=\; 0.72^{\circ}/\text{count}
$$

**Step 2 — Quadrature decoding:**

In quadrature, both channels A and B produce square waves 90° out of phase. The four state transitions per line are: rising edge of A, rising edge of B, falling edge of A, falling edge of B — that's **4 counts per line**.

$$
\theta_{\text{res,q}} \;=\; \frac{360^{\circ}}{4 N}
$$

$$
\theta_{\text{res,q}} \;=\; \frac{360^{\circ}}{4 \times 500} \;=\; \frac{360^{\circ}}{2000} \;=\; 0.18^{\circ}/\text{count}
$$

**Step 3 — Minimum detectable angular velocity:**

At 1 kHz sampling, the controller sees 1000 counts per second. Converting to counts per minute × counts per revolution → revolutions per minute:

$$
N_{\text{cpm}} \;=\; 1000 \;\text{counts/s} \;\times\; 60 \;\text{s/min} \;=\; 60{,}000 \;\text{counts/min}
$$

$$
\text{RPM}_{\min} \;=\; \frac{60{,}000}{4 \times 500} \;=\; \frac{60{,}000}{2000} \;=\; 30 \;\text{RPM}
$$

$$
\omega_{\min} \;=\; 30 \;\text{RPM} \;\times\; \frac{2\pi}{60} \;\approx\; 3.14 \;\text{rad/s}
$$

**Logical interpretation:** Each sample, the controller either detects zero counts or one count. If it sees one count, the shaft has rotated at least 0.18° during the 1 ms window. Anything slower produces no change → cannot be detected. Hence the minimum detectable speed is **30 RPM**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Ultrasonic Distance from Time of Flight

**Given:** A robot's ultrasonic sensor sends a 40 kHz pulse. The echo is received **t = 8.5 ms** later. Ambient temperature **T = 25 °C**. Find the distance to the obstacle.

**Step 1 — Compute the speed of sound at T = 25 °C:**

$$
v \;=\; 331.4 \;+\; 0.6\,T
$$

$$
v \;=\; 331.4 \;+\; 0.6 \times 25 \;=\; 331.4 \;+\; 15.0 \;=\; 346.4 \;\text{m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the time-of-flight equation:**

The pulse travels *to* the obstacle *and back*, so the total path is **2d**. Hence:

$$
2d \;=\; v \times t
$$

$$
d \;=\; \frac{v \times t}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$
d \;=\; \frac{346.4 \;\text{m/s} \;\times\; 8.5 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{s}}{2}
$$

$$
d \;=\; \frac{346.4 \times 0.0085}{2} \;=\; \frac{2.9444}{2} \;=\; 1.4722 \;\text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{d \;\approx\; 1.47 \;\text{m}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget the factor of 2 (round-trip) and get **d = v × t ≈ 2.94 m**, which is **double the correct answer**. Examiners *specifically* test this — always write the factor-of-2 step explicitly.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Strain-Gauge Force Sensor Output

**Given:** A load cell uses four active strain gauges (full Wheatstone bridge) with gauge factor **G_f = 2.05**, strain **ε = 800 microstrain**, and excitation **V_ex = 5 V**. Find the bridge output voltage and the corresponding force if the cell sensitivity is **S_F = 2 mV/N**.

**Step 1 — Bridge output voltage formula (full bridge, all gauges active):**

$$
V_{\text{out}} \;=\; \frac{V_{\text{ex}}}{4} \;\times\; \frac{\Delta R}{R}
$$

**Step 2 — Relate ΔR/R to strain:**

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} \;=\; G_f \times \varepsilon
$$

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} \;=\; 2.05 \times 800 \times 10^{-6} \;=\; 1.64 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute V_out:**

$$
V_{\text{out}} \;=\; \frac{5}{4} \times 1.64 \times 10^{-3} \;=\; 1.25 \times 1.64 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
V_{\text{out}} \;=\; 2.05 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{V} \;=\; 2.05 \;\text{mV}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert voltage to force:**

$$
F \;=\; \frac{V_{\text{out}}}{S_F} \;=\; \frac{2.05 \;\text{mV}}{2 \;\text{mV/N}} \;=\; 1.025 \;\text{N}
$$

$$
\boxed{F \;\approx\; 1.025 \;\text{N}}
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Accelerometer Tilt and Gravity Component

**Given:** A 3-axis MEMS accelerometer is mounted on a robot. The output readings (in g) are: **a_x = 0.173, a_y = 0, a_z = 0.985**. Find the pitch angle (rotation about y-axis).

**Step 1 — Recall accelerometer model:**

When at rest, the accelerometer measures the projection of gravity **g** on each axis. The tilt angle is found by:

$$
\theta_{\text{pitch}} \;=\; \arctan\!\left(\frac{a_x}{a_z}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
\theta_{\text{pitch}} \;=\; \arctan\!\left(\frac{0.173}{0.985}\right) \;=\; \arctan(0.1756)
$$

$$
\theta_{\text{pitch}} \;=\; 9.97^{\circ} \;\approx\; 10^{\circ}
$$

**Step 3 — Sanity check using vector magnitude:**

$$
\sqrt{a_x^{2} + a_y^{2} + a_z^{2}} \;=\; \sqrt{0.173^{2} + 0^{2} + 0.985^{2}} \;=\; \sqrt{0.0299 + 0.9702} \;=\; \sqrt{1.0001} \;\approx\; 1
$$

The magnitude is **≈ 1 g** ✓ — the sensor is at rest, only gravity is measured. Valid result.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Sensor

| Application | Recommended Sensor | Why (Key Property) |
|---|---|---|
| Joint angle of robot arm | **Absolute optical encoder** | No power-loss error, fine resolution |
| Wheel odometry of mobile robot | **Incremental encoder + quadrature** | Cheap, high resolution, simple |
| Tool center point force in assembly | **6-axis F/T sensor** | Measures all 3 forces + 3 moments |
| Obstacle avoidance (mobile robot) | **Ultrasonic / IR** | Low cost, simple interfacing |
| SLAM / mapping | **LiDAR + RGB-D camera** | High angular resolution, 360° scan |
| Humanoid balance | **IMU (acc + gyro + mag)** | Real-time orientation, high bandwidth |
| Tactile feedback in gripper | **Tactile sensor array** | Pressure distribution, slip detection |
| Harsh environment joint sensing | **Resolver** | Rugged, EMI immune, wide temp range |
| Hydraulic cylinder position | **LVDT** | Frictionless, infinite resolution, linear output |
| Color/object recognition | **RGB camera + vision algo** | Rich information, non-contact |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification of Robotic Sensors (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robotic Sensors]:::root --> B[Proprioceptive]:::branch
    A --> C[Exteroceptive]:::branch

    B --> B1[Position Encoders]:::leaf
    B --> B2[Velocity Tachometers]:::leaf
    B --> B3[Current Sensors]:::leaf
    B --> B4[Joint Torque Sensors]:::leaf
    B --> B5[IMU Gyroscope]:::leaf

    C --> C1[Ultrasonic Range]:::leaf
    C --> C2[IR Proximity]:::leaf
    C --> C3[Capacitive Proximity]:::leaf
    C --> C4[Vision Camera]:::leaf
    C --> C5[LiDAR]:::leaf
    C --> C6[Force Torque Sensor]:::leaf
    C --> C7[Tactile Skin]:::leaf
    C --> C8[Temperature Humidity]:::leaf

    B1 --> D1[Optical Incremental]:::sub
    B1 --> D2[Optical Absolute Gray Code]:::sub
    B1 --> D3[Magnetic Encoder]:::sub
    B1 --> D4[Resolver]:::sub
    B1 --> D5[LVDT Linear]:::sub

    C4 --> E1[Monocular]:::sub
    C4 --> E2[Stereo Pair]:::sub
    C4 --> E3[RGB-D Depth]:::sub
    C4 --> E4[Event Camera]:::sub

    classDef root fill:#1f2937,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef branch fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0c4a6e,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef leaf fill:#10b981,stroke:#064e3b,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    classDef sub fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#7c2d12,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
```

> **How to read this diagram:** Start at the root `Robotic Sensors`, branch into the *proprioceptive / exteroceptive* split (top half — internal state; bottom half — external world), then drill into the specific sensor families and their sub-types. **Memorize the two top-level branches first**; examiners love asking "classify the following sensors…".

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Robotic Sensor System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PHY[Physical Stimulus]:::input --> SENS[Sensing Element]:::core
    SENS --> TRX[Signal Transduction]:::core
    TRX --> SIG[Signal Conditioning Amplification Filtering]:::core
    SIG --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter]:::core
    ADC --> MCU[Microcontroller Processor]:::cpu
    MCU --> CTRL[Robot Controller Decision]:::cpu
    CTRL --> ACT[Actuator Motor Driver]:::output
    ACT --> ROB[Robot Motion Action]:::output
    ROB -.->|Feedback| PHY

    MCU --> FBK[Sensor Fusion Filter]:::cpu
    FBK --> CTRL

    classDef input fill:#6366f1,stroke:#1e1b4b,color:#ffffff
    classDef core fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0c4a6e,color:#ffffff
    classDef cpu fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#000000
    classDef output fill:#10b981,stroke:#064e3b,color:#ffffff
```

> **How to read this diagram:** Trace the signal flow left-to-right. *Physical Stimulus → Sensing Element → Transduction → Conditioning → ADC → Processor → Controller → Actuator → Robot Motion → Feedback Loop*. The dashed return arrow represents the **closed-loop control** fundamental to robotics. The *Sensor Fusion* block (orange) indicates that multiple sensor outputs are mathematically combined (e.g., Kalman filter) before reaching the controller.

### 4.3 Optical Incremental Encoder — Quadrature Signal Generation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DISC[Encoder Disk With N Slots]:::hw --> LED[IR LED Light Source]:::hw
    LED --> PD[Photodetector A and B]:::hw
    PD --> Q1[Quadrature Channel A Square Wave]:::sig
    PD --> Q2[Quadrature Channel B Square Wave 90 deg Phase Shift]:::sig
    Q1 --> DEC[Quadrature Decoder Up Down Counter]:::cpu
    Q2 --> DEC
    DEC --> POS[Joint Position Angle theta]:::out
    DEC --> VEL[Joint Velocity omega]:::out

    classDef hw fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0c4a6e,color:#ffffff
    classDef sig fill:#10b981,stroke:#064e3b,color:#ffffff
    classDef cpu fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#000000
    classDef out fill:#a855f7,stroke:#581c87,color:#ffffff
```

> **How to read this diagram:** The slotted disk physically modulates light; two photodetectors (offset so that one channel lags the other by 90°) generate quadrature square waves; the decoder counts edges and infers direction; outputs are **angle θ and angular velocity ω**. This is the *standard* way KTU examiners illustrate an encoder.

### 4.4 Ultrasonic Sensor — Time-of-Flight Ranging

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TX[Microcontroller Triggers 40 kHz Burst]:::ctrl --> PZT_TX[Piezoelectric Emitter Sends Ultrasound]:::hw
    PZT_TX --> AIR[Sound Wave Travels Through Air At v = 343 m s]:::env
    AIR --> OBJ[Hits Obstacle And Reflects]:::env
    OBJ --> PZT_RX[Echo Returns To Piezoelectric Receiver]:::hw
    PZT_RX --> AMP[Amplifier And Bandpass Filter]:::proc
    AMP --> DET[Echo Detector Measures Time t]:::proc
    DET --> CALC[Compute Distance d = v t divided by 2]:::ctrl
    CALC --> OUT[Distance Value To Robot Controller]:::out

    classDef ctrl fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#000000
    classDef hw fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0c4a6e,color:#ffffff
    classDef env fill:#22c55e,stroke:#14532d,color:#ffffff
    classDef proc fill:#6366f1,stroke:#1e1b4b,color:#ffffff
    classDef out fill:#a855f7,stroke:#581c87,color:#ffffff
```

> **How to read this diagram:** This is the **end-to-end pipeline** of a single ultrasonic ranging cycle. The *env* blocks (green) represent the physical medium (air + obstacle). The factor-of-2 division in the *CALC* block is the round-trip correction — the same factor you must apply in the derivation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The questions below follow the **exact KTU 2024 Scheme pattern**:
> - Part A: 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks (short answer).
> - Part B: 1 choice (A or B) × 14 marks (with sub-parts of 7 + 7).
> - All questions are tagged with the simulated KTU Exam paper.

---

### Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper 1]**
**Differentiate between proprioceptive and exteroceptive sensors used in robotics. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

| Aspect | Proprioceptive Sensors | Exteroceptive Sensors |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Measure **internal** state of the robot (joint angle, wheel speed, battery) | Measure **external** environment (distance, light, force from object) |
| Sense analogue | Human muscle / joint sense | Human eye / ear / skin |
| Examples (1) | **Optical encoder** on a joint shaft | **Ultrasonic sensor** for obstacle distance |
| Examples (2) | **Tachogenerator** on a motor | **Force/Torque sensor** at the wrist |

*(Award full 3 marks if the student gives a clear distinction with two examples of each. One mark for definition, two marks for examples.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 1]**
**An incremental optical encoder has 1024 lines. Calculate (i) its basic angular resolution, and (ii) its quadrature-decoded resolution.**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

**Step 1 — Basic resolution:**

$$
\theta_{\text{res}} \;=\; \frac{360^{\circ}}{N} \;=\; \frac{360^{\circ}}{1024} \;\approx\; 0.3516^{\circ}
$$

**Step 2 — Quadrature resolution (4× improvement):**

$$
\theta_{\text{res,q}} \;=\; \frac{360^{\circ}}{4 \times 1024} \;=\; \frac{360^{\circ}}{4096} \;\approx\; 0.0879^{\circ}
$$

**Result:** Basic = **0.3516°**, Quadrature = **0.0879°** (≈ 3.1× improvement over basic — wait, actually 4× improvement in count count → 1/4 the angle).

*(Award 1.5 marks for the basic formula & result, 1.5 marks for the quadrature extension.)*

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice: Answer EITHER A OR B)

---

#### **Question A (14 marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat labelled block diagram, explain the working of an **optical incremental encoder** with quadrature decoding. Derive the formula for the basic angular resolution and the quadrature-decoded resolution. State one advantage and one disadvantage of using a resolver instead of an optical encoder.

**(b) [7 marks]** An ultrasonic range sensor on a mobile robot emits a 40 kHz pulse and receives the echo after **t = 6.2 ms**. The ambient temperature is **T = 30 °C**.
- (i) Calculate the speed of sound in air at 30 °C.
- (ii) Compute the distance to the obstacle.
- (iii) If the maximum measurable range of the sensor is 5 m, what is the maximum time the receiver must wait for an echo? Justify why ultrasonic sensors are unsuitable for very close-range sensing (< 10 cm).

---

**Model Solution — Part A(a):**

**Working (4 marks):**

1. The **encoder disk** has alternating transparent and opaque slots (N lines/rev).
2. An **IR LED** is on one side; two **photodetectors** (A and B) are on the other side, aligned to slots such that the B-channel signal is **90° electrical** out of phase with A.
3. As the disk rotates, the photodetectors generate two square waves in **quadrature**.
4. A **decoder circuit (up/down counter)** counts the edges:
   - 4 edges per line → resolution multiplied by 4.
   - Lead/lag relationship between A and B determines the **direction of rotation**.
5. The counter output is converted to **angle θ** by θ = (N_count / 4N) × 360°.

**Formulas (2 marks):**

$$
\theta_{\text{res}} \;=\; \frac{360^{\circ}}{N} \qquad\qquad \theta_{\text{res,q}} \;=\; \frac{360^{\circ}}{4N}
$$

**Resolver vs Optical Encoder (1 mark):**

- **Advantage of resolver:** rugged, works in harsh environments (high temp, vibration, EMI) — used in aerospace and military.
- **Disadvantage of resolver:** requires **complex demodulation circuitry** (RDC — Resolver-to-Digital Converter) and is more expensive than a simple optical encoder.

---

**Model Solution — Part A(b):**

**(i) Speed of sound at T = 30 °C:**

$$
v \;=\; 331.4 \;+\; 0.6\,T \;=\; 331.4 \;+\; 0.6 \times 30 \;=\; 331.4 \;+\; 18.0 \;=\; 349.4 \;\text{m/s}
$$

**(ii) Distance to obstacle:**

$$
d \;=\; \frac{v \times t}{2} \;=\; \frac{349.4 \times 6.2 \times 10^{-3}}{2} \;=\; \frac{2.1663}{2} \;=\; 1.083 \;\text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{d \;\approx\; 1.08 \;\text{m}}
$$

**(iii) Maximum wait time for 5 m range:**

$$
t_{\max} \;=\; \frac{2 d_{\max}}{v} \;=\; \frac{2 \times 5}{349.4} \;=\; \frac{10}{349.4} \;\approx\; 28.6 \;\text{ms}
$$

**Why unsuitable for < 10 cm:**

For very short distances, the round-trip time **t < 1 ms** is smaller than the **emission decay time** of the piezo transducer. The receiver cannot distinguish the transmitted pulse's "ring-down" from the immediate echo, producing **blind zones** typically 10–30 cm wide. This is a known KTU board point.

---

#### **Question B (14 marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **classification of robotic sensors** based on (i) signal output type, (ii) energy requirement, and (iii) measurement direction. For each category, give **two examples**.

**(b) [7 marks]** A 4-active-arm **Wheatstone-bridge strain gauge** load cell has gauge factor **G_f = 2.1**, excitation **V_ex = 10 V**, and is subjected to a strain **ε = 500 microstrain**. The load-cell sensitivity is **S_F = 1.5 mV/V/V** (output referred to excitation and applied force), and the cell is rated for **100 N** at full scale.
- (i) Calculate the bridge output voltage under the applied strain.
- (ii) Compute the corresponding force, assuming linear operation.
- (iii) If the cell is used in a robot gripper and the desired force resolution is **0.1 N**, what is the minimum ADC resolution (in bits) required, given a 0–10 V ADC range?

---

**Model Solution — Part B(a):**

**Classification (3 categories × 2 marks each = 6 marks; 1 mark for examples summary):**

**(i) Signal Output Type:**
- **Analog sensors** — continuous output voltage/current proportional to measurand.
  *Examples: Potentiometer, LVDT, thermistor, strain gauge bridge.*
- **Digital sensors** — output in discrete pulses or binary codes.
  *Examples: Optical encoder, Hall-effect switch, digital temperature sensor (DS18B20).*

**(ii) Energy Requirement:**
- **Active sensors** — require external excitation (power/light).
  *Examples: Strain gauge, LVDT, RTD, photoresistor (LDR).*
- **Passive (self-generating) sensors** — generate their own electrical output from the measurand.
  *Examples: Piezoelectric accelerometer, thermocouple, solar cell, dynamo tachometer.*

**(iii) Measurement Direction:**
- **Proprioceptive** — internal state of robot.
  *Examples: Joint encoder, motor current sensor, IMU gyroscope.*
- **Exteroceptive** — external environment.
  *Examples: Camera, ultrasonic range sensor, force/torque sensor.*

---

**Model Solution — Part B(b):**

**(i) Bridge output voltage:**

Step 1 — Compute ΔR/R:

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} \;=\; G_f \times \varepsilon \;=\; 2.1 \times 500 \times 10^{-6} \;=\; 1.05 \times 10^{-3}
$$

Step 2 — Apply full-bridge formula:

$$
V_{\text{out}} \;=\; \frac{V_{\text{ex}}}{4} \times \frac{\Delta R}{R} \;=\; \frac{10}{4} \times 1.05 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
V_{\text{out}} \;=\; 2.5 \times 1.05 \times 10^{-3} \;=\; 2.625 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{V} \;=\; 2.625 \;\text{mV}
$$

**(ii) Force corresponding to V_out:**

The cell produces 2.5 mV/V at full scale of 100 N (sensitivity 1.5 mV/V/V at 10 V → 15 mV full scale). The full-scale output is:

$$
V_{\text{FS}} \;=\; S_F \times V_{\text{ex}} \;=\; 1.5\;\text{mV/V/V} \times 10\;\text{V} \;=\; 15\;\text{mV}
$$

Linear force:

$$
F \;=\; \frac{V_{\text{out}}}{V_{\text{FS}}} \times F_{\text{FS}} \;=\; \frac{2.625\;\text{mV}}{15\;\text{mV}} \times 100\;\text{N} \;=\; 17.5\;\text{N}
$$

$$
\boxed{F \;=\; 17.5 \;\text{N}}
$$

**(iii) Minimum ADC resolution for 0.1 N force resolution:**

Step 1 — Voltage equivalent of 0.1 N:

$$
V_{\text{step}} \;=\; \frac{0.1\;\text{N}}{100\;\text{N}} \times 15\;\text{mV} \;=\; 0.015\;\text{mV} \;=\; 15\;\mu\text{V}
$$

Step 2 — Number of ADC levels required to cover 0–15 mV with 15 µV steps:

$$
L \;=\; \frac{V_{\text{FS}}}{V_{\text{step}}} \;=\; \frac{15\;\text{mV}}{15\;\mu\text{V}} \;=\; 1000 \;\text{levels}
$$

Step 3 — Bits required:

$$
2^{n} \;\geq\; 1000
$$

$$
n \;\geq\; \log_{2}(1000) \;\approx\; 9.97 \;\Rightarrow\; n \;=\; 10 \;\text{bits minimum}
$$

$$
\boxed{n_{\min} \;=\; 10 \;\text{bits}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgot factor of 2 in ultrasonic distance** → double the actual answer. (-2 to -3 marks!)
> 2. **Confusing °C and K in speed-of-sound formula** → numerical answer wildly off. (-1 mark)
> 3. **Wrong bridge multiplier** (using V_ex/2 instead of V_ex/4 for full-bridge) → incorrect V_out. (-2 marks)
> 4. **Mixing proprioceptive and exteroceptive classifications** → partial credit only. (-1 to -2 marks)
> 5. **No units in final answer** → examiners *will* deduct ½ mark per answer in numericals. **Always write units.**
> 6. **Skipping the formula substitution step** → students who jump from formula to answer lose the *method marks* (up to 50% of the question value).
> 7. **Not justifying "why" in conceptual sub-parts** → KTU valuation explicitly awards marks for *reasoning*, not just definitions.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this checklist and revise 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- ☐ **Proprioceptive = internal** (joint encoders, tachogenerators, current sensors, IMU gyro). **Exteroceptive = external** (cameras, LiDAR, ultrasonic, force/torque, tactile).
- ☐ **Optical encoder resolution:** θ_res = 360°/N. **Quadrature** = 360°/(4N) — *4× improvement*.
- ☐ **Resolver:** rugged transformer; θ = arctan(V₁/V₂); needs RDC chip.
- ☐ **LVDT:** linear, frictionless, infinite resolution; output linear with core displacement.
- ☐ **Ultrasonic distance:** **d = v × t / 2** — never forget the factor of 2!
- ☐ **Speed of sound:** v = 331.4 + 0.6T (m/s, T in °C). Standard: 343 m/s at 20 °C.
- ☐ **Strain gauge:** ΔR/R = G_f × ε. G_f ≈ 2 for metal foil.
- ☐ **Wheatstone bridge (full):** V_out = (V_ex/4) × (ΔR/R). Half-bridge: V_ex/2. Quarter-bridge: V_ex/4 with only one active gauge.
- ☐ **Accelerometer:** V_out = V_0 + S × a. At rest, magnitude = **1 g** (gravity).
- ☐ **Gyroscope:** measures *angular velocity* ω. Integrate to get angle.
- ☐ **IMU = accelerometer + gyroscope (+ magnetometer)** — used for orientation/balance.
- ☐ **Stereo vision depth:** Z = (f × B) / d. Larger baseline → larger depth range.
- ☐ **Tactile sensors** mimic human skin — pressure distribution, slip detection.
- ☐ **6-axis F/T sensor** measures F_x, F_y, F_z, M_x, M_y, M_z — used in assembly, haptic feedback.
- ☐ **Sensor fusion** (Kalman / complementary filter) combines IMU + encoder + vision for robust state estimation.
- ☐ Always state **units** in numerical answers; always show **formula substitution**; always **justify** conceptual answers.

> **End of Notes — Module 2: Robotic Sensors**
> *Prepared in accordance with the KTU 2024 Scheme examination pattern, NEP 2020 outcome-based pedagogy, and revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Levels: Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze).*
