topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT205 - SENSOR TECHNOLOGY FOR ROBOTICS (ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Proximity Sensor: Eddy Current Proximity Sensor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Proximity Sensor: Eddy Current Proximity Sensor

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] Core Definition
> An **Eddy Current Proximity Sensor** is a non-contact, inductive displacement transducer that detects the presence, position, or distance of a metallic target by exploiting the **Faraday–Lenz principle of electromagnetic induction**. A high-frequency alternating current (typically **100 kHz to 2 MHz**) is driven through an excitation coil. When a conducting target enters the coil's alternating magnetic field, **eddy currents** are induced in the target. These currents generate a secondary, opposing magnetic field (Lenz's Law) which loads the primary coil, producing a measurable change in its **electrical impedance** ($Z = R + j\omega L$) that is monotonically related to the **air-gap distance** $d$ between the coil face and the target surface.

The sensor is a *passive, non-contact, position-to-impedance converter* and belongs to the family of **inductive displacement transducers** (alongside the LVDT), but unlike the LVDT, it uses a single coil and does not require a ferromagnetic core to be moved mechanically — instead, the *target itself* acts as the energy-absorbing element.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Whirlpool in a Pond"

> [!NOTE] Intuition Box
> Imagine you (the coil) are rhythmically dipping your hand into a still pond at high frequency — you create ripples (the primary alternating magnetic field). Now imagine a floating cork (the conductive metal target) is placed on the pond. The cork starts spinning — but the *direction* of its spin is opposite to your hand motion (Lenz's Law). The spinning cork creates *its own* ripples that move *against* yours. As the cork gets **closer** to your hand, its opposing ripples grow stronger, making it **harder** for you to dip your hand into the water. This "harder to move" sensation is exactly the increase in **effective impedance** that the coil feels. The coil doesn't *touch* the target — it just senses how strongly the target fights back. That fight-back is your measurement signal.

### 1.3 Salient Physical & Performance Metrics

| Performance Parameter | Typical Engineering Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Excitation Frequency $f$ | **100 kHz – 2 MHz** | Hz |
| Skin Depth $\delta$ (at 1 MHz in aluminum) | **≈ 84** | µm |
| Standard Sensing Range $d$ | **0.1 – 10** | mm |
| Target Material Requirement | Electrically conducting (preferred: **non-ferrous** like Al, Cu, Brass) | — |
| Output Type | Analog voltage/current or switched (threshold) | V or mA |
| Linearity Range | ≈ **60–70 %** of total range | % |
| Resolution | Sub-micron (< 1 µm achievable) | µm |
| Operating Temperature | **–40 °C to +200 °C** (sensor head) | °C |

> [!IMPORTANT] Why "Non-Ferrous" Preference?
> Although eddy current sensors work on **any** conductor, they are most sensitive to **non-ferrous metals** (aluminum, copper, brass, titanium) because ferrous materials also modulate the magnetic permeability $\mu$ — a competing, non-linear effect that **distorts** the clean impedance-vs-distance relationship. Industrial eddy-current probes are therefore the *de facto* standard for non-ferrous crack detection and non-ferrous position measurement.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Six Structured Logic Steps

> [!NOTE] The "Why" Behind Each Step
> Understanding *why* each step happens — not just *what* happens — is the difference between rote memorization and KTU board-level mastery.

1. **Excitation — The Oscillator Stage**
   A built-in **high-frequency oscillator** (often a Colpitts or Wien-bridge topology with frequency $f \approx 1\,\text{MHz}$) drives a sinusoidal current $i(t) = I_0 \sin(\omega t)$ through the sensing coil.
   *Why?* High frequency is essential because (a) it keeps the sensor compact (small coil), (b) it confines the field to a shallow skin depth $\delta$, and (c) it maximizes induced emf $\mathcal{E} = -N \, d\Phi/dt$.

2. **Primary Magnetic Field Generation**
   The alternating current produces an alternating magnetic flux $\Phi(t) = \Phi_0 \sin(\omega t)$ that extends outward from the coil face as an open-ended "fringing" field.
   *Why?* A flat, pancake-style coil produces a field that is strongest along its axis and decays rapidly with distance — ideal for localized proximity sensing.

3. **Induction of Eddy Currents in the Target**
   By Faraday's Law, the time-varying flux $\Phi(t)$ linking the target induces circulating currents — **eddy currents** — within the target's conducting skin layer.
   *Why?* These eddy currents form closed loops in planes *perpendicular* to the magnetic field lines (like the eddy in a river flowing past a rock). Their magnitude scales with **target conductivity $\sigma$**, **permeability $\mu$**, and **frequency $\omega$**.

4. **Reaction Field — Lenz's Law Opposition**
   The eddy currents themselves generate their own secondary magnetic field $\Phi_{\text{ec}}$ that is **180° out of phase** with $\Phi_0$.
   *Why?* Lenz's Law conserves energy: the secondary field opposes whatever change produced it. This opposition is what the coil actually *senses*.

5. **Impedance Modulation of the Primary Coil**
   The net flux linking the coil is reduced: $\Phi_{\text{net}} = \Phi_0 - \Phi_{\text{ec}}$. Since the coil's self-inductance is $L = N\Phi_{\text{net}}/i$, the inductance **decreases** as the target approaches. The eddy currents also dissipate power, so the **effective resistance $R_{\text{eff}}$ increases**.
   *Why?* The coil behaves like a transformer with short-circuited secondary — the closer the target, the stronger the coupling, the greater the power drained.

6. **Signal Conditioning — Amplitude / Frequency Demodulation**
   A detector circuit (peak detector, rectifier, or amplitude ratio demodulator) converts the change in coil impedance $\Delta Z$ into a usable DC voltage $V_{\text{out}}$.
   *Why?* Microcontrollers and PLCs need a clean, monotonic voltage signal — not a raw high-frequency carrier.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] All formulas below are **examination-critical** for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE / MSE. Memorize the units and the physical meaning of each symbol.

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Symbol Glossary | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| F1 | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega \mu \sigma}}$ | $\delta$ — skin depth (m); $\omega = 2\pi f$; $\mu = \mu_0\mu_r$; $\sigma$ — conductivity (S/m) | Depth at which eddy-current density falls to $\approx 37\%$ of surface value |
| F2 | $\mathcal{E} = -N \dfrac{d\Phi_B}{dt}$ | $\mathcal{E}$ — induced emf (V); $N$ — turns; $\Phi_B$ — flux (Wb) | Faraday's Law — root cause of eddy currents |
| F3 | $i_{\text{eddy}} = -\dfrac{1}{R_t}\dfrac{d\Phi_B}{dt}$ | $i_{\text{eddy}}$ — eddy current (A); $R_t$ — resistance of target loop (Ω) | Lenz's law in circuit form |
| F4 | $Z_{\text{coil}} = R_{\text{eff}} + j\omega L_{\text{eff}}$ | $R_{\text{eff}}$, $L_{\text{eff}}$ — altered by target | Total impedance of the loaded coil |
| F5 | $\dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} = -K \cdot \dfrac{\mu_0 \sigma \omega r^4}{8 d^3}$ | $K$ — geometry constant; $r$ — coil radius; $d$ — gap | Fractional inductance drop vs. gap (cubic fall-off) |
| F6 | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R_0} = K' \cdot \dfrac{\mu_0 \sigma \omega r^4}{8 d^3}$ | Same as F5 (power-loss component) | Fractional resistance rise vs. gap |
| F7 | $V_{\text{out}} = A \cdot e^{-B d}$ | $A$, $B$ — sensor-specific constants | Empirical static calibration (exponential form) |
| F8 | $f_{\text{osc}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | $L, C$ — tank components | High-frequency oscillator frequency |
| F9 | $\eta = \dfrac{V_{\text{out,max}} - V_{\text{out,min}}}{V_{\text{supply}}}$ | $\eta$ — sensitivity (V/V) | Sensor sensitivity per unit supply |
| F10 | $\Delta d_{\min} = \dfrac{V_{\text{noise}}}{\left\vert dV_{\text{out}}/dd \right\vert}$ | $\Delta d_{\min}$ — smallest detectable displacement | Theoretical resolution limit |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Aerospace**: Blade tip clearance measurement in jet engines (non-contact at 20 000 rpm, 1200 °C environments with cooled probes).
* **Automotive**: Camshaft/crankshaft speed & position sensing; ABS wheel-speed sensors; valve-lift profiling.
* **Power Generation**: Shaft vibration monitoring in turbines and large motors.
* **Manufacturing**: Runout, concentricity, and roundness measurement of rotating shafts on lathes/grinders.
* **Nondestructive Testing (NDT)**: Surface and near-surface crack detection in aluminum airframe skins.
* **Robotics (course context)**: Joint position feedback in harmonic-drive gearboxes where back-lash must be detected at sub-100 µm resolution.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT] Anti-Shortcut Mandate
> Every algebraic transition below is shown explicitly. Skipping steps loses marks under the KTU 2024 valuation scheme.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Skin Depth $\delta$ of a Plane Conductor

**Starting Premise (Maxwell's 3rd Equation — diffusion form, in the quasi-static limit):**

Inside a good conductor, the magnetic field satisfies the one-dimensional diffusion equation:

$$\dfrac{\partial^2 B(z,t)}{\partial z^2} = \mu \sigma \dfrac{\partial B(z,t)}{\partial t}$$

Assume a trial solution of the form $B(z,t) = B_0 \, e^{-\alpha z} \cos(\omega t - \beta z)$, where $\alpha$ governs spatial decay and $\beta$ governs wavelength inside the conductor.

**Step 1 — Substitute into the diffusion equation.**

Compute the second spatial derivative:

$$\dfrac{\partial^2 B}{\partial z^2} = B_0 \, e^{-\alpha z}\bigl(\alpha^2 - \beta^2\bigr)\cos(\omega t - \beta z) + 2\alpha\beta B_0 \, e^{-\alpha z}\sin(\omega t - \beta z)$$

Compute the first time derivative:

$$\dfrac{\partial B}{\partial t} = -\omega B_0 \, e^{-\alpha z}\sin(\omega t - \beta z)$$

**Step 2 — Equate the coefficients of $\sin$ and $\cos$ separately.**

For the cosine coefficient: $\alpha^2 - \beta^2 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \alpha = \beta$.
For the sine coefficient: $2\alpha\beta = \mu\sigma\omega \;\Rightarrow\; 2\alpha^2 = \mu\sigma\omega$.

**Step 3 — Solve for $\alpha$.**

$$\alpha^2 = \dfrac{\mu\sigma\omega}{2} \;\Rightarrow\; \alpha = \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu\sigma\omega}{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Define skin depth as $\delta \equiv 1/\alpha$.**

$$\boxed{\,\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega\mu\sigma}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{2\pi f\,\mu_0\mu_r\sigma}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f\,\mu\sigma}}\,}$$

> [!NOTE] Engineering Interpretation
> * Doubling the frequency reduces $\delta$ by a factor of $\sqrt{2}\approx 1.414$ — the sensor becomes more *surface-localized*.
> * Copper has $\sigma \approx 5.96\times 10^7\,\text{S/m}$; at $f = 1\,\text{MHz}$, $\delta \approx 66\,\mu\text{m}$.
> * Aluminium ($\sigma \approx 3.5\times 10^7\,\text{S/m}$) gives $\delta \approx 84\,\mu\text{m}$ at 1 MHz.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Impedance of a Loaded Air-Core Coil (Two-Coil Equivalent)

Model the sensor as a **transformer** with the primary coil (sensor coil, self-inductance $L_1$, resistance $R_1$) loosely coupled to the eddy-current loop in the target (secondary, inductance $L_2$, resistance $R_2$).

**Step 1 — Write the coupled differential equations using $M = k\sqrt{L_1 L_2}$.**

$$v_1 = R_1 i_1 + L_1 \dfrac{di_1}{dt} + M\dfrac{di_2}{dt}$$

$$0 = R_2 i_2 + L_2 \dfrac{di_2}{dt} + M\dfrac{di_1}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Solve the second equation for $di_2/dt$ in the phasor domain ($j\omega$).**

$$\tilde{i}_2 = -\dfrac{j\omega M}{R_2 + j\omega L_2}\,\tilde{i}_1$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the first equation and collect the effective series impedance seen from the primary terminals.**

$$\tilde{Z}_{\text{in}} = R_1 + j\omega L_1 + \dfrac{\omega^2 M^2}{R_2 + j\omega L_2}$$

**Step 4 — Rationalize the reflected term by multiplying numerator and denominator by the conjugate.**

$$\tilde{Z}_{\text{in}} = \underbrace{\left(R_1 + \dfrac{\omega^2 M^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + \omega^2 L_2^2}\right)}_{R_{\text{eff}}} + j\omega \underbrace{\left(L_1 - \dfrac{M^2 R_2 \,/\, \omega^2 L_2^2}{1 + (R_2/\omega L_2)^2}\right)}_{L_{\text{eff}}}$$

**Step 5 — Identify two engineering conclusions.**

$$\boxed{\,\Delta R = +\dfrac{\omega^2 M^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + \omega^2 L_2^2}\quad\text{and}\quad \Delta L = -\dfrac{M^2 L_2}{R_2^2 + \omega^2 L_2^2}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] Physical Interpretation
> * $R_{\text{eff}}$ **increases** — the target *absorbs* energy from the coil as eddy-current ohmic loss.
> * $L_{\text{eff}}$ **decreases** — the eddy currents screen the flux from the coil interior (Lenz opposition).
> * The coupling $M$ depends on gap $d$ through the geometry: as $d \to 0$, $M \to M_{\max}$; as $d \to \infty$, $M \to 0$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sensitivity Calibration (Exponential Approximation)

Starting from the rigorous $\Delta L(d) \propto 1/d^3$ behaviour (valid for $d < r$, where $r$ is coil radius) and using a logarithmic transform to linearize, the static output of the detector stage is empirically:

**Step 1 — Define output voltage as a function of gap.**

$$V_{\text{out}}(d) = V_0 + V_{\max}\,e^{-d/\lambda}$$

where $\lambda$ is a characteristic decay length set by coil radius and frequency.

**Step 2 — Differentiate to obtain the static sensitivity $S(d)$.**

$$S(d) \equiv \dfrac{dV_{\text{out}}}{dd} = -\dfrac{V_{\max}}{\lambda}\,e^{-d/\lambda}$$

**Step 3 — Quantify the per-mV output change for a 1 µm change at the optimum operating point $d = \lambda$.**

At $d = \lambda$: $S(\lambda) = -V_{\max}/(e\lambda)$.

For $V_{\max} = 5\,\text{V}$ and $\lambda = 1\,\text{mm}$:

$$S(\lambda) = -\dfrac{5}{e \times 10^{-3}} \approx -1.84\,\text{V/mm} = -1.84\,\text{mV/}\mu\text{m}$$

> [!NOTE] Why This Matters for Robotics
> Robotic harmonic-drive joints can detect axial play below 50 µm — this 1.84 mV/µm sensitivity is exactly the regime where eddy-current sensors outperform LVDTs (which need mechanical core contact) and Hall-effect sensors (which saturate near ferrous mounts).

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Eddy Current vs. Alternatives

| Property | Eddy-Current | LVDT | Hall-Effect | Capacitive |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Contact Type | Non-contact | Contacting core | Non-contact | Non-contact |
| Target Material | Conductive only | Ferromagnetic | Ferromagnetic | Any (incl. dielectric) |
| Best Range | 0.1–10 mm | ±0.1–±100 mm | 1–10 mm | 0.1–10 mm |
| Resolution | Sub-µm | 0.1 µm | 10 µm | 0.01 µm |
| High-Temp Tolerant | **Excellent (with Inconel housing)** | Poor (coil insulation) | Poor (>150 °C drifts) | Moderate |
| Cost | High | High | Low | Moderate |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE] Diagram Strategy
> Because eddy-current sensors involve physical electromagnetic fields that resist simple 2-D rendering, the diagram below presents the **Block-Level Functional Architecture** that maps the full signal path from excitation to digital output — this is the diagram style preferred in KTU 2024 answer scripts for maximum marks.

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PWR["Power Supply Stage"]
        ps[DC Regulator plus minus 15V]
    end

    subgraph OSC["High Frequency Oscillator 100 kHz to 2 MHz"]
        osc[Colpitts Sine Oscillator]
    end

    subgraph SENSE["Sensing Head Electromagnetic Front End"]
        coil[Flat Pancake Excitation Coil L1 R1]
        target[Conductive Target Aluminium Copper Brass]
        eddy[Eddy Current Loops in Target Skin Layer]
    end

    subgraph DET["Signal Conditioning Stage"]
        bridge[AC Bridge or Half Bridge Detector]
        rect[Precision Rectifier plus Filter]
        amp[DC Amplifier Gain A]
    end

    subgraph OUT["Output Stage"]
        analog[Analog Voltage Output 0 to 10 V]
        comp[Comparator plus Threshold]
        switch[Digital Switch Output NPN PNP]
        micro[Microcontroller ADC Input]
    end

    ps --> osc
    osc -->|sinusoidal current i of t| coil
    coil -->|alternating flux phi of t| target
    target --> eddy
    eddy -->|secondary opposing flux| coil
    coil -->|modulated impedance Z eff| bridge
    bridge --> rect
    rect --> amp
    amp --> analog
    amp --> comp
    comp --> switch
    analog --> micro
    switch --> micro
```

### 4.2 Detailed Signal-Flow Annotation

| Block | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| OSC | Colpitts oscillator (often 100 kHz to 2 MHz) | Generates stable sinusoidal drive; frequency drift < 50 ppm/°C is required for sub-µm resolution |
| SENSE | Pancake coil wound on air-core former | Maximizes fringing field; air core avoids $\mu$-nonlinearity of ferrite at high frequency |
| SENSE | Conductive target | Eddy currents flow within top skin-depth layer |
| DET | AC bridge (Wheatstone or half-bridge with reference coil) | Converts tiny $\Delta Z$ to differential voltage |
| DET | Synchronous (lock-in) detector | Rejects 1/f noise and ambient EMI |
| OUT | Comparator + hysteresis | Provides clean switching threshold (e.g., 3 mm = "object present") |
| OUT | Microcontroller ADC | Samples for closed-loop robotic control |

## 4.3 Equivalent Circuit Schematic (Mermaid Network)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PRIM["Primary Coil Loop Sensor Side"]
        R1[R1 series resistance]
        L1[L1 self inductance]
    end
    subgraph SEC["Equivalent Secondary Loop Target Side"]
        R2[R2 eddy current path resistance]
        L2[L2 eddy current loop inductance]
    end
    M[(Mutual Coupling M depends on gap d)]
    R1 --- L1
    L1 --- M
    M --- L2
    L2 --- R2
```

> [!IMPORTANT] Reading Aid
> In the schematic above, $M$ is the **gap-dependent coupling**. As the target approaches the coil, $M$ increases, drawing more power into the $R_2$ branch — which the primary senses as a rise in $R_{\text{eff}}$ and a fall in $L_{\text{eff}}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**Define an eddy current proximity sensor. State two reasons why it is preferred for detecting non-ferrous metal targets.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An eddy-current proximity sensor is a **non-contact inductive transducer** that uses a high-frequency AC-excited coil to induce circulating eddy currents in a conducting target; the resulting change in coil impedance is used to detect target proximity. **(1 mark)**
**Reasons it is preferred for non-ferrous targets (any two): (2 marks)**
1. The operating principle is purely **electrodynamic** (eddy currents depend on conductivity $\sigma$, not permeability $\mu$); ferrous materials introduce competing, non-linear $\mu$-effects that distort the calibration curve.
2. Non-ferrous metals such as aluminium and copper exhibit high $\sigma$, producing **strong, predictable eddy-current losses** that yield a large, monotonic impedance change.
3. Absence of magnetic hysteresis in non-ferrous materials ensures the sensor response is **repeatable and free of hysteresis error**.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**What is skin depth? Show that at 1 MHz in copper, the skin depth is approximately 66 µm. Given: $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m, $\sigma_{\text{Cu}} = 5.96 \times 10^{7}$ S/m, $\mu_r = 1$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Skin depth $\delta$ is the depth below the surface of a conductor at which the amplitude of an electromagnetic wave (or eddy current) decays to $1/e \approx 36.8\%$ of its surface value.

**Formula (1 mark):** $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega\mu\sigma}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f\mu\sigma}}$

**Numerical substitution (1 mark):**

$$\delta = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi \times 10^6 \times 4\pi\times 10^{-7} \times 5.96\times 10^7}}$$

$$= \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2.354\times 10^{8}}} \approx \dfrac{1}{1.534\times 10^{4}} \approx 6.51\times 10^{-5}\,\text{m} \approx 65\,\mu\text{m} \;\checkmark$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING] KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall
> Students commonly lose marks by (a) writing only the formula without deriving the *meaning* of each term, (b) confusing *inductance drop* with *resistance rise* (they change in **opposite** directions — re-read §3.2!), and (c) failing to draw the block diagram. **Always include a labelled diagram — even a hand-drawn version — for full marks.**

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) [7 Marks] With a neat functional block diagram, explain the working principle of an eddy-current proximity sensor. Discuss the role of the high-frequency oscillator and the rectifier stage.

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (2 marks)** — Draw the diagram from §4.1 above (Power Supply → Oscillator → Coil & Target → AC Bridge → Rectifier → Amplifier → Output). Clearly label all blocks.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
1. A **high-frequency oscillator** (typically Colpitts topology, $f \approx 100\,\text{kHz – 2 MHz}$) drives a sinusoidal current $i(t) = I_0\sin\omega t$ through a flat **pancake excitation coil** $L_1$.
2. The coil produces a time-varying magnetic flux $\Phi(t)$ that extends outward as a fringing field. When a conducting target approaches the coil, the alternating flux $\Phi$ cuts the target and **induces eddy currents** in its surface skin layer.
3. By Lenz's law these eddy currents generate a secondary, **opposing flux** $\Phi_{\text{ec}}$, reducing the net flux linking the coil. Consequently, the coil's effective **inductance decreases** ($L_{\text{eff}} \downarrow$) and its effective **resistance increases** ($R_{\text{eff}} \uparrow$) due to eddy-current ohmic loss.
4. This impedance change $\Delta Z(d)$ is monotonically related to the air-gap $d$; after **demodulation and rectification**, it is converted to a DC voltage $V_{\text{out}}(d)$.

**Role of Oscillator (1 mark):**
The high frequency is essential because the induced emf $\mathcal{E} = -Nd\Phi/dt$ scales with $\omega$, ensuring large, measurable $\Delta Z$ even for sub-mm gaps. Higher $f$ also reduces the skin depth $\delta = 1/\sqrt{\pi f\mu\sigma}$, confining sensing to a thin surface layer.

**Role of Rectifier & Filter (1 mark):**
The carrier-frequency signal must be converted to a stable DC level for downstream electronics (PLC, ADC, microcontroller). A precision rectifier followed by a low-pass filter extracts the envelope $V_{\text{out}}(d)$, while a synchronous (lock-in) detector rejects ambient EMI and 1/f noise.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] Starting from the coupled-coil equations, derive expressions for the change in effective resistance $\Delta R$ and effective inductance $\Delta L$ of an eddy-current sensor when a conductive target is placed at gap $d$. Clearly state the physical meaning of each term.

**Model Solution:**

The sensor is modelled as a loosely coupled transformer: primary (sensor coil) with $R_1, L_1$; secondary (eddy-current loop in target) with $R_2, L_2$; mutual inductance $M = k\sqrt{L_1L_2}$.

**Step 1 — Phasor equations (1 mark):**

$$\tilde{V}_1 = (R_1 + j\omega L_1)\tilde{I}_1 + j\omega M\,\tilde{I}_2$$

$$0 = (R_2 + j\omega L_2)\tilde{I}_2 + j\omega M\,\tilde{I}_1$$

**Step 2 — Solve secondary for $\tilde{I}_2$ (1 mark):**

$$\tilde{I}_2 = -\dfrac{j\omega M}{R_2 + j\omega L_2}\,\tilde{I}_1$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back into primary equation (1 mark):**

$$\tilde{Z}_{\text{in}} = \dfrac{\tilde{V}_1}{\tilde{I}_1} = R_1 + j\omega L_1 + \dfrac{\omega^2 M^2}{R_2 + j\omega L_2}$$

**Step 4 — Rationalise (2 marks):**

$$\tilde{Z}_{\text{in}} = \underbrace{\left(R_1 + \dfrac{\omega^2 M^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + \omega^2 L_2^2}\right)}_{R_{\text{eff}}} + j\omega \underbrace{\left(L_1 - \dfrac{M^2 L_2}{R_2^2 + \omega^2 L_2^2}\right)}_{L_{\text{eff}}}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the changes (2 marks):**

$$\boxed{\,\Delta R = +\dfrac{\omega^2 M^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + \omega^2 L_2^2}\,}\qquad \boxed{\,\Delta L = -\dfrac{M^2 L_2}{R_2^2 + \omega^2 L_2^2}\,}$$

**Physical meaning of each term (embedded in derivation):**
* $M$ — gap-dependent coupling; as $d \downarrow$, $M \uparrow$.
* $R_2$ — ohmic resistance of the eddy-current loop (depends on target $\sigma$).
* $L_2$ — self-inductance of the eddy-current loop.
* Positive $\Delta R$ represents **power dissipation** in the target.
* Negative $\Delta L$ represents **flux screening** by the opposing eddy-current field.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the phenomenon of skin effect. Derive the expression for skin depth in a conductor and compute its value for aluminium at 2 MHz. Given: $\sigma_{\text{Al}} = 3.5 \times 10^{7}$ S/m, $\mu_r = 1$.

**Model Solution:**

**Phenomenon (2 marks):** Skin effect is the tendency of an alternating current — including induced eddy currents — to concentrate near the surface of a conductor, with current density falling exponentially with depth. It arises because the **self-induced eddy currents** within the bulk cancel the central current while reinforcing the surface current.

**Derivation (3 marks):** See §3.1 above for the full derivation yielding:

$$\boxed{\,\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega\mu\sigma}}\,}$$

**Numerical computation (2 marks):**

$$\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{2\pi \times 2\times 10^6 \times 4\pi\times 10^{-7} \times 3.5\times 10^7}}$$

$$= \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{5.529\times 10^{8}}} = \sqrt{3.617\times 10^{-9}} \approx 6.01\times 10^{-5}\,\text{m} \approx 60\,\mu\text{m}$$

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] Compare eddy-current proximity sensors with LVDT and capacitive proximity sensors on the basis of (i) target material requirement, (ii) contact type, (iii) typical range, (iv) hysteresis, (v) high-temperature suitability, (vi) linearity, and (vii) cost. Justify the choice of eddy-current sensor for non-ferrous crack detection in aerospace airframes.

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Criterion | Eddy-Current | LVDT | Capacitive |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Target material | Conductive only (preferably non-ferrous) | Ferromagnetic core | Any (conductor or dielectric) |
| (ii) Contact type | **Non-contact** | Contacting core (mechanical linkage) | Non-contact |
| (iii) Typical range | 0.1–10 mm | ±0.1 to ±100 mm | 0.1–10 mm |
| (iv) Hysteresis | **Very low** (no magnetic material) | Low | Moderate (humidity-sensitive) |
| (v) High-temp suitability | **Excellent** (Inconel housing, >200 °C) | Poor (coil insulation) | Poor (dielectric drifts) |
| (vi) Linearity | Moderate (60–70 %) | **Excellent (>99 %)** | Moderate |
| (vii) Cost | High | High | Moderate |

**Justification for aerospace non-ferrous crack detection (2 marks):**
1. **Material compatibility**: Aerospace airframes are predominantly **aluminium alloys** — perfectly suited as eddy-current targets with high $\sigma$.
2. **Non-contact operation**: Crack inspection must be performed without coupling fluid or surface pressure; eddy current achieves this.
3. **Crack sensitivity**: A surface crack disrupts the eddy-current path locally, producing a detectable impedance anomaly. Sensitivity is optimal because skin depth ($\approx 60$–$100\,\mu\text{m}$ at 1–2 MHz) is comparable to typical crack depths.
4. **Harsh-environment survival**: Probes survive fuel, hydraulic fluid, and temperatures from –55 °C to +200 °C with Inconel housings.
5. **No surface preparation** needed (unlike dye-penetrant or magnetic-particle methods).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT] Final-Memory Anchor
> The following bullets form a 30-second "look-back" that should be rehearsed before walking into the KTU examination hall.

* ✅ Eddy-current sensors are **non-contact, inductive**, and detect **conductors only**.
* ✅ The operating principle is rooted in **Faraday's Law** (induction) and **Lenz's Law** (opposition).
* ✅ Coil impedance change has **two components**: $\Delta R \uparrow$ (loss) and $\Delta L \downarrow$ (screening).
* ✅ Skin depth formula: $\delta = \sqrt{2/(\omega\mu\sigma)}$; **higher frequency ⇒ shallower sensing**.
* ✅ Sensitivity follows $\propto 1/d^3$ for $d < r$; output is roughly **exponential** in $d$.
* ✅ High frequency (**100 kHz – 2 MHz**) is essential for high induced emf and compact geometry.
* ✅ Best suited for **non-ferrous metals** (Al, Cu, Brass, Ti).
* ✅ Resolution can reach **sub-micron**; typical range is **0.1–10 mm**.
* ✅ Six functional blocks: **Oscillator → Coil+Target → Bridge → Rectifier → Amplifier → Output**.
* ✅ Applications: **NDT crack detection, blade-tip clearance, vibration monitoring, robotic joint feedback**.
* ✅ Common KTU mistake: forgetting to draw the **block diagram** in long answers — always include one.
* ✅ Common KTU mistake: saying "inductance increases" — it **decreases** (re-read §3.2!).
* ✅ Common KTU mistake: confusing $\mu$ (permeability) with $\sigma$ (conductivity) in the skin-depth formula — memorise them as the **two material parameters** that appear under the radical.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inductive Proximity Sensor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT205 - SENSOR TECHNOLOGY FOR ROBOTICS (ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inductive Proximity Sensor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Inductive Proximity Sensor (Definition)**
> An **Inductive Proximity Sensor** is a non-contact solid-state electronic transducer that detects the presence (or absence) of a metallic target object within a defined sensing zone by exploiting the principle of **electromagnetic induction** and the associated loss of energy due to induced **eddy currents** in the target.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (Module 2 — *Sensors for Industrial Automation*), an inductive proximity sensor belongs to the class of **non-contact displacement/presence sensors**, operating on the principle of variation in the **oscillator amplitude** caused by energy dissipation in a nearby metallic body. These sensors are foundational building blocks of industrial robotics, conveyor automation, and CNC machine tool feedback systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terminology — KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> - **Rated Operating Distance (Sn):** The conventional operating distance measured using a *standard target* at rated supply voltage and ambient temperature (20 °C).
> - **Standard Target:** A square mild-steel plate of thickness **1 mm**, with side length equal to the diameter of the sensor's active face.
> - **Shielded vs Non-Shielded:** Refers to whether the sensor's coil is encased in a metal collar (flush-mountable) or exposed (longer range, non-flush).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine a **security walk-through metal detector at an airport**. When you walk through the archway with metallic objects in your pockets, the detector beeps because the alternating magnetic field produced by the archway induces tiny circulating currents (called *eddy currents*) in the metal. These currents draw energy from the field, and the detector's electronics "sense" this energy loss.

An **inductive proximity sensor** works on the exact same principle — but in a miniature, point-like geometry:

1. A tiny **coil** inside the sensor produces a high-frequency alternating magnetic field (typically **100 kHz – 1 MHz**).
2. When a **metallic target** (steel, aluminum, copper, brass, etc.) approaches the sensor face, **eddy currents** are induced inside the target.
3. These eddy currents act like a brake on the magnetic field — they **absorb energy** and **dampen the oscillations** of the internal LC tank circuit.
4. The sensor's electronics detect this amplitude drop and **switch a transistor output** (ON or OFF).

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Takeaway:** Think of the inductive sensor as an *electromagnetic leash* that "feels" metal coming close. The closer the metal, the stronger the leash-pull on the oscillator, the bigger the signal change at the output. **No magnet required, no physical touch, no moving parts** — making it ideal for dirty, oily, or explosive industrial environments.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and standard values are routinely used in KTU derivations and numerical problems:

- **Standard Target Material:** Mild Steel (Fe 360, defined by ISO 630)
- **Standard Target Thickness:** **1 mm**
- **Reference Ambient Temperature:** **20 °C** (293 K)
- **Typical Oscillator Frequency:** **100 kHz – 1 MHz**
- **Typical Switching Hysteresis:** **1 % – 15 %** of Sn
- **Supply Voltage Range (KTU industrial standard):** **10 V – 30 V DC** (commonly **24 V DC**)
- **Reduction Factor Reference Metal:** Mild Steel (factor = **1.0**)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Stepwise Logic

The inductive proximity sensor operates on the principle of **electromagnetic induction** coupled with **eddy-current energy dissipation**. The sequence of events is enumerated below:

1. **Oscillator Stage (LC Tank):** A Colpitts or Hartley oscillator drives current through an inductor coil (L) wound around a ferrite core. The LC tank resonates at a fixed high frequency, producing a stable alternating magnetic field at the sensor's active face.

2. **Target Approach:** As a conductive (metallic) target approaches, the time-varying magnetic flux $\Phi(t)$ from the coil links with the target body.

3. **Faraday's Law in Action:** According to **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, an alternating EMF is induced inside the target. This induced EMF drives circulating **eddy currents** $I_{eddy}$ in a closed loop within the target material.

4. **Lenz's Law Opposition:** By **Lenz's Law**, these eddy currents generate their *own* opposing magnetic field. This opposition **dampens the original magnetic flux** and extracts energy from the oscillator's tank circuit.

5. **Amplitude Drop Detection:** The amplitude of the LC oscillation drops significantly (typically 10–30 %) once the target is within the rated sensing distance. A **peak detector / demodulator** circuit monitors this amplitude.

6. **Threshold Comparison & Trigger:** When the amplitude falls below a pre-set threshold, a Schmitt-trigger comparator changes state, activating the **output driver stage**.

7. **Output Stage:** The output driver typically uses either an **NPN** (sinking) or **PNP** (sourcing) open-collector transistor to switch a DC load (relay coil, PLC input, indicator LED). Output configurations include **NO (Normally Open)**, **NC (Normally Closed)**, or **antivalent (NO + NC)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Inductive" and not "Capacitive"?**
> Inductive sensors respond **only to conductive materials** (metals) because the eddy-current mechanism requires free charge carriers (electrons) inside the target. Non-conductive materials (plastic, wood, glass, oil) are essentially invisible to an inductive sensor. For non-metals, a **capacitive proximity sensor** is used instead.

---

### 2.2 Operating Distances — The Four KTU Definitions

KTU examiners frequently test the precise distinction between the four operating distances. These are *not* interchangeable:

| Symbol | Name | Definition | Typical Relation to Sn |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Sn** | Rated operating distance | Distance at which a standard target is detected at rated voltage and 20 °C | Reference (1.0 × Sn) |
| **Sr** | Effective operating distance | Distance measured at rated voltage and 20 °C with a standard target | $0.9 \, Sn \le Sr \le 1.1 \, Sn$ |
| **Su** | Useful operating distance | Distance guaranteed across the full $-25\,°C$ to $+70\,°C$ temperature range and $85$ % to $110$ % of rated voltage | $0.81 \, Sn \le Su \le 1.21 \, Sn$ |
| **Sa** | Assured operating distance | Distance guaranteed to switch reliably over the full tolerance band | $0 \le Sa \le 0.81 \, Sn$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** If a problem statement says "at rated conditions, 20 °C, standard target" → use **Sr**. If it says "over full temperature and voltage range" → use **Sa**. If it gives you Sn and asks for the "guaranteed switching distance" → multiply by **0.81** to get Sa.

---

### 2.3 Shielded vs Non-Shielded Construction

| Parameter | Shielded (Flush) | Non-Shielded (Non-Flush) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Construction** | Ferrite pot-core around coil directs field only forward | No shielding ring; field expands radially as well |
| **Mounting** | Flush-mountable in metal | Requires a metal-free zone around the sensor face |
| **Sensing Range (Sn)** | Smaller (e.g., 2 mm for M12 body) | Larger (typically **1.5× to 2×** Sn of shielded) |
| **Mechanical Robustness** | High | Lower — sensitive to surrounding metal |
| **Typical Application** | Tight installations, robotic grippers | Open spaces, long-range detection |

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula Sheet — Inductive Proximity Sensor (KTU 2024)**
> All quantities are in **SI units** unless otherwise noted. Symbols follow IEC 60947-5-2 / KTU syllabus notation.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :-: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $S_n = \dfrac{d}{2}$ (approx., for shielded cylindrical sensors) | Empirical relation: Sn ≈ half the active-face diameter | mm |
| 2 | $S_a \le 0.81 \, S_n$ | Assured operating distance bound | mm |
| 3 | $H\,(\%) = \dfrac{S_r - S_{off}}{S_r} \times 100$ | Hysteresis (difference between switch-on and switch-off point) | % |
| 4 | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{on} + T_{off}}$ | Maximum switching frequency (Hz), where T_on and T_off are the make/break times | Hz |
| 5 | $S_{actual} = S_n \times K_{metal}$ | Effective sensing distance for a given metal, where $K_{metal}$ is the reduction factor | mm |
| 6 | $E_{eddy} = \dfrac{1}{2} L_{target} \, I_{eddy}^{2}$ | Energy stored in eddy-current loop dissipated per cycle (qualitative) | J |
| 7 | $V_{induced} = -N \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$ | Faraday's law governing eddy current generation | V |
| 8 | $K_{steel} = 1.0,\ K_{brass} \approx 0.5,\ K_{Cu} \approx 0.4,\ K_{Al} \approx 0.45,\ K_{SS\,304} \approx 0.7$ | Reduction factors (relative to mild steel) for common metals | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Formatting Rule:** Notice that the table above uses $\vert$ notation in formulas to avoid breaking the markdown pipe. Do not write $\vert x \vert$ directly inside table rows.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Inductive proximity sensors are the *workhorses* of discrete industrial automation. Key KTU-recognized applications include:

- **End-of-travel limit detection** on pneumatic and hydraulic cylinders.
- **Object counting** on conveyor belts (e.g., counting gears, cans, bolts).
- **Position feedback** in robotic pick-and-place mechanisms.
- **Tool-changer presence verification** in CNC machining centers.
- **Rotational speed measurement** by counting gear-tooth passages (tachogenerator mode).
- **Coin / metal-part sorting** in vending and recycling machinery.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Switching Hysteresis (H) From First Principles

**Problem Setup:** A standard target is brought toward the sensor face along the central axis. The sensor turns ON at distance $S_{on}$ (switch-on point) and turns OFF at distance $S_{off}$ (switch-off point), with $S_{on} < S_{off}$. Derive the formula for percent hysteresis $H(\%)$.

**Step 1 — Define the operational meaning of hysteresis.**
Hysteresis is the *deliberate* dead-band introduced in a proximity sensor to **prevent chattering** of the output when the target is parked at the boundary of the sensing zone. Chattering would otherwise cause false triggering due to vibration, electrical noise, or thermal drift.

**Step 2 — Identify the two switching thresholds.**
Let the trip threshold correspond to a target distance $S_{on}$ (closer to the sensor) and the release threshold correspond to $S_{off}$ (farther from the sensor). By definition:

$$S_{on} < S_{off}$$

**Step 3 — Express the dead-band width.**
The difference between switch-off and switch-on distances is the dead-band:

$$\Delta S = S_{off} - S_{on}$$

**Step 4 — Normalize to the rated (or effective) sensing distance.**
KTU 2024 standard uses $S_r$ (effective sensing distance at 20 °C, rated voltage) as the reference:

$$H = \dfrac{\Delta S}{S_r} = \dfrac{S_{off} - S_{on}}{S_r}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to percent.**

$$H\,(\%) = \dfrac{S_{off} - S_{on}}{S_r} \times 100$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Typical KTU-allowed hysteresis values are 1 % – 15 %. A hysteresis of < 3 % is considered *low-hysteresis* and is suitable for high-precision indexing. A hysteresis of > 10 % is acceptable for simple presence detection on conveyors.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reduction Factor and Effective Sensing Distance for Non-Ferrous Targets

**Problem Setup:** A manufacturer datasheet specifies $S_n = 4$ mm for mild steel. Determine the *effective* sensing distance for **(a)** stainless steel 304 and **(b)** aluminum 6061.

**Step 1 — Apply the Reduction Factor equation.**

$$S_{actual} = S_n \times K_{metal}$$

**Step 2 (a) — Stainless Steel 304.**
From the KTU reduction-factor table:

$$K_{SS304} \approx 0.7$$

$$S_{actual}^{SS} = 4 \, mm \times 0.7 = 2.8 \, mm$$

**Step 2 (b) — Aluminum 6061.**

$$K_{Al} \approx 0.45$$

$$S_{actual}^{Al} = 4 \, mm \times 0.45 = 1.8 \, mm$$

**Step 3 — Engineer interpretation.**
Stainless steel (despite being non-magnetic in 304 grade) gives a *better* sensing distance than aluminum because of its **higher electrical conductivity** and **deeper skin depth** at the oscillator frequency. The 0.45 factor for aluminum is a direct consequence of its high conductivity and the resulting strong eddy-current losses that act on a thinner surface layer.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** "**SCBA**" — Steel, Copper, Brass, Aluminum → Reduction factors in decreasing order (1.0, 0.4, 0.5, 0.45). Brass is slightly better than pure copper because of its higher resistivity.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Assured Operating Distance (Sa) Bound From $S_n$

**Step 1 — Start from the KTU definition.**

$$S_a \le 0.81 \, S_n$$

**Step 2 — Justify the 0.81 factor.**
The 0.81 factor arises from the **product of the worst-case tolerances** allowed by IEC 60947-5-2:
- Voltage tolerance: $0.9 \times 1.1 = 0.99$ envelope
- Temperature tolerance: $-25\,°C$ to $+70\,°C$ envelope
- Manufacturing tolerance of the coil: $\pm 10\,\%$
- Combined worst-case multiplication, rounded by the IEC committee to **0.81**.

**Step 3 — Compute an example.**
For $S_n = 8$ mm:

$$S_a \le 0.81 \times 8 = 6.48 \, mm$$

Thus, the sensor is *guaranteed* to detect a standard target at any distance between **0 and 6.48 mm** across the full operating envelope. Anything beyond 6.48 mm is *uncertain*; the sensor may or may not switch.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Maximum Switching Frequency

The maximum switching frequency (also called **operating frequency** or **response frequency**) is limited by the make-time $T_{on}$ and break-time $T_{off}$ of the sensor electronics:

$$f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{on} + T_{off}}$$

**Example:** A standard M12 shielded inductive proximity sensor has $T_{on} = T_{off} = 0.5$ ms. Then:

$$f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{0.5 \times 10^{-3} + 0.5 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{1}{10^{-3}} = 1000 \, Hz$$

This means the sensor can detect up to **1000 targets per second** passing its face — more than sufficient for conveyor counting at 60 m/min with 10 cm product spacing.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix (Quick-Reference Comparison)

| Parameter | Mild Steel | Stainless Steel 304 | Brass | Aluminum 6061 | Copper |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Reduction Factor (K)** | **1.00** | 0.70 | 0.50 | 0.45 | 0.40 |
| **Effective Sn (mm) for nominal Sn = 4 mm** | 4.00 | 2.80 | 2.00 | 1.80 | 1.60 |
| **Relative Conductivity (×10⁶ S/m)** | 6.99 | 1.39 | 15.9 | 36.9 | 59.0 |
| **Magnetic Permeability Class** | Ferromagnetic | Non-magnetic | Non-magnetic | Non-magnetic | Non-magnetic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Surprise Result:** Despite copper having the highest electrical conductivity, its sensing distance is *smallest*. This is because very high conductivity leads to **strong skin-effect confinement** at the sensor's frequency — eddy currents are squeezed into a vanishingly thin surface layer, reducing the effective target volume and thus the energy dissipation coupling.

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Inductive Proximity Sensor

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the internal signal-flow architecture of a generic shielded inductive proximity sensor (DC 3-/4-wire type, NPN or PNP output).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PS[Power Supply 10 to 30 V DC] --> REG[Voltage Regulator]
    REG --> OSC[LC Oscillator 100 kHz to 1 MHz]
    OSC --> COIL[Inductive Coil with Ferrite Core]
    COIL -. generates .-> FIELD[Alternating Magnetic Field at Sensor Face]
    FIELD -. couples into .-> TARGET[Metallic Target Standard Mild Steel 1 mm]
    TARGET -. induces .-> EDDY[Eddy Currents in Target]
    EDDY -. damps .-> COIL
    OSC --> PDET[Peak Detector and Demodulator]
    PDET --> COMP[Schmitt Trigger Comparator]
    COMP --> DRV[Output Driver NPN or PNP]
    DRV --> OUT[Output Line to PLC or Relay]
    REG --> PDET
    REG --> COMP
    REG --> DRV
    PS -. sense .-> REG
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **solid arrows** denote power/signal flow paths. The **dotted arrows** denote physical-field interactions (electromagnetic coupling). The closed feedback loop `OSC → COIL ⇣ EDDY ⇢ COIL → OSC` (with double-arrow) represents the *coupling/energy-extraction mechanism* that the detector circuit monitors.

---

### 4.2 Shielded vs Non-Shielded — Field-Propagation Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SH[Shielded Flush Mounting]
        S1[Sensor Body] --> S2[Ferrite Pot Core]
        S2 --> S3[Field Lines Concentrated Forward]
        S3 --> S4[Flush with Metal Surround]
    end
    subgraph NSH[Non-Shielded Non-Flush Mounting]
        N1[Sensor Body] --> N2[Open Ferrite Core]
        N2 --> N3[Field Lines Radiate Sideways and Forward]
        N3 --> N4[Requires Metal-Free Zone]
    end
    S3 -. Sn shorter .-> SHresult[Typical Sn = 2 mm M12 body]
    N3 -. Sn longer 1.5x to 2x .-> NSHresult[Typical Sn = 4 mm M12 body]
```

---

### 4.3 Output Configuration State Diagram (NPN NO / PNP NO / NPN NC / PNP NC)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> TargetAbsent
    TargetAbsent --> TargetPresent : Object enters sensing zone
    TargetPresent --> TargetAbsent : Object leaves and hysteresis exceeded
    state TargetAbsent {
        direction LR
        A1[NPN NO OFF transistor blocks load current]
        A2[PNP NO OFF transistor blocks load current]
        A3[NPN NC ON transistor sinks current]
        A4[PNP NC ON transistor sources current]
    }
    state TargetPresent {
        direction LR
        P1[NPN NO ON transistor sinks current]
        P2[PNP NO ON transistor sources current]
        P3[NPN NC OFF transistor blocks load current]
        P4[PNP NC OFF transistor blocks load current]
    }
```

### 4.4 Operating-Distance Envelope Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sensor Face] -->|0 mm| B[Sa Assured Zone 0 to 0.81 Sn]
    B -->|0.81 Sn| C[Su Useful Zone 0.81 Sn to 1.21 Sn]
    C -->|1.21 Sn| D[Sr Effective Zone 0.9 Sn to 1.1 Sn]
    D -->|Sn| E[Rated Operating Point]
    E -->|1.1 Sn| F[No Detection]
    style B fill:green,stroke:black
    style C fill:yellow,stroke:black
    style D fill:orange,stroke:black
    style F fill:red,stroke:black,color:white
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Interpretation:** The green zone is the *guaranteed* operating region for any target across all tolerances. The red zone is the *definite non-detection* region. The yellow and orange zones are *transitional* and depend on individual sensor tolerance, supply voltage, and temperature.

### 4.5 Eddy-Current Coupling Mechanism (Block-Level Functional View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    COILGEN[Oscillator Coil Carries AC Current I coil] --> FLUXGEN[Time Varying Magnetic Flux Phi t]
    FLUXGEN --> FARADAY[Faraday Law Induces EMF in Target]
    FARADAY --> EDDYCIRC[Eddy Current I eddy Circulates in Target Skin Depth]
    EDDYCIRC --> OPPOSEFLD[Opposing Magnetic Field Phi opp by Lenz Law]
    OPPOSEFLD --> NETFLUX[Net Flux Reduced in Coil Core]
    NETFLUX --> AMPDROP[Oscillator Amplitude Drops]
    AMPDROP --> DETECT[Detector Circuit Trips]
```
