How do we recover a **DC signal** that is positive for $x > 0$ and negative for $x < 0$? **Solution — Phase-Sensitive Detector (PSD):** A phase-sensitive detector (also called a synchronous demodulator) multiplies $V_{out}$ by a reference signal $V_{ref}$ derived from the excitation source: $$V_{ref} = V_0 \cos(\omega t)$$ The multiplier output is: $$V_m = V_{out} \cdot V_{ref} = K x \cos^2(\omega t) \cdot V_0$$ Using the trigonometric identity: $$\cos^2(\omega t) = \frac{1 + \cos(2 \omega t)}{2}$$ Substituting: $$V_m = \frac{K x V_0}{2} [1 + \cos(2 \omega t)]$$ After low-pass filtering, the AC term $\cos(2\omega t)$ is removed, leaving: $$\boxed{V_{DC} = \frac{K V_0}{2} \cdot x}$$ This DC voltage is: - **Positive** when $x > 0$ (core moved toward S₁) - **Negative** when $x < 0$ (core moved toward S₂) - **Zero** when $x = 0$ (null position) ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Operating Regions | Region | Core Position | $M_1$ vs. $M_2$ | $\vert V_{out} \vert$ | Phase (w.r.t. $V_{ex}$) | Sign of $V_{DC}$ | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Linear (+) | $0 < x < +x_{max}$ | $M_1 > M_2$ | $K \cdot x$ | $0°$ (in-phase) | $+$ | | Null | $x = 0$ | $M_1 = M_2$ | $0$ | — | $0$ | | Linear (–) | $-x_{max} < x < 0$ | $M_1 < M_2$ | $K \cdot \vert x \vert$ | $180°$ (out-of-phase) | $-$ | | Saturated (+) | $x > +x_{max}$ | $M_1 \approx M_{max}$ | $\approx$ constant | $0°$ | Clipped | | Saturated (–) | $x < -x_{max}$ | $M_2 \approx M_{max}$ | $\approx$ constant | $180°$ | Clipped | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — LVDT Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[AC Excitation Source
5 kHz, 5V RMS] -->|Vp sin wt| B[Primary Coil P
Np turns] B -->|Magnetic Flux| C1[Secondary Coil S1
Ns turns] B -->|Magnetic Flux| C2[Secondary Coil S2
Ns turns] C1 -->|Es1| D[Differential Output
Es1 minus Es2] C2 -->|Es2| D D -->|Vout AC| E[Phase-Sensitive
Detector] A -->|Vref in-phase| E E -->|VDC proportional to x| F[Low-Pass Filter
Removes 2w component] F -->|Clean DC| G[Output Display
or ADC] H[Movable Ferromagnetic
Core - Armature] -.->|Position x| C1 H -.->|Position x| C2 H -.->|Links Flux| B style A fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#000,color:#fff style B fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff style C1 fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#000,color:#fff style C2 fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#000,color:#fff style D fill:#c62828,stroke:#000,color:#fff style E fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#000,color:#fff style F fill:#00838f,stroke:#000,color:#fff style G fill:#424242,stroke:#000,color:#fff style H fill:#5d4037,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Operating Principle in Three States ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Null: Core at x = 0 Null --> Right: Core moves toward S1 Null --> Left: Core moves toward S2 Right --> Null: Core returns to center Left --> Null: Core returns to center Right --> SaturateR: x exceeds plus xmax Left --> SaturateL: x exceeds minus xmax SaturateR --> Right: Core returns to range SaturateL --> Left: Core returns to range note right of Null Es1 equals Es2 Vout equals 0 V Phase indeterminate end note note right of Right Es1 greater than Es2 Vout in phase Positive DC after PSD end note note left of Left Es2 greater than Es1 Vout 180 deg out of phase Negative DC after PSD end note ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Signal-Processing Chain (Block-Level) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Input["Input Stage"] X1[Mechanical Displacement x mm] --> X2[Core Movement
Inside LVDT] end subgraph Transduction["Electromagnetic Transduction"] X2 --> Y1[Primary Excitation
AC Voltage Vp] Y1 --> Y2[Mutual Inductance
Modulation] Y2 --> Y3[Differential
Secondary Voltage] end subgraph Conditioning["Signal Conditioning"] Y3 --> Z1[Phase-Sensitive
Detector] Z1 --> Z2[Low-Pass
Filter] Z2 --> Z3[Amplifier
and Linearizer] end subgraph Output["Output Stage"] Z3 --> W1[DC Voltage
Proportional to x] W1 --> W2[Display or
Data Acquisition] end style X1 fill:#0d47a1,stroke:#000,color:#fff style Y1 fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff style Y2 fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#000,color:#fff style Y3 fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#000,color:#fff style Z1 fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#000,color:#fff style Z2 fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#000,color:#fff style Z3 fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#000,color:#fff style W1 fill:#c62828,stroke:#000,color:#fff style W2 fill:#424242,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ### 4.4 Physical Construction Schematic (ASCII Representation) ``` ◄─── LVDT BODY ───► ┌──────────────────────────┐ │ ┌──S1──┐ ┌─P─┐ ┌──S2──┐ │ ◄── Coils wound on bobbin │ │░░░░░░│ │░░░│ │░░░░░░│ │ │ │██████│ │████│ │██████│ │ ◄── Core (█) slides linearly │ │██████│ │████│ │██████│ │ │ └──────┘ └────┘ └──────┘ │ │ ◄──► ◄──► ◄──► │ │ x₁ x₂ x₃ │ └──────────────────────────┘ │ │ Rod attached to │ measurand ▼ [Object whose displacement is to be measured] ``` **Reading the schematic:** - **S₁** (left secondary), **P** (center primary), **S₂** (right secondary) wound on a common bobbin. - The **core** (shown as █) is centered initially. As the rod moves right, more core enters S₂, increasing $E_{s2}$. - $x_1, x_2, x_3$ are the three reference positions used in calibration. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define LVDT. List any four of its salient features.** **Model Answer:** The **Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT)** is a passive inductive transducer used to convert **linear displacement** into a proportional **electrical voltage signal**. It consists of a single primary coil and two identical secondary coils wound on a cylindrical former, with a movable soft-iron core providing variable magnetic coupling. **Four Salient Features:** 1. **Frictionless operation** — No physical contact between core and coils → infinite theoretical life. 2. **High resolution** — Limited only by the signal conditioning electronics; practically less than **0.1 µm**. 3. **Excellent linearity** — Typically $\pm 0.1\%$ to $\pm 0.5\%$ of full-scale range. 4. **Bidirectional measurement** — Phase-sensitive detection enables both magnitude and direction sensing from a single device. *(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for features, as per KTU key)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Explain the working of an LVDT at its null position. Why is the output voltage zero at this position?** **Model Answer:** At the **null position**, the movable core is **symmetrically placed** with respect to the two secondary coils S₁ and S₂. This symmetry means the **magnetic flux** from the primary coil couples equally into both secondaries. As a result, the EMFs induced in S₁ and S₂ are **equal in magnitude**: $$E_{s1} = E_{s2}$$ Since the secondaries are connected in **series opposition**, the output voltage is: $$V_{out} = E_{s1} - E_{s2} = 0 \, \text{V}$$ The null position is therefore called the **electrical zero** of the LVDT. It serves as the reference point for displacement measurements — any movement from this position produces a non-zero output whose magnitude and phase indicate how far and in which direction the core has moved. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) --- **Q3.A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3 Choice]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of an LVDT. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An LVDT has a sensitivity of **0.5 V/mm**. If the core is displaced by **+3.2 mm** from the null, calculate the output voltage magnitude and comment on its phase. The excitation frequency is **5 kHz**. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q3.A(a):** **Construction (Block Diagram Description):** The LVDT consists of a hollow cylindrical bobbin on which **three coils** are wound: 1. **Primary coil (P)** at the center, energized by an AC source $V_{ex}$. 2. **Secondary coils S₁ and S₂** — one on each side of the primary, wound identically and connected in **series opposition**. 3. A **soft-iron core** (armature) slides linearly inside the bore of the bobbin, attached via a rod to the measurand. 4. The entire assembly is enclosed in a **mu-metal shield** to prevent external EMI. **Working Principle:** - When the core is at the **null position**, the flux linking S₁ equals that linking S₂. Hence $E_{s1} = E_{s2}$ and the output is zero. - When the core **moves toward S₁**, more flux links S₁ → $E_{s1} > E_{s2}$ → $V_{out}$ is **in-phase** with the excitation. - When the core **moves toward S₂**, more flux links S₂ → $E_{s2} > E_{s1}$ → $V_{out}$ is **180° out-of-phase** with the excitation. - The **magnitude** of $V_{out}$ is linearly proportional to displacement $x$ in the linear operating range. **Output Equation:** $$V_{out} = K \cdot x \cdot \cos(\omega t)$$ **Block Diagram:** ``` AC Source │ ▼ ┌──────────────┐ │ Primary P │ ◄── Mutual Induction └──────────────┘ │ ┌─────┴─────┐ ▼ ▼ ┌─────┐ ┌─────┐ │ S₁ │ │ S₂ │ ◄── Core moves └──┬──┘ └──┬──┘ between them │ │ └─────┬──────┘ ▼ Differential Vout │ ▼ Phase-Sensitive Detector + LPF │ ▼ DC Output ``` *(7 marks: 3 for diagram, 2 for working, 1 for output equation, 1 for concluding remark)* --- **Model Solution for Q3.A(b):** **Given Data:** - Sensitivity: $K = 0.5 \, \text{V/mm}$ - Displacement: $x = +3.2 \, \text{mm}$ (positive → toward S₁) - Excitation frequency: $f = 5 \, \text{kHz}$ **To Find:** $\vert V_{out} \vert$ and its phase relationship. **Calculation:** The output voltage magnitude is given by: $$\vert V_{out} \vert = K \cdot \vert x \vert$$ Substituting the values: $$\vert V_{out} \vert = 0.5 \, \text{V/mm} \times 3.2 \, \text{mm}$$ $$\boxed{\vert V_{out} \vert = 1.6 \, \text{V (RMS)}}$$ **Phase Analysis:** Since $x > 0$ (the core has moved **toward S₁**), the flux linking S₁ is greater than that linking S₂. The induced EMF in S₁ dominates, and the output voltage $V_{out}$ is: $$\boxed{\text{In-phase (} 0° \text{ phase shift) with the excitation voltage } V_{ex}}$$ **Note:** The excitation frequency of 5 kHz is irrelevant to the **magnitude** calculation (it only affects the carrier frequency of the output AC signal), but it must be quoted as it would matter if the problem asked for the instantaneous voltage expression. The instantaneous output voltage is: $$V_{out}(t) = 1.6 \sqrt{2} \cos(2 \pi \times 5000 \cdot t) \, \text{V} = 2.263 \cos(31415.9 \, t) \, \text{V}$$ *(7 marks: 2 for formula, 2 for substitution, 1 for answer, 1 for phase reasoning, 1 for instantaneous form)* --- **Q3.B. [ALTERNATIVE 14-Mark Choice]** **(a)** Derive the output voltage equation of an LVDT in terms of mutual inductances. Show that the output is zero at the null position. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare the advantages and limitations of an LVDT with a strain gauge for displacement measurement. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for Q3.A(b) — Brief Outline:** **For (a) — Derivation:** Follow the derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes, presenting each step (mutual inductance formulation, linear approximation $M_1 = M_0 + kx$, $M_2 = M_0 - kx$, substitution, simplification) with clear logical transitions. **For (b) — Comparison Table:** | Parameter | LVDT | Strain Gauge | |---|---|---| | Measurand | Direct linear displacement | Strain (requires conversion) | | Contact type | Non-contact (frictionless) | Bonded contact | | Linearity | Excellent ($\pm 0.1\%$) | Moderate (requires calibration) | | Temperature sensitivity | Low (with compensation) | High (self-heating, thermal expansion) | | Range | Wide (mm to 500 mm) | Very small (µε to mε) | | Cost | Higher | Lower | | Life | Very long (no wear) | Limited by fatigue and bonding | | Output | AC voltage, needs demodulation | Resistance change, needs Wheatstone bridge | | Resolution | Sub-micron | Microstrain level | --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks (Read Carefully):** > > 1. **Confusing units of sensitivity:** Sensitivity $K$ is given in **V/mm**, NOT V. Students often write $\vert V_{out} \vert = K \cdot x$ without checking units and end up with dimensions of V·mm. **Always verify dimensional consistency.** > > 2. **Forgetting the phase information:** When asked about output voltage, examiners expect **both magnitude and phase**. Writing only $\vert V_{out} \vert = 1.6$ V without stating "in-phase with excitation" costs 1–2 marks. > > 3. **Mis-stating the connection topology:** The secondaries are in **series opposition (anti-series)**, NOT in series-aiding. Writing "series" alone or "parallel" is a common error. > > 4. **Forgetting the AC nature of output:** LVDT does NOT give a DC output directly. The differential secondary voltage is AC at the excitation frequency. A **phase-sensitive detector** is required to convert it to DC. Students who claim "LVDT gives DC output" lose 1 mark immediately. > > 5. **Null position misconception:** Some students believe the null position is when the **primary EMF is zero** — it is actually when $E_{s1} = E_{s2}$, giving $V_{out} = 0$. > > 6. **Skipping the block diagram:** In 7-mark questions, the **diagram carries 2–3 marks** in KTU valuation. Drawing the LVDT schematic is non-negotiable. > > 7. **Confusing LVDT with RVDT:** LVDT is **Linear**; RVDT is **Rotary**. Examiners specifically test this distinction. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read This the Night Before the Exam:** - ☐ **LVDT = Linear Variable Differential Transformer** — converts **linear displacement** → **AC voltage**. - ☐ Construction: **1 primary, 2 secondaries (series-opposed), 1 movable core**, all on a common bobbin. - ☐ Operating principle: **Mutual induction** + **variable coupling** controlled by core position. - ☐ Excitation: **AC** (1–25 kHz typical), NOT DC. A DC excitation would produce zero output. - ☐ **Null position** = core centered → $E_{s1} = E_{s2}$ → $V_{out} = 0$. - ☐ **Displacement direction** is encoded in the **phase** (0° or 180°) of $V_{out}$ relative to $V_{ex}$. - ☐ **Displacement magnitude** is encoded in the **amplitude** of $V_{out}$. - ☐ Output equation: $\boxed{V_{out} = K \cdot x \cdot \cos(\omega t)}$, where $K$ is the sensitivity (V/mm). - ☐ Sensitivity depends on: excitation voltage, frequency, core permeability, coil turns, and geometry. - ☐ **Phase-Sensitive Detector (PSD)** is required to convert the AC output to a clean DC signal whose sign indicates direction. - ☐ **Key advantages:** frictionless, infinite resolution theoretically, high linearity, rugged, bidirectional. - ☐ **Key limitations:** requires AC excitation, sensitive to external magnetic fields (needs shielding), dynamic response limited by carrier frequency. - ☐ **Formula to memorize:** $$\vert V_{out} \vert_{RMS} = K \cdot \vert x \vert \quad \text{and} \quad V_{DC,after\,PSD} = \frac{K V_0}{2} \cdot x$$ - ☐ **Single-coil vs. differential:** A single-coil inductive sensor gives an **absolute** output (subject to drift); the LVDT's differential topology **cancels common-mode errors** like temperature drift. - ☐ **Linear range** is approximately $\pm 60\%$ of the total core travel; outside this, saturation occurs. - ☐ **Applications** to remember for descriptive questions: aerospace (control surface feedback), hydraulic cylinders, robotics, nuclear reactor monitoring, CNC machines. - ☐ **Difference between LVDT and LDR:** LVDT = inductive, mechanical, measures displacement; LDR = light-dependent resistor, measures light intensity. Do not confuse in MCQs. - ☐ **RVDT** is the rotational cousin of LVDT — same principle, but the core rotates through an angle (typically up to $\pm 40°$). ---

topics.title: Accelerometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCRUT205 - SENSOR TECHNOLOGY FOR ROBOTICS (ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION) > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Accelerometer # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition An **accelerometer** is a dynamic electromechanical transducer (sensor) designed to measure the **proper acceleration** experienced by an object — defined as the rate of change of velocity with respect to time in its own instantaneous rest frame. In the context of robotics, accelerometers serve as the foundational inertial measurement unit (IMU) component, enabling a robot to detect linear acceleration along one or more orthogonal axes (typically x, y, z). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *"An accelerometer is an electromechanical device that measures acceleration forces. These forces may be static (like the constant force of gravity pulling at 9.81 m/s²) or dynamic (caused by moving or vibrating the accelerometer)."* Mathematically, the measurand is acceleration: $$a = \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{d^2x}{dt^2}$$ The standard SI unit of acceleration is **meters per second squared (m/s²)**. In practical robotics applications, acceleration is also frequently expressed in **g**, where: $$1\,g = 9.80665\,\text{m/s}^2 \approx 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$$ ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Jelly on a Plate" Imagine placing a small block of jelly on a plate: - When the plate is **stationary**, the jelly stays put. The accelerometer measures **1 g** (Earth's gravity). - When you **suddenly push** the plate forward, the jelly lags behind due to inertia. The sensor's internal mass shifts *backward* relative to its housing — this displacement IS the measurement of acceleration. - When you **stop pushing**, the jelly slides forward — the sensor registers **deceleration**. - When you **drop the plate**, both the plate and jelly fall together at the same rate — the accelerometer reads **0 g** (free-fall). This "inertial lag" of an internal proof mass is the core principle behind virtually every accelerometer ever built. ## 1.3 Classification of Accelerometers > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Classification:** Accelerometers are classified based on the **transduction principle** used to convert mechanical displacement into an electrical signal. | Transduction Type | Sensing Mechanism | Typical Application | |-------------------|-------------------|---------------------| | **Capacitive** | Change in capacitance between movable and fixed plates | Smartphones, MEMS IMUs, robotics | | **Piezoelectric** | Voltage generated by stress on a piezo crystal | Vibration monitoring, high-frequency | | **Piezoresistive** | Resistance change under stress | High-g shock, automotive airbags | | **Tunneling** | Quantum tunneling current variation | Ultra-sensitive seismic sensors | | **Thermal (Convection)** | Position of heated air bubble | High-stability industrial sensors | | **Hall-Effect** | Magnetic field deflection | Industrial harsh environments | ## 1.4 Role in Robotics In robotic systems, accelerometers perform three critical functions: 1. **Tilt Sensing** — Static gravity measurement → robot orientation (pitch & roll) 2. **Vibration Analysis** — Dynamic modal analysis of robot arms 3. **Inertial Navigation** — Integration to estimate velocity/position when GPS is unavailable (dead reckoning) > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistake:** An accelerometer cannot directly measure *velocity* or *position*. It must be **integrated** mathematically — a process that introduces drift errors. Never claim an accelerometer "measures velocity." # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Generalized Second-Order Mass-Spring-Damper Model Every accelerometer — regardless of physical construction — can be mathematically modeled as a **single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) second-order mechanical system** consisting of: - A **proof mass (m)** suspended by a spring of stiffness **k** - A **damper** with damping coefficient **c** - A **reference frame (housing)** attached to the object under measurement When the housing accelerates with input acceleration $a_i(t)$, the proof mass experiences an **inertial force** $F = m \cdot a_i(t)$ acting opposite to the direction of motion, causing a relative displacement $x(t)$ between the mass and the housing. ### 2.1.1 Free Body Diagram & Equation of Motion Applying Newton's Second Law to the proof mass in the non-inertial (housing) reference frame: $$m\ddot{x}(t) + c\dot{x}(t) + kx(t) = -m \cdot a_i(t)$$ > [!NOTE] > The **negative sign** arises because the inertial force acts *opposite* to the housing acceleration. The output displacement $x(t)$ is measured relative to the housing. ### 2.1.2 Standard Form Normalization Dividing the entire equation by mass $m$: $$\ddot{x}(t) + 2\zeta\omega_n\dot{x}(t) + \omega_n^2 x(t) = -a_i(t)$$ Where the **natural (undamped) frequency** and **damping ratio** are defined as: $$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \quad [\text{rad/s}]$$ $$\zeta = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{km}} \quad [\text{dimensionless}]$$ ## 2.2 Transfer Function Derivation Taking the Laplace Transform of the standard equation with **zero initial conditions**: $$s^2 X(s) + 2\zeta\omega_n s X(s) + \omega_n^2 X(s) = -A_i(s)$$ $$X(s)\left[s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2\right] = -A_i(s)$$ Therefore the displacement-to-input-acceleration transfer function is: $$\frac{X(s)}{A_i(s)} = \frac{-1}{s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2}$$ ## 2.3 Frequency Response Analysis For sinusoidal input $a_i(t) = A_i \sin(\omega t)$, the steady-state magnitude response is: $$\left|\frac{X(j\omega)}{A_i(j\omega)}\right| = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\left(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2\right)^2 + \left(2\zeta\omega_n\omega\right)^2}}$$ The phase response is: $$\angle\frac{X(j\omega)}{A_i(j\omega)} = -\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2\zeta\omega_n\omega}{\omega_n^2 - \omega^2}\right)$$ ### 2.3.1 Operating Regions | Frequency Region | Condition | Behavior | |------------------|-----------|----------| | **Low Frequency** | $\omega \ll \omega_n$ | Displacement ∝ Input acceleration (usable) | | **Resonance** | $\omega = \omega_n$ | Amplification peak; depends on $\zeta$ | | **High Frequency** | $\omega \gg \omega_n$ | Mass decoupled; output → 0 | | **Useful Band** | $\omega < 0.5\,\omega_n$ | Linear flat response region | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Design Rule:** For an accelerometer to faithfully measure acceleration, the input frequency **must be well below** the natural frequency. A rule of thumb: operate below **$\omega_n / 5$** for less than 5% amplitude error. ## 2.4 Sensitivity and Transduction Coefficients ### 2.4.1 Static Sensitivity (DC / Low-Frequency) As $\omega \to 0$, the static displacement per unit acceleration becomes: $$S_{static} = \lim_{\omega \to 0}\left|\frac{X(j\omega)}{A_i(j\omega)}\right| = \frac{1}{\omega_n^2} = \frac{m}{k}$$ This proves a fundamental design principle: **higher mass and lower stiffness yield higher sensitivity**. ### 2.4.2 Capacitive Sensing Sensitivity For a **differential capacitive accelerometer**, the capacitance change due to displacement $x$ is: $$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0 \mp x}$$ The differential output is: $$\Delta C = C_1 - C_2 = 2\varepsilon_0 A \cdot \frac{x}{d_0^2 - x^2} \approx \frac{2\varepsilon_0 A x}{d_0^2} \quad \text{for } x \ll d_0$$ Where: - $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{F/m}$ (vacuum permittivity) - $A$ = electrode overlap area [m²] - $d_0$ = nominal gap distance [m] ### 2.4.3 Piezoelectric Sensitivity $$V_{out} = d_{33} \cdot \sigma \cdot t = d_{33} \cdot \frac{F}{A} \cdot t$$ Where: - $d_{33}$ = piezoelectric charge coefficient [C/N] - $\sigma$ = applied stress [Pa] - $t$ = piezoelectric layer thickness [m] ### 2.4.4 Piezoresistive Sensitivity $$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \pi_l \cdot \sigma$$ Where $\pi_l$ is the longitudinal piezoresistive coefficient [1/Pa]. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $a = dv/dt = d^2x/dt^2$ | Definition of acceleration | m/s² | | 2 | $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = -m a_i$ | Equation of motion | N (internal) | | 3 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | Natural frequency | rad/s | | 4 | $f_n = \omega_n / (2\pi)$ | Natural frequency in Hz | Hz | | 5 | $\zeta = c / (2\sqrt{km})$ | Damping ratio | dimensionless | | 6 | $Q = 1/(2\zeta)$ | Quality factor | dimensionless | | 7 | $X(s)/A_i(s) = -1/(s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2)$ | Transfer function | s²/m | | 8 | $S_{static} = m/k$ | Static sensitivity | s²/m | | 9 | $1\,g = 9.80665\,\text{m/s}^2$ | Standard gravity | m/s² | | 10 | $\Delta C \approx 2\varepsilon_0 A x / d_0^2$ | Capacitive sensing | F | | 11 | $\omega_{max} \leq 0.2\,\omega_n$ | Safe operating bandwidth | rad/s | ## 2.6 Engineering Utility Across Fields - **Aerospace**: Inertial Navigation Systems (INS), rocket guidance, satellite attitude control - **Automotive**: Airbag deployment triggers, Electronic Stability Control (ESC), rollover detection - **Robotics**: IMU for SLAM, terrain mapping, manipulator vibration suppression - **Consumer Electronics**: Screen rotation, step counting, gaming controllers - **Industrial**: Predictive maintenance via vibration spectral analysis, structural health monitoring # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Equation of Motion from D'Alembert's Principle **Step 1: Identify forces on the proof mass in the inertial (lab) frame** Let $x_m(t)$ be the absolute position of the proof mass and $x_h(t)$ be the absolute position of the housing. The relative displacement is: $$x(t) = x_m(t) - x_h(t)$$ **Step 2: Write absolute acceleration** $$\ddot{x}_m(t) = \ddot{x}(t) + \ddot{x}_h(t) = \ddot{x}(t) + a_i(t)$$ **Step 3: Apply Newton's Second Law in inertial frame** Forces on the mass: spring force $-kx$, damping force $-c\dot{x}$, and (no external driving force in lab frame since the mass is internal): $$m\ddot{x}_m = -kx - c\dot{x}$$ **Step 4: Substitute Step 2 into Step 3** $$m\left[\ddot{x}(t) + a_i(t)\right] = -kx(t) - c\dot{x}(t)$$ **Step 5: Rearrange** $$m\ddot{x}(t) + c\dot{x}(t) + kx(t) = -m \cdot a_i(t) \quad \blacksquare$$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Steady-State Frequency Response Magnitude **Step 1: Substitute $s = j\omega$ into the transfer function** $$\frac{X(j\omega)}{A_i(j\omega)} = \frac{-1}{(j\omega)^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n(j\omega) + \omega_n^2}$$ **Step 2: Simplify $j^2 = -1$** $$= \frac{-1}{-\omega^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n(j\omega) + \omega_n^2} = \frac{-1}{(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2) + j(2\zeta\omega_n\omega)}$$ **Step 3: Compute magnitude** $$\left|\frac{X(j\omega)}{A_i(j\omega)}\right| = \frac{\vert -1 \vert}{\sqrt{(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2)^2 + (2\zeta\omega_n\omega)^2}}$$ Since $|-1| = 1$: $$\left|\frac{X(j\omega)}{A_i(j\omega)}\right| = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2)^2 + (2\zeta\omega_n\omega)^2}} \quad \blacksquare$$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Resonant Frequency and Peak Amplification **Step 1: Define normalized frequency ratio** Let $r = \omega / \omega_n$. Then the magnitude squared becomes: $$M^2(r) = \frac{1}{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}$$ **Step 2: Find extremum by setting $dM^2/dr = 0$** For minimum denominator, take derivative of $(1-r^2)^2 + 4\zeta^2 r^2$: $$\frac{d}{dr}\left[(1-r^2)^2 + 4\zeta^2 r^2\right] = -4r(1-r^2) + 8\zeta^2 r = 0$$ $$-4r(1-r^2) + 8\zeta^2 r = 0$$ Dividing by $4r$ (assuming $r \neq 0$): $$-(1-r^2) + 2\zeta^2 = 0 \implies r^2 = 1 - 2\zeta^2$$ **Step 3: Resonant frequency** $$\omega_{res} = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$$ This exists **only when** $\zeta < 1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707$. **Step 4: Peak magnification** Substituting $r^2 = 1 - 2\zeta^2$: $$M_{peak} = \frac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \quad \blacksquare$$ ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Static Sensitivity from Transfer Function **Step 1: Take the limit as $\omega \to 0$** $$\lim_{\omega \to 0}\left|\frac{X(j\omega)}{A_i(j\omega)}\right| = \lim_{\omega \to 0}\frac{1}{\sqrt{(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2)^2 + (2\zeta\omega_n\omega)^2}}$$ **Step 2: Evaluate** At $\omega = 0$, all terms with $\omega$ vanish: $$S_{static} = \frac{1}{\omega_n^2}$$ **Step 3: Substitute $\omega_n^2 = k/m$** $$S_{static} = \frac{m}{k} \quad \blacksquare$$ ## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam-Style) **Problem:** A MEMS accelerometer has a proof mass of $m = 1\,\mu\text{g} = 1\times 10^{-9}\,\text{kg}$ and a spring constant of $k = 1\,\text{N/m}$. Damping coefficient $c = 1.99\times 10^{-6}\,\text{N·s/m}$. Calculate: (a) Natural frequency $\omega_n$ and $f_n$ (b) Damping ratio $\zeta$ (c) Static sensitivity (d) Output displacement when subjected to $5\,g$ acceleration **Solution:** **(a)** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m} = \sqrt{1 / 10^{-9}} = \sqrt{10^9} = 31622.78\,\text{rad/s}$ $$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{31622.78}{2\pi} \approx 5032.9\,\text{Hz} \approx 5.03\,\text{kHz}$$ **(b)** $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}} = \dfrac{1.99\times 10^{-6}}{2\sqrt{1 \times 10^{-9}}} = \dfrac{1.99\times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 3.162\times 10^{-5}}$ $$\zeta = \frac{1.99 \times 10^{-6}}{6.324 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 0.0315$$ **(c)** $S_{static} = m/k = 10^{-9}/1 = 1\times 10^{-9}\,\text{m·s}^2/\text{m} = 1\,\text{nm per m/s}^2$ **(d)** Acceleration input: $a_i = 5g = 5 \times 9.81 = 49.05\,\text{m/s}^2$ For a static (DC) input, displacement is: $$x = S_{static} \times a_i = 10^{-9} \times 49.05 = 4.905 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{m} = 49.05\,\text{nm}$$ ## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing Accelerometer Type for Robotic Application | Application | Frequency Range | Magnitude | Best Choice | Reason | |-------------|-----------------|-----------|-------------|--------| | Robot tilt/orientation | DC - 50 Hz | ±1 g to ±2 g | Capacitive MEMS | Low noise, low power, DC response | | Robotic arm vibration | 50 Hz - 5 kHz | ±10 g | Piezoelectric | High bandwidth, no DC drift | | Collision detection | DC - 1 kHz | ±50 g to ±100 g | Piezoresistive | High shock survival, wide range | | Spacecraft attitude | DC - 100 Hz | ±0.1 g | Servo / Quartz | Extreme stability | | Seismic robotics (search & rescue) | 0.1 Hz - 500 Hz | ±2 g | Capacitive / Tunneling | Ultra-low noise floor | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Generic Accelerometer Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Input Acceleration
a subscript i] --> B[Mechanical Coupling
Housing] B --> C[Proof Mass m
Inertial Reference] C --> D[Spring Element k
Restoring Force] C --> E[Damper c
Energy Dissipation] D --> F[Relative Displacement x] E --> F F --> G[Transduction Stage
Capacitive / Piezo / Piezoresistive] G --> H[Signal Conditioning
Amplifier and Filter] H --> I[ADC Conversion] I --> J[Digital Output
a in m per s squared] K[Bias Voltage Vref] --> H L[Clock Signal] --> I ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Detailed Capacitive MEMS Accelerometer Signal Path ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA[Applied Acceleration a] --> nodeB[Proof Mass Deflection] nodeB --> nodeC[Change in Gap Distance d] nodeC --> nodeD[Differential Capacitance Delta C] nodeD --> nodeE[Charge Amplifier] nodeE --> nodeF[Synchronous Demodulator] nodeF --> nodeG[Low Pass Filter] nodeG --> nodeH[Output Voltage Vout] nodeH --> nodeI[Calibration and Temperature Compensation] nodeI --> nodeJ[Digital Signal Output] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Process Flow — Piezoelectric Accelerometer Charge Path ```mermaid flowchart LR p1[Mechanical Vibration Input] --> p2[Seismic Mass on Piezo Crystal] p2 --> p3[Charge Generation q equals d33 times F] p3 --> p4[Charge Amplifier with Feedback Capacitor Cf] p4 --> p5[High Pass Filter removes DC Drift] p5 --> p6[Output Voltage proportional to Acceleration] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Frequency Response Characteristic Curve (Schematic Block View) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph LOW["Low Frequency Region omega less than 0.2 times omega_n"] L1[Flat Response] --> L2[Displacement proportional to Acceleration] L2 --> L3[Ideal Operating Region] end subgraph MID["Mid Frequency Region near omega_n"] M1[Resonance Peak] --> M2[Magnitude Amplified by Q factor] M2 --> M3[Avoid this Region for Measurement] end subgraph HIGH["High Frequency Region omega greater than 2 times omega_n"] H1[Roll Off at minus 40 dB per decade] --> H2[Mass decoupled from Housing] H2 --> H3[No useful Output Signal] end LOW --> MID MID --> HIGH ``` ## 4.5 Conceptual Cross-Section View (MEMS Capacitive Accelerometer) ``` ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Hanging Beams (Springs k) │ │ ┌─────┐ │ │ │ │ Proof Mass (m) │ │ │ M │ │ │ │ │ ┌──┬──┬──┐ │ │ └──┬──┘ │C1│C2│C3│ Fixed Electrodes│ │ │ └──┴──┴──┘ │ │ ▼ │ │ [Diff. Capacitance Readout] │ └──────────────────────────────────────┘ ↑ Vibration causes mass to shift → ↑ Capacitances C1, C2, C3 change differentially ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *Define an accelerometer. List the three main types based on transduction principle.* **Model Answer:** An accelerometer is an electromechanical sensor that measures proper (non-gravitational) acceleration by detecting the inertial displacement of an internal proof mass relative to its housing. The output is typically a voltage proportional to the acceleration experienced. The three main types based on transduction are: 1. **Capacitive** — measures displacement via change in capacitance 2. **Piezoelectric** — measures force via charge generated in piezo crystal 3. **Piezoresistive** — measures stress via change in resistance --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *What is the difference between static and dynamic sensitivity of an accelerometer?* **Model Answer:** **Static sensitivity** refers to the sensor's response at very low frequencies (including DC), where the proof mass displacement is directly proportional to the input acceleration according to $x = (m/k) \cdot a$. **Dynamic sensitivity** refers to the response at non-zero frequencies, where the displacement is frequency-dependent due to the inertial and damping properties. The dynamic sensitivity is: $$S(\omega) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2)^2 + (2\zeta\omega_n\omega)^2}}$$ Static sensitivity equals $m/k$, while dynamic sensitivity peaks at the resonant frequency for underdamped systems. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *(a)* Derive the transfer function of a second-order accelerometer modeled as a mass-spring-damper system. *(b)* A capacitive MEMS accelerometer has proof mass $m = 5\times 10^{-9}\,\text{kg}$, spring constant $k = 2\,\text{N/m}$, and damping ratio $\zeta = 0.3$. Determine: (i) natural frequency $f_n$, (ii) static sensitivity, (iii) displacement for $2\,g$ input, (iv) resonant frequency. **(a) Derivation (7 Marks):** Starting from Newton's law on the proof mass in the housing frame: $$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = -m \cdot a_i$$ Taking the Laplace transform (zero initial conditions): $$(ms^2 + cs + k)X(s) = -m A_i(s)$$ Transfer function: $$\boxed{\frac{X(s)}{A_i(s)} = \frac{-m}{ms^2 + cs + k} = \frac{-1}{s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2}}$$ **(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):** **(i)** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m} = \sqrt{2 / 5\times 10^{-9}} = \sqrt{4\times 10^8} = 2\times 10^4\,\text{rad/s}$ $$f_n = \frac{2\times 10^4}{2\pi} \approx 3183.1\,\text{Hz} \approx 3.18\,\text{kHz}$$ **(ii)** $S_{static} = m/k = 5\times 10^{-9}/2 = 2.5\times 10^{-9}\,\text{s}^2/\text{m}$ **(iii)** $a_i = 2g = 2 \times 9.81 = 19.62\,\text{m/s}^2$ $$x = S_{static} \times a_i = 2.5\times 10^{-9} \times 19.62 = 4.905\times 10^{-8}\,\text{m} = 49.05\,\text{nm}$$ **(iv)** $\omega_{res} = \omega_n\sqrt{1-2\zeta^2} = 2\times 10^4\sqrt{1-2(0.09)} = 2\times 10^4\sqrt{0.82}$ $$\omega_{res} \approx 2\times 10^4 \times 0.9055 \approx 18{,}110\,\text{rad/s} \approx 1.81\times 10^4\,\text{rad/s}$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *(a)* With a neat functional block diagram, explain the working principle of a capacitive MEMS accelerometer. *(b)* Compare piezoelectric and piezoresistive accelerometers. What is the role of an accelerometer in a robotic IMU? **(a) Capacitive MEMS Accelerometer Working (7 Marks):** The capacitive MEMS accelerometer consists of a small proof mass suspended by compliant beams between fixed capacitive plates. The functional operation: 1. **Input acceleration** $a_i$ acts on the housing 2. **Proof mass displacement** $x$ relative to housing occurs due to inertia 3. **Capacitance change** — The gap $d_0$ between mass and fixed plates changes: $C_1$ increases while $C_2$ decreases (differential) 4. **Differential capacitance** $\Delta C = C_1 - C_2 \approx 2\varepsilon_0 A x / d_0^2$ 5. **Charge amplifier** converts $\Delta C$ to voltage 6. **Signal conditioning** (filtering, demodulation, amplification) 7. **Digital output** proportional to acceleration Block Diagram (see Section 4.2): Input Acceleration → Proof Mass → Capacitance Change → Charge Amplifier → Demodulator → Output **(b) Comparison Table (7 Marks):** | Parameter | Piezoelectric | Piezoresistive | |-----------|---------------|----------------| | Sensing principle | Charge generation under stress | Resistance change under stress | | DC response | **No** (requires AC/vibration) | **Yes** (measures static acceleration) | | Frequency range | 1 Hz to 30 kHz | DC to 5 kHz | | Sensitivity | High at high freq | High at low freq | | Temperature stability | Good | Requires compensation | | Typical range | ±50 g to ±500 g | ±2 g to ±1000 g | | Output | Charge / Voltage | Resistance change (Wheatstone bridge) | | Application | Vibration analysis, modal testing | Crash sensors, tilt sensing | **Role in Robotic IMU:** An accelerometer in a robotic IMU provides: 1. **Gravity vector measurement** → pitch & roll estimation 2. **Linear acceleration** → dead reckoning when GPS unavailable 3. **Vibration feedback** → active damping control in robotic arms 4. **Combined with gyroscope** (sensor fusion via Kalman filter) → full 6-DOF pose estimation --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU Board Examiner Observations):** > > 1. **Sign Convention Errors** — Forgetting the negative sign in $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = -m a_i$. KTU examiners deduct **1 mark** for missing signs. > 2. **Units Confusion** — Writing $f_n$ when the question asks for $\omega_n$ (rad/s vs Hz). Always specify units explicitly. > 3. **Skipping Intermediate Steps** — In numerical problems, KTU expects the formula substitution step to be shown. Writing only the final answer loses up to **2 marks**. > 4. **Confusing natural frequency with resonant frequency** — $\omega_n \neq \omega_{res}$ for damped systems. Use $\omega_{res} = \omega_n\sqrt{1-2\zeta^2}$ correctly. > 5. **Wrong Capacitance Formula** — Using $C = \varepsilon_0 A / d$ without differential form for MEMS accelerometers. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist - ✅ Accelerometer = inertial sensor measuring proper acceleration - ✅ Core principle: inertial displacement of proof mass vs housing - ✅ Universal model: SDOF mass-spring-damper system - ✅ Governing equation: $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = -m a_i$ - ✅ Transfer function: $X(s)/A_i(s) = -1/(s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2)$ - ✅ Natural frequency: $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ - ✅ Damping ratio: $\zeta = c/(2\sqrt{km})$ - ✅ Static sensitivity: $S = m/k$ - ✅ Resonant frequency: $\omega_{res} = \omega_n\sqrt{1-2\zeta^2}$ - ✅ Operate well below $\omega_n$ (typically $\omega < 0.2\omega_n$) - ✅ Capacitive MEMS: most common in robotics; differential gap sensing - ✅ Piezoelectric: high-frequency vibration; cannot measure DC - ✅ Piezoresistive: wide dynamic range; used in automotive airbag systems - ✅ Application: tilt sensing, IMU, vibration monitoring, navigation - ✅ $1\,g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$ — always quote this when needed - ✅ Accelerometer measures acceleration — never directly velocity or position

topics.title: Gyroscope (Working Principle Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT205 - SENSOR TECHNOLOGY FOR ROBOTICS (ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gyroscope (Working Principle Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Gyroscope** is a precision sensor device that measures, maintains, or determines the **orientation** and **angular velocity** of a system relative to an inertial reference frame. It operates on the fundamental physics principle of **Conservation of Angular Momentum**, wherein a rapidly spinning rotor exhibits resistance to changes in its axis of rotation (a property known as **Rigidity in Space** or **Gyroscopic Inertia**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In robotics, gyroscopes are the cornerstone of **Inertial Measurement Units (IMUs)** used for attitude estimation, heading correction, drone stabilization, humanoid balance control, and SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) navigation. The term *"Gyroscope"* in the syllabus refers strictly to its **Working Principle** — not its manufacturing classification.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Spinning Top on a Tricycle Wheel"

Imagine a child sitting on a **bicycle wheel** suspended by a string from one end of its axle. When the wheel is **not spinning**, it is flimsy — the slightest push tips the axle over immediately because gravity applies an unbalanced torque.

Now imagine the wheel **spinning rapidly**. Suddenly, the axle refuses to fall flat. Instead of tipping downward, the axle **slowly rotates in a horizontal plane** — tracing a circle in the air. The wheel is "fighting" to keep its spin axis pointing in the same direction in space.

> **Real-World Analogy:** This is the same effect that keeps a **spinning top** upright while it dances on a table, and the same effect that makes the **Earth's axis** stay pointed at Polaris (the North Star) throughout its orbit.

| Physical Property | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Spin angular velocity (of rotor) | $\omega$ | **rad/s** |
| Precession angular velocity | $\Omega$ | **rad/s** |
| Moment of inertia of rotor | $I$ | **kg·m²** |
| Angular momentum | $L$ | **kg·m²/s** |
| Earth's rotation rate (reference) | $\omega_E$ | $7.29 \times 10^{-5}$ **rad/s** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constant — Earth's Rotation:** The Earth itself is a giant gyroscope, spinning at $\omega_E \approx 7.29 \times 10^{-5} \text{ rad/s}$. This is the natural inertial reference against which navigational gyroscopes compare their orientation.

## 1.3 Classification Snapshot (Context Only)

While the KTU syllabus asks for the *working principle*, roboticists must be aware of three major families:

1. **Mechanical / Spinning-Mass Gyroscope** — Classic rotor + gimbal assembly; the textbook reference for "gyroscopic effect."
2. **Optical Gyroscope (RLG & FOG)** — Uses interference of light (Sagnac Effect); no moving parts.
3. **MEMS Gyroscope** — Micro-electro-mechanical vibrating structure that exploits the **Coriolis Effect**; dominant in modern consumer and industrial robotics.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Physics — Step-by-Step Logic

A gyroscope's behaviour emerges from **three coupled physical principles**. Master these, and you master the entire topic.

### Step 1: Angular Momentum is a Conserved Vector
A spinning rotor stores **angular momentum** $\vec{L}$, a vector quantity that points along the spin axis (by the right-hand rule).

$$\vec{L} = I \vec{\omega}$$

The rotor wants $\vec{L}$ to remain **fixed in direction** in inertial space. Any attempt to tilt the axis requires a torque — and the rotor responds by **precessing** rather than tilting directly.

### Step 2: Newton's Second Law in Rotational Form
A torque changes angular momentum. If an external torque $\vec{\tau}$ is applied, $\vec{L}$ changes according to:

$$\vec{\tau} = \frac{d\vec{L}}{dt}$$

Because $\vec{L}$ is conserved (no external torque), the axis stays put. This is **Rigidity in Space**.

### Step 3: If a Torque IS Applied → Precession
When gravity or any external torque acts on the spinning axis, the rotor does **not** tip over. Instead, the torque vector causes $\vec{L}$ to rotate *perpendicular* to itself. This slow rotation of the spin axis is called **Precession**.

The torque magnitude for precession is:

$$\tau = I \omega \Omega \sin\theta$$

For the common case where the spin axis is perpendicular to the precession axis ($\theta = 90^\circ$):

$$\boxed{\Omega = \frac{\tau}{I \omega}}$$

### Step 4: MEMS Variant — The Coriolis Trick
A MEMS gyroscope has no spinning wheel. Instead, a proof mass is driven to oscillate linearly at velocity $\vec{v}$. When the entire chip rotates at rate $\vec{\Omega}$, the mass experiences the **Coriolis Force**:

$$\vec{F}_{cor} = -2m (\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v})$$

This sideways force deflects a secondary sensing structure, and the deflection capacitance is read out as a voltage proportional to the input rotation rate.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Units | Applies To |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\vec{L} = I \vec{\omega}$ | Angular momentum of spinning rotor | $\text{kg·m}^2/\text{s}$ | Mechanical Gyro |
| 2 | $\vec{\tau} = \dfrac{d\vec{L}}{dt}$ | Rate of change of angular momentum | N·m | General Rotation |
| 3 | $\tau = I \omega \Omega \sin\theta$ | Gyroscopic torque for precession | N·m | Mechanical Gyro |
| 4 | $\Omega = \dfrac{\tau}{I \omega}$ | Precession angular velocity | rad/s | Mechanical Gyro |
| 5 | $\vec{F}_{cor} = -2m (\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v})$ | Coriolis force on moving mass | N | MEMS Gyro |
| 6 | $V_{out} \propto \Omega$ | Output voltage proportional to rotation rate | V | All Gyros |
| 7 | $I = \dfrac{1}{2} m r^2$ (solid disc) | Moment of inertia of disc rotor | $\text{kg·m}^2$ | Mechanical Gyro |
| 8 | $I = m r^2$ (thin ring) | Moment of inertia of ring rotor | $\text{kg·m}^2$ | Mechanical Gyro |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In humanoid robotics, gyroscopes feed data into the **Complementary Filter** or **Kalman Filter** of the IMU, fusing with accelerometer data to yield drift-corrected pitch and roll. In drones, they form the inner loop of the **PID flight controller**, executing attitude corrections at 1 kHz.

## 2.3 Direction of Precession — Right-Hand Rule Trick

The direction of precession is determined by the right-hand rule applied to the **torque vector**:

1. Point your right-hand fingers along $\vec{L}$ (spin axis, thumb direction).
2. Curl them toward the applied torque $\vec{\tau}$.
3. Your thumb now points in the direction of precession $\vec{\Omega}$.

> **Mnemonic:** *"Torque pushes L sideways — L doesn't tilt, it swings."*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Gyroscopic Precession Rate

We derive the precession angular velocity of a spinning rotor under an applied external torque.

**Given:**
- A symmetric rotor with moment of inertia $I$ about its spin axis.
- Spin angular velocity: $\vec{\omega}$ (along the spin axis $\hat{k}$).
- External torque: $\vec{\tau}$ applied about an axis perpendicular to the spin axis.

**Step 1 — Define the angular momentum vector.**
The angular momentum of the rotor about its center of mass is given by Newton's rotational law:

$$\vec{L} = I \vec{\omega}$$

**Step 2 — Apply an external torque perpendicular to $\vec{L}$.**
Newton's second law in angular form states:

$$\vec{\tau} = \frac{d\vec{L}}{dt}$$

Substituting $\vec{L} = I\vec{\omega}$:

$$\vec{\tau} = I \frac{d\vec{\omega}}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Geometric interpretation of $d\vec{\omega}/dt$.**
Since $|\vec{\omega}|$ (the spin speed) is constant, the change $d\vec{\omega}$ is purely a **change in direction**, not magnitude. A vector that changes only in direction sweeps out an angle $d\phi$ in time $dt$, with magnitude:

$$\left| \frac{d\vec{\omega}}{dt} \right| = \omega \Omega$$

where $\Omega = d\phi / dt$ is the **precession angular velocity**.

**Step 4 — Combine to obtain precession rate.**
Taking the magnitudes of both sides and substituting:

$$\tau = I \omega \Omega$$

Rearranging for $\Omega$:

$$\boxed{\Omega = \frac{\tau}{I \omega}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Higher spin speed $\omega$ → slower precession. This is why high-speed rotors (e.g., 20,000 RPM fibre-optic gyros) are highly stable.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Gyroscopic Reaction Torque

A common KTU question asks: *"If a gyroscope mounted on a robot arm is forced to tilt, what reaction torque does it exert on the arm?"*

**Given:** A rotor spinning at $\omega$ rad/s. The robot arm forces the spin axis to precess at $\Omega$ rad/s (e.g., to keep the robot balanced).

**Step 1 — Use the precession torque equation.**
From derivation 1, the torque required to produce precession at rate $\Omega$ is:

$$\tau_{precess} = I \omega \Omega$$

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's Third Law.**
The reaction torque exerted by the gyroscope on the robot structure is equal in magnitude and opposite in direction:

$$\boxed{\tau_{reaction} = -I \omega \Omega}$$

**Step 3 — Sign convention for robotic actuators.**
Robot joint motors must **overcome** this reaction torque. Hence, the actuator torque rating must satisfy:

$$\tau_{motor} \geq \tau_{reaction} + \tau_{load}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Impact:** This is the reason **reaction wheels** in satellites are sized to deliver thousands of N·m at very low $\Omega$ (using high $I$ and $\omega$) to re-orient spacecraft without consuming propellant.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — MEMS Gyroscope Output via Coriolis Effect

**Given:** A MEMS proof mass $m$ vibrating linearly at velocity $\vec{v}$ in the $x$-direction. The chip (and hence the mass) rotates at $\vec{\Omega} = \Omega \hat{z}$.

**Step 1 — Identify the Coriolis acceleration in the rotating frame.**
In a frame rotating with the chip, a moving particle experiences the Coriolis pseudo-force:

$$\vec{F}_{cor} = -2m (\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v})$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the cross product.**
With $\vec{\Omega} = \Omega \hat{z}$ and $\vec{v} = v \hat{x}$:

$$\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v} = (\Omega \hat{z}) \times (v \hat{x}) = \Omega v (\hat{z} \times \hat{x}) = \Omega v \hat{y}$$

So the Coriolis force acts in the $y$-direction (perpendicular to both motion and rotation):

$$\vec{F}_{cor} = -2m \Omega v \hat{y}$$

**Step 3 — Convert force to displacement of the sensing structure.**
The proof mass is suspended by springs of stiffness $k$. The Coriolis force displaces it by $y$:

$$k y = 2 m \Omega v \quad \Rightarrow \quad y = \frac{2 m v}{k} \Omega$$

**Step 4 — Convert displacement to output voltage.**
The displacement changes the capacitance $C$ of a parallel-plate sensor. The output voltage is:

$$\boxed{V_{out} = K_{gyro} \cdot \Omega \quad \text{where} \quad K_{gyro} = \frac{2 m v V_{ref}}{k d_0}}$$

with $d_0$ being the nominal gap and $V_{ref}$ the reference voltage.

**Interpretation:** The output is **linearly proportional** to the input rotation rate $\Omega$ — the foundational property that makes the gyroscope a useful sensor.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Mechanical Gyroscope Working Principle

This flow captures the physical mechanism of a classic spinning-mass gyroscope from input torque to measured output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[External Disturbance Torque tau_ext] --> A1[Applied to Spin Axis of Rotor]
    A1 --> A2{Is Rotor Spinning at omega?}
    A2 -- No --> A3[Rotor Tips Over - No Gyroscopic Effect]
    A2 -- Yes --> A4[Conservation of Angular Momentum L = I omega]
    A4 --> A5[Torque changes Direction of L not Magnitude]
    A5 --> A6[Spin Axis Rotates Perpendicular to Torque]
    A6 --> A7[This Slow Rotation is PRECESSION at Omega]
    A7 --> A8[Encoder Measures Angular Position vs Inertial Frame]
    A8 --> A9[Output: Angular Velocity or Orientation Angle]
    A9 --> A10[Robot IMU / Flight Controller Reads Value]
    A10 --> A11[Closed-Loop Correction Applied to Actuators]

    style A0 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style A4 fill:#B5EAD7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style A7 fill:#C7CEEA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style A11 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

**Reading the Diagram:** Follow the arrows top-to-bottom. The crucial decision point is **A2** — without spin ($\omega = 0$), there is no gyroscopic effect. This is the entire physical "switch" of the sensor.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — MEMS Gyroscope Working Principle

The MEMS variant replaces the spinning rotor with a vibrating proof mass and the Coriolis effect takes the place of precession.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Drive Oscillator at Frequency f_drive] --> B2[Proof Mass Vibrates with Velocity v in X axis]
    B2 --> B3{Is Chip Rotating at Omega about Z axis?}
    B3 -- No --> B4[No Coriolis Force - No Output]
    B3 -- Yes --> B5[Coriolis Force F_c = 2m Omega x v in Y axis]
    B5 --> B6[Proof Mass Displaces in Y by delta y]
    B6 --> B7[Capacitive Sensing Plates Detect delta y]
    B7 --> B8[Charge Amplifier Converts C to Voltage]
    B8 --> B9[ADC Samples V_out at f_drive]
    B9 --> B10[Microcontroller Computes Omega = V_out / K_gyro]
    B10 --> B11[Output: Angular Velocity in deg per s]

    style B1 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style B5 fill:#B5EAD7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style B7 fill:#C7CEEA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style B11 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

---

## 4.3 System-Level Functional Architecture — Gyroscope in a Robotic IMU

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SENSING_CORE [SENSING CORE]
        C1[Vibrating MEMS Structure]
        C2[Coriolis Pickoff Plates]
        C3[Low-Noise ASIC Front End]
        C1 <--> C2
        C2 --> C3
    end

    subgraph SIGNAL_CHAIN [SIGNAL PROCESSING CHAIN]
        C3 --> D1[Programmable Gain Amplifier]
        D1 --> D2[Sigma Delta ADC]
        D2 --> D3[Digital Decimation Filter]
        D3 --> D4[Temperature Compensation LUT]
    end

    subgraph FUSION [ROBOTIC FUSION LAYER]
        D4 --> E1[Kalman / Complementary Filter]
        E1 --> E2[Quaternion-based Attitude Output]
        E2 --> E3[Robot Controller CAN Bus]
    end

    E3 --> F1((Drone Flight Control))
    E3 --> F2((Humanoid Balance))
    E3 --> F3((Mobile Robot Heading))
    E3 --> F4((AGV / AMR Navigation))

    style C1 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style E1 fill:#B5EAD7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style F1 fill:#C7CEEA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style F2 fill:#C7CEEA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style F3 fill:#C7CEEA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style F4 fill:#C7CEEA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Aid:** The `SENSING CORE` block represents the physics. The `SIGNAL PROCESSING CHAIN` block represents the electronics. The `FUSION` block represents the algorithms. The end-nodes `F1`–`F4` represent real robotic applications — exactly what KTU examiners look for to award full application-level marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**
**Define a gyroscope. State the physical principle on which it works.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **gyroscope** is a device used to measure or maintain orientation and angular velocity based on the principle of **conservation of angular momentum**. *(1 mark)*

The device consists of a **rotor** mounted on gimbals that allows it to spin freely about its axis. *(1 mark)*

When the rotor spins at high angular velocity $\omega$, its angular momentum $\vec{L} = I\vec{\omega}$ is large, and the spin axis tends to maintain its direction in space, resisting external torques. *(1 mark)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**
**What is precession? Write the expression for the precession rate of a gyroscope.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Precession** is the phenomenon in which the spin axis of a gyroscope rotates slowly about a perpendicular axis when an external torque is applied, *instead of tilting* in the direction of the torque. *(2 marks)*

The precession angular velocity $\Omega$ is given by:

$$\Omega = \frac{\tau}{I \omega}$$

where $\tau$ is the applied torque, $I$ is the moment of inertia, and $\omega$ is the spin speed. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT** write only the formula for precession — examiners want the **derivation** with proper use of $\vec{\tau} = d\vec{L}/dt$. Skipping this loses up to **4 marks**.
> 2. **Do NOT** confuse *rigidity in space* (mechanical gyro) with *Coriolis effect* (MEMS gyro). Examiners often set a trick question mixing both. State the principle **explicitly** at the start.
> 3. **Always** draw the direction of $\vec{L}$, $\vec{\tau}$, and $\vec{\Omega}$ using the right-hand rule — a missing diagram can cost you **2 marks**.
> 4. **Numerical mistakes** in $\Omega = \tau/(I\omega)$ — many students divide by $\omega^2$. Double-check before submission.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **construction and working of a mechanical gyroscope**. Derive the expression for its **precession rate**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A mechanical gyroscope consists of:
- A **rotor (spinning wheel)** — symmetric, high moment of inertia $I$, driven by an electric motor at high $\omega$ (typically several thousand RPM).
- A **spindle** — the spin axis of the rotor.
- **Gimbals** — concentric rings allowing the rotor freedom to rotate about three mutually perpendicular axes (3 degrees of freedom).
- **Bearings** — low-friction supports.
- **Frame / housing** — connected to the vehicle (e.g., robot, ship, aircraft).

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
When the rotor spins at angular velocity $\omega$, it possesses angular momentum $\vec{L} = I\vec{\omega}$ along the spin axis. Due to **conservation of angular momentum**, $\vec{L}$ resists change in direction. When an external torque $\tau$ is applied to tilt the axis, $\vec{L}$ does not tilt but instead **precesses** about the torque axis.

**Derivation of Precession Rate (2 marks):**
From $\vec{\tau} = d\vec{L}/dt$ and the geometric relation $d\vec{\omega}/dt = \omega \Omega$ (since $|\vec{\omega}|$ is constant and only its direction changes):

$$\tau = I \omega \Omega \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\Omega = \frac{\tau}{I \omega}}$$

---

**(b)** A **MEMS gyroscope** operates on a different principle. Explain its **working using the Coriolis effect** and derive the relation between its **output voltage and input angular rate**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
A MEMS gyroscope contains a **proof mass** suspended by compliant springs and driven to oscillate linearly at velocity $\vec{v}$ along the drive axis (say, $x$-axis). When the chip rotates at rate $\vec{\Omega}$ (say, about $z$-axis), the moving mass experiences the **Coriolis force**:

$$\vec{F}_{cor} = -2m (\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v})$$

This force acts **perpendicular to both** $\vec{v}$ and $\vec{\Omega}$, deflecting the mass along the $y$-axis. The deflection is sensed capacitively and converted to a voltage proportional to $\Omega$.

**Derivation of Output Voltage (4 marks):**
With $\vec{\Omega} = \Omega \hat{z}$ and $\vec{v} = v \hat{x}$:

$$\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v} = \Omega v (\hat{z} \times \hat{x}) = \Omega v \hat{y}$$

$$\therefore F_{cor} = 2 m \Omega v$$

This force deflects the spring-mass system by $y$ (spring stiffness $k$):

$$k y = 2 m \Omega v \quad \Rightarrow \quad y = \frac{2 m v}{k} \Omega$$

The capacitive sensor (gap $d_0$, area $A$, reference $V_{ref}$) gives:

$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ref} \cdot \varepsilon_0 A}{d_0^2} \cdot y = \frac{2 m v V_{ref} \varepsilon_0 A}{k d_0^2} \cdot \Omega$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} = K_{gyro} \cdot \Omega \quad \text{where} \quad K_{gyro} = \frac{2 m v V_{ref} \varepsilon_0 A}{k d_0^2}}$$

This linear relationship is the basis of MEMS gyroscope calibration.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

**(a)** What is **rigidity in space**? Explain how it is the **foundation of gyroscopic stability** in a spinning rotor, and discuss its **two key engineering consequences** in robotics. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of Rigidity in Space (2 marks):**
**Rigidity in space** (also called **gyroscopic inertia**) is the property of a spinning body by which its **axis of rotation tends to maintain a fixed direction in inertial space**, independent of the rotation or translation of its supporting frame.

This property arises directly from the **conservation of angular momentum** $\vec{L} = I\vec{\omega}$. With $\omega$ large, $L$ is large, and any torque that tries to change the direction of $\vec{L}$ must be correspondingly large to have a noticeable effect in a short time.

**Mathematical Statement (2 marks):**
With $\vec{\tau} = d\vec{L}/dt$ and the geometric precession relation:

$$\Omega = \frac{\tau}{I \omega}$$

For large $I\omega$ (high spin speed), the same applied $\tau$ produces a much smaller precession $\Omega$ — i.e., the axis is "rigid."

**Two Engineering Consequences in Robotics (3 marks):**

1. **Attitude Hold in Drones:** A quadcopter's flight controller uses gyroscope feedback at 1 kHz to maintain level flight. The high spin speed of MEMS proof masses gives strong rigidity, enabling stable hovering even in moderate wind.

2. **Heading Reference for AGVs / AMRs:** In warehouse robots, the gyroscope provides a **short-term stable heading** reference. While drift occurs over long periods, rigidity allows the robot to make tight 90° turns with high precision between absolute corrections (e.g., from wheel encoders or fiducial markers).

---

**(b)** A **robot arm** carries a **spinning disc gyroscope** with moment of inertia $I = 0.05 \text{ kg·m}^2$ rotating at $\omega = 200 \text{ rad/s}$. The arm forces the gyroscope's spin axis to precess at $\Omega = 0.5 \text{ rad/s}$ about a perpendicular axis. Calculate **(i)** the gyroscopic torque, and **(ii)** the reaction torque on the arm's motor. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Moment of inertia: $I = 0.05 \text{ kg·m}^2$
- Spin angular velocity: $\omega = 200 \text{ rad/s}$
- Precession angular velocity: $\Omega = 0.5 \text{ rad/s}$

**(i) Gyroscopic Precession Torque (3 marks):**

$$\tau_{gyro} = I \omega \Omega$$

Substituting values:

$$\tau_{gyro} = 0.05 \times 200 \times 0.5$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{gyro} = 5.0 \text{ N·m}}$$

**(ii) Reaction Torque on the Arm Motor (4 marks):**

By **Newton's Third Law**, the gyroscope exerts an equal and opposite reaction torque on the structure that forces its precession:

$$\tau_{reaction} = -\tau_{gyro} = -5.0 \text{ N·m}$$

The negative sign indicates the reaction torque acts in the **opposite direction** to the precession velocity $\vec{\Omega}$.

The arm's motor must therefore be rated to deliver at least:

$$\tau_{motor} \geq 5.0 \text{ N·m} \quad \text{(plus any payload torque)}$$

**Conclusion:** The arm motor must overcome a 5.0 N·m gyroscopic reaction to maintain controlled motion. Ignoring this in design leads to motor stalling during fast arm rotation — a classic robotics integration failure.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Gyroscope (Working Principle Only)**

- 🔑 **Definition:** Sensor that measures orientation/angular velocity using **Conservation of Angular Momentum**.
- 🔑 **Core Equation:** $\vec{L} = I\vec{\omega}$ (angular momentum of spinning rotor).
- 🔑 **Gyroscopic Inertia / Rigidity in Space:** High $I\omega$ → axis resists tilting.
- 🔑 **Precession:** When torque *is* applied, axis swings *perpendicular* to torque, not in its direction.
- 🔑 **Precession Rate:** $\Omega = \dfrac{\tau}{I \omega}$ — higher spin → slower precession.
- 🔑 **Direction of Precession:** Given by the right-hand rule applied to $\vec{\tau}$ relative to $\vec{L}$.
- 🔑 **Gyroscopic Torque:** $\tau = I \omega \Omega$ — must be overcome by robot actuators.
- 🔑 **MEMS Gyroscope:** No spinning rotor; relies on **Coriolis Effect** $\vec{F}_{cor} = -2m(\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v})$.
- 🔑 **MEMS Output:** $V_{out} = K_{gyro} \cdot \Omega$ — linear with input rotation rate.
- 🔑 **Applications in Robotics:** IMU attitude estimation, drone flight control, humanoid balance, AGV/AMR heading, SLAM inertial navigation.
- 🔑 **Examiner Traps:** (a) Confusing mechanical and MEMS principles, (b) Forgetting to state the conservation law explicitly, (c) Missing the right-hand-rule diagram, (d) Treating $\Omega$ and $\omega$ as the same quantity.
- 🔑 **Earth's Rotation:** $\omega_E \approx 7.29 \times 10^{-5}$ rad/s — the natural inertial reference.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PIR Sensor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCRUT205 - SENSOR TECHNOLOGY FOR ROBOTICS (ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION) > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** PIR Sensor # MODULE 3: PIR SENSOR — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Passive Infrared (PIR) Sensor** is a pyroelectric transducer that detects infrared radiation emitted by objects within its field of view, converting incident thermal radiation into a measurable electrical signal without itself emitting any form of probing energy. In the context of the KTU 2024 robotics curriculum, it is formally classified as a *non-contact, field-effect based, occupancy/motion transducer* used primarily for human-presence detection, autonomous navigation triggers, and security interlocks in robotic systems. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (PCRUT205 / Module 3):** The PIR sensor is grouped under *Thermal/Infrared Transducers* and forms the basis for understanding how mobile robots perceive human presence, intruders, and warm-bodied obstacles without physical contact. The word **"Passive"** in PIR is critical: the sensor does not illuminate, transmit, or radiate any energy into the environment. It only **listens** to the natural blackbody IR emission of objects. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Defining Properties of a PIR Sensor** > 1. It is **pyroelectric** (charge generation depends on temperature *change*, not static temperature). > 2. It is **differential** (uses two sensing slots wired in opposite polarity to reject common-mode signals). > 3. It is **lens-conditioned** (Fresnel/condenser lens defines field of view). > 4. It is **AC-coupled** (responds only to *changes* in IR flux, not steady-state IR levels). ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain-English Explanation Imagine you are sitting quietly in a dark room. You cannot see anyone, but the moment a person walks in, you *feel* a presence because the thermal air currents shift and warmth reaches your skin. A **PIR sensor does exactly the same job for a robot**: it is the robot's "thermal sixth sense" that *feels* the warmth of a passing human body. More specifically: - Your skin detects **temperature change** over time. - A PIR detects **infrared flux change** over time. - The **eye/brain** interpretation is replaced by an **analog comparator + microcontroller** in robotics. - The **focal cone of awareness** of the human eye is replaced by a **Fresnel lens** that segments the room into detection zones. When a warm body (e.g., a human at ~**310 K** body temperature) **crosses the boundary between two detection zones**, the differential pyroelectric element produces a measurable voltage pulse. The robot interprets this pulse as a **motion event**. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value (Human Body Target) | |---|---| | **Blackbody peak wavelength of human IR** | $\lambda_{\text{peak}} \approx 9.4\ \mu\text{m}$ | | Stefan-Boltzmann constant | $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$ | | **Typical human skin emissivity** | $\varepsilon \approx 0.98$ | | Human skin radiative exitance | $M \approx 500\ \text{W/m}^2$ | | **Sensor spectral window** | **8 µm — 14 µm** (long-wave IR / LWIR) | | Typical detection range | 3 m — 7 m (lens dependent) | | Operating voltage | 3.3 V — 5 V DC | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Examiners' Favourite:** Students often confuse PIR with *active* IR (AIR) sensors. Active IR sensors (like the Sharp GP2Y0A21) emit IR and measure reflection. **PIR does not emit** — it only senses. This single fact is a guaranteed 2-mark question in Part A. # DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 The Pyroelectric Effect — The Physical Heart of PIR Certain crystalline materials (notably **Lithium Tantalate — LiTaO₃**, **Lead Zirconate Titanate — PZT**, and **Polyvinylidene Fluoride — PVDF**) possess a permanent spontaneous electric polarization $P_s$. When the temperature of the crystal changes, the magnitude of $P_s$ changes, causing a measurable surface charge displacement. This is the **pyroelectric effect**. The constitutive equation governing the pyroelectric current generated is: $$i_p = p \, A \, \frac{dT}{dt}$$ where: - $i_p$ = pyroelectric current (Amperes) - $p$ = pyroelectric coefficient (C/m²·K) — material property - $A$ = area of the sensing element (m²) - $dT/dt$ = rate of change of sensor temperature (K/s) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Passive" Reason Why PIR is a Motion Sensor:** Notice that the current $i_p$ is proportional to $dT/dt$, **not** $T$. A perfectly still warm body produces *zero* signal. Motion is *required* to create a temperature gradient across the crystal surface. ## 2.2 The Differential Sensing Architecture Every commercial PIR module (e.g., HC-SR501) uses **two identical pyroelectric elements** mounted side-by-side inside a TO-5 or TO-39 metal can. They are wired in **series-opposition (anti-series)** configuration: $$V_{\text{out}}(t) = K \cdot \left[ \Phi_1(t) - \Phi_2(t) \right]$$ where $\Phi_1(t)$ and $\Phi_2(t)$ are the instantaneous IR fluxes falling on the two elements, and $K$ is the system transimpedance gain (V/W). ### Why differential? — Step-by-step logic: 1. **Common-mode rejection:** If the entire background warms up (sunrise, room heater turns on), $\Phi_1 \approx \Phi_2$, so $V_{\text{out}} \approx 0$. The sensor is immune to ambient thermal drift. 2. **Motion selectivity:** When a warm body crosses from Zone A (incident on Element 1) to Zone B (incident on Element 2), the two fluxes differ transiently, producing a clean bipolar pulse. 3. **EMI/RFI immunity:** External electrical noise couples equally into both elements and cancels. ## 2.3 The Fresnel Lens — Field-of-View Shaping A **Fresnel lens** (segmented plano-convex polyethylene lens) is placed in front of the can. It focuses IR onto the two elements in a pattern of alternating "fingers" or "zones." As a person walks, they cross these zones alternately, producing a characteristic **alternating positive-negative pulse train** — the *signature of human motion*. The thin-lens focal equation governs lens placement: $$\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{u}$$ For a typical PIR module, the lens is designed such that the IR focal points of zones A and B land precisely on Elements 1 and 2 respectively. ## 2.4 Signal Conditioning with BISS0001 The raw pyroelectric signal is in the **microvolt to millivolt** range. A **BISS0001** (or equivalent) ASIC is used for: - **Two-stage op-amp amplification** (gain typically 70 dB — 80 dB) - **Bandpass filtering** (typically 0.3 Hz — 10 Hz, eliminating DC drift and high-frequency noise) - **Window comparator** with adjustable threshold - **Retriggerable monostable multivibrator** for pulse output The total voltage gain of the analog front-end is: $$V_{\text{final}} = G_1 \cdot G_2 \cdot V_{\text{pyro}} = G_{\text{total}} \cdot V_{\text{pyro}}$$ ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Concept | Equation | Symbols | Units | |---|---|---|---| | Stefan-Boltzmann Radiative Exitance | $M = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | $\varepsilon$, $\sigma$, $T$ | W/m² | | Wien's Displacement Law (peak wavelength) | $\lambda_{\text{peak}} = b/T$, $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K | $b$ (Wien's constant) | m | | Pyroelectric Current | $i_p = p A \dfrac{dT}{dt}$ | $p, A, dT/dt$ | A | | Differential Output Voltage | $V_{\text{out}} = K(\Phi_1 - \Phi_2)$ | $K$ (V/W) | V | | Total Analog Gain | $G_{\text{total}} = G_1 \cdot G_2$ | $G_1, G_2$ (stage gains) | dimensionless | | Noise-Equivalent Power (NEP) | $\text{NEP} = \dfrac{V_n}{R_v \sqrt{\Delta f}}$ | $V_n$, $R_v$, $\Delta f$ | W/√Hz | | Detectivity | $D^* = \dfrac{\sqrt{A}}{\text{NEP}}$ | $A$ (area) | cm·Hz$^{1/2}$/W | | Specific Detectivity (figure of merit) | $D^* = \dfrac{\sqrt{A \Delta f}}{V_n / R_v}$ | — | cm·Hz$^{1/2}$/W | | Fresnel Lens Imaging | $1/f = 1/v - 1/u$ | $f, v, u$ | m | | Retriggerable Mono Time | $T_x = 24576 \cdot R_{x} C_{x}$ | $R_x, C_x$ (BISS0001 pins) | s | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Many students write Stefan-Boltzmann as $M = \sigma T^4$ — missing the emissivity term $\varepsilon$. For human skin $\varepsilon \approx 0.98$, but for polished metal it can be 0.05. **Always include $\varepsilon$ in board answers.** ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility in Robotics - **Autonomous security patrolling robots** — Detect intruders in restricted zones. - **Service/assistant robots** — Wake up when a human approaches to save power. - **AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle) safety bumpers** — Complement LiDAR with thermal sensing where optical sensors fail (smoke, dust, fog). - **Sanitation and elderly-care robots** — Detect human fall events (sudden IR signature change). - **Smart home integration** — Trigger HVAC, lighting, and entertainment systems on human entry. # STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation: Peak Wavelength of Human Blackbody Emission A human body at skin temperature $T \approx 306.15\ \text{K}$ ($33\ ^\circ\text{C}$) is an almost perfect blackbody in the long-wave IR region. We want the wavelength at which the spectral exitance is maximum. **Step 1 — Apply Wien's Displacement Law.** By Wien's Displacement Law, the peak spectral radiance of a blackbody occurs at: $$\lambda_{\text{peak}} \cdot T = b$$ where $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m·K}$ is Wien's displacement constant. **Step 2 — Rearrange for $\lambda_{\text{peak}}$.** $$\lambda_{\text{peak}} = \frac{b}{T}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the numerical value.** $$\lambda_{\text{peak}} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m·K}}{306.15\ \text{K}}$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the quotient.** $$\lambda_{\text{peak}} = 9.466 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}$$ $$\lambda_{\text{peak}} \approx 9.47\ \mu\text{m}$$ **Step 5 — Compare to PIR spectral window.** The PIR sensor's spectral window is **8 µm — 14 µm**. Since $9.47\ \mu\text{m}$ lies inside this window, the human body is a strong source for the PIR sensor. ✔ ## 3.2 Derivation: Total Blackbody Radiative Exitance of Human Skin **Step 1 — Apply Stefan-Boltzmann Law with emissivity.** $$M = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the constants.** $$M = (0.98)(5.67 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4)(306.15\ \text{K})^4$$ **Step 3 — Compute the fourth power of $T$.** $$(306.15)^4 = (306.15)^2 \cdot (306.15)^2 = 93727.82 \cdot 93727.82 \approx 8.787 \times 10^9$$ **Step 4 — Multiply the constants.** $$M = 0.98 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 8.787 \times 10^9$$ **Step 5 — Carry out the arithmetic.** $$M = 0.98 \times 498.20\ \text{W/m}^2$$ $$M \approx 488.24\ \text{W/m}^2$$ **Interpretation:** Each square metre of human skin radiates approximately **488 W** of IR power. A typical exposed skin area of $0.5\ \text{m}^2$ thus radiates ~**244 W** total, which is well above the noise floor of any commercial PIR. ## 3.3 Derivation: Pyroelectric Current from a Step IR Flux Suppose a step change of IR flux $\Delta \Phi$ (in W/m²) falls on a LiTaO₃ element of area $A$ and the thermal time constant of the element is $\tau$. The temperature rise is: $$T(t) = \frac{\Delta \Phi \cdot A}{C_{\text{th}}} \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right)$$ where $C_{\text{th}}$ is the thermal capacitance (J/K) of the element. Differentiating with respect to time: $$\frac{dT}{dt} = \frac{\Delta \Phi \cdot A}{C_{\text{th}} \cdot \tau} e^{-t/\tau}$$ Substituting into the pyroelectric current equation: $$i_p(t) = p A \cdot \frac{\Delta \Phi \cdot A}{C_{\text{th}} \cdot \tau} e^{-t/\tau}$$ $$\boxed{\,i_p(t) = \frac{p A^2 \Delta \Phi}{C_{\text{th}} \tau} e^{-t/\tau}\,}$$ This shows the output is a **decaying exponential pulse** peaking at $t = 0$ with amplitude $i_{p,\text{peak}} = pA^2\Delta\Phi / (C_{\text{th}}\tau)$. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity vs. Noise Trade-off | Parameter | Effect of Increasing | Engineering Trade-off | |---|---|---| | Sensing area $A$ | Increases $i_p$ by $A^2$ | Larger sensor = narrower FOV | | Pyroelectric coefficient $p$ | Higher current | Limits material choice (LiTaO₃ best) | | Thermal time constant $\tau$ | Slower decay = better low-freq | Limits max motion speed detectable | | Amplifier gain $G_{\text{total}}$ | Boosts weak signals | Amplifies noise too; saturation risk | | Bandwidth $\Delta f$ | Faster response | More noise integrated | | Lens focal length $f$ | Tighter zones = better resolution | Smaller detection footprint | ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example > **Problem (KTU Style):** A PIR module uses a LiTaO₃ sensor with $p = 230\ \mu\text{C/m}^2\text{K}$, $A = 1\text{ mm}^2 = 1 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2$, and detects a human at 4 m. The IR flux change at the element is $\Delta\Phi = 0.5\ \text{W/m}^2$ and the thermal time constant is $\tau = 0.25\ \text{s}$. The element thermal capacitance is $C_{\text{th}} = 4 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{J/K}$. The analog gain is $G_{\text{total}} = 70\ \text{dB} = 3162$. Find the peak output voltage. **Solution:** $$i_{p,\text{peak}} = \frac{p A^2 \Delta\Phi}{C_{\text{th}} \tau}$$ $$i_{p,\text{peak}} = \frac{(230 \times 10^{-6})(1 \times 10^{-6})^2 (0.5)}{(4 \times 10^{-5})(0.25)}$$ $$i_{p,\text{peak}} = \frac{1.15 \times 10^{-16}}{1.0 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.15 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{A}$$ Now convert current to voltage via the front-end transimpedance $R_f \approx 10\ \text{k}\Omega$: $$V_{\text{pyro}} = i_{p,\text{peak}} \cdot R_f = (1.15 \times 10^{-11})(1 \times 10^4) = 1.15 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{V} \approx 0.115\ \mu\text{V}$$ After amplification: $$V_{\text{final}} = G_{\text{total}} \cdot V_{\text{pyro}} = 3162 \times 1.15 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{V}$$ $$V_{\text{final}} \approx 3.64 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{V} = 0.364\ \text{mV}$$ This is a typical raw signal level before the comparator threshold. The BISS0001 comparator typically triggers at **3 mV — 5 mV**, so multiple sensing cycles may be needed to confirm motion. # STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — PIR Sensor Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[IR Source
Human Body] --> B[Fresnel Lens
Polyethylene Segments] B --> C[Window 1] B --> D[Window 2] C --> E[Pyroelectric
Element 1] D --> F[Pyroelectric
Element 2] E --> G[Differential
JFET Stage] F --> G G --> H[Bandpass Filter
0.3 to 10 Hz] H --> I[Two Stage
Amplifier] I --> J[Window Comparator
Threshold] J --> K[Retriggerable
Monostable] K --> L[Digital Output
HIGH or LOW] M[Oscillator 16 kHz] --> K N[Voltage Regulator
