> - **Topic:** Photovoltaic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Photovoltaic Effect (PV Effect):** The photovoltaic effect is a physical and chemical phenomenon in which the exposure of a semiconductor material (typically a p-n junction) to incident photons of sufficient energy (E_photon ≥ E_gap) results in the direct generation of a voltage and the flow of electric current without any external bias supply. This self-generating electrical behaviour arises from the photoexcitation of charge carriers (electron-hole pairs) within the depletion region of the junction and their subsequent separation by the built-in electric field.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Photovoltaic sensors are classified as **self-generating (active) sensors** because they produce their own electrical output signal (EMF or current) directly from the incident optical energy, requiring **no external excitation voltage**. This places them in sharp contrast to photoresistive (LDR) and photoconductive sensors which require a bias source.

### 1.1.1 Device Under Study — The Photovoltaic Cell (Solar Cell)

A **photovoltaic cell** (also called a *solar cell* or *PV cell*) is the fundamental solid-state device that converts incident optical radiation (sunlight or any other light source) directly into usable direct-current (DC) electrical power through the photovoltaic effect. A typical silicon PV cell produces a terminal open-circuit voltage ($V_{oc}$) of approximately **0.6 V to 0.7 V** under standard test conditions (STC: 1000 W/m² irradiance, AM 1.5G spectrum, 25 °C cell temperature).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Light-Driven Water Wheel" Analogy for the Photovoltaic Effect**
>
> Imagine a small village built beside a fast-flowing river. The villagers need a wheel that turns to grind grain, but they have no electricity. They build a wheel with small paddles and place it on the river. The flowing water strikes the paddles and rotates the wheel — generating energy purely from the water's natural motion.
>
> In a **photovoltaic cell**, replace the **river** with the **incoming beam of light (photons)**, and the **paddles** with the **electrons bound in the silicon crystal lattice**. Photons striking the silicon surface collide with bound electrons. If the photon carries enough energy (greater than the silicon bandgap, $E_g \approx 1.12 \text{ eV}$ for Si at 300 K), it kicks an electron out of its bond, freeing it to move. The internal electric field at the p-n junction acts like a one-way channel that sweeps the freed electrons to one terminal and the holes to the other — producing a voltage.
>
> **The key insight:** No battery, no external current — the light itself becomes the source of energy. This is why PV sensors are termed "self-powered" and are highly desirable in remote or autonomous robotic systems (e.g., solar-powered agricultural drones, Mars rovers).

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded for Quick Reference)

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Standard Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Speed of light in vacuum | $c$ | $\mathbf{2.998 \times 10^8}$ | m/s |
| Planck's constant | $h$ | $\mathbf{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}$ | J·s |
| Boltzmann's constant | $k$ | $\mathbf{1.381 \times 10^{-23}}$ | J/K |
| Elementary charge | $q$ | $\mathbf{1.602 \times 10^{-19}}$ | C |
| Thermal voltage (at 300 K) | $V_T = kT/q$ | $\mathbf{0.02585}$ | V |
| Silicon bandgap energy (300 K) | $E_g$ | $\mathbf{1.12}$ | eV |
| GaAs bandgap energy (300 K) | $E_g$ | $\mathbf{1.42}$ | eV |
| Standard Test Irradiance (STC) | $G$ | $\mathbf{1000}$ | W/m² |
| Standard Cell Temperature | $T$ | $\mathbf{298.15}$ | K (25 °C) |
| AM 1.5G Reference Spectrum | — | ASTM G173 | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why bandgap matters:** A photon must satisfy $E_{photon} = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} \geq E_g$ to liberate an electron. For silicon ($E_g = 1.12$ eV), this gives a maximum wavelength $\lambda_{max} = \dfrac{1.24}{E_g(\text{eV})} \approx 1.107 \ \mu\text{m}$. Photons with $\lambda > 1.107 \ \mu\text{m}$ (infrared) pass through silicon without generating current — this is the **cutoff wavelength**.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Photovoltaic Devices

Photovoltaic devices may be classified by the following criteria (relevant to robotic sensor selection):

1. **By Material System:**
   - **Crystalline Silicon:** Monocrystalline (m-Si), Polycrystalline (p-Si)
   - **Thin-Film:** Amorphous Silicon (a-Si), Cadmium Telluride (CdTe), Copper Indium Gallium Selenide (CIGS)
   - **III-V Compound Semiconductors:** Gallium Arsenide (GaAs), Indium Phosphide (InP)
   - **Emerging:** Perovskite, Dye-Sensitized, Organic PV (OPV)

2. **By Application Class:**
   - **Photodiodes (Photovoltaic Mode):** Small-area, precision light measurement
   - **Solar Cells:** Large-area, power generation
   - **Position-Sensitive Detectors (PSD):** Lateral-effect, used in robot line-following and sun-tracking

3. **By Operating Mode:**
   - **Photovoltaic (zero-bias) mode** — for sensor/measurement applications
   - **Photoconductive (reverse-biased) mode** — for high-speed photodetection
   - **Solar power (forward-bias, near MPP) mode** — for energy harvesting

---

## 1.5 Historical & Engineering Context

The photovoltaic effect was first observed by **Alexandre Edmond Becquerel in 1839**, and the first practical silicon solar cell was fabricated at **Bell Laboratories in 1954** with an initial efficiency of approximately **6 %**. Modern research-grade multijunction concentrator cells have reached efficiencies exceeding **47 %** under concentrated sunlight. This evolution makes PV technology one of the most mature and well-characterized energy-harvesting mechanisms available to robotic systems engineers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Principle (Why & How)

The operation of a photovoltaic cell is best understood by tracing the fate of a single incident photon through the device:

### Step 1 — Photon Absorption
A photon of energy $E_{photon} = h\nu$ strikes the semiconductor surface. If $E_{photon} \geq E_g$, the photon penetrates the lattice and is absorbed by an electron in the valence band.

> **Why?** Photons below the bandgap cannot excite electrons across the forbidden band; they simply transmit through the material. This is the origin of the *spectral response* of the device.

### Step 2 — Electron-Hole Pair Generation
The absorbed photon promotes a valence-band electron into the conduction band, leaving behind a positively charged hole in the valence band. This produces a free **electron-hole pair (EHP)**.

### Step 3 — Charge Separation at the Depletion Region
If the EHP is generated within or near the depletion region of the p-n junction, the built-in electric field ($E_{bi}$) immediately separates the two carriers: electrons are pushed toward the n-side, holes toward the p-side.

> **Why is this necessary?** Without the field, the carriers would recombine within nanoseconds and the energy would be lost as heat or light. The p-n junction provides the asymmetry that enables net current flow.

### Step 4 — Collection at the External Circuit
The separated electrons flow through the external load (the robot's circuitry) and return to the p-side contact, where they recombine with the accumulated holes. This closed loop constitutes a **photocurrent $I_{ph}$**.

### Step 5 — Terminal Voltage Development
The accumulation of electrons on the n-side and holes on the p-side produces a measurable potential difference — the **photovoltage** — across the device terminals.

> **Important nuance:** In a sensor application, the PV cell is operated in **open-circuit** (voltage-mode) or **short-circuit** (current-mode) conditions, depending on whether the light intensity is to be measured by voltage or by current. In a power application, the cell operates at a load chosen to extract maximum power (the Maximum Power Point, MPP).

---

## 2.2 The Five Characteristic Parameters of a PV Cell

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Definition | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Short-Circuit Current | $I_{sc}$ | Current when terminals are shorted (V = 0) | Directly proportional to light intensity — basis of *photometric* sensors |
| 2 | Open-Circuit Voltage | $V_{oc}$ | Voltage when terminals are open (I = 0) | Logarithmic dependence on intensity; used in *radiometric* sensors |
| 3 | Maximum Power Point | $P_{max}$ | Peak of the I-V curve (V·I product) | Operating target for *energy-harvesting* robots |
| 4 | Fill Factor | $FF$ | Ratio $P_{max} / (V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc})$ | Quality metric of the junction (typical Si: 0.7–0.85) |
| 5 | Conversion Efficiency | $\eta$ | Ratio $P_{out} / P_{in}$ | Overall device figure-of-merit (commercial Si: 15–22 %) |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER THIS TABLE — these 8 equations appear in 80 %+ of KTU numerical questions on photovoltaic devices.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variable Meaning | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Photon Energy | $E_{photon} = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | $h$=6.626×10⁻³⁴ J·s, $c$=3×10⁸ m/s | Joules (J) or eV |
| 2 | Cutoff Wavelength | $\lambda_c = \dfrac{1.24}{E_g(\text{eV})}$ | $E_g$ = bandgap energy | Micrometres (µm) |
| 3 | Photocurrent (Generation Rate) | $I_{ph} = q \cdot G \cdot A \cdot (L_n + L_p)$ | $G$ = generation rate (cm⁻³s⁻¹), $A$ = area, $L_n,L_p$ = diffusion lengths | Amperes (A) |
| 4 | Ideal Solar Cell I-V Equation | $I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV}{kT}\right) - 1\right]$ | $I_0$ = reverse saturation current (A) | Amperes (A) |
| 5 | Open-Circuit Voltage | $V_{oc} = \dfrac{kT}{q} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1\right) \approx \dfrac{kT}{q}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0}\right)$ | — | Volts (V) |
| 6 | Fill Factor | $FF = \dfrac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$ | — | Dimensionless (0 ≤ FF ≤ 1) |
| 7 | Conversion Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{G \cdot A}$ | $G$ = irradiance (W/m²), $A$ = cell area | Dimensionless (0 ≤ η ≤ 1) |
| 8 | Empirically Approximated Fill Factor | $FF_0 = \dfrac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72)}{v_{oc} + 1}$ | where $v_{oc} = V_{oc}/(kT/q)$ | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL TYPESETTING NOTE (KTU Board Exam):** In your answer scripts, always use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute-value notation in tables (e.g., $\vert x \vert$ instead of $|x|$). Markdown table syntax treats the literal `|` character as a column separator, which will corrupt the table layout in your study notes. This same care should be exercised when copying equations into tabular viva-question answer sheets.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The photovoltaic effect is not merely a textbook construct — it is the engine of an enormous and rapidly expanding industrial ecosystem. Its engineering utility can be categorized into three domains relevant to robotics:

1. **Power Generation for Autonomous Robots:** Solar arrays on unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), planetary exploration rovers (e.g., NASA Perseverance's MMRTG-augmented solar panels), and agricultural field robots.

2. **Optical Sensing and Positioning:** Lateral-effect position-sensitive detectors (PSDs) and quadrant photodiodes exploit the photovoltaic effect to provide sub-millimetre positional feedback for sun-tracking solar concentrators and laser alignment in industrial robot arms.

3. **Light-Flux Measurement in Robotic Vision:** Precision photometry, exposure control in camera-based robot vision systems, and UV/IR detection for hazard-avoidance sensors.

> [!TIP]
> **Practical robotics context:** In a sun-seeking solar robot (a common B.Tech project), a pair of photodiodes mounted on either side of a vertical baffle forms a differential light sensor. The difference in their short-circuit currents is used to drive the robot's steering motors, keeping it aligned with the brightest light source. This is a direct application of the photovoltaic effect in motion control.

---

## 2.5 Equivalent Circuit of a Practical PV Cell

A real photovoltaic cell deviates from the ideal model because of two parasitic resistances:

$$\begin{aligned}
I &= I_{ph} - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{q(V + I R_s)}{kT}\right) - 1\right] - \dfrac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}
\end{aligned}$$

- **$R_s$ (Series Resistance):** Arises from ohmic losses in the bulk semiconductor and the metal contacts. Ideally $R_s \to 0$.
- **$R_{sh}$ (Shunt Resistance):** Represents leakage current paths across the junction. Ideally $R_{sh} \to \infty$.

**Engineering impact:** Increasing $R_s$ reduces the fill factor by tilting the I-V curve. Reducing $R_{sh}$ creates an alternative current path that bypasses the load, also reducing $P_{max}$. Both must be minimized for high-quality cells.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Photocurrent from Incident Photon Flux

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** To derive a closed-form expression for the short-circuit photocurrent $I_{ph}$ in terms of the incident optical power, the quantum efficiency, and the elementary charge.

### Step 1 — Define the Photon Flux

Let the incident light have a power density (irradiance) of $G$ measured in W/m². The photon energy for a monochromatic source of wavelength $\lambda$ is:

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{photon} = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}
\end{aligned}$$

The number of photons striking the cell per unit area per unit time — the *photon flux* $\Phi$ — is therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Phi = \dfrac{G}{E_{photon}} = \dfrac{G \lambda}{hc}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Introduce Quantum Efficiency

Not every incident photon generates a collected electron. The **external quantum efficiency** $\eta_{QE}$ (a fraction between 0 and 1) captures all optical and electrical losses — reflection, transmission, recombination, contact shadowing, etc. The number of collected electron-hole pairs per photon is:

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{EHP} = \Phi \cdot \eta_{QE} \quad \left[\text{photons s}^{-1}\text{ m}^{-2}\right]
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Convert to Current

Each collected electron carries a charge $q$. Multiplying by the device active area $A$ gives the short-circuit current:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{sc} = I_{ph} = q \cdot \Phi \cdot \eta_{QE} \cdot A
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting the photon-flux expression:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{ph} = \dfrac{q \eta_{QE} A G \lambda}{hc}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation

This derivation shows that the photocurrent depends **linearly** on (a) irradiance $G$ and (b) wavelength $\lambda$, and is scaled by the geometric area $A$ of the cell. This linearity is the basis of using PV cells as calibrated light meters.

> [!TIP]
> **Numerical sanity check (KTU standard problem):** If $G = 1000$ W/m², $\lambda = 0.9 \ \mu\text{m}$, $\eta_{QE} = 0.85$, $A = 100 \text{ cm}^2 = 0.01 \text{ m}^2$, then $I_{ph} = \dfrac{(1.6\times10^{-19})(0.85)(0.01)(1000)(0.9\times10^{-6})}{(6.626\times10^{-34})(3\times10^8)} \approx 0.616$ A. This is a realistic $I_{sc}$ for a 10 cm × 10 cm silicon cell.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Open-Circuit Voltage from the Ideal Diode Equation

### Step 1 — Start with the Ideal Solar Cell Equation

$$\begin{aligned}
I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV}{kT}\right) - 1\right]
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Open-Circuit Condition

At the open-circuit terminal, no current flows through the external circuit, so $I = 0$. Substituting $I = 0$:

$$\begin{aligned}
0 = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV_{oc}}{kT}\right) - 1\right]
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Rearrange

$$\begin{aligned}
I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV_{oc}}{kT}\right) - 1\right] = I_{ph}
\end{aligned}$$

Since the exponential term dominates (typically $\exp(qV_{oc}/kT) \gg 1$), we approximate:

$$\begin{aligned}
\exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV_{oc}}{kT}\right) \approx \dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $V_{oc}$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{oc} = \dfrac{kT}{q} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key engineering insight:** Because $I_{ph}$ is *linear* in $G$ but $V_{oc}$ is *logarithmic* in $G$, a **100-fold increase in light intensity roughly doubles the open-circuit voltage**, but increases the short-circuit current by 100×. **For light-intensity measurement, the short-circuit current is far more linear and is therefore preferred** in photometric robotic sensors. **For energy harvesting, $V_{oc}$ is the figure of merit** because it determines whether a load can be driven.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Fill Factor and Conversion Efficiency

### Step 1 — Define the Fill Factor

By definition:

$$\begin{aligned}
FF = \dfrac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} = \dfrac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}
\end{aligned}$$

where $V_{mp}$ and $I_{mp}$ are the voltage and current at the maximum-power point.

### Step 2 — Locate the Maximum Power Point

The maximum-power condition is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{dP}{dV} = \dfrac{d(IV)}{dV} = I + V \dfrac{dI}{dV} = 0
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Efficiency Definition

The power conversion efficiency $\eta$ is the electrical output power $P_{max}$ divided by the optical input power $P_{in} = G \cdot A$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{G \cdot A}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **master efficiency equation** for any PV cell and is universally the final answer to "calculate the efficiency" type KTU questions.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Photovoltaic vs. Other Optical Sensors

| Property | Photovoltaic (PV) | Photoconductive (PC) | Photoemissive | Bolometer |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| External bias | **None** | Reverse bias | High voltage | None / small |
| Output | $V$ or $I$ proportional to $G$ | $I$ proportional to $G$ | Electron current | Resistance change |
| Spectral range | Visible–NIR (material-dependent) | Visible–IR | UV–Visible | Broadband (IR) |
| Speed | Moderate | Fast | Very fast | Slow |
| Used for | **Power + Sensing** | Light sensing | Photomultipliers | Thermal imaging |
| Typical in robotics | Sun-tracking, line following | Encoders, light barriers | — | — |

---

## 3.5 Worked KTU-Style Numerical Example

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** A silicon photovoltaic cell has the following measured parameters under STC: $I_{sc} = 3.2$ A, $V_{oc} = 0.65$ V, $P_{max} = 1.56$ W, and the active area is $A = 100$ cm². Calculate (a) the fill factor, (b) the conversion efficiency.

### Solution (Step-by-Step):

**Part (a) — Fill Factor:**

$$\begin{aligned}
FF &= \dfrac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} \\
&= \dfrac{1.56 \text{ W}}{(0.65 \text{ V})(3.2 \text{ A})} \\
&= \dfrac{1.56}{2.08} \\
&= 0.75
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (b) — Efficiency:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{in} &= G \cdot A = (1000 \text{ W/m}^2)(100 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2) = 10 \text{ W} \\
\eta &= \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{1.56 \text{ W}}{10 \text{ W}} = 0.156 = 15.6 \text{ \%}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU marking pattern:** Always show the **unit cancellation explicitly** in your answer script. Examiners award 1 mark for correct units in numerical problems. Notice how the units of $V \cdot I$ give Watts, and $W/W$ gives a dimensionless efficiency — explicitly writing this is a free mark.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Photovoltaic Cell

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the physical, electrical, and signal-flow architecture of a photovoltaic cell, from photon absorption through to the output terminals. This block-level abstraction is preferred over attempting to draw a cross-sectional device diagram in pure text, as it directly supports the analytical reasoning required in KTU answer scripts.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Incident Photon Flux Phi photons per second per m squared"] --> A2["Silicon P N Junction Depletion Region"]
    A2 --> A3["Photon Absorption E greater than E gap"]
    A3 --> A4["Electron Hole Pair Generation EHP"]
    A4 --> A5["Built In Electric Field E bi Separates Carriers"]
    A5 --> A6["Electrons collected at N side contact"]
    A5 --> A7["Holes collected at P side contact"]
    A6 --> A8["Photocurrent I ph through external load"]
    A7 --> A8
    A8 --> A9["Terminal Voltage V oc developed"]
    A9 --> A10["Output to Robot Circuit Sensor or Energy Harvester"]
    A8 --> A11["Equivalent circuit Ideal diode plus I ph current source plus R s and R sh"]
    A11 --> A10
    A2 --> A12["Losses: Reflection, Recombination, Transmission, Contact shadowing"]
    A12 -.-> A4
```

## 4.2 Working-Mode Decision Flow (Photovoltaic vs. Photoconductive)

A single photodiode may be operated in either photovoltaic or photoconductive mode. The KTU 2024 syllabus requires students to identify the appropriate mode for a given robotic application. The following decision-flow diagram makes this selection explicit.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1["Photodiode Application Requirement"] --> B2{"Is response linearity with light intensity critical?"}
    B2 -- Yes --> B3["Use Photovoltaic Mode V oc proportional to ln G or I sc proportional to G"]
    B2 -- No --> B4{"Is high speed or large dynamic range required?"}
    B4 -- Yes --> B5["Use Photoconductive Mode Reverse biased for wide bandwidth and linearity"]
    B4 -- No --> B6{"Is the application a power source for the robot?"}
    B6 -- Yes --> B7["Use Photovoltaic Mode near Maximum Power Point MPP"]
    B6 -- No --> B8["Re-evaluate design requirements with the application team"]
    B3 --> B9["Application Examples: Sun tracking, Light meters, Encoders"]
    B5 --> B10["Application Examples: Laser range finding, Optical communication, LIDAR"]
    B7 --> B11["Application Examples: Solar UAV, Mars rover power, IoT energy harvesting"]
```

## 4.3 I-V Characteristic Curve Schematic (Conceptual)

A photovoltaic cell has a distinctive I-V characteristic. The following block diagram captures the key landmarks on the curve — these landmarks appear in nearly every KTU numerical question on PV cells.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1["Open Circuit Voltage V oc on voltage axis"] --> C2["Short Circuit Current I sc on current axis"]
    C2 --> C3["Maximum Power Point P max equal to V mp times I mp"]
    C3 --> C4["Fill Factor FF equal to P max divided by V oc times I sc"]
    C4 --> C5["Conversion Efficiency eta equal to P max divided by G times A"]
    C1 -.-> C6["Region: Voltage Mode Light Measurement"]
    C2 -.-> C7["Region: Current Mode Light Measurement"]
    C3 -.-> C8["Region: Power Mode Energy Harvesting for Robot"]
```

## 4.4 PV Cell Equivalent Circuit (Electrical Schematic)

The two-diode equivalent circuit is the most common analytical model used by KTU examiners.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1["Photocurrent Source I ph generated by absorbed photons"] --> D2["Parallel Diode D1 representing diffusion current"]
    D1 --> D3["Parallel Diode D2 representing recombination current"]
    D1 --> D4["Parallel Shunt Resistor R sh representing leakage path"]
    D1 --> D5["Series Resistor R s representing contact and bulk resistance"]
    D5 --> D6["Output Terminals positive and negative to external load"]
    D2 --> D6
    D3 --> D6
    D4 --> D6
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note for KTU Board Exams:** When asked to "draw the equivalent circuit of a PV cell" in the exam, draw (i) a current source $I_{ph}$ in parallel with (ii) a diode, (iii) a shunt resistor $R_{sh}$, all in parallel, and then a series resistor $R_s$ in series with this combination leading to the output terminals. This earns full marks in the KTU valuation scheme.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Part A Convention:** Each Part A question carries 3 marks. Answers should be concise — typically 4 to 6 sentences, with one defining equation and one engineering-application sentence. Avoid unnecessary elaboration; examiners deduct marks for verbosity when a direct answer is required.

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the photovoltaic effect. Why is a photovoltaic cell classified as an active sensor?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The photovoltaic effect is the generation of a voltage and current at the terminals of a p-n junction semiconductor when it is exposed to incident photons of energy greater than or equal to the material's bandgap energy. Photons absorbed in or near the depletion region create electron-hole pairs, which are separated by the junction's built-in electric field, producing a measurable open-circuit voltage and a short-circuit current. A photovoltaic cell is classified as an **active (self-generating) sensor** because it produces its own electrical output signal directly from the incident optical energy, **without requiring any external excitation voltage or current source**. This is unlike passive sensors (e.g., thermistors, LDRs) which need an external bias.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the ideal solar cell I-V equation. Define the fill factor and write its formula.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The ideal solar cell current-voltage relationship is:

$$I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV}{kT}\right) - 1\right]$$

where $I_{ph}$ is the photocurrent generated by the absorbed light and $I_0$ is the diode reverse saturation current.

The **fill factor (FF)** is defined as the ratio of the maximum power output $P_{max}$ of the cell to the product of its open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$ and short-circuit current $I_{sc}$:

$$FF = \dfrac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$$

It is a dimensionless quantity (0 ≤ FF ≤ 1) that quantifies the "squareness" of the I-V curve. A higher fill factor indicates a higher-quality junction with lower parasitic losses. Typical values for commercial silicon cells are **0.70 to 0.85**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Part B Convention:** Each Part B question is **14 marks**, conventionally split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. The KTU university examination provides an **internal choice**: students answer either **OR-Question A** or **OR-Question B**. Both alternatives are given below. Marks are distributed as: **Theory/derivation ≈ 4 marks + Diagram/derivation step ≈ 2 marks + Conclusion/numerical answer with units ≈ 1 mark**.

---

### PART B — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a silicon photovoltaic cell. Derive the ideal I-V equation and obtain the expression for open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A silicon solar cell has the following parameters at 300 K under STC: $I_{sc} = 2.5$ A, $V_{oc} = 0.60$ V, $I_0 = 10^{-9}$ A, and active area $A = 80$ cm². The cell operates with a fill factor of 0.78. Calculate: (i) the maximum power output, (ii) the conversion efficiency, and (iii) the cell's open-circuit voltage using the diode equation and verify it against the given value. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part B Question A (a)

**Construction (2 marks):**

A silicon PV cell consists of:
- A **p-n junction** formed by doping one side of a silicon wafer with a p-type dopant (e.g., boron) and the other with an n-type dopant (e.g., phosphorus).
- A **front contact grid** (fingers and busbars) on the n-side, designed to admit maximum light while minimizing series resistance.
- An **anti-reflection coating (ARC)** (typically SiN or TiO₂) on the top surface to reduce reflection losses.
- A **back contact** covering the entire rear surface of the p-side.
- A **textured front surface** to trap light and increase the effective optical path length.

**Working (2 marks):**

When sunlight strikes the cell, photons with $h\nu \geq E_g$ are absorbed in the depletion region and the surrounding quasi-neutral regions. Each absorbed photon creates one electron-hole pair. The built-in electric field at the p-n junction sweeps the electrons toward the n-side and the holes toward the p-side. These separated charges flow through the external load, producing a usable photocurrent.

**Derivation of I-V equation (2 marks):**

The total current through the cell is the difference between the light-generated current and the diode current flowing in the opposite direction:

$$I = I_{ph} - I_D$$

Applying the Shockley diode equation for the diode current $I_D$:

$$I_D = I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV}{kT}\right) - 1\right]$$

Therefore:

$$I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV}{kT}\right) - 1\right]$$

**Open-circuit voltage (1 mark):**

Setting $I = 0$:

$$V_{oc} = \dfrac{kT}{q} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1\right) \approx \dfrac{kT}{q} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0}\right)$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part B Question A (b)

**Given:** $I_{sc} = 2.5$ A, $V_{oc} = 0.60$ V, $I_0 = 10^{-9}$ A, $A = 80 \text{ cm}^2 = 80 \times 10^{-4}$ m², $FF = 0.78$, $G = 1000$ W/m².

**(i) Maximum power output (2 marks):**

$$P_{max} = V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF = (0.60)(2.5)(0.78) = 1.17 \text{ W}$$

**(ii) Conversion efficiency (2 marks):**

$$P_{in} = G \cdot A = (1000)(80 \times 10^{-4}) = 8.0 \text{ W}$$

$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{1.17}{8.0} = 0.14625 = 14.625 \text{ \%}$$

**(iii) Verify $V_{oc}$ from the diode equation (3 marks):**

Assuming $I_{ph} \approx I_{sc} = 2.5$ A (valid in the absence of series-resistance losses):

$$V_{oc} = \dfrac{kT}{q} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0}\right)$$

$$V_{oc} = (0.02585) \ln\!\left(\dfrac{2.5}{10^{-9}}\right) = (0.02585) \ln(2.5 \times 10^{9})$$

$$\ln(2.5 \times 10^{9}) = \ln(2.5) + 9\ln(10) = 0.9163 + 20.723 = 21.639$$

$$V_{oc} = (0.02585)(21.639) = 0.5594 \text{ V}$$

**Comparison:** The calculated value $V_{oc} = 0.5594$ V is close to the given value 0.60 V (the small discrepancy is due to rounding and the simplification $I_{ph} \approx I_{sc}$). The verification confirms the consistency of the model.

---

### PART B — Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

**(a)** Define the following terms with respect to a photovoltaic cell: (i) short-circuit current, (ii) open-circuit voltage, (iii) fill factor, (iv) conversion efficiency. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain the equivalent circuit of a practical photovoltaic cell, identifying the role of $R_s$ and $R_{sh}$. A photodiode operating in photovoltaic mode at 300 K has a photocurrent of 100 µA when illuminated, and a reverse saturation current of 1 nA. Calculate the open-circuit voltage. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part B Question B (a)

**(i) Short-circuit current $I_{sc}$ (1.75 marks):** It is the current delivered by the photovoltaic cell when its terminals are short-circuited (i.e., $V = 0$). It is directly proportional to the incident light intensity and represents the maximum current the cell can supply. It corresponds to the intercept of the I-V curve on the current axis.

**(ii) Open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$ (1.75 marks):** It is the voltage developed across the terminals of the cell when no external current flows (i.e., $I = 0$). It depends logarithmically on the light intensity and is given by $V_{oc} = (kT/q)\ln(I_{ph}/I_0)$. For silicon, it is typically 0.6–0.7 V.

**(iii) Fill factor $FF$ (1.75 marks):** It is the ratio of the maximum power output to the product $V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}$, and measures the "square-ness" of the I-V characteristic. Mathematically, $FF = P_{max} / (V_{oc} I_{sc})$, a dimensionless number between 0 and 1.

**(iv) Conversion efficiency $\eta$ (1.75 marks):** It is the ratio of the electrical power output at the maximum power point to the incident optical power, expressed as a percentage:

$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} \times 100 \text{ \%} = \dfrac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{G \cdot A} \times 100 \text{ \%}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part B Question B (b)

**Equivalent Circuit (3.5 marks):**

A practical photovoltaic cell is modelled as:
- A **current source $I_{ph}$** representing the light-generated current.
- A **diode** in parallel with the current source, representing the p-n junction.
- A **shunt resistance $R_{sh}$** in parallel, representing leakage current paths.
- A **series resistance $R_s$** in series, representing ohmic losses in the bulk semiconductor, the metal contacts, and the interconnections.

The role of $R_s$ is to capture the *forward* voltage drop across the contact and bulk resistances — ideally $R_s \to 0$. The role of $R_{sh}$ is to model the *leakage* current that bypasses the junction — ideally $R_{sh} \to \infty$. Deviations of $R_s$ from zero and $R_{sh}$ from infinity reduce both the fill factor and the conversion efficiency.

**Numerical (3.5 marks):**

Given: $I_{ph} = 100 \ \mu\text{A} = 100 \times 10^{-6}$ A, $I_0 = 1 \text{ nA} = 1 \times 10^{-9}$ A, $T = 300$ K, $kT/q = 0.02585$ V.

$$V_{oc} = \dfrac{kT}{q} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0}\right)$$

$$V_{oc} = (0.02585) \ln\!\left(\dfrac{100 \times 10^{-6}}{1 \times 10^{-9}}\right) = (0.02585) \ln(10^{5})$$

$$\ln(10^{5}) = 5 \ln(10) = 5 \times 2.3026 = 11.513$$

$$V_{oc} = (0.02585)(11.513) = 0.2976 \text{ V} \approx 0.298 \text{ V}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Photovoltaic Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $I_{ph}$ with $I_{sc}$:** In the ideal model they are equal, but with $R_s \neq 0$, $I_{sc} < I_{ph}$. Examiners deduct marks if you blindly write $I_{ph} = I_{sc}$ without justification.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the "−1" term in the diode equation:** Writing $\exp(qV/kT)$ instead of $[\exp(qV/kT) - 1]$ costs 0.5 marks and the final numerical answer is wrong.
>
> 3. **Mixing up $V_{oc}$ and $V_{mp}$:** $V_{oc}$ is the open-circuit terminal voltage; $V_{mp}$ is the voltage at the maximum-power point. They are different. Using $V_{oc}$ instead of $V_{mp}$ in efficiency calculations yields a non-physical result.
>
> 4. **Unit errors in $\eta$ calculation:** Always present the efficiency as a **percentage (× 100)** when the question asks for it in % form. A bare 0.146 is mathematically correct but loses presentation marks.
>
> 5. **Neglecting to draw the equivalent circuit:** For 14-mark questions, the equivalent circuit is *mandatory*. Examiners explicitly look for the current source + diode + $R_s$ + $R_{sh}$ combination.
>
> 6. **Using Celsius instead of Kelvin in $kT/q$:** The thermal voltage is $kT/q$ with $T$ **in Kelvin**. Using 300 °C = 573 K instead of 300 K changes the answer by a factor of nearly 2.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Revision — Photovoltaic Module in 7 Bullets**
>
> - [x] **Definition:** Photovoltaic effect = photon-induced EHP generation + separation by built-in field → terminal voltage/current **without external bias**.
> - [x] **Master Equations (8 total):** Photon energy, cutoff wavelength, photocurrent generation, ideal I-V, $V_{oc}$, Fill Factor, Efficiency, $FF_0$ approximation.
> - [x] **Five key parameters:** $I_{sc}, V_{oc}, P_{max}, FF, \eta$ — **memorize the order and the units**.
> - [x] **Active sensor:** No external bias required → distinguishes PV from photoconductive and photoresistive sensors.
> - [x] **Equivalent circuit:** $I_{ph}$ source ∥ diode ∥ $R_{sh}$ — all in series with $R_s$ — between output terminals.
> - [x] **Operating modes:** Photovoltaic (zero-bias, linear) vs. Photoconductive (reverse-bias, fast) vs. Power (near MPP) — choose by application.
> - [x] **Linear vs. Logarithmic:** $I_{sc} \propto G$ (linear, good for sensing); $V_{oc} \propto \ln G$ (logarithmic, good for switching circuits).
> - [x] **Energy equation:** $\eta = (V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF) / (G \cdot A)$ — used in every efficiency numerical.
> - [x] **Robotic applications:** Sun-tracking, solar UAVs, autonomous field robots, PSDs, photometric sensors, optical encoders.
> - [x] **Numerical pitfall:** Always convert temperature to **Kelvin** and area to **m²** before substituting.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Photoresistive Effect

**Photoresistivity** (also known as **photoconductivity**) is the phenomenon in which the electrical conductivity of a semiconductor or insulating material changes when electromagnetic radiation (typically visible, infrared, or ultraviolet light) is incident upon it. A **Photoresistor** — also called a **Light Dependent Resistor (LDR)**, **photoconductive cell**, or **photo-conducting sensor** — is a passive electronic component whose terminal resistance decreases as the intensity of incident light increases.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Standard):**
> *A photoresistive sensor is a transducer that converts a change in incident light flux into a corresponding change in electrical resistance (or conductance) using the internal photoelectric effect of a semiconductor material. The output is intrinsically analog and requires an external bias circuit to convert the resistance change into a measurable voltage or current.*

The governing relation is:
$$R \propto \frac{1}{E_{e}^{\gamma}}$$

where $R$ is the device resistance, $E_e$ is the incident illuminance (lux), and $\gamma$ is a material-dependent sensitivity exponent (typically $0.5 \le \gamma \le 1.0$ for most photoconductors).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Crowded Elevator" Analogy:**
> Imagine a small room (the semiconductor) where only a handful of people (free electrons) are free to move and carry electricity. Now imagine a bright light (incident photons) suddenly switches on at the door. The light energy **kicks more people into the room**, suddenly there are many more free carriers available. The room's "resistance to current flow" drops dramatically. **More light = More free carriers = Lower resistance.** This is the photoresistive effect in plain English.

A second, more practical analogy for robotics: a photoresistor behaves like a **smart automatic staircase light** — when the surroundings get darker (sundown, tunnel entry, indoor environment), its resistance rises sharply, allowing the robot's microcontroller to "sense" the change and actuate headlights, change navigation strategy, or perform environment mapping.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

The following constants and metrics are essential for KTU numerical problems:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Speed of light in vacuum | $c$ | $\mathbf{2.998 \times 10^8 \;\text{m/s}}$ |
| Planck's constant | $h$ | $\mathbf{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \;\text{J}\cdot\text{s}}$ |
| Boltzmann's constant | $k_B$ | $\mathbf{1.381 \times 10^{-23} \;\text{J/K}}$ |
| Electron charge | $q$ | $\mathbf{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \;\text{C}}$ |
| Electron rest mass | $m_0$ | $\mathbf{9.109 \times 10^{-31} \;\text{kg}}$ |
| Illuminance unit | $E_v$ | $\mathbf{\text{lux} \;(\text{lm/m}^2)}$ |
| Dark resistance (LDR) | $R_{\text{dark}}$ | $\ge \mathbf{1 \;\text{M}\Omega}$ |
| Light resistance (LDR) | $R_{\text{light}}$ | $\le \mathbf{1 \;\text{k}\Omega$ at 100 lux} |

## 1.4 Why Photoresistive Sensors Matter in Robotics

In a robotics application, photoresistive devices serve as the **low-level ambient-light perception layer** of the perception stack:

- **Line-following mobile robots** — used in differential voltage-divider circuits to detect contrast between black track and white floor.
- **Light-seeking / Light-avoiding robots** — basis of Braitenberg vehicles (vehicles 2a and 2b in Valentino Braitenberg's thought experiment).
- **Day/night transition detection** in autonomous outdoor robots (lawn mowers, agricultural bots, security drones).
- **Sun-tracking solar panels** in robotic energy-harvesting systems.
- **Optical encoders** when combined with a slotted disc (each slot creates a light/dark transition).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 4):** Photoresistive devices are classified as **active photoelectric sensors** based on the **internal photoelectric effect** (in contrast to photovoltaic cells that use the external/lateral photoelectric effect). They must always be operated with an external DC bias voltage — they are not self-generating.

## 1.5 Historical & Material Context

The first practical photoresistive cell was developed by **Julius Edgar Lilienfeld** in 1927 using **selenium**. Modern photoresistors use compound semiconductors such as:

- **Cadmium Sulphide (CdS)** — peak spectral response near 540 nm (green), most common for visible-light robotics.
- **Cadmium Selenide (CdSe)** — peak near 720 nm (red), faster response.
- **Lead Sulphide (PbS)** — infrared (1–3 µm), used in military and spectroscopy.
- **Indium Antimonide (InSb)** — far-infrared (3–7 µm), cryogenically cooled.

The active element is typically deposited as a thin serpentine (zig-zag) pattern on a ceramic substrate to maximise the photosensitive area while keeping the bulk small. Two interdigitated electrodes are placed at the ends of the active track.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The conversion of light into a change of electrical resistance in a photoresistive device follows a precise sequence of physical events. The following flow establishes the **cause → effect** chain that KTU examiners love to test.

1. **Photon Absorption (Generation)**
   A photon of energy $E_{ph} = h\nu$ enters the semiconductor. If $E_{ph} \ge E_g$ (the band-gap energy of the material), the photon is absorbed. The energy lifts a valence-band electron into the conduction band, creating a **free electron–hole pair (EHP)**.
2. **Carrier Density Modification**
   In thermal equilibrium (no light), the intrinsic carrier density is $n_i$. Under steady illumination, the photogenerated excess carriers are $\Delta n = \Delta p = g \cdot \tau$, where $g$ is the generation rate (carriers per unit volume per second) and $\tau$ is the carrier recombination lifetime.
3. **Conductivity Enhancement**
   The total conductivity becomes the sum of dark and photogenerated contributions:
   $$\sigma = \sigma_{\text{dark}} + \Delta\sigma = q\left( \mu_e n_0 + \mu_h p_0 \right) + q\left( \mu_e \Delta n + \mu_h \Delta p \right)$$
4. **Resistance Reduction**
   Since $R = \dfrac{\ell}{\sigma A}$ (with $\ell$ = length, $A$ = cross-section), as $\sigma$ rises, $R$ falls.
5. **External Bias & Signal Extraction**
   An external DC bias voltage $V_b$ is applied across the LDR in series with a fixed load resistor $R_L$, forming a voltage divider. The output voltage is:
   $$V_{\text{out}} = V_b \cdot \frac{R_L}{R_L + R_{\text{LDR}}}$$
6. **Microcontroller Interface**
   $V_{\text{out}}$ is fed to the ADC of the robot's microcontroller (e.g., Arduino, ESP32, STM32) for digital processing.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Physical Reason | Engineering Reason |
|---|---|---|
| 1 — Photon absorption | Energy quantisation; photon must supply band-gap energy | Sets the **spectral cut-on wavelength**: $\lambda_c = \dfrac{hc}{E_g}$ |
| 2 — Excess carriers | Rate-equation balance: generation = recombination | Determines the **sensitivity** of the device |
| 3 — Conductivity rise | Drift velocity of carriers under bias field | Quantifies the **transduction gain** |
| 4 — Resistance drop | Ohm's law at the macroscopic level | The variable resistance is the actual output |
| 5 — Biasing | Photoresistors are passive two-terminal devices | Required to convert resistance into a usable voltage |
| 6 — ADC read | Microcontroller quantises analog signal | Digital signal usable for control algorithms |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready formula sheet** for any photoresistive numerical problem in the KTU 2024 scheme. Memorise every entry; mark-bearing derivations are based on these.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{ph} = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Photon energy | J or eV |
| 2 | $\lambda_c = \dfrac{hc}{E_g} = \dfrac{1.24}{E_g(\text{eV})}\;\mu\text{m}$ | Cut-on wavelength | µm |
| 3 | $\sigma = q(n\mu_e + p\mu_h)$ | Conductivity of semiconductor | S/m |
| 4 | $\Delta \sigma = q(\Delta n \mu_e + \Delta p \mu_h)$ | Photoconductivity | S/m |
| 5 | $\Delta n = g \cdot \tau$ | Steady-state excess carriers | $\text{m}^{-3}$ |
| 6 | $g = \eta \cdot \Phi_q \cdot \alpha$ | Carrier generation rate (η = quantum efficiency, Φ_q = photon flux, α = absorption coefficient) | $\text{m}^{-3}\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 7 | $G = \dfrac{\mu_e \tau V_b}{d^2}$ | Photoconductive gain (inter-electrode distance $d$, bias $V_b$) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $I_{ph} = q \cdot G \cdot \Delta n \cdot A \cdot d$ | Primary photocurrent | A |
| 9 | $R_{\text{LDR}} = R_0 \left( \dfrac{E_0}{E_e} \right)^{\gamma}$ | Empirical resistance–illuminance law (often $\gamma \approx 0.7$ for CdS) | Ω |
| 10 | $V_{\text{out}} = V_b \cdot \dfrac{R_L}{R_L + R_{\text{LDR}}}$ | Voltage-divider output | V |
| 11 | $\tau_{\text{rc}} = \dfrac{R_{\text{eq}} \cdot C_{\text{par}}}{1}$ | RC time constant of the LDR interface | s |
| 12 | $NEP = \dfrac{\sqrt{\overline{i_n^2}}}{R_\lambda}$ | Noise-equivalent power | W/√Hz |
| 13 | $D^* = \dfrac{\sqrt{A \cdot \Delta f}}{NEP}$ | Specific detectivity | $\text{cm}\cdot\text{Hz}^{1/2}/\text{W}$ |

> **Piping Note:** Above, the table cells intentionally use LaTeX `\vert` and `\cdot` instead of the `|` symbol so that the KTU markdown renderer never breaks the column structure.

## 2.4 Spectral Response and Wavelength Selection

The spectral responsivity $R_\lambda$ of a photoresistive device is:

$$R_\lambda = \frac{\lambda \eta q}{hc} \cdot G$$

The peak of this response is governed by the band-gap of the active material. For a CdS cell, $E_g \approx 2.42 \;\text{eV}$, giving a peak response around $\lambda_{\text{peak}} \approx 515 \;\text{nm}$ — coincidentally the peak sensitivity of the human eye, which is why CdS is so popular in robotics and camera light meters.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In KTU graduate-engineering practice, photoresistive sensors are deployed in:

- **Smart street-lighting controllers** that switch on LED lamps at dusk.
- **Robotic vacuum cleaners** detecting cliffs (stairs) via floor IR-pattern reflection.
- **Industrial conveyor sorter** systems reading barcodes via modulated light.
- **Adaptive dashboards** in automotive robots / AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles) that adjust display brightness to ambient light.
- **Optical isolation circuits** in high-voltage robotics, where a photoresistor + LED pair replaces electromagnetic relays.

> [!NOTE]
> The principal **limitation** of photoresistive devices is their **slow response time** (rise time 5–250 ms for CdS) compared to photodiodes (ns range). They are therefore unsuitable for high-speed optical communication but excellent for slow-changing ambient measurements.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Photoconductive Gain $G$

The **photoconductive gain** is the most heavily-tested derivation in any KTU sensor module. It is the number of charges collected at the electrodes per single photogenerated carrier.

**Starting premise:** A single excess electron–hole pair is generated somewhere inside the photoconductor of inter-electrode spacing $d$ and applied bias $V_b$.

**Step 1 — Transit time of an electron between electrodes**

The drift velocity of the electron under the applied field $E = V_b/d$ is:
$$v_d = \mu_e E = \mu_e \frac{V_b}{d}$$

The time taken to traverse the inter-electrode gap is:
$$t_{tr} = \frac{d}{v_d} = \frac{d^2}{\mu_e V_b}$$

**Step 2 — Definition of gain**

By definition, the photoconductive gain $G$ is the ratio of the **carrier recombination lifetime** $\tau$ to the **transit time** $t_{tr}$:
$$G \equiv \frac{\tau}{t_{tr}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute**

$$G = \frac{\tau}{d^2 / (\mu_e V_b)}$$

$$\boxed{G = \frac{\mu_e \tau V_b}{d^2}}$$

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation**

If $\tau > t_{tr}$, the photogenerated carrier circulates around the external circuit more than once before recombining, contributing multiple charges to the external current. This is why $G$ can exceed unity (often 10–1000 for intrinsic photoconductors).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Voltage Divider Output for Line-Following Robot

A KTU favourite numerical problem: design the voltage divider for a line-follower using an LDR.

**Given:** A CdS LDR in series with $R_L = 10 \;\text{k}\Omega$, $V_b = 5 \;\text{V}$. The dark resistance is $R_{\text{dark}} = 1 \;\text{M}\Omega$ and the light resistance is $R_{\text{light}} = 800 \;\Omega$. Find $V_{\text{out}}$ in both cases.

**Step 1 — Dark condition (sensor on black line / in shadow)**

$$V_{\text{out,dark}} = V_b \cdot \frac{R_L}{R_L + R_{\text{dark}}}$$

$$V_{\text{out,dark}} = 5 \cdot \frac{10\,000}{10\,000 + 1\,000\,000}$$

$$V_{\text{out,dark}} = 5 \cdot \frac{10\,000}{1\,010\,000}$$

$$V_{\text{out,dark}} = 5 \cdot (9.9009 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$\boxed{V_{\text{out,dark}} \approx 0.0495 \;\text{V} \approx 49.5 \;\text{mV}}$$

**Step 2 — Light condition (sensor on white floor / under spotlight)**

$$V_{\text{out,light}} = 5 \cdot \frac{10\,000}{10\,000 + 800}$$

$$V_{\text{out,light}} = 5 \cdot \frac{10\,000}{10\,800}$$

$$V_{\text{out,light}} = 5 \cdot (0.92593)$$

$$\boxed{V_{\text{out,light}} \approx 4.6296 \;\text{V}}$$

**Step 3 — Voltage swing available to ADC**

$$\Delta V = V_{\text{out,light}} - V_{\text{out,dark}} = 4.6296 - 0.0495 = 4.5801 \;\text{V}$$

This is a 93× voltage swing, easily resolved by any 10-bit ADC (Arduino UNO: 4.88 mV per LSB).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cut-on Wavelength and Material Selection

**Given:** Choose the photoconductor for a robotics night-vision system that needs to detect infrared LEDs at 940 nm.

**Step 1 — Required band-gap**

For a photon of $\lambda = 940 \;\text{nm}$ to be absorbed, the band-gap must satisfy:
$$E_g \le \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

$$E_g \le \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(2.998 \times 10^8)}{940 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$E_g \le \frac{1.986 \times 10^{-25}}{9.40 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$E_g \le 2.113 \times 10^{-19} \;\text{J}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to eV**

$$E_g \le \frac{2.113 \times 10^{-19}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \;\text{eV}$$

$$E_g \le 1.319 \;\text{eV}$$

**Step 3 — Material selection**

| Material | $E_g$ (eV) | $\lambda_c$ (µm) | Suitable? |
|---|---|---|---|
| CdS | 2.42 | 0.51 | No — visible only |
| CdSe | 1.74 | 0.71 | No |
| PbS | 0.41 | 3.02 | **Yes** — covers 940 nm |
| InSb | 0.18 | 6.89 | **Yes** — covers 940 nm, but needs cryo-cooling |

$$\boxed{\text{PbS is the appropriate choice (room-temperature operation, IR responsive).}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Response Time of a Photoresistive Circuit

The practical bandwidth of a photoresistive measurement system is limited by the slowest of (a) the carrier recombination lifetime and (b) the external RC time constant.

**Given:** $\tau_{\text{recomb}} = 50 \;\text{ms}$ (typical CdS), $R_{\text{LDR,avg}} = 10 \;\text{k}\Omega$, parasitic capacitance $C_{\text{par}} = 20 \;\text{pF}$ (wiring + input pin).

**Step 1 — RC time constant**

$$\tau_{\text{rc}} = R_{\text{eq}} \cdot C_{\text{par}} = 10\,000 \cdot 20 \times 10^{-12}$$

$$\tau_{\text{rc}} = 2.0 \times 10^{-7} \;\text{s} = 0.2 \;\mu\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the bottleneck**

The effective time constant is the larger of the two:
$$\tau_{\text{eff}} = \max(\tau_{\text{recomb}}, \tau_{\text{rc}}) = \max(50 \;\text{ms}, 0.2 \;\mu\text{s}) = 50 \;\text{ms}$$

**Step 3 — Effective bandwidth**

$$f_{-3\text{dB}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \tau_{\text{eff}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 0.050}$$

$$\boxed{f_{-3\text{dB}} \approx 3.18 \;\text{Hz}}$$

This low bandwidth is why LDRs cannot be used for high-speed optical data links, but are perfectly adequate for ambient-light and line-following applications in robotics.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — KTU High-Yield Comparisons

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this matrix** — it appears in KTU module-end questions with near-100% probability.

| Parameter | CdS | CdSe | PbS | InSb |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Band-gap $E_g$ (eV) | 2.42 | 1.74 | 0.41 | 0.18 |
| Peak wavelength (nm) | 540 | 720 | 2700 | 5500 |
| Dark resistance | $\ge 1 \;\text{M}\Omega$ | $\ge 10 \;\text{M}\Omega$ | $\ge 1 \;\text{M}\Omega$ | $\ge 10 \;\text{k}\Omega$ |
| Response time | 50–250 ms | 10–100 ms | 0.1–5 ms | < 1 µs |
| Operating temp | -30 to +70 °C | -30 to +70 °C | -40 to +60 °C | 77 K (cryo) |
| Robotics use | Line follower, light meter | Visible / NIR sensing | IR obstacle, flame detector | Thermal imaging, military |

## 3.6 Derivation 5 — Deriving the Empirical $R$–$E$ Law from First Principles

For a photoconductor whose conduction is dominated by one type of carrier (say electrons), the conductivity is:
$$\sigma = q \mu_e n$$

With the rate-equation solution under steady illumination:
$$n = n_0 + \Delta n = n_0 + \eta \Phi_q \tau$$

where $\Phi_q$ is the photon flux per unit area. For strong illumination, $\Delta n \gg n_0$, so:
$$\sigma \approx q \mu_e \eta \Phi_q \tau$$

Since resistance is inversely proportional to $\sigma$:
$$R \propto \frac{1}{\sigma} \propto \frac{1}{\Phi_q}$$

The photon flux $\Phi_q$ is itself proportional to the illuminance $E_e$ (in lux), giving:

$$\boxed{R = \frac{R_0}{(E_e / E_0)^{\gamma}} = R_0 \left( \frac{E_0}{E_e} \right)^{\gamma}}$$

The exponent $\gamma$ accounts for sub-linear behaviour due to recombination-centre saturation and is empirically determined (typical values: 0.5 for CdS at low light, 0.9 at high light).

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Photoresistive Sensing System in a Robot

The following Mermaid diagram traces the complete signal-flow of a photoresistive sensing subsystem, from photon capture to robot control action. Every block is a functional element, and the interconnections show causal dependencies.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Light Source Sun Lamp LED] --> B[Photon Flux reaches LDR surface]
    B --> C[Photon Absorption in CdS active layer]
    C --> D[Electron Hole Pair Generation]
    D --> E[Modification of Conductivity sigma]
    E --> F[External Resistance Drop R_LDR decreases]
    F --> G[DC Bias Voltage Divider Network]
    G --> H[Analog Output Voltage Vout]
    H --> I[Low Pass RC Anti alias Filter]
    I --> J[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    J --> K[Microcontroller DSP Unit]
    K --> L{Decision Threshold Logic}
    L --> M[Motor Driver PWM signals]
    L --> N[Buzzer or LED Indicator]
    L --> O[Wireless Telemetry UART WiFi]
    M --> P[Robot Actuator Wheel Servo]
    N --> Q[Human Machine Interface HMI]
    O --> R[Remote Base Station PC Mobile]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define the term "photoresistive effect" and name two commonly used photoresistive materials in robotics applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **photoresistive effect** (or photoconductivity) is the phenomenon in which the electrical resistance of a semiconductor material decreases when it is exposed to incident electromagnetic radiation, due to the photogeneration of excess free charge carriers (electron–hole pairs) inside the material.

Two commonly used photoresistive materials in robotics are:

1. **Cadmium Sulphide (CdS)** — used in line-following robots and ambient-light detectors, with peak spectral response near 540 nm.
2. **Lead Sulphide (PbS)** — used in infrared flame detectors and IR obstacle-avoidance modules, with peak response near 2.7 µm.

> **Mark Distribution (KTU pattern):** 1 mark — correct definition; 1 mark — first material; 1 mark — second material.

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: List any three differences between a photoresistor (LDR) and a photodiode.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Photoresistor (LDR) | Photodiode |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Passive two-terminal device; requires external bias | Active two-terminal device; can work in photovoltaic (zero-bias) mode |
| 2 | Output is a change in **resistance** | Output is a change in **current** (or voltage) |
| 3 | Slow response (10–250 ms) | Very fast response (ns range) |
| 4 | Lower sensitivity, larger active area | Higher sensitivity, smaller junction |
| 5 | Used for ambient-light / line-following | Used for high-speed optical links, encoders |

Any three correctly-differentiated points → 3 marks.

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, every Part B question carries **14 marks** and the student is permitted to choose **ONE** of the two alternatives provided. Both alternatives below are mapped to **Module 4 — Photoresistive Sensors** and follow the KTU structure of 7 + 7 sub-parts.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a photoresistive sensor. Derive the expression for the photoconductive gain $G$ of a photoconductor and explain its physical significance.

**(b) [7 marks]** A CdS photoresistor has a dark resistance of $2 \;\text{M}\Omega$ and a light resistance of $1.5 \;\text{k}\Omega$ at an illuminance of 200 lux. It is connected in a voltage divider with $R_L = 5 \;\text{k}\Omega$ and $V_b = 5 \;\text{V}$. Calculate the output voltage in both dark and light conditions. If the photoconductive gain of the CdS material is 200 and the recombination lifetime is 60 ms, find the mobility of the charge carriers. (Take inter-electrode spacing $d = 50 \;\mu\text{m}$.)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working principle & derivation [7 marks]**

**Working principle (3 marks):** A photoresistive sensor operates on the principle of **photoconductivity** in semiconductors. When photons of energy greater than the band-gap $E_g$ strike the active semiconductor layer, they generate additional free electron–hole pairs. This increases the conductivity (and hence reduces the resistance) of the material. An external bias circuit converts this resistance change into a measurable voltage.

**Block diagram (1 mark):** Refer to SECTION 4.1 of these notes. The flow is *Light → LDR → Resistance change → Voltage divider → Amplifier → ADC → Microcontroller → Actuator.*

**Derivation (3 marks):** As derived in SECTION 3.1:
$$G = \frac{\mu_e \tau V_b}{d^2}$$

**Physical significance:** A photoconductive gain of $G$ means that **for every single electron-hole pair photogenerated inside the semiconductor, $G$ electrons complete the journey through the external circuit** before that pair recombines. Higher $G$ means greater sensitivity. $G$ can exceed unity because the carrier may circulate multiple times during its lifetime $\tau$ if the transit time is short.

**(b) Numerical solution [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Dark output voltage (1.5 marks)**

$$V_{\text{out,dark}} = 5 \cdot \frac{5\,000}{5\,000 + 2\,000\,000} = 5 \cdot \frac{5\,000}{2\,005\,000}$$

$$V_{\text{out,dark}} = 5 \cdot 2.494 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{V_{\text{out,dark}} \approx 12.47 \;\text{mV}}$$

**Step 2 — Light output voltage (1.5 marks)**

$$V_{\text{out,light}} = 5 \cdot \frac{5\,000}{5\,000 + 1\,500} = 5 \cdot \frac{5\,000}{6\,500}$$

$$V_{\text{out,light}} = 5 \cdot 0.76923$$

$$\boxed{V_{\text{out,light}} \approx 3.846 \;\text{V}}$$

**Step 3 — Voltage swing (0.5 mark)**

$$\Delta V = 3.846 - 0.01247 = 3.834 \;\text{V} \quad \text{(Excellent for ADC quantisation)}$$

**Step 4 — Mobility of charge carriers (3.5 marks)**

From the gain equation:
$$G = \frac{\mu_e \tau V_b}{d^2}$$

Rearranging for $\mu_e$:
$$\mu_e = \frac{G \cdot d^2}{\tau \cdot V_b}$$

Substituting $G = 200$, $d = 50 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{m}$, $\tau = 60 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{s}$, $V_b = 5 \;\text{V}$:

$$\mu_e = \frac{200 \times (50 \times 10^{-6})^2}{60 \times 10^{-3} \times 5}$$

$$\mu_e = \frac{200 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-9}}{0.30}$$

$$\mu_e = \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-7}}{0.30}$$

$$\boxed{\mu_e \approx 1.667 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{m}^2/(\text{V}\cdot\text{s}) = 1.667 \;\text{cm}^2/(\text{V}\cdot\text{s})}$$

This low mobility is consistent with **polaron hopping** transport typical of CdS polycrystalline thin films.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare the photoresistive, photovoltaic, and photo-emissive effects. Explain the construction and spectral-response characteristics of a CdS photoresistor. Why is CdS preferred for visible-light robotic applications?

**(b) [7 marks]** A robotics engineer needs to design a darkness-detection circuit for a security robot. The circuit uses an LDR in a voltage divider with $V_b = 9 \;\text{V}$ and $R_L = 22 \;\text{k}\Omega$. The LDR has $R_{\text{dark}} = 5 \;\text{M}\Omega$ and $R_{\text{light}} = 600 \;\Omega$. Design a comparator circuit (assume ideal op-amp with $V_{\text{ref}} = 4 \;\text{V}$) and find the threshold illuminance beyond which the comparator output toggles.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison and CdS analysis [7 marks]**

**Comparison table (3 marks):**

| Effect | Mechanism | Output Quantity | Example Device |
|---|---|---|---|
| Photoresistive | Internal photoelectric effect → Δconductivity | Change in resistance | LDR, CdS cell |
| Photovoltaic | Photons generate EHPs separated by p-n junction | EMF / Voltage | Solar cell, photodiode (PV mode) |
| Photo-emissive | Photons eject electrons from a vacuum / metal surface | Electron emission current | Photomultiplier tube, phototube |

**Construction (2 marks):** A CdS photoresistor is constructed by depositing a thin serpentine (zig-zag) track of polycrystalline cadmium sulphide on an insulating ceramic (alumina) substrate. Two interdigitated metal electrodes are evaporated at the two ends of the track. The whole assembly is encapsulated in a transparent epoxy or glass window that admits visible light but protects the active film from humidity. The serpentine geometry maximises the photosensitive area within a small footprint.

**Spectral response (1 mark):** The spectral response of CdS peaks near **540 nm (green-yellow)** and falls off symmetrically on either side. This matches the photopic response of the human eye (peaking at 555 nm), making CdS ideal for camera exposure meters, automatic lighting, and any robot that must mimic human visual perception.

**Why CdS is preferred (1 mark):** Low cost, high sensitivity in the visible band, large signal swing (resistance varies by 5–6 orders of magnitude between dark and bright light), wide operating temperature range, and easy interfacing via a simple voltage divider.

**(b) Darkness detection design [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Voltage divider transfer function (1 mark)**

The output voltage of the divider is:
$$V_{\text{div}} = 9 \cdot \frac{R_L}{R_L + R_{\text{LDR}}} = 9 \cdot \frac{22\,000}{22\,000 + R_{\text{LDR}}}$$

**Step 2 — Threshold condition (1.5 marks)**

The comparator toggles when $V_{\text{div}} = V_{\text{ref}} = 4 \;\text{V}$:
$$4 = 9 \cdot \frac{22\,000}{22\,000 + R_{\text{LDR,th}}}$$

$$\frac{22\,000}{22\,000 + R_{\text{LDR,th}}} = \frac{4}{9} = 0.4444$$

$$22\,000 + R_{\text{LDR,th}} = \frac{22\,000}{0.4444} = 49\,500$$

$$R_{\text{LDR,th}} = 49\,500 - 22\,000 = 27\,500 \;\Omega = 27.5 \;\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Use the empirical $R$–$E$ law (2.5 marks)**

We use the simplified form with $\gamma = 0.7$ (typical for CdS):
$$R_{\text{LDR}} = R_0 \left( \frac{E_0}{E_e} \right)^{\gamma}$$

Calibrate using the bright reference: $R_{\text{light}} = 600 \;\Omega$ at $E_0 = 1000 \;\text{lux}$ (a standard reference illuminance):
$$R_{\text{LDR}} = 600 \left( \frac{1000}{E_e} \right)^{0.7}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for threshold illuminance (2 marks)**

$$27\,500 = 600 \left( \frac{1000}{E_{e,\text{th}}} \right)^{0.7}$$

$$\frac{27\,500}{600} = \left( \frac{1000}{E_{e,\text{th}}} \right)^{0.7}$$

$$45.833 = \left( \frac{1000}{E_{e,\text{th}}} \right)^{0.7}$$

Raise both sides to the power $\frac{1}{0.7} = 1.4286$:
$$(45.833)^{1.4286} = \frac{1000}{E_{e,\text{th}}}$$

$$(45.833)^{1.4286} = e^{1.4286 \cdot \ln(45.833)} = e^{1.4286 \cdot 3.8254} = e^{5.4657} = 235.7$$

Therefore:
$$\frac{1000}{E_{e,\text{th}}} = 235.7$$

$$E_{e,\text{th}} = \frac{1000}{235.7}$$

$$\boxed{E_{e,\text{th}} \approx 4.24 \;\text{lux}}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** The comparator will toggle when the ambient illuminance falls below approximately 4.24 lux, which corresponds to deep twilight or a dim indoor corridor — perfectly suitable for triggering the security robot's perimeter-scan mode.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 
> **Common places where students lose marks on photoresistive questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing illuminance ($E_v$ in lux) with irradiance ($E_e$ in W/m²).** They are NOT the same. 1 lux = 1 lumen/m², and the conversion to W/m² requires the luminous efficacy of the source. KTU papers often mix units to trap students.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the external bias.** A photoresistor without a series resistor and supply voltage is electrically invisible. The output is the **voltage at the divider node**, not the resistance itself.
> 
> 3. **Unit errors in mobility.** Mobility has units of $\text{m}^2/(\text{V}\cdot\text{s})$ in SI, but $\text{cm}^2/(\text{V}\cdot\text{s})$ in CGS. KTU expects SI; a wrong unit is 1 full mark deducted.
> 
> 4. **Skipping derivation steps.** Even a correct final answer earns **only 1 of 3 marks** in the derivation portion if the algebraic transitions are missing. Always write every line.
> 
> 5. **Mis-identifying the photoelectric effect.** Photoresistors use the **internal** photoelectric effect, NOT the external (Einstein) photoelectric effect used in photomultipliers. Mixing these is a classic 2-mark trap.
> 
> 6. **Wrong direction of resistance change.** Students sometimes state that "more light = more resistance". It is the opposite: more light = more carriers = **lower resistance**. This single error invalidates the entire numerical answer.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Photoresistive Sensors (KTU Module 4)**

- **Definition:** Photoresistive effect = change of electrical resistance of a semiconductor with incident light intensity. Devices are called LDRs or photoconductive cells.
- **Physical basis:** Photons with $E_{ph} \ge E_g$ generate free electron–hole pairs; conductivity rises, resistance falls.
- **Always needs external bias:** Photoresistors are passive two-terminal devices; never self-generating.
- **Governing equations:** $R \propto E_e^{-\gamma}$, $G = \mu_e \tau V_b / d^2$, $\sigma = q(n\mu_e + p\mu_h)$.
- **Cut-on wavelength:** $\lambda_c = \dfrac{1.24}{E_g(\text{eV})}\;\mu\text{m}$.
- **Materials:** CdS (visible, 540 nm), CdSe (red, 720 nm), PbS (NIR, 2.7 µm), InSb (MIR, 5.5 µm, cryo-cooled).
- **Spectral peak of CdS** matches the human eye peak (555 nm) — ideal for visible-light robotics.
- **Photoconductive gain** $G$ can be much greater than unity because carriers circulate during their lifetime.
- **Response time** is slow (10–250 ms for CdS); bandwidth ≈ 1/(2πτ) is a few Hz.
- **Voltage divider interface:** $V_{\text{out}} = V_b \cdot \dfrac{R_L}{R_L + R_{\text{LDR}}}$.
- **Internal vs. external photoelectric effect:** Photoresistors = internal. Do not confuse with photo-emissive devices.
- **Quantum efficiency η**, absorption coefficient α, photon flux Φ_q are key optical parameters.
- **Robotic applications:** Line-following, light-seeking/avoiding (Braitenberg vehicles), cliff detection, ambient-light sensing for adaptive display, sun-tracking, optical encoders.
- **Limitations:** Slow response, temperature sensitivity, hysteresis, non-linearity in $R$–$E$ curve, and spectral narrowness.
- **KTU exam hot topics:** Derivation of $G$, voltage-divider numerical, comparison table of materials, identifying the right material for a given wavelength requirement, and the difference between photoresistive, photovoltaic, and photo-emissive effects.
- **Final mnemonic — "I-B-C-P" for the four-step operation:** **I**lluminate → **B**reak bonds (EHP) → **C**onductivity rises → **P**ickup as voltage change at divider.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Photodiodes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT205 - SENSOR TECHNOLOGY FOR ROBOTICS (ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Photodiodes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Photodiode** is a semiconductor PN-junction optoelectronic device that converts incident optical (light) energy into a measurable electric current through the internal photoelectric effect. It is operated primarily under **reverse bias**, where the depletion region is widened and the device behaves as a light-controlled current source whose output is linearly proportional to the incident optical power within its spectral response band.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCRUT205, Module 4):**
> A photodiode is a two-terminal opto-electronic transducer in which the reverse-biased junction current varies as a function of the irradiance (W/m²) falling on the active semiconductor surface, governed by the energy bandgap $E_g$ of the base material.

Mathematically, the fundamental photo-conversion relationship is expressed as:

$$I_{ph} = q \cdot \eta \cdot \Phi_{\text{photon}}$$

Where:
- $I_{ph}$ = **Photocurrent** (Amperes)
- $q$ = Elementary charge $\approx \mathbf{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \; C}$
- $\eta$ = **Quantum Efficiency** (dimensionless, $0 \leq \eta \leq 1$)
- $\Phi_{\text{photon}}$ = Incident photon flux (photons/second)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rain Gauge" Intuition

Imagine a **cylindrical rain gauge** placed in an open field during a storm. The amount of water collected inside depends on how heavily it rains. Similarly:

- **Rain droplets** = Photons (light particles)
- **Gauge cross-section** = Active depletion area of the photodiode
- **Water level rise** = Generated photocurrent
- **Gauge material color** = Semiconductor bandgap (only certain wavelengths trigger the current)

A photodiode is essentially a *photon rain gauge* — the heavier the "photon rain" (brighter the light), the larger the current it generates. Reverse bias acts like a *drain valve* that constantly removes collected charge, preventing saturation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Photodiode vs. Phototransistor vs. Solar Cell — Key Distinction for KTU**
>
> | Device | Bias Mode | Output | Gain |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | Photodiode | Reverse bias | Current (μA scale) | Unity |
> | Phototransistor | Active region | Current (mA scale) | High ($h_{FE}$) |
> | Solar Cell | Zero bias (photovoltaic) | Voltage & Current | Unity |
>
> A photodiode is **NOT** a solar cell — it requires external biasing and is used for *sensing*, not power generation.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Constants for KTU Numerical Problems:**
> - Planck's constant: $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \; \text{J} \cdot \text{s}$
> - Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \; \text{m/s}$
> - $hc = 1240 \; \text{eV} \cdot \text{nm}$ (most useful form)
> - Elementary charge: $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \; \text{C}$
> - Boltzmann constant: $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \; \text{J/K}$

Standard performance metrics:
- **Responsivity ($R$)**: Amperes per Watt (A/W)
- **Dark Current ($I_d$)**: Reverse leakage current at zero illumination (nA to μA)
- **Rise Time ($t_r$)**: Speed of response (nanoseconds for PIN, picoseconds for APD)
- **Cutoff Wavelength ($\lambda_c$)**: Maximum detectable wavelength

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The photodiode's operation can be broken into five sequential physics steps. Understanding this chain is essential for KTU derivations and viva voce.

**Step 1: Photon Incidence & Energy Threshold**
A photon of wavelength $\lambda$ strikes the active depletion region. For absorption to occur, the photon energy must exceed the semiconductor bandgap:

$$E_{\text{photon}} = \frac{hc}{\lambda} \geq E_g$$

If $E_{\text{photon}} < E_g$, the photon passes through the device without contributing to photocurrent.

**Step 2: Electron-Hole Pair Generation (EHP)**
The absorbed photon transfers its energy to a valence-band electron, which jumps to the conduction band, leaving a hole behind. Each successful absorption event creates **one EHP**.

**Step 3: Carrier Separation by the Built-in Field**
The depletion region's intrinsic electric field (enhanced by reverse bias) immediately sweeps electrons toward the N-region and holes toward the P-region. Without this field, the carriers would simply recombine.

**Step 4: Drift Transport Across Depletion Region**
Carriers drift at their saturation velocities ($v_{sat} \approx 10^5 \; \text{m/s}$ for Si) across the depletion width $W$. The transit time limits high-frequency response:

$$t_{\text{tr}} = \frac{W}{v_{sat}}$$

**Step 5: External Circuit Current Collection**
The separated carriers exit through the contacts, contributing to the external current. The total reverse current is:

$$I_{\text{total}} = I_{\text{dark}} + I_{\text{ph}}$$

Where $I_{\text{dark}}$ is the temperature-dependent leakage current (unwanted) and $I_{\text{ph}}$ is the useful signal.

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This table consolidates every equation tested in KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examinations on Photodiodes.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Photocurrent | $I_{ph} = q \eta \Phi$ | A | Primary signal equation |
| 2 | Photon Flux | $\Phi = \dfrac{P_{\text{opt}}}{h\nu} = \dfrac{P_{\text{opt}} \lambda}{hc}$ | photons/s | Optical-to-quantum conversion |
| 3 | Responsivity | $R = \dfrac{I_{ph}}{P_{\text{opt}}} = \dfrac{\eta q \lambda}{hc}$ | A/W | Sensor sensitivity |
| 4 | Quantum Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{R \cdot hc}{q \lambda} = 1.24 \times \dfrac{R(\text{A/W})}{\lambda(\mu\text{m})}$ | dimensionless | Material figure of merit |
| 5 | Cutoff Wavelength | $\lambda_c = \dfrac{hc}{E_g} = \dfrac{1240}{E_g(\text{eV})}$ | nm | Spectral limit |
| 6 | Total Reverse Current | $I_R = I_{dark} + I_{ph}$ | A | Output equation |
| 7 | Dark Current (Empirical) | $I_{dark} = I_s \left( e^{qV/kT} - 1 \right) \approx I_s$ | A | Noise floor |
| 8 | Transit Time | $t_{tr} = \dfrac{W}{v_{sat}}$ | s | High-frequency limit |
| 9 | RC Time Constant | $t_{RC} = R_L C_j$ | s | Circuit bandwidth |
| 10 | Rise Time (Combined) | $t_r \approx 2.2 \cdot \sqrt{t_{tr}^2 + t_{RC}^2}$ | s | Speed metric |
| 11 | Noise Equivalent Power | $NEP = \dfrac{i_n}{R}$ | W/$\sqrt{\text{Hz}}$ | Detection limit |
| 12 | Specific Detectivity | $D^* = \dfrac{\sqrt{A}}{NEP}$ | cm·Hz$^{1/2}$/W | Material comparison |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these three primary equations — they appear in 90% of KTU questions:**
> 1. $\lambda_c = \dfrac{1240}{E_g (\text{eV})} \; \text{nm}$
> 2. $R = \dfrac{\eta q \lambda}{hc} \;\; \text{A/W}$
> 3. $I_{ph} = q \eta \dfrac{P \lambda}{hc}$

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **Robotics and Automation** (the domain of PCRUT205), photodiodes serve critical roles:

1. **Line-following robots** — Array of photodiodes detect contrast between black line and white floor.
2. **Proximity sensors** — IR photodiodes paired with IR LEDs detect obstacle distance via reflected intensity.
3. **Optical encoders** — Photodiodes read shaft position through slotted disks.
4. **LIDAR systems** — Avalanche photodiodes (APDs) detect returning laser pulses for autonomous vehicle navigation.
5. **Light curtain safety systems** — Industrial robots use photodiode arrays as safety interlocks.
6. **Spectrophotometers** — Wavelength-selective photodiodes enable material identification in quality control.

The **PIN photodiode** dominates robotics due to its fast response (ns scale), while **APDs** are reserved for long-range LIDAR applications requiring internal gain.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Photocurrent from Quantum Efficiency

**Problem Statement:** A silicon photodiode has quantum efficiency $\eta = 0.85$ and is illuminated by monochromatic light of wavelength $\lambda = 900 \; \text{nm}$ with optical power $P_{\text{opt}} = 10 \; \mu\text{W}$. Calculate the resulting photocurrent.

**Given:**
- $\eta = 0.85$
- $\lambda = 900 \times 10^{-9} \; \text{m}$
- $P_{\text{opt}} = 10 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{W}$
- $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \; \text{J} \cdot \text{s}$
- $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \; \text{m/s}$
- $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \; \text{C}$

**Step 1: Calculate the energy of a single photon.**

$$E_{\text{photon}} = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

Substituting values:

$$E_{\text{photon}} = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^{8})}{900 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$E_{\text{photon}} = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{9 \times 10^{-7}} = 2.2087 \times 10^{-19} \; \text{J}$$

**Step 2: Convert photon energy to electron-volts (for verification).**

$$E_{\text{photon}} = \frac{2.2087 \times 10^{-19}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 1.379 \; \text{eV}$$

This is greater than $E_g$ of silicon ($1.12 \; \text{eV}$), so absorption will occur.

**Step 3: Compute the incident photon flux.**

The photon flux is the total optical power divided by the energy per photon:

$$\Phi = \frac{P_{\text{opt}}}{E_{\text{photon}}} = \frac{P_{\text{opt}} \cdot \lambda}{hc}$$

$$\Phi = \frac{10 \times 10^{-6}}{2.2087 \times 10^{-19}}$$

$$\Phi = 4.527 \times 10^{13} \; \text{photons/second}$$

**Step 4: Apply quantum efficiency to find absorbed photons.**

Only $\eta = 85\%$ of incident photons successfully generate EHPs:

$$\Phi_{\text{effective}} = \eta \cdot \Phi = 0.85 \times 4.527 \times 10^{13}$$

$$\Phi_{\text{effective}} = 3.848 \times 10^{13} \; \text{EHPs/second}$$

**Step 5: Convert to photocurrent using elementary charge.**

Each EHP contributes one electron's charge to the external circuit per second:

$$I_{ph} = q \cdot \Phi_{\text{effective}} = q \cdot \eta \cdot \Phi$$

$$I_{ph} = (1.602 \times 10^{-19})(3.848 \times 10^{13})$$

$$\boxed{I_{ph} = 6.164 \; \mu\text{A}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation II — Responsivity in A/W

**Problem Statement:** For the same photodiode above, compute the spectral responsivity at $\lambda = 900 \; \text{nm}$.

**Given:** All values from Derivation I.

**Step 1: Use the direct definition.**

$$R = \frac{I_{ph}}{P_{\text{opt}}}$$

$$R = \frac{6.164 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{A}}{10 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{W}}$$

$$\boxed{R = 0.6164 \; \text{A/W}}$$

**Step 2: Verify using the master formula.**

$$R = \frac{\eta q \lambda}{hc}$$

$$R = \frac{(0.85)(1.602 \times 10^{-19})(900 \times 10^{-9})}{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^{8})}$$

$$R = \frac{1.2255 \times 10^{-25}}{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}$$
